summary: Read-only impl-readiness validation of the decentralized-config design (ADRs 0001-0020, P1-P17, design.md, requirements, code) across 10 lenses; adversarially verified, deduped, severity-ranked, with principle-aligned option analysis for decision-findings
logs: V impl-readiness review: 10 lenses over ADRs 0001-0020 + P1-P17 + living docs + code
37 findings need a maintainer decision -> generating principle-aligned options

findings.title: Gitignored secrets.env (and local-paths) on non-active profiles are silently lost by the flatten-from-branches vault backup
findings.issue: The legacy vault blanket-gitignores secrets.env/*.env/*.key/*.pem/.credentials.json. The design's backup 'iterates all profile branches and serializes their resources' and states non-checked-out profiles contribute only their committed state — but gitignored secrets are NEVER committed, so the per-branch serialization captures them for no profile (the active-profile capture is framed as 'uncommitted WIP', not gitignored secret files). Every project's real secrets.env across all profiles is dropped at backup time, and the legacy vault is then offered for removal — destroying the only surviving copy.
findings.why: AD12/ADR-0006 promise migration is 'lossless'. Secrets are the highest-value, hardest-to-recreate user data; losing them at the irreversible breaking cutover (old vault then removed) is irreversible data loss.
findings.proposedResolution: Decide and document that the backup must be a filesystem-level archive of the working tree INCLUDING gitignored files (secrets.env, local-paths.yml), not a git-tree serialization. Either (a) git checkout/worktree each profile and tar its full working tree (acknowledge non-active-profile secrets are genuinely unreachable and warn the user to commit/export them first), or (b) explicitly scope the loss and emit a pre-removal warning enumerating every gitignored secret file found. State the mechanism in ADR-0006 and design §9; add a test for secrets surviving migration.

findings.title: §9 phasing plan (Phases 0-3) predates the entire S-cycle/coordinate body — the largest block of E work has no phase home, and §11 test plan has no matching rows
findings.issue: §9 stops at Phase 3 and was written before ADR-0016/0017/0018/0019/0020. No phase is assigned to: embedded repo/llms/pack COORDINATE schema + migration, the 2×2 sharing verb wiring, lib/manifest.sh deletion + structure-based discovery, sync-before-publish fix, cco config validate, cco update --check, cco config protect, cco project internalize (new semantics), coords-lookup, and internalize-as-cache. All are hand-waved to '→ E' which §9 never defines/numbers. §11's test table mirrors this: rows exist only for Phases 0-3, so the riskiest new code (publish merge, token-leak) has no test contract. §3 says §9 'drives the phased implementation'.
findings.why: E is sequenced immediately after V. If the phase map an implementer follows omits roughly half the decided scope, that scope silently won't be built or will be built ad-hoc with no ordering/dependency discipline, and §11 gives no design-driven test contract for the new code (workflow.md mandates design-driven tests). (Note: the DATA-bucket J0 bootstrap IS assigned in Phase 2; producing the updated phase map is itself a chartered V deliverable, which is why this is high not blocker.)
findings.proposedResolution: Add explicit S-era phases to §9 (and matching §11 rows): put the embedded coordinate schema + migration in Phase 0 (layout); add Phase 4 'sharing realignment' = manifest deletion + structure-based discovery + init-at-first-publish + sync-before-publish fix + 2×2 verb wiring + nomenclature migration; Phase 5 'sharing extensions' = pack-coordinate schema+migration + three-layer pack resolution + cco config validate + internalize-as-cache + export --bundle-packs + cco project internalize + cco update --check + cco config protect + S8 checklist. Give 'E' a phase number so every '→ E' reference resolves to a scheduled slot. Add §11 test rows for each new phase (sync-before-publish no-clobber, structure-based discovery, pack-coordinate migration, validate reachability, S8 no-token-leak).

findings.title: requirements.md (a live 'authoritative' doc) still places internal data in the config bucket (~/.cco/tags.yml, ~/.cco/backups) and uses superseded CLI verbs (cco index refresh, ADRs 0001-0010, flat publish/install)
findings.issue: requirements.md still asserts the superseded model in multiple places: tags live at ~/.cco/tags.yml (AD2/FR-C4/RD-authoring row) — reversed to <data>/cco/tags.yml internal/DATA by ADR-0011/0015/0016; the vault backup goes to ~/.cco/backups/ (FR-M1) — moved to <state>/cco/backups/ by ADR-0016 D6/C1; FR-C2 names the retired verb cco index refresh --scan — folded into cco resolve --scan by ADR-0017 D2; the header + Artifacts cite 'ADRs 0001-0010' though 0011-0020 now refine the model; and line 310 lists flat 'existing publish/install' without the ADR-0018 projects-don't-publish / 2×2 qualifier (FR-C4 itself already states the 2×2, so the doc self-contradicts). design.md is corrected throughout; requirements.md was never propagated.
findings.why: requirements.md is one of the four named source-of-truth docs an implementer reads for the AD/FR contract. Following it, E would write internal data into the versioned/allowlisted config bucket ~/.cco (re-introducing the exact P6/C1/C4 leak the refactor removes), re-add the dropped !tags.yml allowlist line, wire a retired cco index command and removed project publish/install verbs, and never discover the ADRs that overturned these statements. Mitigated only because the byte-level authoritative doc (design.md) is correct and this review gates E.
findings.proposedResolution: Update requirements.md: AD2/FR-C4/RD-authoring row → tags at <data>/cco/tags.yml (DATA bucket; internal, CLI-managed cco tag add/rm; synced Axis-1 required, never team; ADR-0011/0015/0016, note the nature flip CLI-canonical→internal); FR-M1 → <state>/cco/backups/vault-<date>.tar.gz (STATE, machine-local, never synced; ADR-0016 D6/C1); FR-C2 → cco resolve --scan (drop cco index refresh; note cco path set/list as low-level editor; ADR-0017 D2); header + Artifacts → 'ADRs 0001-0020' with a one-line note that 0011-0020 refine the model post-2026-06-15; line 310 → 'publish/install (packs/templates only — 2×2 matrix, ADR-0018; projects use export/import only)'. Mirror design.md wording.

findings.title: `source` provenance relocation (config-bucket → DATA) is mislabelled 'Reuse', is a cross-file relocation, has a key-rename + relocation migration that is unspecified, and the machine-local `publish_target` datum is unaccounted for
findings.location: ADR-0016 D5 (lines 175-188); ADR-0019 §Open→E (lines 213,221-226); design.md §2.2 DATA block (lines 140-142); lib/paths.sh:82-88; lib/cmd-pack.sh:651-693,1024-1092; lib/cmd-project-update.sh:87,95-134,193-200,287-289; lib/cmd-project-install.sh:202-207; lib/cmd-remote.sh:146-161
findings.issue: Today the per-resource `source` file lives in the config bucket at <repo|pack>/.cco/source and uses keys `source:`/`path:` (plus `publish_target`, `updated`). ADR-0016 D5 requires it in DATA keyed by identity (<data>/cco/{packs,projects,templates}/<id>/source) with keys `url:`/`ref:`/`resource:` and 'no machine-specific leak'. Three gaps: (1) the table labels it 'Reuse' though it is a cross-bucket relocation that must move every call site and rewrite _cco_pack_source/_cco_project_source; (2) the migration is unspecified — neither the key rename (source→url, path→resource) nor the relocation is defined, so the migrator cannot be written from the design and a wrong guess silently breaks cco update/cco pack update which read path:/source:; (3) publish_target is a machine-local remote-registry NAME (resolved against the DATA `remotes` registry) that exists in code but appears in NEITHER the new schema NOR any ADR — leaving it in the required-synced DATA source file re-opens the dual-axis leak ADR-0013 closed; dropping it silently loses the publish convenience.
findings.why: E must write a migration that relocates and renames every installed .cco/source and decides publish_target's home; with target and source schemas disagreeing on three field names and publish_target unclassified, the migrator cannot be written, update commands break, and the P6/C4 leak the whole refactor removes is reopened.
findings.proposedResolution: Reclassify the ADR-0019 table entry from 'Reuse' to 'Relocate + Reuse-logic'. Add an explicit migration spec: enumerate legacy source fields (source,ref,path,updated,publish_target) → new DATA source fields (url,ref,resource,...); define relocation <repo>/.cco/source → <data>/cco/projects/<id>/source and <pack>/.cco/source → <data>/cco/packs/<name>/source; re-point _cco_pack_source/_cco_project_source (+ new template variant) and the ~6 call sites. Classify publish_target as a machine-local pointer into the never-synced remotes registry: move it to STATE (per-identity, never) or re-derive on demand from `remotes` by url match — it must NOT travel in the required-synced source file. Record in ADR-0016 D5 and schedule as a pack/project-scope migration in the new sharing phase.

findings.title: The new repos:/packs:/extra_mounts: project.yml schema breaks the existing YAML parsers (path-keyed/name-only) and the local-paths.sh @local resolver — these are parser/module rewrites, not field additions, all under bash 3.2
findings.location: design.md §2.4 schema (lines 235-300); lib/yaml.sh:291-312 (yml_get_repos keys on '- path:'), 350-410 (extra_mounts source/target), 414-427 (yml_get_packs); lib/local-paths.sh:1-1132 (@local + per-repo local-paths.yml)
findings.issue: yml_get_repos keys entries on '- path:' and emits path:name; the new schema has NO path (paths moved to the index) and uses '- name:' + url/ref, so the parser must be re-keyed. yml_get_packs strips only '- ', so a map entry '- name: foo' emits the literal 'name: foo'; the new pack map (url/ref/resource) requires real field extraction. yml_get_extra_mounts keys on '- source:'+'target:' but §2.4 uses '- name:'. Separately, local-paths.sh (1132 lines) is built on the @local marker convention and per-repo .cco/local-paths.yml — both deleted by the new index model; _sanitize/_extract/_restore go away and the resolve path must re-point to the STATE index. The design frames the resolver as 'reused' and the coordinate embed as 'additive', understating that entry-delimiters and entry-shapes change (silent mis-parse) and the whole storage/sanitize/marker layer is replaced. All new awk parsers must stay bash-3.2-clean.
findings.why: These parsers feed compose generation, sync, resolve, and validate. 'Embed inline'/'reuse' reads as low-effort but is concrete code-level rewrites; mis-parsing the new files or retrofitting the index behind the old @local/local-paths.yml plumbing re-introduces the C4/P6 per-repo internal file in the config bucket.
findings.proposedResolution: List the yaml.sh parser rewrites as explicit E deliverables of the schema-migration line: yml_get_repos → name-keyed +url/ref; yml_get_packs → map entries +url/ref/resource; reconcile extra_mounts head (name vs source/target). Soften §3/§5 'reused' to 'the prompt/clone resolution UX is reused; the storage layer (per-repo local-paths.yml + @local markers + sanitize/extract/restore) is replaced by the STATE index', and in §9 list the specific deleted functions (_sanitize_project_paths, _extract_local_paths, _restore_local_paths, the @local branches in _resolve_*/_project_effective_paths/_assert_resolved_paths). Add parser tests to §11 and confirm bash-3.2 (no declare -A) for all new line-oriented stores.

findings.title: The remotes registry is a single file (url+token together) inside the config bucket; the DATA/STATE token split (M3) is a real reimplementation, not a path remap
findings.location: design.md §2.2 (DATA remotes name→url; STATE remotes-token 0600); lib/cmd-remote.sh:98-203; lib/paths.sh:39-41 (_cco_remotes_file → $USER_CONFIG_DIR/.cco/remotes)
findings.issue: Today tokens and URLs co-habit one file under the config bucket: _cmd_remote_add writes name=url then name.token=token to the same file; remote_get_token/remote_resolve_token_for_url/_cmd_remote_list all grep name.token= from it. The new model splits de-tokenized name→url into DATA (synced cross-PC, required) and the token into STATE remotes-token (0600, never-sync). This requires a new STATE token store + read/write helpers, rewriting all 6 cmd-remote.sh functions, and removing the vault-git coupling at lines 123-127/172-174 (which git-remote-adds to $USER_CONFIG_DIR/.git — a vault being deleted). 'Reuses the source + remote_cache machinery' implies the store is reusable as-is.
findings.why: If tokens stay in the DATA remotes file, the required cross-PC sync of DATA propagates secrets across machines — a P11(b) security-invariant violation. The store must be split first; the magnitude (new STATE store + 6 function rewrites + decoupling from a deleted vault) must be an explicit E item.
findings.proposedResolution: Add an explicit E item to split the remotes store: DATA <data>/cco/remotes (name→url only) + STATE <state>/cco/remotes-token (0600, never synced); rewrite the 6 cmd-remote.sh functions + the paths.sh helper; drop the vault-git git-remote add/remove coupling (cmd-remote.sh:123-127,172-174); add a migration for existing in-bucket .cco/remotes.

findings.title: design.md §11 Test Plan has zero coverage for the entire S cycle and coordinate model, including the sync-before-publish data-loss defect and the S8 no-token-leak invariant
findings.issue: The test plan covers layout/index/sync/migrate/config/tags but has NO test cases for any S-cycle (ADR-0018/0019/0020) or coordinate-model (ADR-0016/0017) decision: pack/repo/llms coordinate schema + migration; sync-before-publish 3-way merge (the documented fast-forward-clobber defect in cmd-pack.sh — the lone data-loss bug in shipping code); 2×2 verb wiring (incl. projects-don't-publish guard); structure-based discovery; cco config validate reachability; cco update --check; internalize-as-cache; export --bundle-packs; cco config protect / S8 no-token-leak checklist (ADR-0020 D5 explicitly defines S8 as 'a checklist + tests').
findings.why: design.md is 'the single source of truth for the refactor' and §9 phases drive a design-driven test workflow (workflow.md). With no test rows, the riskiest, most defect-prone work (publish merge semantics, token-leak safety) ships with no defined verification contract — high rework/regression risk. This is the §11 twin of the §9 phasing gap (F2) and stems from the same not-updated-after-S root cause.
findings.proposedResolution: After the §9 phase home for S-cycle work is decided (F2), add §11 test rows: coordinate schema + migration (name-only→coordinate, idempotent); sync-before-publish (co-maintainer divergence → 3-way merge, no clobber); 2×2 verbs incl. project-has-no-publish guard; structure-based discovery + init-at-first-publish/merge-on-existing; three-layer pack resolution + collision; cco config validate reachability; internalize-as-cache prompt + export --bundle-packs dependency-closure; cco update --check; S8 no-token-leak checklist tests; cco config protect scaffold.

findings.title: The ~/.cco allowlist .gitignore is specified with three divergent member lists across ADR-0008, design.md §6.1, and ADR-0016 D8 — and none whitelists the must-commit secrets.env.example
findings.issue: ADR-0008 §3 fixes the allowlist as '* then !packs/ !templates/ !global/.claude/ !.gitignore'. design.md §6.1 extends it with setup*.sh/mcp-packages.txt/languages. ADR-0016 D8's authoritative ~/.cco tree adds committed files in NEITHER list: secrets.env.example (the C3 skeleton that MUST be committed per FR-S4), setup-build.sh, and languages/setup.sh/mcp-packages.txt. Because the allowlist is a *-deny whitelist, any committed file not explicitly !-whitelisted is silently un-stageable — secrets.env.example and setup-build.sh would be excluded.
findings.why: P1/FR-S4 say secrets.env.example is config that MUST be committed (the skeleton documenting required vars), and ADR-0016 D8 places it in ~/.cco committed; but the concrete ADR-0008 §3 line would gitignore it. An implementer wiring allowlist staging from the literal line ships a store where committed skeletons silently never reach git — a correctness bug surfacing only when a second PC pulls and finds no secrets.env.example. The three lists must be reconciled to one authoritative enumeration before E.
findings.proposedResolution: Fix the single authoritative ~/.cco allowlist to whitelist every committed member of the ADR-0016 D8 tree: !packs/ !templates/ !global/.claude/ !.gitignore !languages !setup.sh !setup-build.sh !mcp-packages.txt !secrets.env.example (confirm secrets.env stays denied). Record it once (design.md §6.1 or ADR-0016 D8) and annotate ADR-0008 §3 as superseded-in-detail. Verify the secret-scan .example exemption (FR-S3) keeps secrets.env.example stageable while blocking secrets.env.

findings.title: Backup artifact is self-contradictory: 'plain branchless flatten' vs 'preserves full vault history'; and the backup PATH conflicts across ADR-0006/FR-M1 (~/.cco) and design §2.2/§9 (STATE)
findings.issue: design §9:694-697 defines the backup as a 'plain backup (no git branches in the archive)' produced by flattening (line 702-703: 'no branch traversal'), yet line 706 claims 'the backup archive preserves full vault history' as the rollback story. A flattened branchless serialization cannot preserve git commit/branch history; a raw tar of the vault dir (incl .git) preserves history but is neither flat nor branchless. Separately, the backup PATH conflicts: ADR-0006 §Decision-2 and FR-M1 write to ~/.cco/backups/ while the C1 fix relocated it to <state>/cco/backups/ (design §2.2/§9, ADR-0016 D6) — and ~/.cco is authored-config-only per ADR-0008/P6.
findings.why: This is the central migration artifact; its exact shape determines whether secrets are captured (F1), whether rollback works, and whether the reader needs git. The history claim and the path conflict cannot both be implemented; following ADR-0006/FR-M1 writes the backup into the authored-only ~/.cco (P6 violation, allowlist could try to commit a secrets-laden archive). The dominant repeated spec (design §9 twice + FR-M1) is the flattened-plain form, so line 706 reads as stray wording — hence high not blocker.
findings.proposedResolution: Pick ONE artifact: (A) raw tar.gz of the whole vault dir incl .git (preserves history + on-disk gitignored secrets; reader may need to checkout/traverse), or (B) flattened plain export (no .git; profiles→tags metadata; NOT history-preserving — fix line 706 to say 'preserves all profile resources flattened with origin-profile tags'). Reconcile ADR-0006, FR-M1, and design §9. Separately, edit ADR-0006 §Decision-2 and requirements.md FR-M1 to write the backup to <state>/cco/backups/vault-<date>.tar.gz (STATE, machine-local, never synced; relocated from ~/.cco by ADR-0016 D6 / inventory C1), keeping design.md canonical.

findings.title: Profile→tag seeding timing/owner is split (first-run flatten vs per-project migrate) and packs/templates have no migrate entry point, leaving their profile→tag origin unreachable
findings.location: design.md §9:641-643 (per-project cco migrate prompt) vs :694-697 (flatten translates profile→tag); §9:523/534 (cco migrate is project-only); ADR-0010 §5:91-95; ADR-0016 D5:161-167 (tags.yml packs:/templates: keys)
findings.issue: Two contradictory placements: (1) design :641-643 + ADR-0010 §5 say cco migrate <project> PROMPTS per project to convert profiles→tags and seeds <data>/cco/tags.yml (so the user is prompted N times, lazily); (2) :694-697 says the FLATTEN at first-run backup already translates each profile into a tag. Worse, PACKS and TEMPLATES are never migrated per-project (no cco migrate <pack>; they land in ~/.cco directly) — so for pack/template profile→tag conversion there is no entry point at all, yet tags.yml typed keys include packs: and templates:. Their profile origin is captured nowhere actionable.
findings.why: tags.yml is the cross-PC-synced DATA replacement for profiles; if seeding is ambiguous or unreachable for packs/templates, the migration loses the profile organization for two of the three resource kinds. E cannot build a coherent prompt/seed flow from the contradictory specs.
findings.proposedResolution: Decide ONE seeding point. Recommended: seed tags.yml ONCE at first-run flatten time for ALL resources (projects + packs + templates), since all profiles are known then; make cco migrate <project>'s prompt a one-time global choice (convert-all vs untagged) recorded by a STATE marker, not a per-project question. Document where pack/template profile→tag seeding happens given they have no migrate command. Update design §9 and ADR-0010 §5 to agree.

findings.title: Memory relocation re-run is not specified as non-clobbering — a second cco migrate can overwrite accumulated machine-local memory (the only copy)
findings.issue: design :705 guarantees 'Re-runs never overwrite an existing .cco/ without confirm' — but that guard is scoped to the in-repo .cco/ only. The memory copy targets STATE <state>/cco/projects/<id>/memory/. After the first migrate the user accumulates new machine-local auto-memory there (memory is now STATE, no sync). If cco migrate <project> is re-run (advertised as idempotent), an unguarded copy-from-backup overwrites/merges the now-newer live memory with stale backup memory.
findings.why: ADR-0009 drops cross-PC memory sync as an accepted regression, making the single machine-local copy the ONLY copy. A re-runnable migrate clobbering it is irrecoverable, and the 'idempotent' claim is unsafe for the memory step as written.
findings.proposedResolution: Specify that the memory copy is skipped (or confirm-gated) when <state>/cco/projects/<id>/memory/ already exists/non-empty — copy-if-missing only, mirroring the .cco/ re-run guard. State this in ADR-0009 §6 and design §9 step 2; cover in test_migrate.sh (re-run preserves accumulated memory).

findings.title: Same project (or pack) name on multiple profile branches collides into one <id> at migrate, silently overwriting per-profile variants
findings.location: design.md §9:694 (flatten) + :640/§2.2:200 (<state>/cco/projects/<id>/memory/); ADR-0016 D5:185 (<id> = logical name); ADR-0009 §6:73; lib/cmd-start.sh:73-90 (per-branch project resolution)
findings.issue: In the legacy vault the SAME project name can exist on multiple profile branches with DIFFERENT content (cmd-start.sh resolves projects per-branch; isolation is by branch). The new model keys everything by logical name <id>. The flatten 'iterates all profile branches and serializes their resources' but has NO rule for what happens when profileA/projects/X and profileB/projects/X both exist: their .cco/, memory/, source/meta all land under the same <id> and overwrite/merge non-deterministically — losing one profile's variant. (Downgraded blocker→high: the stated profiles→tags model implies the same project across profiles is one resource carrying multiple origin tags, so divergent same-name-different-content is the edge case; with only ~2 known users the maintainer can inspect the real data.)
findings.why: Profiles exist precisely to let the same project differ per profile. Flattening to a single name-keyed namespace is a documented choice, but the COLLISION at migration is unhandled and lossy; a user with the same project name across profiles loses data, and E has no spec for it.
findings.proposedResolution: Define the collision policy in design §9 / ADR-0010 §5: the flatten must detect same-name-different-content across profiles and either (a) namespace the backup entry (project__profile) and require the user to choose at migrate, or (b) refuse/warn and require a rename. Specify how memory/, source, and .cco/ are handled for each variant. Add a test_migrate.sh case for the multi-profile same-name project.

findings.title: `cco project internalize` is labelled 'net-new (→ E)' but already exists in code with a different (project-source-disconnect / knowledge-copy) semantic under the now-abolished project-install model
findings.location: ADR-0019 §D3/D4 (lines 118-119), Reuse/Build-new table (line 218); lib/cmd-project-update.sh:230-311; lib/cmd-project-install.sh:9; lib/cmd-pack.sh:750-861 (cmd_pack_internalize); bin/cco:173
findings.issue: ADR-0019 D4 and the Build-new table list cco project internalize as net-new for E. But cmd_project_internalize already exists and does something different: it disconnects an INSTALLED project from its remote source (the central-vault install model ADR-0018 D2 abolishes), setting .cco/source to 'source: local'. The target D3/D4 semantic is 'cut the coordinate → self-contained source' — but a project carries no project-level coordinate (only its referenced packs/repos/llms do), so cco project internalize must actually mean 'vendor/internalize referenced packs' (export --bundle-packs territory), a different operation than pack-level cco pack internalize (which today also does a broader knowledge-source copy). No ADR reconciles the existing implementations vs the target semantics, and ADR-0018 retires the install model the existing command serves.
findings.why: Mislabeling existing code as net-new means E re-implements (regressing tested behavior — test_pack_internalize.sh exists) or is surprised by the collision; and lumping internalize under one verb for {pack,project} in the 2×2 hides that the two have different referents, so a user cannot predict what it does to their files.
findings.proposedResolution: Correct ADR-0019's 'net-new' claim (the verb exists) and change the Build-new verdict to 'Refactor'. Decide the fate of the existing project-install/internalize machinery under the no-project-publish model (ADR-0018 D2): either remove project install/internalize and define cco project internalize purely as 'vendor referenced packs into <repo>/.cco/packs as caches' (relate to export --bundle-packs), or keep a distinct sense and specify it. For cco pack internalize, change 'Reuse' to 'Refactor': internalize = delete the coordinate from the manifest (in-repo/~/.cco copy becomes authored source); drop/redefine the old knowledge-source copy step. Move internalize out of the 2×2 row into a 'Lifecycle' line and disambiguate per <res>. Note behavior changes for existing users.

findings.title: Manifest deletion is far deeper than 'delete lib/manifest.sh' (install/publish discovery is manifest-gated across 5+ files) AND has contradictory phase placement (Phase 3 vs E vs unscheduled), with Phase-2 cco init still calling manifest_init
findings.location: ADR-0018 D3; ADR-0012 Open:120-121; design.md §9 Phase 2:633-643 & Phase 3:644; resource-coherence-inventory.md:179; lib/cmd-init.sh:138, lib/cmd-vault.sh:137, lib/remote.sh:109, lib/cmd-pack.sh:469-518,1005-1006, lib/cmd-project-install.sh:105-222, lib/cmd-project-publish.sh:299-300
findings.issue: manifest.yml is the discovery mechanism: cmd_pack_install hard-dies 'no manifest.yml or pack.yml found' and lists packs via _manifest_get_names; cmd_project_install dies if no manifest.yml; _clone_for_publish calls manifest_init to seed an empty repo. Replacing this needs net-new structure-based discovery (git ls-tree packs/*/, templates/*/) plus init-at-first-publish without manifest_init. Placement is contradictory: inventory:179 says cleanup 'rides the Phase-3 cutover', §6.2/§12 say '→ E', and §9 names manifest in NO phase. Meanwhile Phase 2 introduces a NEW cco init while the current cmd-init.sh:138 calls manifest_init — so it must decide on day one whether to emit a manifest (then rip it out, churning brand-new code) or skip it (then in-between phases read a manifest init never wrote).
findings.why: An implementer reading 'delete lib/manifest.sh' may not realize it breaks cco pack/project install discovery entirely until the structure-based replacement lands (a prerequisite, not a follow-up). Contradictory placement means the dependency 'manifest removed BEFORE structure-based discovery' is neither stated nor ordered, and the new cco init has no instruction, risking keeping the §9 invariant (each phase runnable + tests green).
findings.proposedResolution: Pick one placement (recommend the new sharing phase) and make structure-based discovery an explicit prerequisite of the manifest deletion (same phase, before/with the delete). Enumerate the call sites to rewrite (cmd-pack.sh install + publish-init, cmd-project-install.sh, remote.sh _clone_for_publish, cmd-init.sh; note cmd_pack_install's single-pack pack.yml-at-root fallback to build on). Instruct Phase 2's new cco init to NOT emit/refresh a manifest from the start. Reconcile inventory:179 ('Phase-3 cutover') with the §6.2/§12 '→ E' statements.

findings.title: Phase-1 cco resolve / cco start require pack-coordinate healing & three-layer resolution, but the pack-coordinate schema lands in 'E' (later) and its layer-1 home ~/.cco is only stood up in Phase 3
findings.location: ADR-0019 D2 layer-b (line 75) & D5 (line 128, mount order ~/.cco/packs→url→<repo>/.cco/packs); design.md §9 Phase 1:626-632 (cco resolve) & Phase 3:632 (~/.cco config surface); §2.4:288-300; pack-coordinate schema+migration → E (§12, inventory:208-209)
findings.issue: ADR-0019 makes Phase-1 cco resolve responsible for healing missing PACK coordinates (D2 layer b) and cco start for three-layer pack resolution (D5), but the pack-coordinate schema, the name-only→coordinate migration, and the lib/packs.sh three-layer refactor are all 'E' work — unscheduled and after Phase 1. So Phase 1 cannot implement pack healing/resolution because the schema does not exist yet. Compounding this, the D5 mount order begins at ~/.cco/packs (layer 1), but the ~/.cco store management surface (cco config, allowlist staging) is deferred to Phase 3 — and no §9 statement orders the sharing phase strictly after Phase 3. Either Phase 1 ships a resolve that silently lacks pack support (retrofitted later, double-touching the resolution loop) or the pack work must be pulled earlier; neither is stated.
findings.why: Cross-phase dependency with no declared ordering: a Phase-1 command depends on a Phase-E schema and a Phase-3 store. Building Phase 1 to a repos/mounts-only model then bolting on pack healing in E re-touches the resolve/start loop twice and risks hard-coding a model that fights the unified P14 reachability model.
findings.proposedResolution: State the dependency and decide the order: either (a) build Phase 1's cco resolve/cco start resolution loop generic over referenced-resource coordinates (repos/llms/packs) from the start so the sharing phase only adds a backend (supported by §2.4's unified model), or (b) explicitly defer ALL pack-coordinate behavior incl. heal-at-resolve to the sharing phase and note in §9 Phase 1 that resolve covers repos+mounts only. In the updated §9, place the sharing phase(s) strictly after Phase 3 with a one-line dependency note (packs mount-layer-1 = ~/.cco/packs needs the Phase-3 ~/.cco store).

findings.title: sync-before-publish: the 3-way-merge mechanism is mandated but the merge base (installed commit) is unspecified, pack publish has no base/ ancestor today, and three concurrent refactors converge on the same publish function with no single owning spec
findings.location: ADR-0019 §D4 (lines 107-119), §Open→E (line 225); ADR-0018 §D3; ADR-0013 H6 (base/ → STATE); lib/cmd-pack.sh:932-1024 (clone HEAD, cp -R, push origin HEAD); lib/cmd-project-publish.sh:375-389; lib/remote.sh:86-118; lib/cmd-project-update.sh:187,295 (base/)
findings.issue: ADR-0019 D4 mandates cco pack publish sync-before-push (pull + 3-way merge against the installed commit) reusing the cco project update 3-way base/ merge, but: (1) a pack authored in ~/.cco/packs has no recorded source/base until after first publish, so the 3-way inputs (base/ours/theirs) are undefined for the author-then-first-publish path; (2) base/ is today only a project-update concept — packs have NO base/ ancestor; (3) the publish clones remote HEAD fresh, cp -R's local content on top, and pushes a descendant — so it clobbers the co-maintainer's remote state (the cloned-then-overwrite flow discards remote-only changes) rather than non-FF-failing; (4) ADR-0019 D4 + ADR-0018 D3 (init-at-first-publish, structure-based merge-on-existing) + ADR-0013 H6 (base/ → STATE) all touch the SAME cmd_pack_publish/_clone_for_publish function and merge engine, and while the ADRs cross-link, no single ADR states WHICH base/ ancestor the pack-publish merge uses now that base/ is in STATE.
findings.why: E is told to 'fix a defect' and 'reuse the 3-way merge' but the merge base, first-vs-subsequent distinction, base storage, and the convergence of three refactors on one function are unspecified — without one consolidated publish-path spec the multi-maintainer data-loss the ADR exists to close is not actually closed (the clobber recurs).
findings.proposedResolution: Produce one consolidated publish-path spec (in §6.2 or an E-handoff) that sequences: clone/treeless-discover (0018 D3) → sync-before-publish 3-way merge → init-or-merge commit → push. Define the merge base: first publish (no source) = init/push-if-FF; subsequent = record the published commit as the base (in DATA source / a CACHE base copy), then clone → 3-way merge (base = last-published commit, ours = local pack, theirs = remote HEAD) → abort on conflict. State which base/ ancestor pack publish uses now that base/ moved to STATE /update, and note the cross-dependency on the source-relocation item (F4). Correct ADR-0019's 'fast-forward clobber' wording to match the clone-then-overwrite behavior.

findings.title: Index 'global-vs-namespaced logical names' (H7) is a hidden DECISION deferred to E as 'impl-time', and atomicity/locking is unspecified
findings.issue: ADR-0016 D4 defines a single flat machine-global paths: name→abs-path index enforcing 'one logical name → one absolute path per machine' and asserts the global model ('a repo used by two projects = one paths: entry'), yet simultaneously defers 'the global-vs-namespaced name question (H7)' to E as impl-time — a contradiction. Global vs per-project namespacing is not a mechanism choice: it changes the index schema, the uniqueness invariant (AD5), and whether two projects can reuse a name for different repos. design §3:323-324 already documents the global-uniqueness constraint and the cco init/join refusal UX, so the design leans global; what is formally open is the explicit confirmation (namespaced reserved post-v1?) and the atomic-write/locking mechanism for concurrent resolve --scan + start.
findings.why: cco resolve, cco join's refusal logic, and compose mount generation all read this index. If E picks per-project namespacing later, the uniqueness invariant and join semantics must change — a redesign, not a code detail; and concurrent index readers/writers need a locking decision before Phase 0/1.
findings.proposedResolution: Decide global-vs-namespaced now. Recommend committing to the global-flat model already implied by D4 + design §3 (with the documented uniqueness + collision-refusal UX), so H7 reduces to pure mechanism (atomic write = temp-file + rename; state whether any lock is needed for concurrent readers/writers). Remove the contradictory 'deferred to E' framing for the schema/uniqueness half; if namespacing is intended instead, reserve it post-v1 with the global invariant fixed for v1.

findings.title: `cco new` (ad-hoc/temporary session) — a real top-level entry point sharing the compose/mount code being rewritten — is entirely unaddressed by the decentralized-config design
findings.location: design.md §7 (command table), §8 (journeys); requirements.md §3 + :310; bin/cco:141 (new) cmd_new); lib/cmd-new.sh
findings.issue: cco new is a real, shipped top-level entry (bin/cco:141; 'Sessions: new — Start a temporary session with specific repos') taking repeatable --repo <path> and generating its own docker-compose.yml with literal absolute mount sources, never touching project.yml or any index. The entire decentralized-config corpus (design §7/§8, requirements, all 20 ADRs) never mentions it; the §7 'Removed' list names only cco vault * and cco project create. The design does not say whether cco new survives post-cutover, whether it resolves through the STATE index, how it interacts with J0 first-run bootstrap, or whether it is dropped.
findings.why: E will rewrite path resolution (STATE index, J0 bootstrap, host-side resolver, absolute mounts in compose). cco new shares the compose-generation/mount-path code (cmd-start.sh territory). If its behavior is not specified, E either silently breaks an existing entry point or implements ad-hoc behavior diverging from cco start's H1 resolution order, and a user relying on cco new for scratch sessions has no documented path.
findings.proposedResolution: Decide: does cco new survive v1, and if so does it (a) bypass the index with literal --repo paths (no resolution/J0 index write), or (b) resolve through the same index/H1 ordering as cco start? Add a row to the §7 'Run' section (cco new [--repo <path>] — ad-hoc session, no project.yml, paths literal) and note in §8 that J0 still runs first. If dropped, state it explicitly in the §7 'Removed' list.

findings.title: Coordinate-add verbs (cco repo add, cco llms add) — the primary embed-at-add reachability mechanism (P14 layer a) — and cco config coords/validate are missing from the §7 command table
findings.location: design.md §7 (command table, lines 519-532) vs §2.4:270-271; ADR-0019 D2 layer-a (line 74); ADR-0016 D3; bin/cco; lib/cmd-llms.sh:34-39
findings.issue: P14 reachability rests on layer-a 'embed-at-add': cco repo/llms/project add-pack derives and embeds the coordinate so a shared project is resolvable by default. cco project add-pack/remove-pack exist and appear, but cco repo add and cco llms add (the verbs that embed repo/llms coordinates into project.yml) appear ONLY in prose and are absent from the §7 table — the only discoverable command reference. cco repo is a net-new namespace (no repo) case in bin/cco today); cco llms add is semantically distinct from today's cco llms install (download into CACHE). cco config coords --diff/--sync (D3) and cco config validate (D9/D2 layer c) are likewise absent from the table.
findings.why: §7 is the command contract for E. If embed-at-add (the layer that makes shared projects resolvable by default) plus the coordinate diff/sync/validate verbs are not enumerated, E may treat them as optional prose, weakening P14 to heal/warn-only; and the default-resolvability guarantee depends on users invoking discoverable verbs rather than hand-editing project.yml.
findings.proposedResolution: Add a 'Coordinates / references' row to the §7 table: cco repo add <name> [--url --ref] (net-new namespace), cco llms add <name> --url [--variant] (manifest-embed, distinct from cco llms install/CACHE download; auto-derive url from origin/coords-lookup), cco config coords --diff/--sync (ADR-0016 D3), cco config validate (ADR-0016 D9 / ADR-0019 D2) — with the layered-reachability note. Ensure cco project internalize (net-new per ADR-0019 D4) is listed alongside cco <res> internalize.

findings.title: Top-level repo README.md (the GitHub landing page) is entirely absent from the cutover inventory and carries multiple actively-false old-model references
findings.location: resource-coherence-inventory.md (README.md not listed; line 140 points to a different docs/user-guides/README.md) vs /workspace/claude-orchestrator/README.md:28,29,106,116,117
findings.issue: The single most user-facing document — the project front page README at repo root — is not inventoried anywhere. It carries actively-false references: line 28 'share your own with cco manifest' (manifest removed, ADR-0012); line 29 + 117 'Vault versioning — cco vault versions your user-config/ with git' (vault/user-config removed, ADR-0008); line 106 'cco project create my-app' (removed → init/join/migrate); line 116 'Config Repo sharing ... cco manifest to export' (Config Repo retired → sharing repo, ADR-0018; manifest removed). (Downgraded blocker→high: the front-page README is the most-discoverable file in any sweep and the fix is mechanical-once-flagged.)
findings.why: A doc-coherence sweep that omits the front-page README ships a release whose landing page promises a 'Vault versioning' feature and a 'cco manifest' command that no longer exist — exactly the 'capability promise that disappears / instructing users to run removed commands' failure the inventory's own Purpose says it exists to prevent. Without a line item, the sweep will demonstrably miss it.
findings.proposedResolution: Add a top-level entry (Section C, heavy rewrite) for the repo-root README.md: rewrite line 28/29 (vault→git on .cco + cco config; manifest→structure-based sharing repo), line 106 (cco project create→init/join/migrate), the 'Config Repo sharing' feature row (→ sharing repo, drop cco manifest), and the 'Vault versioning' feature row (→ cco config save/push/pull + per-user tags). Phase 3. Disambiguate from docs/user-guides/README.md.

findings.title: internal/tutorial/ subtree (interactive tutorial + setup-* skills, runtime-used) is entirely missing from the inventory and is built end-to-end on the old model
findings.issue: The framework's runtime onboarding tutorial under internal/tutorial/ is built on the old model: CLAUDE.md:88-205 hard-codes /workspace/user-config/ (projects/, packs/, global/.claude/); setup-project/SKILL.md and setup-pack/SKILL.md instruct 'cco project create --template config-editor' and create under user-config/projects|packs/; tutorial-behavior.md references 'cco project create' and 'cco vault push'; project.yml mounts target /workspace/user-config. The inventory grep'd templates/ and defaults/ but never enumerated internal/. The org-policy CLAUDE.md defines internal/tutorial/ as a runtime-used resource, so it is in the inventory's stated scope.
findings.why: These setup-* skills are near-duplicates of the config-editor skills the inventory rates as THE primary hotspot (A.1) — same cco vault save reminders, same user-config/ paths. At cutover the tutorial would actively walk a new user through removed commands; with zero coverage the E sweep will not touch it.
findings.proposedResolution: Add a new inventory subsection (e.g. A.4 'internal/tutorial — runtime tutorial, substantial rewrite') enumerating internal/tutorial/.claude/CLAUDE.md, the three SKILL.md files, tutorial-behavior.md, and project.yml, with the same user-config→~/.cco / <repo>/.cco + cco config/init/join/migrate remap as A.1. Phase 3.

findings.title: ADR-0002 Decision still says the index records 'tags' and lists the retired cco index refresh --scan verb, with no refinement back-pointer
findings.issue: ADR-0002 §Decision-2 says the index 'also records project → [member repo names] and tags'; §Decision-3/§Open list cco index refresh --scan. Tags were removed from the index by ADR-0010 §4 and ADR-0016 D4; the cco index refresh verb was retired by ADR-0017 D2. ADR-0002 carries an §Open annotation for RD-paths→ADR-0007 but NONE for the tags-out-of-index reversal or the CLI rename. The forward-annotation convention (refining ADR annotates) is followed elsewhere; ADR-0010/0016/0017 do annotate forward, so this is recoverable — but a reader landing on ADR-0002 first sees 'index records tags' as a live decision.
findings.why: Of the refinement chain, this is the one earlier ADR whose non-superseded Decision text asserts a fact later ADRs reversed WITHOUT a back-pointer. Risk is reader confusion, not wrong build (design.md/inventory are correct).
findings.proposedResolution: Add a one-line refinement note to ADR-0002 §Decision-2 ('tags removed from the index → per-user <data>/cco/tags.yml, ADR-0010/0015/0016') and §Decision-3/Open ('CLI consolidated onto cco resolve [--scan], ADR-0017 D2; cco index refresh retired'). Annotate, do not rewrite (no-edit-history convention).

findings.title: ADR-0014 D2 table still presents the coordinate as 'a canonical registry, stored once' — reversed to per-unit embedding by ADR-0016 D2, with no inline back-pointer
findings.issue: ADR-0014 D2's coordinate cell says it lives in 'a canonical registry, stored once (placement → M)'. ADR-0016 D2 reverses this: the coordinate is decentralized, embedded per-unit in the versioned manifest (package.json model), NOT a central registry — because the by-construction-shared repo has no publish boundary (P5). ADR-0016 forward-annotates ADR-0014 as 'refined' and P12 records the correction, but ADR-0014's own D2 cell carries no inline 'refined by 0016' marker. ADR-0016 D8 explicitly forbids 'no central coordinate registry file'.
findings.why: This is the most-referenced refinement chain (0014→0016→0017→0019); ADR-0019 D1 and the pack-coordinate model build on per-unit embedding. A reader who opens ADR-0014 (which NAMES the coordinate model) before ADR-0016 could scaffold a ~/.cco coordinate registry file that ADR-0016 D8 forbids. Mitigated to confusion-risk by the forward-annotation + P12.
findings.proposedResolution: Add an inline note to ADR-0014 D2 after the table: 'Placement REFINED by ADR-0016 D2: the coordinate is embedded per-unit in the versioned manifest, NOT a central registry (the by-construction-shared repo has no publish boundary, P5). The category and DRY-by-name intent stand.'

findings.title: design.md §1 architecture diagram/bullets are stale: 'Domain A (managed, deferred)' / 'depth deferred' and the retired term 'Config Repos'
findings.location: design.md:62-63 (diagram edges), :68 (bullet '~/.cco/ … Domain A; depth deferred')
findings.issue: The §1 diagram labels the personal-remote edge 'Domain A (managed, deferred)' and the node 'Config Repos'; the bullet says '~/.cco/ … (Domain A; depth deferred)'. ADR-0008 RESOLVED the ~/.cco versioning/management model (explicit manual commits + allowlist + cco config push/pull) — no longer 'deferred'; 'Config Repos' uses the term ADR-0018 D1 retired in favor of 'sharing repo'. §1 was not touched by the M or S realignment (header note lists only §2.1/2.4/6.2/7/12).
findings.why: §1 is the first thing an implementer reads. 'Domain A deferred' falsely signals the personal-store management is undesigned (it is fully specified in §6.1/ADR-0008), and 'Config Repos' perpetuates the retired nomenclature the S cycle is migrating doc-wide.
findings.proposedResolution: Relabel the diagram edge to 'Domain A (cco config push/pull, ADR-0008)' and the node 'Config Repos' → 'sharing repo'; change the bullet '(Domain A; depth deferred)' → '(Domain A; management = ADR-0008)'. Update the §1 status note to include §1 in the realigned sections.

findings.title: extra_mounts schema change (M5): §2.4 uses '- name:' but code/FR use '- source:'/'target:'; the machine-agnostic container target has no home in the new schema
findings.location: design.md §2.4:257-259, §3:268,310-312; requirements.md AD5:96; decisions/0016 §Open→E:380; lib/yaml.sh:350-410; lib/local-paths.sh:515-560
findings.issue: The new project.yml gives extra_mounts entries only name + readonly, with the absolute host path from the index. The current parser/resolver are built on source: (host path) + target: (container mount point); the new name-only schema has NO field for the container target. repos deterministically map to /workspace/<name>, but extra_mounts previously had an explicit arbitrary target (e.g. mounting assets at a non-/workspace path) and the new schema provides no way to express it. M5 is punted to E ('Best resolved against real code') with no decision.
findings.why: Without a rule for the container target (drop target and always mount at /workspace/<name>? keep a separate machine-agnostic target field?), E cannot generate the compose mount for extra_mounts, and the migration from source:/target: cannot be written — blocking the extra_mounts portion of Phase-0 compose generation.
findings.proposedResolution: Decide the new extra_mounts shape in §2.4: - name: (host path from index) + optional machine-agnostic target: (default /workspace/<name>) + readonly:. Specify the migration source→index entry + name, target preserved. Update §3 to note the index supplies only the host path for the mount name, and feed both into the yaml.sh parser rewrite. Promote M5 from impl-detail to a stated schema decision before Phase 0.

findings.title: cco config validate contract: predicates listed, but argument scope, per-failure exit codes, output format, the mechanical machine-agnostic/absolute-path detection, hook-installer existence, and heal-backfill non-TTY semantics are unspecified
findings.issue: D9 pins exit-code-only + 'cf. cco sync --check' + never-blocks, and gives three predicates (every referenced id has a coordinate; ids unique per section; machine-agnostic). Still unspecified: does cco config validate take a project/--all argument or scan cwd; per-failure-class exit codes; output format (per-id table vs counts); how 'machine-agnostic / no real paths' is detected mechanically (regex for absolute paths?); whether the optional pre-commit/push hook is installed by a command or hand-wired (D2 layer d says 'wired into the user's git hook' with no installer); whether 'reachable coordinate' means presence-only or a network fetch; and whether heal-at-resolve backfill is TTY-only and a no-op in non-TTY/CI (cco resolve already cannot prompt non-TTY).
findings.why: This command is the keystone of P14 reachability (layers c/d) and ADR-0020 D4's footgun mitigation. E cannot implement it as a contract test without the exact I/O, and the present-vs-reachable + non-TTY-heal forks determine offline-CI/silent-mount behavior.
findings.proposedResolution: Pin in design.md §7 / ADR-0019 D9: invocation (cco config validate [project|--all], cwd-first), per-failure exit codes (0 clean; 1 reachability gap; 2 non-machine-agnostic), output (one line per failing id + summary), the absolute-path detection rule, validate checks coordinate PRESENCE + uniqueness + machine-agnosticism only (no network) by default with an optional --reachable probe, heal/backfill is TTY-interactive only and a no-op (one-line notice, never blocking) in non-TTY, and whether a hook installer command exists or docs show a manual snippet.

findings.title: cco config protect contract: scaffolded CODEOWNERS content/location and per-host instruction text are unspecified, the literal <repo>/.cco/CODEOWNERS location is ineffective on GitHub, and the ship-in-v1 decision is open
findings.issue: ADR-0020 D4 says cco config protect 'scaffolds a <repo>/.cco/CODEOWNERS and prints platform-specific instructions'. Unspecified: the CODEOWNERS body, host detection (parse origin url?), and per-host text. GitHub honors CODEOWNERS only at repo root, .github/, or docs/ — a file at <repo>/.cco/CODEOWNERS would NOT be recognized, so the literal scaffold cannot enforce review routing (a no-op). The ADR also leaves 'ship in v1 or doc-only' as an open maintainer decision (recorded preference: docs baseline + opt-in helper), while §7 lists it as a committed-looking surface — so the scope of E depends on the undecided choice and a phase map should not schedule an item whose in/out status is unresolved.
findings.why: The scaffold as literally described is ineffective on GitHub, so E would build a no-op; and E cannot scope the task (CODEOWNERS scaffold + per-host Rulesets/Gitea/GitLab emitter vs guide copy) until the ship/defer decision is made.
findings.proposedResolution: Confirm the recorded preference (documentation baseline in v1; helper deferred) or commit to building it. If shipping, specify: CODEOWNERS at a host-recognized location (repo root or .github/) with a /.cco/** rule, a host-detection rule from origin, and the exact per-host instruction strings, noting GitHub's CODEOWNERS location constraint. Mark its status clearly in the updated §9/§7 so 'optional' does not become 'unscheduled'.

findings.title: `languages` is the sole 'config exception' kept in ~/.cco but code shows it is CLI-generated (auto-generated, written by cco init), contradicting the authored-only bucket precondition (P1/P6)
findings.location: ADR-0013 §D4; ADR-0016 D8 / authoritative table (languages → ~/.cco config; mutator 'user (IDE)'); guiding-principles P1; lib/update-meta.sh:45-70, lib/cmd-init.sh:40-78
findings.issue: ADR-0013 D4 classifies languages as config (a user preference that regenerates language.md) and ADR-0016 D8 places a hand-edited ~/.cco/languages file, insisting ~/.cco stays authored-content-only as the precondition for the clean git-repo model (P6). But in current code languages is NOT hand-edited: it lives inside .cco/meta marked '# Auto-generated by cco — do not edit', is read by _read_languages, and is set by cco init (interactive prompt / --lang) which then substitutes placeholders to GENERATE language.md. So languages is cco-written/CLI-driven, while ADR-0016 lists its mutator as 'user (IDE)'. Either languages is hand-edited (then where does the generator producing language.md fit?) or it is cco-written (then it cannot sit in an authored-only bucket per D8/P6).
findings.why: If languages is cco-generated it violates P1/P6 by sitting in the authored-only ~/.cco; E needs to know whether to put a generator on it (then it cannot be authored-only) or treat it as pure user-edited config, and how the meta-extraction migration preserves cco init's prompt flow.
findings.proposedResolution: State explicitly that languages is user-hand-edited (IDE) and language.md is the GENERATED artifact (which belongs in CACHE/overlay, not committed) — or, if languages itself is cco-written, reclassify it out of ~/.cco. Resolve which side of P1 it sits on and align ADR-0013 D4 / ADR-0016 D8 with the code reality and the cco init prompt flow.

findings.title: Three pack resolution locations + url-presence discriminator: <repo>/.cco/packs/X is polymorphic (source XOR cache by an in-file flag) and local-wins precedence can silently shadow a stale authored pack
findings.issue: A pack can be (1) ~/.cco/packs/X working copy, (2) url-fetched into ~/.cco/packs, (3) <repo>/.cco/packs/X with-coordinate (cache) or (3') without-coordinate (authored source). Whether <repo>/.cco/packs/X is source or cache is determined SOLELY by coordinate presence — one in-file flag with opposite lifecycle meaning (P15 source vs cache), an SRP-violating polymorphic location disambiguated at read time. The mount-precedence (local-first) can silently shadow a stale authored-in-repo pack with a same-named global one; D5 says validate 'flags' the collision but mount picks ~/.cco — a silent-wrong-build risk, not just a warning. repos and llms each have ONE resolution backend; packs alone have three layers plus a polymorphic location.
findings.why: This is the highest-complexity corner of the model and is net-new for E; the polymorphic location and local-wins shadowing are real footgun surfaces where a same-named authored pack masks an upstream one (or vice-versa). (The warn-not-block stance is a deliberate accepted P14/P17 trade-off, hence medium.)
findings.proposedResolution: On paper: (a) state the invariant that <repo>/.cco/packs/X MUST have a coordinate iff it is a cache, and that cco config validate treats a no-coordinate in-repo pack that ALSO exists at ~/.cco/packs/X as an ERROR (not a warning), since mount-precedence would shadow it; (b) add a worked example table of all 3-layer combinations with the resolved winner, so E codes one resolver, not ad-hoc branches.

findings.title: Old maintainer-design subtrees (vault/, sharing/, resource-lifecycle/) get no supersede/retire decision and will silently contradict the new design after cutover
findings.issue: The inventory excludes the NEW decentralized-config/ tree as source-of-truth but is silent on ~15 sibling maintainer-design docs that fully describe the model being removed (vault profiles, Config Repos + manifest, @local + local-paths, resource-lifecycle .cco-remotes migration). It cites vault/local-path-resolution-design.md only as a 'retired' link target without an action to mark the whole subtree superseded. Per the org documentation rule, superseded design docs should be marked superseded and linked to the successor, not left contradicting the new design.
findings.why: After cutover these maintainer docs will silently contradict the decentralized-config design with no superseded banner — future agents/maintainers re-discovering settled questions. Lower user-facing risk than README/tutorial, but a real doc-coherence gap the sweep should decide on.
findings.proposedResolution: Add an inventory item (a 'maintainer-design history' subsection): decide the disposition of docs/maintainer/configuration/{vault,sharing,resource-lifecycle}/ — recommended: add a 'Superseded by docs/maintainer/configuration/decentralized-config/' header to each at Phase-3 cutover (preserve as history, do not delete). Maintainer to confirm retire-vs-archive policy.

findings.title: Docs index/navigation READMEs carry stale old-model TOC descriptions (vault/profiles/Config Repos/manifest) and are not inventoried
findings.location: docs/README.md:9,38,87; docs/maintainer/README.md:14,36,45; docs/maintainer/configuration/README.md:14,15,34; docs/user-guides/README.md:18 — none in resource-coherence-inventory.md §C
findings.issue: The four docs index files route users with descriptions of removed features: 'vault, profiles, Config Repos', a 'Vault & Multi-PC Sync' link, 'Config Repos, manifest ... vault profiles'. The inventory C-sections list the target guides but not these index/landing pages.
findings.why: Index pages are the discovery entry point; if they still advertise vault/profiles/manifest sections after the underlying guides are rewritten, the navigation contradicts the content and points at deleted sections. A coherence sweep needs them itemized so link text + TOC rows are realigned (and any link to a retired vault/ design doc fixed).
findings.proposedResolution: Add a C.4 (or dedicated 'docs index pages') row listing the four README index files with: drop 'vault/profiles/Config Repos/manifest' from TOC rows; relabel to git-on-.cco + cco config + per-user tags + sharing repo. Phase 3.

findings.title: S-era 'Config Repo' → 'sharing repo' nomenclature realignment for user docs is not flagged anywhere (43 occurrences across 9 docs)
findings.location: resource-coherence-inventory.md (no user-doc action for the rename) vs 43 'Config Repo' occurrences across README.md + docs/getting-started/concepts.md + docs/reference/cli.md + docs/user-guides/{authentication,configuration-management,knowledge-packs,project-setup,README,structured-agentic-development}.md
findings.issue: ADR-0018 D1 / P13 retired the term 'config repo' for the 'config bucket' vs 'sharing repo' distinction. 43 user-facing occurrences of 'Config Repo' remain across 9 docs. The inventory predates S for its C-section tables and never adds a sweep action item for this rename; the Old→New marker legend and cross-cutting patterns do not mention it.
findings.why: Without an inventory line, the sweep will leave 'Config Repo' (a retired term) scattered through user docs alongside the new 'sharing repo' term used in the rewritten sections — inconsistent vocabulary in the same release.
findings.proposedResolution: Add a row to the Old→New legend: 'Config Repo (term)' → 'sharing repo (ADR-0018 D1/P13)'; and a cross-cutting pattern entry noting the term must be swept across all 9 user docs. Phase 3.

findings.title: concepts.md and knowledge-packs.md misclassified as C4 'light/mechanical' but contain the removed manifest + Config-Repo sharing workflow and the old central-llms model
findings.issue: The C4 entries describe these as light path/command substitution. But concepts.md:78-83 has a full 'Sharing packs and project templates (Config Repos)' block calling 'cco manifest refresh generates a manifest.yml' (manifest removed, ADR-0012); knowledge-packs.md:284-295 has the same publish workflow plus line 304 'LLMs.txt files are stored centrally in user-config/llms/' (llms→CACHE content + per-unit coordinate, ADR-0014/0016). These are structural rewrites, not mechanical path swaps.
findings.why: Misclassifying as C4-light underestimates the work and risks a sweep that does a path s/// pass and leaves the removed 'cco manifest refresh / manifest.yml' workflow text intact — instructing users to run a deleted command. Severity/phase estimation drives sweep effort allocation.
findings.proposedResolution: Promote both files from C4 to C3 (medium): note the manifest-removal + Config-Repo→sharing-repo rewrite at concepts.md:78-83 and knowledge-packs.md:284-295, and the llms centralization rewrite at knowledge-packs.md:304 (→ CACHE content + per-unit coordinate). Keep the path/cco-vault-push swaps already listed.

findings.title: §6.2 implementation note lists cmd-project-publish.sh / cmd-project-install.sh as merely 'revised' although ADR-0018 D2 removes the project publish/install surface, and the 2×2 row is mislabelled 'transform' though 6 verbs are net-new and 2 removed
findings.location: design.md §6.2:511-513, §7:530 (Sharing row 'transform'); ADR-0018 D2; bin/cco:167-181,206-218; lib/cmd-template.sh:55-58
findings.issue: §6.2 says '(cmd-project-publish.sh, cmd-project-install.sh, cmd-pack.sh, cmd-remote.sh, remote.sh) is revised accordingly', but ADR-0018 D2 removes cco project publish/install entirely (projects get export/import only) — those two files implement removed verbs, so they are deleted/repurposed, not 'revised'. Separately, §7 marks the whole Sharing 2×2 row 'transform' though the verb inventory shows pack has no import, project has no export/import (and project publish/install are removed), and template has none of publish/install/export/import — so the matrix needs 6 net-new verbs (pack import; project export/import; template publish/install/export/import) plus 2 removals. ADR-0019 D7 also says templates are scaffold-only with no live reference, sitting oddly next to giving them publish/install.
findings.why: 'Revised'/'transform' sets the wrong scope/estimation: an implementer may preserve the project-publish path instead of removing it, or leave templates at create/remove only, breaking the claimed pack/template symmetry; and the matrix risks shipping half-built (e.g. import advertised but undispatched).
findings.proposedResolution: Reword §6.2:511-513 so cmd-project-publish.sh/cmd-project-install.sh are removed-or-reduced-to-export/import (project publish/install verbs deleted, ADR-0018 D2) while cmd-pack.sh/cmd-remote.sh/remote.sh are revised. In §7 split the Sharing row into existing-verb refactors (pack publish/install, project internalize) vs net-new (pack import; project export/import; template publish/install/export/import) vs removals (project publish/install), marking the net-new Build-new. Reconcile ADR-0018 D2 (templates full 2×2) with ADR-0019 D7 (templates scaffold-only): if templates only export/import, correct §6.2/ADR-0018 accordingly. Add the missing import/export test cases to §11.

findings.title: Carried items H6/M3/H7 are inconsistently phased: H6 named in Phase 3 AND handed to 'E' AND 'absorbed'; M3/H7 only in 'E' with no phase
findings.location: design.md §9 Phase 3:656-657 + §11:729 (H6); analysis-roadmap.md:157,219-220; resource-coherence-inventory.md:207; M3/H7 → E with no phase
findings.issue: H6 (merge-engine base/meta path remap to STATE) is placed in Phase 3 (design:656-657, tested at §11:729), in 'E' (analysis-roadmap:157, inventory:207), AND characterized as 'absorbed by M/R3' (analysis-roadmap:219-220) — three placements. M3 (cmd-remote.sh token/registry decoupling) and H7 (index concurrency/namespacing) appear only in 'E' with no phase relative to 0-3. M3 (de-tokenizing the remotes registry) is a prerequisite for the S8 no-token-leak invariant (ADR-0020 D5).
findings.why: Double/triple placement means an implementer may do H6 twice or skip it; M3 is an undeclared prerequisite for the sharing-phase S8 invariant; H7 floats unanchored. H6 touches the cco update merge engine which interacts with both the Phase-3 base/meta→STATE move and the sharing-phase publish/update merge (sync-before-publish reuses the 3-way base/).
findings.proposedResolution: Pick a single phase for H6 (recommend Phase 3, co-located with the base/meta→STATE relocation; remove the duplicate 'H6 → E'/'absorbed' claims from roadmap/inventory). Place M3 explicitly before the sharing phase (S8 depends on the de-tokenized registry; Phase 3 is natural since cmd-remote.sh is touched there). Place H7 (index concurrency) in Phase 1 (where the index is built) or note it as a hardening follow-up.

findings.title: Coordinate schema + migration (repos/llms — pre-S) is unscheduled in §9 and its placement drifted pre-S→S→E across documents, contradicting AD12 'new layout only'
findings.location: design.md §2.4:235-300, §9 Phase 0:607-625; ADR-0016 D2 / ADR-0017 D1; resource-coherence-inventory.md:188 ('llms:/repos: schema/migration → S')
findings.issue: The project.yml coordinate model (embedded url/ref/variant per repos[]/llms[]) was decided pre-S and §2.4 makes it core to the project.yml shape. Phase 0 creates 'New committed .cco/ (logical names only, new layout only)' but says nothing about the coordinate fields, while §2.4 says project.yml carries coordinates from the start. The inventory punts 'llms:/repos: schema/migration → S' and S punts schema to 'E' — so the coordinate schema Phase-0's project.yml must contain is scheduled nowhere, and its placement drifted across docs.
findings.why: Phase 0 writes the canonical project.yml; if coordinates are part of that schema (§2.4 says they are), Phase 0 must include them — otherwise Phase 0 ships a project.yml a later phase must schema-migrate, contradicting AD12's 'new layout only, no dual-read'. The schema's phase home is genuinely ambiguous and the docs disagree.
findings.proposedResolution: Decide where the project.yml coordinate fields land: either (a) Phase 0 (layout) defines the full coordinate-bearing project.yml schema (repos/llms url/ref/variant), with only the pack coordinate added in the sharing phase; or (b) explicitly note Phase 0 ships logical-names-only and coordinate fields are an additive Phase-4 step. Reconcile inventory:188 ('→ S') with §12/§6.2 ('→ E').

findings.title: pack name-only→coordinate migration: no pack migration scope/dirs exist and the backfill derivation rule (which legacy packs acquire a url vs stay bare) is unspecified, severing DRY/update if guessed wrong
findings.location: ADR-0019 §D1/D2/D3 (lines 50-105), §Open→E (lines 221-226); design.md §2.4:261-266; migrations/ (only global/ and project/ dirs); CLAUDE.md / .claude/rules/update-system.md (list pack/template scopes); lib/cmd-project-pack-ops.sh:103-112
findings.issue: ADR-0019 mandates packs: name-only → {name,url?,ref?,resource?} migration, and CLAUDE.md/update-system.md list pack and template as migration scopes — but migrations/ on disk has only global/ and project/, project.yml packs are bare list items today, and the backfill rule is unspecified: for a name-only pack referencing a previously-installed pack with a source the url is derivable, but for a purely-local authored pack url stays absent (the D3 discriminator = url-absence = authored-in-repo P15 source). No 'embed-at-migrate' layer is specified to match embed-at-add.
findings.why: This is a schema-breaking change to a user-owned file (project.yml). Without the derivation rule the migrator would fabricate wrong coordinates or silently reclassify every existing referenced pack as authored-in-repo source, severing DRY/update propagation (P15) — the precise gap ADR-0019 exists to close. (Adjusted blocker→high originally, then aligned to medium here: it is a Phase-3 migration item whose discriminator semantics are defined and whose obvious rule is the proposed resolution.)
findings.proposedResolution: Define the pack-coordinate migration as an 'embed-at-migrate' layer (uniform with embed-at-add): for each name-only packs: entry, look up the installed pack's DATA source and backfill url/ref/resource when present; if no source (locally authored), leave url absent (intentional authored-in-repo; validate later flags if shared). Add the pack/template migration scope directories. Document in the new sharing/Phase-3 migration and the ADR-0019 E hand-off.

findings.title: cco resolve --scan index-rebuild semantics undefined: merge vs clobber, and scan-time name-conflict policy — risking silent loss of cco path set overrides
findings.issue: cco resolve --scan <dir> '(re)builds the index' by scanning for .cco/project.yml. Undefined whether it merges discovered paths into the existing index (preserving cco path set overrides and out-of-scan-dir entries) or replaces it wholesale (losing externally-installed/manually-set entries). The absorbed cco index refresh's merge behavior isn't carried over, and the conflict policy when a scan finds a name already bound to a different path is unspecified. The design leans toward merge+refuse (AD5 refusal documented in §3:323-324; cco path set retained as a sibling) but never says it for --scan.
findings.why: On a fresh machine a destructive rebuild is fine, but on an existing machine a clobber would wipe cco path set overrides and out-of-scan-dir mappings — silent data loss of machine-local state. E needs the merge-vs-replace decision and conflict policy to implement it safely.
findings.proposedResolution: Specify in §3: --scan merges (upsert) discovered name→path entries into the existing index, never deletes entries outside <dir>; on a name-already-bound-to-different-path conflict apply the AD5 refusal (warn + keep existing, or prompt). Note bootstrap (empty index) is the trivial case.

findings.title: sync-meta last-synced fingerprint: format deferred (fair) but the load-bearing write/clear semantic that drives Case-B-vs-Case-C selection is under-defined
findings.issue: The fingerprint format/rollback richness are deferred as impl details (fair, a content hash), but the SEMANTIC is load-bearing and under-defined: §4.6 says it must distinguish a repo 'edited locally since the last sync' from one that 'merely received a sync', driving cco join's Case-B-update-all vs Case-C-prompt branch. The synced set is defined in §4.1 (project.yml + claude/** + secrets.env.example), but WHEN the fingerprint is written/cleared (on receive? on source edit?) is not stated — ambiguous enough that two engineers produce different divergence verdicts.
findings.why: If two implementations disagree on what 'divergent' means, cco join and the cco start divergence notice behave inconsistently across machines, and the H1-ordered reminder aggregator reports differently.
findings.proposedResolution: Specify the minimum contract (not the byte format): fingerprint = hash over the exact synced set (§4.1), written on both successful sync-receive and at source-side sync, cleared/recomputed on any local edit detected at read time; 'divergent' = current synced-set hash != last-synced fingerprint. Leave only the hash algorithm and rollback richness as true impl detail.

findings.title: cco update --check spans three buckets (DATA source / CACHE remote_cache / STATE base) and breaks on a freshly-synced second PC; output format and ref-vs-HEAD advancement semantics unspecified
findings.issue: cco update --check lists installed resources whose upstream source (DATA, synced required) has advanced vs the local copy via remote_cache (CACHE, never-synced) and the installed base/ (STATE, never-synced). On a second PC that synced source but has no local install/base/remote_cache, the check has provenance but no local baseline to diff against — the 'reuse R3 machinery' claim assumes co-location which the bucket split breaks. Also unspecified: output shape (per-resource lines? columns?), how 'advanced' is computed when source pins a ref (a pinned ref: v1.0 never 'advances' even if HEAD moves), and --offline/--no-cache interaction.
findings.why: E will wire the check assuming co-location and on a freshly-synced PC it errors or reports misleading 'no updates'; without an output contract it cannot get a contract test; the ref-vs-HEAD rule determines correctness. (The natural 'not installed here' degradation and reuse of existing cco update conventions lower this from high.)
findings.proposedResolution: Specify in §7/ADR-0018 D5: output (one line per resource: <kind> <name>: <local-ref> → <available> + summary); the advancement rule (pinned ref = compare to that ref's remote commit; unpinned = HEAD); the check spans three buckets and degrades gracefully — report 'not installed on this machine' when source (DATA) exists but the non-synced install/base/remote_cache do not; and --offline/--no-cache interaction.

findings.title: Solo-adopter Case C 'reserved hooks' (index config_path field + cco start precedence) are named but the field is absent from the v1 index schema, and the index's disposable-STATE nature already makes the hook non-breaking
findings.issue: ADR-0018 D6 says v1 'reserves the enabling hooks: an index config_path field (STATE) and cco start discovery precedence (<repo>/.cco → ~/.cco/projects/<name>)'. But the authoritative v1 index schema (§3 / ADR-0016 D4) has only version/paths/projects — no config_path. 'Reserved' is ambiguous: is the field added to v1 now or only mentioned, and is the cco start precedence v1 or post-v1? (Adjusted to nit-level impact: the index is machine-local, scan-rebuildable STATE, so adding an optional field post-v1 is inherently non-breaking — 'reserve' is effectively satisfied by the index's regenerable nature.)
findings.why: The 'reserve so not painted out' goal is largely neutralized because the index is disposable STATE; the remaining issue is a one-line clarification of whether v1 reads config_path (read-but-unused) and whether the precedence is v1 or post-v1.
findings.proposedResolution: Clarify in §3 + §12: either add an OPTIONAL config_path per-project field to the v1 index schema (read-but-unused, defaulting to <repo>/.cco) and document the precedence as a no-op-in-v1 code path, or state explicitly that Case C will be an additive (non-breaking) schema addition (safe because the index is regenerable STATE) and remove the 'reserved hook' framing. Confirm whether cco start precedence is v1 or post-v1.

findings.title: Profiles→tags migration for projects records organizational metadata only, not selection/visibility, and may reference unresolved project names — framed as 'lossless', masking a behavioral change
findings.issue: Legacy profiles selected WHICH projects were visible/on-disk (a workspace-selection mechanism). The migration 'seeds origin profile as a tag', but a project's tag lives in <data>/cco/tags.yml (per-user DATA) while the project config is in <repo>/.cco keyed in the STATE index (paths/repos only, no tags). cco list --tag reads tags.yml, but for the tag to map to a project, that project must be in the index AND tagged; after a lazy per-project migrate, an un-migrated project has a tag entry pointing at a name with no index/path. So the seed can reference projects that don't resolve, and the old 'switch profile → these projects appear' behavior has no equivalent — presented as 'lossless'.
findings.why: Users relying on profiles for workspace selection will find migrated tags non-functional for unmigrated projects, with no 'show everything in profile X' that resolves to runnable projects. The migration docs frame this as lossless, masking the gap E must handle (how cco list --tag renders a tagged-but-unresolved project).
findings.proposedResolution: Clarify in ADR-0010/design §9 that profile→tag seeding for PROJECTS records organizational metadata only (not selection/visibility), that tags may reference not-yet-migrated/not-yet-resolved names, and how cco list --tag behaves for a tagged-but-unresolved project (show with 'unresolved' marker vs hide). State it as an accepted change (like ADR-0009's memory regression) rather than 'lossless'.

findings.title: First-run 'backed-up marker' guard has no defined home in the STATE layout (no global meta file), risking re-archive or skipped backup before the irreversible vault removal
findings.location: design.md §9:678-680 + §7:550-556 / §9:633-635 (J0 per-root bootstrap); §2.2:148-159 + ADR-0016 D6:193-199 (STATE layout, meta only per-project)
findings.issue: First-run backup idempotency hinges on a 'backed-up marker in STATE meta', but (a) there is NO global meta file in the STATE layout (meta is only per-project projects/<id>/meta); the marker has no defined home; and (b) J0 is per-root idempotent and may create STATE fresh. If STATE is deleted/relocated (CCO_STATE_HOME override) while the legacy vault still exists, the marker vanishes and first-run re-archives — but the vault may already have been removed, producing an empty/partial backup; conversely a mistakenly-present marker makes a genuine first run skip the backup.
findings.why: The backup is the sole safety net before the irreversible vault removal. An undefined marker location makes the guard unverifiable and the idempotency claim unfounded; E will invent an ad-hoc location.
findings.proposedResolution: Define a concrete global marker (e.g. <state>/cco/migration-state or a global <state>/cco/meta — note §2.2 has no global STATE meta today) and specify: backup runs iff (legacy vault present AND marker absent); on success write marker; vault removal is independent and only after a verified backup. Add the global-meta file to the §2.2 STATE layout. Test the second-first-run no-re-archive path.

findings.title: No rollback/abort handling if migrate fails mid-write (partial .cco/ + index entry); the re-run guard then blocks recovery
findings.issue: cco migrate <project> is a multi-step mutation: read backup → write .cco/ in the repo → register in the STATE index. Failure handling is specified only for the read precondition (M8). There is no spec for a failure BETWEEN writing a partial .cco/ and registering the index. The rollback story (.cco/ is in the repo's git) fails because a freshly-migrated .cco/ is NOT yet committed, so git provides no rollback; and the re-run guard ('never overwrite an existing .cco/ without confirm') then refuses to fix the partial write without manual deletion.
findings.why: Leaves an install half-migrated with no clean recovery, and the re-run guard actively blocks recovery of a partial write.
findings.proposedResolution: Specify atomic/transactional migrate: write .cco/ to a temp dir and move into place only after secret-scan + full success, then register the index; on any failure remove the partial .cco/ so re-run is clean. Or detect a partial/incomplete .cco/ (e.g. missing project.yml) and treat it as re-creatable rather than protected. Document in §9 and test an interrupted migrate.

findings.title: coords-lookup persistence-vs-on-demand left explicitly undecided, forking the CACHE layout and invalidation logic
findings.issue: D3 specifies coords-lookup as a CACHE file AND says it 'may also be computed on demand without persisting', then defers 'coords-lookup persistence-vs-on-demand' to E; D7 lists it as a real CACHE file. This is a design choice with observable consequences (a persisted file needs invalidation/regeneration triggers on every manifest edit; on-demand has a scan cost per cco config coords --diff), and it drives whether D7's CACHE layout entry exists at all.
