findings.why: Affects the CACHE byte-level layout, invalidation logic, and the coordinate-consistency tooling cost profile; two engineers build materially different things (a cached file with staleness handling vs a pure scan function). Low blast radius (advisory/regenerable).
findings.proposedResolution: Choose: since it is explicitly advisory/regenerable, recommend on-demand-only for v1 (drop the CACHE file from D7) to avoid invalidation complexity, recording persistence as a future optimization; or fix it as a cached file with a defined regeneration trigger.

findings.title: cco config is an overloaded grab-bag namespace spanning two buckets (personal ~/.cco vs project <repo>/.cco) and four unrelated concerns; never enumerated as a coherent subcommand set
findings.issue: cco config accretes: (1) save/push/pull — versioning + remote sync of the PERSONAL ~/.cco bucket; (2) coords --diff/--sync — cross-unit coordinate consistency, a PROJECT <repo>/.cco concern; (3) validate — share-readiness of a project's referenced resources; (4) protect — CODEOWNERS scaffold for <repo>/.cco. §7 lists only the catch-all 'cco config …' plus a separate cco config protect row, never enumerating save/push/pull/coords/validate. So cco config simultaneously means 'manage ~/.cco' AND 'operate on <repo>/.cco' — two different buckets under one verb.
findings.why: E needs an authoritative subcommand list for cco config. As written, a user running cco config save (personal ~/.cco) vs cco config validate (project repo) cannot tell from the noun which bucket they are touching, and E risks inconsistent cwd-vs-global scoping per subcommand.
findings.proposedResolution: Enumerate the full cco config subcommand set in §7 in one place: save/push/pull (scope ~/.cco), coords --diff/--sync and validate (scope cwd project's <repo>/.cco), protect (scope cwd repo). Annotate each with its bucket. Consider whether project-scoped validate/coords should live under a project-scoped namespace (cco project validate/coords) to keep cco config = ~/.cco only — a conscious maintainer decision rather than an accidental dual-bucket overload.

findings.title: Template sharing verbs (publish/install/export/import) claimed by the 2×2 are absent from the §7 Templates row and the code; pack/template symmetry vs scaffold-only tension unresolved
findings.issue: ADR-0018 D2 and §6.2 state packs AND templates use the full 2×2 and the sharing-repo layout is packs/ + templates/. But the §7 'Authoring' row only gives cco template create; there is no §7 row listing cco template publish/install/export/import, and cmd-template.sh has only list/show/create/remove — so these are entirely net-new for templates. The single §7 Sharing row writes 'packs/templates: cco <res> publish|install' as if templates already share, but the template command surface is invisible there. ADR-0019 D7 says templates are scaffold-only with no live reference, sitting oddly next to giving them publish/install.
findings.why: The 2×2 completeness fails for the template axis. E reads §7 for what verbs to wire; the template half is invisible, risking templates being left at create/remove only (breaking the claimed symmetry) or built ad-hoc, and the scaffold-only vs live-channel tension needs an explicit answer.
findings.proposedResolution: Reconcile ADR-0018 D2 (templates full 2×2) with ADR-0019 D7 (templates scaffold-only, no live reference). If templates DO publish/install, add an explicit §7 Templates row (cco template publish|install|export|import, Build-new). If templates only export/import (snapshot, consistent with 'no live reference'), correct §6.2/ADR-0018 D2 to say templates use export/import only and publish/install is packs-only.

findings.title: Coordinate-consistency tooling cco config coords --sync has no defined conflict-resolution rule (which coordinate wins across diverging units)
findings.issue: P12/ADR-0016 D2 make the per-unit manifest the sole source of truth (no global registry). D3 adds a derived coords-lookup (CACHE) plus cco config coords --diff/--sync/--sanitize that 'apply a coordinate globally across units'. --diff is advisory/safe, but --sync/--sanitize write one coordinate across N manifests with NO stated winner-selection rule when units disagree. (ADR-0016 D3 and P12 explicitly assert this introduces no global source of truth — the manifests stay canonical — so this is a missing impl-detail deferred to S/E, not a principle contradiction.)
findings.why: If --sync has no conflict-resolution rule, E will invent one, and the auto-elected winner would silently establish a de-facto authority. The semantics should be pinned to preserve P12's 'consistency by tooling, not storage, no implicit authority'.
findings.proposedResolution: Specify cco config coords --sync semantics: require an explicit source unit (--from <unit>) or interactive pick, never auto-elect a winner (mirroring cco sync --from); document that --diff is read-only/advisory and --sync/--sanitize are user-directed bulk edits, not reconciliation.

findings.title: cco resolve source-selection precedence and the layered heal/validate reachability model are user-invisible — undefined unresolved-start prompt copy and passive-⚠ next-step affordance
findings.issue: The §4.4 prompt description DOES name the command (resolve now → cco resolve), so the model-level handoff exists. What is unspecified: the EXACT unresolved-start prompt copy/wording, any cco start --explain/dry-run affordance, and the passive-⚠ (D2 layer e) → cco config validate next-step copy (only the Case-C divergence notice has fixed copy). On a fresh-machine clone (JC/JF) the index is empty and the user's FIRST experience is an unresolved-repo prompt.
findings.why: If the prompt does not clearly name cco resolve and explain the choice, the onboarding journey is opaque. Cheap copy/UX-polish to pin now.
findings.proposedResolution: Add to §4.4/§8: the unresolved-start prompt copy must name the command (e.g. 'repo X unresolved — run cco resolve to point/clone it, or proceed without mounting it'); the passive-⚠ at cco start/cco list must name cco config validate as the next step; state that cco start prints which source it used. No new commands — just defined, discoverable copy.

findings.title: Three overlapping 'what would change?' discovery surfaces on cco update (--check vs --diff vs --dry-run vs --news) with no documented division of labor
findings.location: design.md §7:531, §6.2:506; ADR-0018 D5; lib/cmd-update.sh:28,39,40,81; CLAUDE.md update flags
findings.issue: cco update already ships --diff (summary/full diffs of framework defaults), --news (changelog), and --dry-run (pending migrations). The design ADDS cco update --check (list resources with available upstream updates) and cco <res> update --dry-run (preview one resource's 3-way merge). A user wanting 'what's updatable / what would change' faces --check vs --diff vs --dry-run vs --news with no documented mental model. (Overlap is narrower than it seems — --check/<res> update target sharing-repo upstream resources while --diff/--news answer the framework-defaults question; the shared --dry-run token is the genuinely ambiguous collision.)
findings.why: A new flag layered onto an already-crowded cco update flag space without a stated boundary makes the common question ('what can I update?') ambiguous and pushes disambiguation cost onto every guide/user; cheap to fix on paper.
findings.proposedResolution: Add a short table to §6.2/§7 mapping each flag to its question: --check = is a newer upstream available (HEAD compare, no merge); --diff = line-level changes of framework defaults; --dry-run = preview the 3-way merge / pending migrations; --news = changelog of new features. Disambiguate the shared --dry-run token.

findings.title: ADR-0007 Decision-2 lists 'the remotes registry + tokens' in STATE without an inline back-pointer to the ADR-0015 registry→DATA split
findings.issue: ADR-0007 §Decision-2 puts 'the remotes registry + tokens' under STATE. ADR-0015 D2 (annotated '> Refines ADR-0007 §Decision-2') and ADR-0016 D5/table split this: de-tokenized registry → DATA (required), token → STATE (never, 0600). ADR-0007's own text is unannotated for this split (it only annotates the index/RD-paths items). ADR-0015 carries the forward-pointer and design.md §2.2 + ADR-0016 are correct.
findings.why: Lower-risk chain-completeness item: an implementer reading ADR-0007's STATE inventory in isolation would put the registry in the wrong bucket; mitigated because ADR-0015 quotes the refinement and the authoritative docs are correct.
findings.proposedResolution: Optional: add a one-line inline note to ADR-0007 §Decision-2 pointing to ADR-0015 D2 ('remotes registry split: de-tokenized name→url → DATA; token stays STATE 0600 never-sync'), keeping the no-rewrite convention.

findings.title: DATA tags.yml and remotes registry share an identical name→url shape with config coordinates, with the discriminator living only in prose — no structural guard against misfiling a private endpoint into committed config
findings.issue: ADR-0016 D5 itself flags: the DATA remotes registry and the manifest coordinate are 'same name→url shape, opposite Axis-2 → opposite bucket'. The only thing distinguishing a never-team remotes endpoint from a team-shared coordinate is which bucket/file it lands in — no schema-level marker on remotes (unlike tags.yml which already uses typed top-level keys). A future maintainer adding a third name→url map has no structural guard against misfiling it into the wrong bucket, silently leaking a private endpoint into committed config.
findings.why: Adding a 5th bucket or a new url-shaped resource is exactly the extensibility scenario to probe: same-shape-different-meaning maps whose correctness rests on which file you write to; one wrong write reintroduces a token/endpoint leak. Hardening, not a present defect (current two stores are correctly placed).
findings.proposedResolution: Add a one-line structural discriminator to each url-shaped store (e.g. a kind:/scope: header field or a typed top-level key, as tags.yml already uses) so misfiling is detectable by cco config validate/S8, not just by reviewer eyeballing. Note it as an E build-detail in ADR-0016.

findings.title: ADR-0016 D9 and ADR-0019 D2 restate the cco config validate validity contract near-verbatim — a DRY/maintenance hazard if a future extension lands in only one place
findings.issue: The three-clause validity contract for cco config validate is written out in full in both ADRs. ADR-0019 D2 DOES open with the attribution '(ADR-0016 D9)', so canonicality is unambiguous — but the restatement is verbatim rather than a pure reference, so a future extension (e.g. the pack-shadowing check from D5) could be added in only one place and the two silently diverge.
findings.why: cco config validate is a single function E builds once; a verbatim duplicated contract is a maintenance hazard (mild, since canonicality is already cited).
findings.proposedResolution: Make ADR-0016 D9 the canonical contract and have ADR-0019 D2 reference it ('the validity contract of ADR-0016 D9, extended with the pack-reachability clause') rather than restating it verbatim.

findings.title: manifest.yml removal has no migrator: inert manifests committed in sharing repos / left in ~/.cco are not explicitly cleaned or intentionally-left
findings.issue: ADR-0012 deletes lib/manifest.sh + cco manifest + the manifest call sites and says no migration is needed because cleanup 'rides the Phase-3 cutover'. But Phase-3 only removes the local vault; it does NOT touch manifest.yml committed inside users' published sharing repos nor any manifest.yml left in ~/.cco after de-vaulting. New structure-based discovery (ADR-0018) ignores manifest.yml, so a stale committed manifest is harmless-but-confusing — yet the design never states it is intentionally left or cleaned. (Adjusted to nit: ADR-0012 already classifies this 'no standalone migration'/deferred-Open, the finding concedes it is not data-loss, and a discovery-ignored manifest has zero functional effect.)
findings.why: A migration-completeness/doc-clarity wish: E may assume a cleanup exists. For the 2-user base the risk is low; leaving it unspecified means it falls through.
findings.proposedResolution: Add a one-line statement to design §9 Phase 3 (or inventory open #1): 'Inert manifest.yml in ~/.cco is deleted by the Phase-3 cleanup; manifest.yml committed in sharing repos is harmless and intentionally left (ignored by structure-based discovery) — no migrator.' Confirm this is the intended decision.

findings.title: BL3 absolute-mount conversion: cmd-start.sh compose mounts are relative to one project_dir and must become host-absolute across three roots; the generated-compose location / --project-directory interaction is under-stated in §9
findings.location: design.md §9 Phase 0 BL3:615-619; lib/cmd-start.sh:441-495,759 (relative ./ mounts via --project-directory); ADR-0016 D6:203
findings.issue: The generated compose binds ./.cco/claude-state, ./memory, ./.cco/managed, ./.claude, ./project.yml — all anchored to one project_dir via --project-directory. In the new model these split across THREE roots (project.yml + .claude → <repo>/.cco; claude-state + memory → STATE; managed → CACHE). A single --project-directory cannot anchor sources under different roots, so each mount source must become host-absolute and the compose base dir chosen. §9 BL3 says 'pick the compose base dir (STATE)' but does not restate where the generated compose lives (ADR-0016 D6 places it at <state>/cco/projects/<id>/docker-compose.yml) or the --project-directory interaction once every mount is absolute. (Mostly mechanism; one under-stated decision.)
findings.why: Phase 0 is the foundation touching the most security-sensitive generated artifact (the compose the container runs); the relative-path assumption is load-bearing across ~5 mount lines, and an ambiguous compose base / generated-compose location propagates inconsistency into every mount and the clean command.
findings.proposedResolution: State in §9 Phase 0: the generated docker-compose.yml lives at <state>/cco/projects/<id>/docker-compose.yml (per ADR-0016 D6), compose is invoked with that as --project-directory, and ALL mount sources are host-absolute (no ./). Enumerate the cmd-start.sh:441-495 mount lines and their new absolute sources per bucket (project.yml/.claude → <repo>/.cco; claude-state/memory → STATE; managed → CACHE; packs/llms → CACHE overlay).

findings.title: CACHE llms name-collision disambiguation deferred, but the per-unit model makes same-name-different-url references inevitable — undefined whether to re-fetch, key by url-hash, or warn-reuse
findings.issue: Under the per-unit package.json model, two units may reference the same llms name with DIFFERENT urls; the name-keyed CACHE (<cache>/cco/llms/<name>/) then has a real collision. ADR-0016 calls it 'low-stakes since content is re-fetchable; cache-state records resolved_url to disambiguate' and defers the mechanism to E. Unspecified: does cco re-fetch when the requesting unit's url != the cached resolved_url, key the cache by url-hash, or warn-and-reuse?
findings.why: Determines whether a project silently runs with another project's llms content (wrong docs in context) or always re-fetches. Low blast radius (regenerable content) but a correctness fork.
findings.proposedResolution: Pick one rule and record it: on llms resolve, if cached resolved_url != requested url → re-fetch and overwrite (last-writer per machine) with a one-line notice, OR key the CACHE dir by name+short-hash(url). Recommend name-keyed + re-fetch-on-url-mismatch since content is regenerable.

findings.title: The entire 4-bucket XDG resolver (DATA/STATE/CACHE/CONFIG) is net-new; §9 Phase 0 / §2.2 wording '(in lib/paths.sh)' reads as a location rather than a from-scratch build
findings.location: design.md §2.2 'Resolver (ADR-0007/0015)' + §9 Phase 0 H4 ('the XDG resolver (in lib/paths.sh)'); §11:726; lib/paths.sh:1-93
findings.issue: lib/paths.sh contains ZERO XDG/CCO_*_HOME logic (grep returns 0 hits); every helper resolves under $USER_CONFIG_DIR (=<repo>/user-config/) and paths.sh is a dual-read shim (_cco_resolve_path new-vs-old INSIDE config dirs) — the opposite of host-side XDG resolution. The host-side/anti-in-container guard, unset/empty/non-absolute XDG validation, 0700, and the $CCO_*_HOME overrides are all from scratch. (Mitigated: §11:726 lists the XDG resolver matrix under the Phase-0 NEW column and §9 says 'while rewriting lib/paths.sh', so the net-new nature IS surfaced; only the H4 parenthetical reads as a location.)
findings.why: Phase 0 H4 reads as if the resolver exists and needs a guard added; in reality it is a from-scratch module that everything else (index, DATA stores, CACHE overlays, J0 bootstrap) depends on, and an implementer may bolt XDG onto the dual-read shim instead of replacing it.
findings.proposedResolution: Reword §9 Phase 0 / §2.2 to state the XDG resolver is NET-NEW in lib/paths.sh (no reuse) and the current dual-read _cco_resolve_path shim is deleted under the breaking cutover (AD12/FR-S5). List the resolver's required outputs (CONFIG ~/.cco, DATA, STATE, CACHE bases) as a Phase-0 build item, not a guard-addition.

findings.title: cco resolve consolidation instructs dropping the non-existent 'cco index refresh' verb, obscuring the actual refactor (cco project resolve → cco resolve)
findings.issue: ADR-0017 D2 says cco resolve --scan absorbs 'cco index refresh --scan' and the Reuse table says 'Drop' that verb — but cco index refresh does not exist in the current code (grep: 0 matches); the real existing verb is cco project resolve (cmd-project-query.sh:242). The docs instruct dropping a design-era placeholder while the actual refactor is cco project resolve → cco resolve + new --scan/--all flags. design.md §3:333 itself acknowledges 'cco resolve is today's cco project resolve'.
findings.why: Pure clarity: the rename/fold instruction points at a non-existent command, so an implementer may waste effort hunting for cco index refresh; no behavioral consequence.
findings.proposedResolution: Reword ADR-0017 D2 / design §7 to: 'cco resolve replaces cco project resolve (lib/cmd-project-query.sh); --scan and --all are new flags. There is no pre-existing cco index refresh to drop (it was a design-era placeholder).'

options.title: Gitignored secrets.env / local-paths on non-active profiles lost by the flatten-from-branches vault backup at the irreversible cutover

options.name: A — Working-tree archive per profile (checkout/worktree each branch, tar full tree incl. gitignored), with explicit scope + pre-removal warning
options.description: Redefine the Phase-2 first-run backup (design.md §9 / ADR-0006 Dec-2) as a FILESYSTEM-LEVEL archive of the working tree, not a git-tree serialization. The backup step iterates each profile branch and, for each, performs a `git checkout <profile>` (or `git worktree add`) and tars the FULL working tree — including the blanket-gitignored secrets.env / *.env / *.key / *.pem / .credentials.json AND local-paths.yml — alongside the per-branch profile→tag flatten metadata. Because the legacy vault holds only ONE materialized working tree at a time, only the checked-out profile's gitignored secrets are physically on disk; the others were never committed and are genuinely unreachable. The backup therefore (1) captures the active profile's real secrets verbatim, (2) for non-active profiles, captures whatever IS recoverable (committed state) and records that gitignored secrets could not be captured, and (3) before the offer-to-remove, enumerates every gitignored secret/local file FOUND and emits a blocking-style warning telling the user to checkout + commit/export each other profile's secrets BEFORE accepting removal. The M8 verify-before-remove invariant extends to: never offer removal until the working-tree capture (incl. gitignored valuables) is verified. Reuses the existing `_SAFE_TO_REMOVE_PATTERNS` / `_is_safe_to_remove` logic (lib/cmd-vault.sh:1470) — everything gitignored NOT in the safe list is 'valuable, must be stashed'. Document the mechanism in ADR-0006 (revise Dec-2, add a Consequence) + design §9; add test_migrate.sh assertions that secrets.env survives the active-profile capture and that a pre-removal warning lists uncaptured secrets.
pros: Eliminates the silent-loss vector for the COMMON case (single/active profile, or user on each machine has the relevant profile checked out): the user's real secrets.env and local-paths.yml are physically archived

Honest about the genuine physical limit (gitignored data on a never-checked-out branch literally does not exist on disk) — it warns instead of pretending, satisfying informed-consent before the irreversible delete

Reuses an already-designed primitive: lib/cmd-vault.sh:1470 _SAFE_TO_REMOVE_PATTERNS already encodes 'gitignored-but-valuable must be stashed before removal' — same policy, consistent with existing code intent
Keeps the profile→tag flatten (committed config) intact; the working-tree capture is layered ON TOP, so the lossless-config promise (ADR-0006) is preserved and extended to secrets
Minimal new surface: a checkout loop + tar already exists conceptually in the flatten step; M8 verify-before-remove invariant simply gains 'verify valuable gitignored captured'
cons: Iterating branches by checkout mutates the working tree and (per cmd-vault.sh:29-32 / D8) must block while Docker sessions are active and restore local-paths correctly on every exit path — adds error-path complexity to a teardown-sensitive command; `git worktree` mitigates but adds its own edge cases

Cannot recover gitignored secrets on truly-never-checked-out profiles — the warning shifts a manual burden onto the user just before the cutover (acceptable per the 2-user known base, ADR-0006, but still a residual loss for the negligent user)

Archiving real secrets into <state>/cco/backups/vault-<date>.tar.gz creates a plaintext-secrets-at-rest artifact (machine-local, never synced per design §9 — but it is a new unencrypted copy on disk; mitigated by 0600 perms, must be documented)
options.principleAlignment: Upholds ADR-0006 'migration is lossless' (extends it to the highest-value data) and its M8 verified-backup-before-remove invariant. Honors P1 (secrets.env is explicitly CONFIG — user-authored, gitignored only for secrecy — so losing it violates the config-preservation contract). Consistent with P6/P8 (the backup is STATE, machine-local, never synced — no leak into config buckets or Axis-1/2 sync). Aligns with the in-code precedent at lib/cmd-vault.sh:1470. Slight strain on the 'clean teardown / minimal legacy reader' goal of ADR-0006 (Dec-1, Consequences) by adding checkout/worktree complexity to the backup path.

options.name: B — Single full-tree archive of the legacy vault directory as-is (no branch traversal), gitignored files included, plus profile→tag flatten as committed-only metadata
options.description: Decouple the two concerns the current design conflates. (1) SAFETY: take ONE filesystem-level `tar` of the entire legacy vault working directory exactly as it sits on disk — every tracked file PLUS every gitignored file present (the active profile's secrets.env, *.key, *.pem, local-paths.yml). No checkout, no working-tree mutation, no Docker-session gate. This is the irreversible-loss guard. (2) FIDELITY: separately run the existing profile-branch flatten (`git show`/serialize committed state per branch → profile→tag metadata) to preserve non-active profiles' COMMITTED config. The migrate reader then reads committed config from the flatten and the active profile's secrets from the archived working tree. Because only the active profile's secrets are ever on disk, the archive captures exactly what is physically recoverable — with zero extra git acrobatics. Before offer-to-remove, scan the archive (M8) and warn if any other profile's committed tree references secrets that are absent (i.e., were gitignored and unrecoverable). Document in ADR-0006 + design §9; test: archive contains active secrets.env byte-for-byte; removal blocked until archive verified.
pros: Simplest and safest mechanically: a single tar of the directory as-is cannot mutate the working tree, needs no checkout loop, no D8 Docker-session gate, no local-path extract/restore trap — far lower error-path risk on a teardown-critical command
Guarantees the active profile's real secrets are captured byte-for-byte (the only secrets physically present), closing the primary blocker for the realistic single-active-profile user
Cleanly separates the IRREVERSIBLE-loss guard (raw archive) from the FIDELITY transform (profile→tag flatten) — each does one job, easier to reason about and test
Preserves full legacy git history if the archive includes the vault's .git dir (rollback per ADR-0006 Dec-3 'backup archive preserves full vault history' is literally satisfied)
cons: Like A, cannot recover gitignored secrets on never-checked-out profiles — same residual physical limit (warning required)

If a user keeps DIFFERENT secrets per profile and only one is checked out, the archive silently has only that one — the warning must be explicit and the user must understand the limit before accepting removal

A raw whole-directory tar includes transient junk (claude-state, rag-data, .tmp) — larger archive; the migrate reader must know to ignore safe-to-remove patterns (cmd-vault.sh:1470 list) when reading, so the safe/valuable distinction still has to be encoded
Plaintext-secrets-at-rest in the archive (same mitigation as A: 0600, machine-local, documented)
options.principleAlignment: Upholds ADR-0006 (lossless backup, M8 verify-before-remove, Dec-3 'backup preserves full vault history' — strengthened by including .git). Honors P1 (secrets.env preserved as the config it is) and P6/P8 (archive is machine-local STATE, never synced). Most consistent with ADR-0006's stated GOAL of a 'clean removal' + 'minimal legacy reader' (Dec-1, Consequences) since it adds NO branch-traversal machinery to the safety path. The flatten still feeds the lossless profile→tag conversion (ADR-0010/0011/0015) so config fidelity is unchanged.

options.name: C — Pre-backup secret-harvest pass that walks every profile branch's tracked config, identifies referenced/expected secret files, and prompts the user to materialize+export each profile's secrets before the cutover
options.description: Add an explicit, interactive secret-harvest step ahead of the backup. cco walks every profile branch (committed tree only, read-only via `git show`/`ls-tree` — no working-tree mutation), determines which profiles exist and which gitignored secret files each profile is EXPECTED to have (from secrets.env.example skeletons, project.yml references, the gitignore patterns), then drives the user through a CLI loop: 'Profile X has gitignored secrets that exist only if currently checked out — checkout X and run `cco vault export-secrets X` (or commit them to a temporary branch) to include them in the backup.' For the active profile, secrets are harvested automatically. The backup itself remains a git-tree flatten PLUS the harvested secret bundle. Nothing is removed until every known profile has either had its secrets harvested or the user has explicitly acknowledged (per-profile) that they accept the loss. Document in ADR-0006 + design §9; test: harvest loop enumerates all profiles, removal blocked until each profile acknowledged.
pros: Highest fidelity: actively helps the user recover secrets from EVERY profile (not just the active one), maximally honoring 'lossless' across multi-profile installs
Read-only branch walk for discovery (ls-tree/git show) avoids working-tree-mutating checkouts in the discovery phase, reducing D8/restore-trap risk relative to A
Strongest informed-consent: per-profile acknowledgement makes the irreversible delete a deliberate, enumerated choice rather than a single blanket warning
cons: Most complex and highest-friction UX for a cutover that ADR-0006 deliberately scoped to a TINY, known, already-migrating 2-user base — heavy machinery for a corner case the maintainer explicitly de-prioritized

Introduces a NEW legacy-vault verb (`cco vault export-secrets`) into a vault that Phase 3 is about to DELETE entirely — adds to exactly the legacy surface ADR-0006 Dec-1 wants gone, contradicting the 'minimal legacy reader' principle

Still cannot magically recover secrets the user never had checked out anywhere; the per-profile prompt forces manual checkout/commit cycles, slow and error-prone, and re-opens the working-tree-mutation risk it tried to avoid the moment the user actually checks a profile out

Over-engineered relative to the realistic data: the user's secrets for the active project are the ones that matter; secrets for dormant profiles are unlikely to be both present-only-on-another-machine AND irrecoverable
options.principleAlignment: Upholds ADR-0006 lossless intent most aggressively and P1 (secrets = config to preserve), with strong informed-consent. BUT strains ADR-0006 Dec-1 + Consequences ('breaking cutover, minimal legacy reader, clean removal') by ADDING legacy-vault tooling on the eve of deletion, and ignores ADR-0006's explicit Context (tiny known base → reject heavy compatibility machinery). P6/P8 respected (harvested bundle is machine-local STATE). Net: principle-aligned on preservation, principle-straining on the deliberately-minimal teardown philosophy.

options.recommended: A — Working-tree archive per profile (checkout/worktree each branch, tar full tree incl. gitignored), with explicit scope + pre-removal warning
options.rationale: The root defect is a category error in the design's backup spec: it serializes the GIT TREE, but secrets.env / *.key / *.pem / local-paths.yml are blanket-gitignored (lib/cmd-vault.sh:45-50,71-78) and therefore NEVER in any tree — so a per-branch git serialization captures secrets for zero profiles, and the active-profile capture is mis-framed as 'uncommitted WIP' rather than 'the only on-disk secrets'. The fix must be a FILESYSTEM-level capture that includes valuable gitignored files. Option A is recommended because it (1) directly closes the blocker for the realistic case — the active profile's real secrets and local-paths get archived byte-for-byte before the irreversible delete; (2) is honest about the genuine physical limit (gitignored data on a never-checked-out branch does not exist on disk) and warns + blocks removal instead of pretending, satisfying informed consent ahead of the one-way cutover (ADR-0006 M8); and (3) reuses an already-designed primitive — lib/cmd-vault.sh:1470 _SAFE_TO_REMOVE_PATTERNS already encodes 'gitignored-but-not-in-safe-list ⇒ valuable ⇒ must stash before removal', the exact policy this finding needs, so the design is internally consistent rather than novel. It upholds P1 (secrets.env is config, preserved), P6/P8 (archive is machine-local STATE, never synced), and ADR-0006's lossless promise + verify-before-remove invariant. Option B is a very close second and is mechanically SAFER (one tar, no working-tree mutation, no D8 gate, no restore-trap) — if implementation review finds the per-branch checkout loop too risky on the teardown path, B should be adopted instead, since for the dominant single-active-profile case B captures exactly the same recoverable secrets with far less complexity; A's only real advantage over B is opportunistically capturing per-profile secrets when the user does cycle checkouts, which the warning in B can instead delegate to the user. Option C is rejected: it adds new legacy-vault tooling on the eve of Phase-3 deletion, directly contradicting ADR-0006's deliberately-minimal teardown for a tiny known base. Whichever of A/B is chosen, the resolution must be written into ADR-0006 (revise Dec-2, add a Consequence on plaintext-secrets-at-rest with 0600 perms) and design §9, and test_migrate.sh must assert (a) active-profile secrets.env survives in the archive and (b) offer-to-remove is blocked until a verified capture lists any uncaptured gitignored secrets.

options.title: `source` provenance relocation (config-bucket → DATA): fix the 'Reuse' mislabel, specify the rename+relocation migration, and classify the machine-local `publish_target`

options.name: Option A — Relocate+Reclassify+Specify-migration; publish_target re-derived on demand (drop from source)
options.description: Adopt the finding's proposedResolution with the strictest publish_target treatment. (1) Re-label the ADR-0019 §Open→E / Reuse-Drop-Build table entry for `.cco/source` from 'Reuse' to 'Relocate + reuse-resolution-logic' (the *resolution* logic is reused; the *storage location* and the *reader/writer* are not). (2) Add an explicit migration spec to ADR-0016 D5: enumerate legacy fields → new DATA fields field-by-field: `source:`→`url:`, `path:`→`resource:`, `ref:` kept, plus drop the machine/provenance-bookkeeping fields that have no home in the 'no-machine-specific-leak' target (`commit:`, `installed:`, `updated:`, `version:`) by relocating them to STATE `/update` meta where the install-commit/version bookkeeping already conceptually belongs (the STATE `meta` already carries `remote_cache.commit`); define the relocation `<repo>/.cco/source` → `<data>/cco/projects/<id>/source`, `<pack>/.cco/source` → `<data>/cco/packs/<name>/source`, `~/.cco/llms/<name>/source` already covered (content/cache-state→CACHE per ADR-0016 D2, so the llms `source` file is NOT relocated to DATA — it is split per D2/D7). (3) Re-point `_cco_pack_source`/`_cco_project_source` (paths.sh:82-88) to the DATA path, add a `_cco_template_source` variant, and rewrite the ~6 call sites + `_is_installed_project` reader so the canonical reader keys off `url:`/`resource:`/`ref:` (retaining the existing bare-URL/`native:`/`local` backward-compat branches as legacy detection that triggers the migrator). (4) Classify `publish_target` as machine-local: it must NOT travel in the `required`-synced DATA source file. Drop it from the source schema entirely and **re-derive it on demand** by matching the source `url:` against the DATA `remotes` registry (`remote_get_url` reverse-lookup) — exactly as `_resolve_publish_remote` already falls back. (5) Schedule the whole thing as a pack/project/template-scope migration in the new sharing/E phase, idempotent, with the legacy-format detection above as the trigger.
pros: Closes all three gaps the finding raises in one coherent spec, and is directly implementable by E (every field and path is named).

publish_target re-derived (not stored) is the cleanest C4/dual-axis-leak fix: zero machine-local datum lives in the required-synced file, so the leak ADR-0013/C4 closed stays closed; no new STATE file to manage.
Honors P6 (no internal/machine datum in any synced bucket) and ADR-0015 D3's 'no machine-specific leak' literally — the DATA source becomes pure upstream coordinate (url/ref/resource).

Honors P12/ADR-0016 D5 disambiguation: source (DATA, required, never-team) stays distinct from the manifest coordinate (config) and the remotes registry (DATA) — same name→url shape, opposite axes, kept separate.

Relocating commit/installed/version to STATE meta aligns with ADR-0016 D6 (version-tied state is `never`-sync, STATE) and removes the contradiction of a 'required'-synced file carrying machine/commit bookkeeping that would re-leak across PCs.
The migration is idempotent and reuses the existing legacy-format detection in `_is_installed_project`, satisfying the repo's update-system migration rules.
cons: Re-deriving publish_target loses the *explicit* recorded intent: if a pack's `url` is not (or no longer) in the local `remotes` registry, the convenience default for `cco pack publish` disappears (user must pass the remote arg) — a mild UX regression vs today's stored pointer.

Moving commit/installed/version out of `source` into STATE meta is a second relocation the finding did not explicitly scope; it widens the migration surface and touches `_update_single_project`/`_update_single_pack` version-bookkeeping writes.
Largest single migration: relocation + rename + field-drop + reader rewrite + STATE-meta writes — more code paths to get idempotent and more to test than a minimal rename.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P1 (source stays cco-managed/internal), P2/P6 (internal→DATA, nothing machine-local in synced file), P11(b) (correct sync-class: coordinate=required, commit/token-pointer=never), P12 + ADR-0016 D5 disambiguation (source vs coordinate vs registry), ADR-0013/C4 (no dual-axis leak reopened), ADR-0015 D3 ('no machine-specific leak' enforced literally), ADR-0016 D6 (version-tied state→STATE). Strengthens ADR-0019's Reuse/Drop/Build table (correcting the mislabel). No principle strained except a minor UX convenience (not a principle).

options.name: Option B — Relocate+Reclassify+Specify-migration; publish_target persisted in STATE (per-identity, never-sync)
options.description: Identical to Option A for the relocation, the 'Reuse'→'Relocate + reuse-logic' relabel, the field rename (`source`→`url`, `path`→`resource`, keep `ref`), the reader/writer rewrite, the `_cco_*_source` re-pointing + template variant, and the migration scheduling. The ONLY difference is publish_target's home: instead of re-deriving it on demand, persist it as a machine-local STATE datum keyed by identity — e.g. `<state>/cco/packs/<name>/publish-target` (or a `publish_target:` line inside STATE `/update` meta), classified `never`-sync. The migrator reads the legacy `publish_target:` from `<pack>/.cco/source`, writes it to the STATE location, and strips it from the relocated DATA source file. `_resolve_publish_remote` and the `cmd-remote.sh` remove-warning read the STATE pointer instead of grepping `.cco/source`. commit/installed/version: same as the finding's minimal reading — keep `commit:`/`updated:` as bookkeeping but place them in STATE meta (since DATA source must be machine-agnostic), OR keep the DATA source minimal and move them to STATE meta as in A (recommended sub-choice: move to STATE meta).
pros: Preserves the explicit publish_target convenience — `cco pack publish <name>` with no remote arg keeps working exactly as today, even when the url isn't in the remotes registry.
Still fully closes the C4/dual-axis leak: publish_target lives in STATE (`never`-sync), so it never travels in the required-synced DATA file (P6 satisfied).

Clean separation of concerns: the DATA `source` is pure upstream coordinate (url/ref/resource); the machine-local publish convenience is isolated in STATE, the same tier as remotes-token (ADR-0016 D6, M3).
Same implementability as A — every field/path named; reuses the legacy-format detection for the migrator trigger.
cons: Adds a NEW STATE file/field (`publish-target` per pack identity) — a small new internal surface to create, migrate, and keep in sync, where A needs none.
publish_target in STATE can go stale relative to the remotes registry (a registry rename leaves a dangling STATE pointer); A's on-demand re-derivation can never go stale.
Slightly more migration code than A's drop-and-derive for the publish_target path (read legacy → write STATE → strip), though arguably simpler reasoning than A's STATE-meta field-drop.
options.principleAlignment: Same alignment as A on P1/P2/P6/P11/P12/ADR-0013-C4/ADR-0015 D3. Additionally exercises ADR-0016 D6's STATE-partition model (a machine-local pointer beside remotes-token). Mild strain: introduces one more never-sync STATE datum (acceptable per P11(b) — `never` is the correct class for a machine-local registry pointer), and a stale-pointer risk that A avoids.

options.name: Option C — Minimal: keep keys, relocate only; thin compatibility shim; defer field-rename and publish_target classification to E
options.description: Smallest-footprint correction that still fixes the mislabel and unblocks E without re-litigating field names. (1) Relabel the ADR-0019 table entry to 'Relocate + reuse-logic' (the mislabel fix is non-negotiable and cheap). (2) Specify ONLY the relocation in ADR-0016 D5: `<repo>/.cco/source`→`<data>/cco/projects/<id>/source`, `<pack>/.cco/source`→`<data>/cco/packs/<name>/source`, template variant added — but KEEP the legacy key names (`source:`/`path:`/`ref:`/`commit:`) inside the relocated file so the existing readers (`_is_installed_project`, `_update_single_pack`) work unchanged after a pure path re-point of `_cco_*_source`. The 'url/ref/resource' naming in D5 is annotated as the *eventual* target, with the rename deferred to E as a follow-up (cosmetic; the readers don't care about the label as long as writer+reader agree). (3) publish_target: keep it in the relocated DATA source for now but explicitly FLAG it in ADR-0016 D5 as an unresolved machine-local datum that violates 'no machine-specific leak', and record a blocking E task to move/derive it before any required-sync (T) ships — i.e. classify-by-deferral with a guard: T's per-store allowlist must exclude the publish_target field until E resolves it.
pros: Smallest migration: a pure relocation + path re-point of two helpers, no reader rewrite, lowest regression risk for `cco update`/`cco pack update` in the near term.
Fixes the cheapest, highest-value gap immediately (the 'Reuse' mislabel and the missing relocation spec) without forcing the field-rename decision now.
Keeps the door open: D5's url/ref/resource naming becomes the documented target, decided later with full E context.
cons: Does NOT actually resolve the finding: the key-rename is still unspecified (just deferred), and publish_target is left in the required-synced file behind a fragile 'allowlist-exclude' guard that re-opens exactly the C4/dual-axis leak ADR-0013 closed if anyone ships T before E catches up — directly strains P6/ADR-0013.
D5 would then contradict the on-disk reality (D5 says url/ref/resource; disk has source/path/ref) — a documentation/implementation divergence, the very class of defect this review exists to remove.
Two migrations instead of one (relocate now, rename+publish_target later) — more churn for users, two breaking touch-points on the same file.
The 'guard until E' for publish_target is a latent footgun: a `required`-sync store with a machine-local field excluded only by an allowlist is brittle and easy to regress.
options.principleAlignment: Partially upholds P2 (relocation to DATA) and fixes the table mislabel, but STRAINS P6/ADR-0013-C4/ADR-0015 D3 by knowingly leaving publish_target (machine-local) in a `required`-synced file behind a soft guard, and STRAINS the documentation-first / single-source-of-truth discipline by leaving D5's schema diverged from disk. P11(b) sync-class correctness is deferred rather than satisfied. Acceptable only as a stop-gap; not a settled resolution.

options.recommended: Option A — Relocate+Reclassify+Specify-migration; publish_target re-derived on demand (drop from source)
options.rationale: Option A is the only option that closes all three gaps the finding raises with no residual principle strain, and it is fully implementable by E because every field, path, and call site is named. The decisive point is publish_target: the whole refactor exists to evict machine-local/internal data from synced config buckets and close the C4 dual-axis leak (ADR-0013, P6, ADR-0015 D3's 'no machine-specific leak'). publish_target is a machine-local NAME resolved against the per-machine DATA `remotes` registry — it is precisely the kind of datum that must not ride a `required`-synced file. Re-deriving it on demand (url → reverse-lookup in `remotes`, which `_resolve_publish_remote` already does as a fallback) eliminates it as a stored datum entirely: there is nothing to leak, nothing to keep in sync, nothing to go stale. Storing it in STATE (Option B) also closes the leak and keeps the explicit convenience, so B is the close runner-up and the right pick IF the maintainer values the no-arg `cco pack publish` default enough to accept one more never-sync STATE file and a stale-pointer risk — that is the single UX-vs-simplicity trade the maintainer should weigh. A and B are identical on everything else (relabel 'Reuse'→'Relocate + reuse-logic'; rename source→url / path→resource, keep ref; move commit/installed/version bookkeeping to STATE meta where version-tied state belongs per ADR-0016 D6; re-point `_cco_*_source` + add a template variant; rewrite `_is_installed_project` keeping the legacy-format branches as the migrator trigger; one idempotent pack/project/template-scope migration). Note llms `source` is NOT relocated to DATA — ADR-0016 D2/D7 already splits it (coordinate→manifest, content/cache-state→CACHE), so the migration scope is projects+packs+templates only. Option C is rejected as a resolution: deferring the rename leaves D5 diverged from disk, and parking publish_target in the required-synced file behind an allowlist guard knowingly re-opens the C4 leak — exactly the defect class this review removes; it is acceptable only as an explicit stop-gap, not a decision. Recommend A; fall back to B on maintainer preference for the publish convenience.

options.title: Make the DATA/STATE remotes-store split (M3) an explicit, decomposed E item with a cutover-aligned migration — it is a real reimplementation, not a path remap

options.name: Option A — Decompose M3 into an explicit E checklist (store split + 6-function rewrite + vault-git decoupling + cutover-backup migration), keep it owned by E
options.description: Promote the existing one-line E entry 'M3 cmd-remote.sh decoupling' (design.md §9 line 378; ADR-0016 Reuse/Drop line 367) into an explicit, decomposed E sub-checklist with sized work items, WITHOUT adding a new ADR (the verdict is already settled by ADR-0013 D2 / ADR-0015 D3/§refines-0007 / ADR-0016 D6). The decomposition: (1) NEW STATE token store `<state>/cco/remotes-token` (0600) + a STATE-path helper; (2) NEW DATA registry `<data>/cco/remotes` (name->url only) + a DATA-path helper; (3) rewrite all 6 cmd-remote.sh functions (remote_get_url/remote_get_token/remote_resolve_token_for_url/remote_list_names + _cmd_remote_add/remove/set-token/remove-token/list) to read URLs from DATA and tokens from STATE; (4) DROP the vault-git coupling (cmd-remote.sh:123-127 add, 172-174 remove) since the `~/.cco` git-remote-add was a vault affordance and the vault is removed; (5) DROP the legacy dual-read helper `_cco_remotes_file` (paths.sh:39-41) per the ADR-0006 no-dual-read cutover; (6) the legacy `.cco/remotes` is brought forward ONLY via the `cco migrate`/first-run backup path (ADR-0006/0016 §9), split there into DATA url + STATE token — NOT a standalone dual-read migration. Add a Phase-3 test row (extends design.md §11) covering the url/DATA + token/STATE split and the no-token-leak check (the security verification S already owns per ADR-0015 D-verify). The 5 callers (cmd-pack.sh, cmd-project-install.sh, cmd-project-publish.sh, cmd-project-update.sh, update-remote.sh) consume only the public helpers, so they need NO change — the split is encapsulated behind remote_get_url/remote_get_token/remote_resolve_token_for_url.
pros: Records the true magnitude (new STATE store + new DATA store + 6-function rewrite + vault decoupling + cutover-backup split) so E does not under-estimate it as a path remap — directly answers the finding's core concern

Adds NO new ADR and reopens NO settled decision: the destination/sync verdict is already final (ADR-0013 D2, ADR-0015 D3, ADR-0016 D6); only the impl decomposition is missing — keeps the design surface stable

Encapsulation is preserved: the public helpers (remote_get_url/get_token/resolve_token_for_url) keep the same signatures, so the 5 downstream callers are untouched — minimizes blast radius and is faithful to the existing code

Aligns the migration with the already-decided ADR-0006 breaking-cutover model: legacy `.cco/remotes` rides the vault backup (read once by `cco migrate`), not a lingering dual-read path — so it correctly tears down `_cco_remotes_file` instead of preserving it
Folds the existing S-owned no-token-leak verification (ADR-0015 D-verify / ADR-0016 line 374) into a concrete test row, closing the loop between the security invariant and a Phase-3 check
cons: Leaves the security invariant as a test/verification rather than an enforced structural guarantee — a future regression that writes a token to the DATA file would only be caught by the test, not prevented (mitigated by Option C's allowlist)
Decomposition lives in §9/§11 prose + the ADR-0016 Reuse/Drop table; without a dedicated ADR, the rationale for the 4-way decomposition is slightly more diffuse than a single ADR section
Requires touching three living docs (design.md §9 + §11, ADR-0016 Reuse/Drop note) to land the decomposition coherently
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P1 (token+url are both internal, CLI-managed — never hand-edited), P6 (hidden internal, never in a config bucket), P11(b) (token sync-class = `never` for security; url = `required`), P3/P4 (the single file had heterogeneous sync-profiles → split signal, co-locate by profile), P10/P11 (split by role+sync-profile not by surface co-location). Honors ADR-0006 (breaking cutover, no dual-read; legacy data via backup only). Consistent with ADR-0013 D2, ADR-0015 D3 (refines ADR-0007 §Dec-2), ADR-0016 D6/D7 and the M3 verdict (ADR-0016 lines 355/367/378). Strains nothing already settled.

options.name: Option B — Record M3 as a dedicated implementation ADR (E-0021) capturing the reimplementation scope, callers-unaffected analysis, and cutover migration
options.description: Same technical decomposition as Option A, but additionally write a short dedicated ADR (next free = 0021) that records: the as-is co-habitation (url + name.token in one file under the config bucket), the to-be (DATA url-only + STATE token-only 0600), the explicit verdict that this is a reimplementation not a path remap (new STATE token store + new helpers + 6-function rewrite + vault-git decoupling), the caller-impact analysis (5 callers unaffected because they go through the 3 public helpers), and the cutover-backup migration (ADR-0006-aligned, no dual-read). The ADR cross-links ADR-0013 D2 / ADR-0015 D3 / ADR-0016 D6 as the upstream verdicts it implements, and is explicitly framed as 'implementation-scope ADR' (does not change any classification, only records HOW E lands an already-decided split).
pros: Maximum continuity value: a single ADR preserves the full reasoning (as-is/to-be/caller-impact/migration) for the agent that implements E, matching the documentation-first + ADR-for-continuity rules
Makes the 'reimplementation not path remap' verdict unambiguous and citable, directly neutralizing the finding's 'reuses the store as-is' misreading
Gives the cutover migration (legacy `.cco/remotes` -> DATA+STATE via backup) a named home, which currently no doc captures explicitly
Same minimal blast radius as Option A (callers unchanged)
cons: ADRs 0013/0015/0016 already settle the verdict; a new ADR risks being perceived as re-litigating or duplicating settled decisions rather than recording impl — must be carefully scoped as impl-only

The V gate is a READ-ONLY impl-readiness review; spawning a brand-new ADR mid-review slightly inflates the ADR set (0021 was reserved for the NEXT design question, per the roadmap) for what is fundamentally an E-checklist item
Heavier than the finding requires — the finding asks for an explicit E item, not necessarily a new decision record
options.principleAlignment: Same principle alignment as Option A (P1/P3/P4/P6/P10/P11(b); ADR-0006/0013/0015/0016 consistent). Additionally strongest fit with the project's documentation/ADR-continuity rule. Mild tension with the 'don't re-open settled decisions / ADRs distil cross-cutting verdicts' convention if the ADR is not tightly scoped as implementation-only (it must explicitly defer the classification to 0013/0015/0016).

options.name: Option C — Option A plus a structural no-token-leak guarantee (typed DATA registry + sanitizer assertion) instead of test-only verification
options.description: Take Option A's decomposition and harden the security invariant structurally rather than relying on a test. Concretely: (1) the DATA `remotes` registry stores ONLY `name -> url` keys with a typed/whitelisted key shape (reject any `.token`-suffixed key on write — the writer physically cannot persist a token into DATA); (2) add a defensive assertion in the DATA write path and in the (future T) sync allowlist so the DATA store is checked to contain no secret-shaped values before it is ever pushed cross-PC; (3) token lives only in STATE `remotes-token` (0600), with set-token/remove-token the sole writers. Same 6-function rewrite, same vault-git decoupling, same cutover-backup migration as Option A. The no-token-leak check becomes an enforced invariant at the write/sync boundary, with the Phase-3 test asserting the guard fires.
pros: Converts the P11(b) security invariant from a verification into a structural guarantee — a token can never reach the DATA file that DATA-sync will propagate cross-PC, closing the exact risk the finding raises ('required cross-PC sync propagates secrets')

Aligns with the STATE `/session` vs `/update` allowlist-boundary philosophy already adopted (design.md §2.2 line 160; ADR-0013) — the same 'a secret can never be swept into a sync' design intent, applied to DATA
Future-proofs the T (R-state-sync / DATA sync engine) work: the per-store allowlist already assumes a sync boundary check, so the DATA-side guard fits the planned transport (ADR-0015 D6)
cons: More implementation surface than Option A (typed-key validation + sanitizer assertion + the wiring into the future sync allowlist) for a 2-user base where the token has always been excluded from sync already (ADR-0013 line 101 'already excluded today')

Part of the guarantee (the sync-boundary assertion) lives in T's transport, which is post-v1 — so the structural guarantee is only partial until T ships; the DATA write-path key-rejection is the v1-deliverable half
Risks over-engineering relative to the finding, which only asks for an explicit E item; the structural guard is a quality enhancement, not a blocker
options.principleAlignment: Strongest on P6 (hidden internal protected from leak) and P11(b) (security invariant: a secret never syncs — made structural). Consistent with ADR-0013 D2 allowlist-boundary intent and ADR-0015 D6 per-store sync-class allowlist (owned by T). Same ADR-0006/0013/0015/0016 consistency as Option A. Slight tension with YAGNI / lean-surface (ADR-0006 spirit) for the known 2-user base, since the token is already non-synced today.

options.recommended: Option A — Decompose M3 into an explicit E checklist (store split + 6-function rewrite + vault-git decoupling + cutover-backup migration), keep it owned by E
options.rationale: The finding is technically correct that the remotes split is a reimplementation (new STATE token store + new helpers + 6-function rewrite + vault-git decoupling), not a path remap — but the verdict and destination are ALREADY settled (ADR-0013 D2, ADR-0015 D3 refining ADR-0007 §Dec-2, ADR-0016 D6), and ADR-0016 already names 'M3 cmd-remote.sh decoupling' as real E work (lines 355/367/378). So no new decision is needed — what is missing is the explicit, sized DECOMPOSITION of that E item plus a named cutover migration. Option A delivers exactly that without reopening settled ground or inflating the ADR set, which fits the V gate's read-only, design-stable posture. It correctly aligns the migration with the already-decided ADR-0006 breaking-cutover (legacy `.cco/remotes` comes via the vault backup read once by `cco migrate`, and the dual-read `_cco_remotes_file` is TORN DOWN, not preserved — the finding's 'migration for existing in-bucket .cco/remotes' must be cutover-shaped, not a lingering dual-read). It also corrects a minor over-reach in the finding: the 5 downstream callers are unaffected because they go through the 3 public helpers, and the 'reuses source + remote_cache machinery' phrase refers to `cco update --check`, not the remotes store — so the store-split scope is real but encapsulated. Option B is a reasonable upgrade if the maintainer wants a single citable continuity record, but a new ADR for an already-settled verdict risks looking like re-litigation and consumes 0021 (reserved for the next design question). Option C is the right long-term security posture (structural no-token-leak guard), but half of it depends on the post-v1 T transport and it exceeds what the finding asks for a base where the token is already non-synced; its key-rejection half can be folded into Option A's E checklist as a cheap hardening if desired.

options.title: design.md §11 Test Plan has zero coverage for the entire S cycle and coordinate model (sync-before-publish data-loss defect, S8 no-token-leak invariant, pack/repo/llms coordinates, 2×2 verbs, structure discovery, validate/protect)

options.name: Add a phase-keyed S-cycle / coordinate-model test block to §11, sequenced by the F2 phase map (recommended)
options.description: Treat F7 as the explicit §11 twin of the F2 §9 phasing gap (both share the not-updated-after-S root cause) and resolve them together. After F2 decides the §9 phase home for S-cycle work (the natural landing is a new Phase 4 — coordinate model + sharing-repo realignment — since the work strictly post-dates the Phase 3 legacy-vault teardown and depends on the manifest already being deletable), extend the existing §11 phase table with rows keyed to those phases. Concretely: (1) coordinate-schema rows under the same phase as the schema+migration (pack/repo/llms name-only -> {name,url?,ref?,resource?}; idempotent migration; legacy central llms ~/.cco/llms/<name>/source covered by ADR-0016); (2) a dedicated sync-before-publish row exercising co-maintainer divergence -> pull + 3-way base/ merge, NO fast-forward clobber (regression-pinning the lib/cmd-pack.sh:1024 defect); (3) 2x2 verb wiring incl. the projects-don't-publish guard (ADR-0018 D2); (4) structure-based discovery + init-at-first-publish/merge-on-existing (ADR-0018 D3); (5) three-layer pack resolution + name-collision (ADR-0019 D5); (6) cco config validate reachability contract (every referenced id has a coordinate, ids unique per section, machine-agnostic G8 — exit-code-only, non-blocking); (7) internalize-as-cache prompt + export --bundle-packs dependency-closure (ADR-0019 D6); (8) cco update --check (ADR-0018 D5); (9) S8 no-token-leak checklist tests (ADR-0020 D5 — token only in STATE/0600/never, de-tokenized coordinates, config-only bundle); (10) cco config protect scaffold (CODEOWNERS + instructions, ADR-0020 D4). Keep the existing 'Reuse/Rewrite/Remove' framing (e.g. test_manifest.sh -> Remove, test_pack_publish.sh -> Rewrite for sync-before-publish). Severity-pair high with F2 so the phasing decision drives both edits in one pass.
pros: Honors the explicit ADR test mandates verbatim: ADR-0020 D5 defines S8 as 'a checklist + tests'; ADR-0019/0018 Build-new tables list sync-before-publish, validate, structure-discovery, --check as net-new to E — those need a verification contract

Keeps §11's existing phase-keyed structure, so the riskiest E work (publish merge semantics, token-leak) ships with a defined contract — satisfies workflow.md design-driven testing and P10 (test the contract, not internals)
Pins the lone data-loss bug (fast-forward clobber, lib/cmd-pack.sh:1024) with a regression test BEFORE the fix is written — exactly the TDD ordering workflow.md mandates
Resolving F7+F2 jointly removes the ordering hazard the proposedResolution flags ('after the §9 phase home is decided') and prevents a half-synced design
Net-additive to a living doc; no settled decision is touched — purely closes a documentation/coverage gap the V gate exists to find
cons: Hard-coupled to F2: cannot be finalized until the §9 phase map is decided, so it is a two-step edit (phase decision -> test rows), not a standalone fix
Adds a sizeable new block to §11 — the table grows from 4 phase rows to ~5, with dense per-row content (mitigated: matches the existing dense style of Phases 0-3)
If F2 lands the S work as sub-items inside existing phases rather than a new Phase 4, the row keying must be reworked accordingly
options.principleAlignment: Upholds the design-driven-test contract (workflow.md) and P10 (verify the role/contract). Directly discharges ADR-0020 D5 (S8 = checklist + tests), ADR-0019 D4 (sync-before-publish defect -> fix -> needs a regression test), ADR-0018 D5/D3/D2 and ADR-0019 D2/D5/D6 Build-new items. Coherent with P16 (sync-before-publish, never fast-forward-clobber) by making it testable, and P17/P14 (validate/protect surface, never hard-block) by testing exit-code-only behavior. Strains nothing; the only tension is the deliberate F2 dependency, which the V-handoff §3.5 already frames as a joint phasing concern.

options.name: Add a phase-agnostic 'S-cycle & coordinate-model' test sub-section now, decoupled from F2
options.description: Add the same ten test areas as a standalone §11 sub-section (e.g. §11.1 'Coordinate model & sharing cycle tests') grouped by capability rather than by §9 phase, so F7 can be closed immediately without waiting on the F2 phase decision. Each test area cites its ADR/decision but does not assert a phase number; a one-line note says 'phase placement tracks §9 once F2 lands.' The capability groups: coordinate schema+migration; sync-before-publish 3-way merge; 2x2 verbs + projects-don't-publish guard; structure-discovery + init-at-first-publish; three-layer resolution + collision; validate reachability; internalize-as-cache + export --bundle-packs; update --check; S8 no-token-leak; config protect.
pros: Closes F7 immediately with no F2 dependency — the coverage gap (especially the data-loss defect and S8) is documented at once
Capability grouping mirrors how the ADRs themselves are organized (one decision = one test area), making traceability ADR->test trivial
Avoids re-keying churn if F2 ends up restructuring §9 phases significantly
cons: Breaks the established §11 convention (a single phase-keyed table) — introduces a second organizing axis, which is exactly the kind of design-doc inconsistency the V gate should reduce, not add

A capability-grouped block divorced from the phase map weakens the design-driven workflow link: tests are supposed to be written alongside the phase that builds the feature (workflow.md), and a phase-less list invites 'we'll wire phases later' drift — the same not-updated root cause that produced F7
Leaves a known seam (the 'phase placement tracks F2' note) that a future reader must reconcile — a deferred-coherence cost
options.principleAlignment: Still discharges the ADR test mandates (ADR-0020 D5, ADR-0019 D4) at the content level and respects P10. Weakly strains the design-driven-test discipline (workflow.md) because phase-less tests detach from the build sequence. Neutral on P14/P16/P17. Coherent with all settled decisions (purely additive).

options.name: Add only the two highest-risk test rows now (sync-before-publish + S8), defer the rest to E
options.description: Minimal scope: add §11 rows ONLY for the two safety-critical contracts the finding singles out — (a) sync-before-publish 3-way merge with a no-clobber regression test pinning lib/cmd-pack.sh:1024, and (b) the S8 no-token-leak checklist tests (token STATE-only/0600/never, de-tokenized coordinates, config-only bundle). The remaining S-cycle/coordinate test areas (schema+migration, 2x2 verbs, structure discovery, validate, internalize, --check, protect) are recorded as a one-line 'E owns the full S-cycle test matrix per ADR-0018/0019/0020' pointer rather than enumerated rows.
pros: Smallest doc change; directly addresses the two items the finding's 'why' calls out as riskiest (publish merge semantics, token-leak safety)
Pins the data-loss defect and the security invariant — the highest-severity coverage gaps — without waiting on F2
Low risk of churn if F2 reshapes phases
cons: Leaves the bulk of the S cycle (the schema migration, the boundary-less reachability mechanism, the 2x2 guard, structure discovery) with NO verification contract — re-creating a smaller version of the very gap F7 reports; the V gate would likely re-flag the residue
Contradicts the proposedResolution, which enumerates the full set of rows — a partial close looks like the finding was only half-understood

ADR-0019 explicitly lists structure-discovery, validate, --check, internalize as Build-new (-> E); shipping them with a bare pointer instead of a test contract weakens the design-driven workflow for net-new code
The 'E owns the matrix' pointer pushes test design into the coding phase, inverting workflow.md's test-before/alongside ordering
options.principleAlignment: Partially upholds the ADR mandates (covers ADR-0019 D4 and ADR-0020 D5 — the safety pair) but leaves ADR-0018 D2/D3/D5 and ADR-0019 D1/D2/D5/D6 untested at the design level, straining workflow.md design-driven testing and P10 for the deferred items. Coherent with settled decisions (additive) but incomplete against the V gate's completeness criterion.

options.recommended: Add a phase-keyed S-cycle / coordinate-model test block to §11, sequenced by the F2 phase map (recommended)
options.rationale: Option 1 is correct because F7 is, by the finding's own framing and the V-handoff (§3.5 phasing + §3.2 design↔ADR sync), the §11 twin of the F2 §9 gap with the same not-updated-after-S root cause — they must be resolved as one pass to avoid a half-synced design. It keeps §11's single phase-keyed table (no new organizing axis, unlike Option 2) and gives every Build-new item listed in ADR-0018/0019/0020 a verification contract, which is required because workflow.md mandates design-driven tests (contract before/alongside code) and ADR-0020 D5 literally defines S8 as 'a checklist + tests.' It pins the lone data-loss bug (fast-forward clobber at lib/cmd-pack.sh:1024) with a regression test before the fix — the TDD ordering the rules require — and it does so for the security invariant too, which Option 3 also does but Option 3 then strands the rest of the S cycle, re-creating a smaller F7 the V gate would re-flag. The only cost is the deliberate F2 coupling; that coupling is intrinsic to a phase-keyed test plan and is a feature (one coherent phasing decision drives both §9 and §11), not a defect. No settled decision is contradicted — the change is purely additive coverage that closes a gap the V read-only gate exists to surface. Files: /workspace/claude-orchestrator/docs/maintainer/configuration/decentralized-config/design.md (§11 at lines 722-733, depends on §9 at 600-670); defect at /workspace/claude-orchestrator/lib/cmd-pack.sh:1024; ADRs at decisions/0018-…, 0019-… (D2/D4/D5), 0020-… (D4/D5); principles P10/P14/P16/P17 in guiding-principles.md.

options.title: Reconcile the three divergent ~/.cco allowlist .gitignore member lists into one authoritative enumeration that whitelists every committed D8 member (esp. secrets.env.example) without re-including secrets.env

options.name: Option A — Make ADR-0016 D8 the single source of truth and derive the whitelist mechanically from the D8 committed-tree (recommended)
options.description: Designate the ADR-0016 D8 authoritative ~/.cco tree (lines 250-261) as the SINGLE enumeration of committed members, and define the whitelist as the exact 1:1 image of that tree. Concretely the allowlist becomes: `*` then `!.gitignore !packs/ !templates/ !global/.claude/ !languages !setup.sh !setup-build.sh !mcp-packages.txt !secrets.env.example`. `secrets.env` is NOT whitelisted, so it stays denied by the `*` base (correct). Record this one line in ADR-0016 D8 (the authoritative-tree section that already enumerates the members) as the canonical allowlist; annotate ADR-0008 §3 as 'superseded-in-detail by ADR-0016 D8 — see D8 for the full member list' (keep ADR-0008's PRINCIPLE: whitelist + explicit staging + never git add -A); change design.md §6.1's prose enumeration to a cross-reference to D8 instead of a second hand-maintained list. Add an explicit invariant to D8: 'the whitelist is the exact image of the committed-tree; adding/removing a committed member updates BOTH in the same edit.' Confirm in D8 that the FR-S3 content-scan .example exemption is the SECOND barrier (independent of staging) and that secrets.env.example passes the filename+content scan while secrets.env is blocked.
pros: Eliminates the root cause: divergence is impossible when one authoritative tree mechanically generates the whitelist (DRY — one source of truth, not three hand-kept lists)

D8 is already the 'exhaustive two-CONFIG-buckets' section (it self-describes as 'fixes C3/C4'), so it is the natural canonical home — consistent with the consolidation ADR-0016 already performed for the resource taxonomy
Fixes the FR-S4/C3 correctness bug: secrets.env.example is explicitly whitelisted, so committed skeletons reach git and a second PC finds them on pull
Keeps secrets.env denied by construction (base `*`, not whitelisted), so the security invariant (FR-S2/FR-S3) holds with no extra rule
ADR supersession is precise (detail-only), preserving ADR-0008's still-valid principle and audit trail (documentation.md ADR practice: supersede, link, don't delete rationale)
The 'whitelist = exact image of the tree' invariant makes future drift a single-edit discipline and is directly mechanizable in the implementer's cco config save staging code
cons: Requires touching three documents (ADR-0016 D8 to add the canonical line + invariant, ADR-0008 §3 supersession note, design.md §6.1 cross-reference) — but this is the only option that removes the divergence permanently
Slightly more ADR bookkeeping (a supersede-in-detail annotation) than a blunt overwrite
The implementer must still wire the whitelist literally from D8 — but that is now a single unambiguous source
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P1 (config = user-authored, IDE-reachable, versioned — secrets.env.example IS config that must be committed) and P2 (~/.cco holds only authored config). Upholds the DRY discipline distilled in P10/P11/P12 (one canonical value, referenced not duplicated — three hand-kept lists were exactly the denormalization those principles warn against). Honors ADR-0016 (consolidated taxonomy — D8 is the authoritative bucket enumeration) and ADR-0008 (preserves its whitelist+explicit-staging+never-git-add-A PRINCIPLE while superseding only the stale member list). Satisfies requirements FR-S3 (.example exemption, two-barrier) and FR-S4 (secrets.env.example MUST be committed). Does not contradict ADR-0015/0011 (tags.yml stays OUT — not whitelisted, lives in DATA) or ADR-0012 (manifest.yml removed — not whitelisted). Aligns with documentation.md ADR practice (supersede-and-link).

options.name: Option B — Patch all three lists in place to the same explicit enumeration (no single source of truth)
options.description: Keep all three locations as independent enumerations but edit each to the identical full list: `*` then `!.gitignore !packs/ !templates/ !global/.claude/ !languages !setup.sh !setup-build.sh !mcp-packages.txt !secrets.env.example`. Update ADR-0008 §3, design.md §6.1, and ADR-0016 D8 to all carry the same line verbatim. No supersession; no designated canonical home; a note added to each ('keep in sync with the other two') for discipline.
pros: Immediately fixes the correctness bug in every location at once — an implementer reading ANY of the three now gets the correct whitelist
Minimal conceptual change — no ADR supersession semantics to reason about
Each document remains self-contained (a reader of ADR-0008 alone sees the full correct list)
cons: Does NOT remove the root cause — three hand-maintained copies remain, so the SAME drift will recur on the next member addition (this is exactly how the current divergence arose)
Violates DRY: three sources of truth for one value, the denormalization P10/P11/P12 explicitly warn against
'Keep in sync' notes are weak discipline; the next editor will update one and forget the others — re-creating F8
Larger ongoing maintenance burden and a standing latent-bug risk for E and beyond
options.principleAlignment: Satisfies P1/P2 and FR-S3/FR-S4 for the immediate fix (secrets.env.example whitelisted, secrets.env denied). BUT strains the DRY discipline central to P10/P11/P12 (canonical-once, reference-not-duplicate) and the spirit of ADR-0016's consolidation effort — it re-entrenches the multi-list denormalization rather than resolving it. Does not contradict ADR-0015/0011/0012 (tags/manifest still excluded). Weaker alignment with documentation.md (no clear canonical location).

options.name: Option C — Replace the whitelist with a denylist-only model (mirror the <repo>/.cco/.gitignore secret-deny pattern)
options.description: Drop the `*`-deny whitelist for ~/.cco and instead use a small denylist that ignores only secrets and known internal/generated artifacts: `secrets.env`, `*.env`, `*.key`, `*.pem`, `.credentials.json`, `!secrets.env.example`, plus belt-and-braces ignores for anything internal that could appear (installed/, remote_cache, coords-lookup, *.bak). This mirrors the project-level <repo>/.cco/.gitignore secret-deny pattern (design.md line 118-126) and removes the 'silently un-stageable committed file' failure mode entirely — any new authored file is committed by default.
pros: Structurally eliminates the F8 failure class: a committed authored file can never be silently dropped because the default is 'tracked'
Symmetry with the <repo>/.cco/.gitignore (which already uses a secret-denylist), arguably one mental model for both buckets
Lowest staging-code complexity (no per-member whitelist to maintain)
cons: DIRECTLY CONTRADICTS settled ADR-0008 decision #3 and the whole 'allowlist double-barrier' rationale (ADR-0008 Consequences: 'the allowlist double-barrier is structurally safer than the old denylist'). This is a non-superseded decision — reversing it is exactly what the task forbids

Re-introduces the risk ADR-0008 explicitly closed: an internal/state file landing in ~/.cco would be auto-committed (a denylist can't anticipate every future internal artifact), weakening P6 (hide internal, never in a config repo) and G8 (truthful git diff)
Requires the precondition that ~/.cco is 'authored-content-only' (ADR-0007/ADR-0016 D8) to hold perfectly forever — a fragile invariant the whitelist was chosen to NOT depend on
Larger blast radius: changes the security posture of the personal store, not just an enumeration
ADR-0008 explicitly drops the vault's `git add -A`/denylist approach as inferior; this option walks it back
options.principleAlignment: Strains/contradicts P6 (a denylist cannot guarantee internal files never reach the config repo — the whitelist's whole point) and the ADR-0008 'safer-than-denylist' decision (settled, non-superseded — reversing violates the task constraint). Weakly upholds P1/FR-S4 (secrets.env.example committed) but at the cost of the deliberate Axis-1 safety margin. Tension with ADR-0007's 'clean authored-only store' precondition. Not recommended: it solves F8 by discarding a deliberate, still-valid decision rather than reconciling the lists.

options.recommended: Option A — Make ADR-0016 D8 the single source of truth and derive the whitelist mechanically from the D8 committed-tree (recommended)
options.rationale: F8 is a confirmed high-severity correctness bug: the ~/.cco/.gitignore is a `*`-deny whitelist, so any committed file not explicitly `!`-whitelisted is silently un-stageable. ADR-0008 §3 fixes only 4 exceptions (`!packs/ !templates/ !global/.claude/ !.gitignore`); design.md §6.1 adds setup*/mcp-packages/languages; but ADR-0016 D8's authoritative tree (the section that 'fixes C3/C4') additionally places secrets.env.example, setup-build.sh, languages, setup.sh, mcp-packages.txt as committed — and NONE of the whitelist forms includes secrets.env.example, the C3/FR-S4 skeleton that MUST be committed. An implementer wiring staging from ADR-0008 §3 ships a store where the skeleton never reaches git, surfacing only when a second PC pulls. Option A is recommended because it fixes the bug AND removes its cause: it designates the one section that already enumerates the bucket (ADR-0016 D8) as the single source of truth, derives the whitelist as the exact image of that tree, and reduces the other two locations to a supersede-note (ADR-0008 §3) and a cross-reference (design.md §6.1). This honors the DRY discipline that P10/P11/P12 and the entire ADR-0016 consolidation embody — three hand-kept lists were the very denormalization that produced F8. The fix is safe by construction: secrets.env.example is `!`-whitelisted while secrets.env stays denied by the `*` base (no rule re-includes it), and the FR-S3 content-scan .example exemption remains an independent second barrier. Option B fixes the symptom but leaves three copies to drift again; Option C is rejected because switching to a denylist directly reverses settled, non-superseded ADR-0008 decision #3 ('allowlist double-barrier is structurally safer than the old denylist') and weakens P6 — which the task forbids. Confirm during E: the staging code reads the D8 enumeration verbatim, and a test asserts secrets.env.example is stageable while secrets.env is refused (design.md §9 Phase 3 test_config.sh already lists this assertion).

options.title: Resolve the migration backup artifact's self-contradictory shape (flatten-plain vs preserves-history) and reconcile the backup PATH across ADR-0006/FR-M1 (~/.cco) and design §2.2/§9 + ADR-0016 D6 (STATE)

options.name: Option 1 (RECOMMENDED) — Raw tar.gz of the whole legacy vault dir (incl .git); drop the flatten-at-save step; flatten happens at read time in cco migrate; relocate path to STATE
options.description: Define the FR-M1 first-run backup as a single faithful snapshot: tar.gz of the entire legacy vault directory INCLUDING .git (all profile branches) AND the on-disk gitignored files (secrets.env, *.env, .credentials.json, *.key, *.pem). This is a pure capture step — no profile-iteration, no serialization, no profile->tag translation at save time. The profile->tag conversion and the per-project read move ENTIRELY into `cco migrate <project>` (the sole legacy reader, ADR-0006 §39): migrate checks out / reads the relevant profile branch from the archived .git, extracts that project's config, flattens profiles into tag seeds (<data>/cco/tags.yml, ADR-0015), and writes a machine-agnostic <repo>/.cco/. design §9 line 706 ('the backup archive preserves full vault history') becomes literally TRUE and is kept verbatim. The flatten wording in design §9:694-697/702-703 and FR-M1:275-279 is rewritten: the archive is raw; the flatten/no-branch-traversal description moves to the `cco migrate` reader contract. Path: edit ADR-0006 §Decision-2 and requirements.md FR-M1 to write to <state>/cco/backups/vault-<date>.tar.gz (STATE, machine-local, never synced), matching design §2.2/§9 and ADR-0016 D6 / inventory C1, with design.md remaining canonical.
pros: Maximally lossless — captures profile branch history AND on-disk gitignored secrets (secrets.env etc.), directly satisfying ADR-0006's lossless-migration promise (§21-22) and closing the F1 secrets-capture concern; secrets are recoverable per-project at migrate time

The backup step becomes a trivial, robust `tar czf` — no branch-walking serializer to write/test, which is exactly ADR-0006's ethos of removing migration machinery and keeping the legacy reader minimal (only cco migrate understands branches)
