Resolves the self-contradiction by making line 706 true rather than deleting it: the archive genuinely preserves full vault history
Idempotency / verify-before-read (M8) is simpler on a single opaque archive: archive integrity = `tar tzf` succeeds, no per-resource schema validation
Rollback story is the strongest of all options: the original vault, branches and all, is recoverable byte-for-byte
cons: The reader (cco migrate) must do branch traversal / `git --work-tree` extraction against the archived .git — slightly more reader complexity than reading a pre-flattened plain layout (contradicts design §9:702-703 'no branch traversal', which must be rewritten)
Archive is larger (includes .git objects for all branches + claude-state if present); mitigated by gzip and by this being a one-time machine-local artifact in CACHE-adjacent STATE

The backup deliberately contains secrets in cleartext on disk — acceptable because it lives in STATE (0700, never synced, never committed, P6/P8) and is exactly where the secrets already lived, but it must be documented and the offer-to-remove must warn
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P1 (secrets.env is user-edited config; capturing it preserves the only copy of those values), P6/P8 (archive in STATE 0700, never-team, never-synced — no leak surface), and ADR-0006 (lossless + minimal-reader). Aligns the path with ADR-0016 D6 / inventory C1 / design §2.2 (STATE relocation). Strengthens M8 (verify-before-read). Does NOT strain any settled decision; it makes design §9 line 706 self-consistent instead of contradicting it.

options.name: Option 2 — Flattened plain export (no .git), explicitly secrets-inclusive; correct line 706 to drop the false history claim; relocate path to STATE
options.description: Keep the dominant repeated spec (design §9 twice + FR-M1): the save/backup step iterates all profile branches and serializes each profile's resources into a plain, branchless tar.gz, translating each profile into a tag annotation on its resources (profiles -> tags). Make TWO things explicit that are currently under-specified: (1) the flatten MUST also copy the on-disk gitignored files (secrets.env, *.env, .credentials.json, *.key, *.pem) into the plain layout per origin profile — otherwise migration silently loses secrets (F1); (2) since a flattened branchless layout cannot preserve git commit/branch history, design §9 line 706 is corrected from 'the backup archive preserves full vault history' to 'the backup archive preserves all profile resources flattened, with each resource tagged by its origin profile' (rollback = re-import the flatten, not the git history). `cco migrate` reads the plain layout with a normal retrieve, no branch traversal (design §9:702-703 stands). Path: same STATE relocation as Option 1 — edit ADR-0006 §Decision-2 and FR-M1 to <state>/cco/backups/vault-<date>.tar.gz.
pros: Honours the dominant, twice-repeated spec — smallest doc churn to the migration narrative; design §9:702-703 'no branch traversal' stays true
The reader (cco migrate) is the simplest possible: an ordinary plain retrieve, no git plumbing against an archived .git
Already encodes the profiles->tags model at the artifact boundary, matching ADR-0010/0011/0015 (origin profile -> tag seed) so the migrate-time tag seeding is trivial
Smaller archive (no .git objects, no other-branch duplication beyond what flatten emits)
cons: Requires writing and testing the branch-walking serializer at save time — more migration machinery, against ADR-0006's minimise-the-reader ethos (the complexity moves to the writer instead of being eliminated)

History is genuinely lost: a true byte-for-byte rollback to the old vault is impossible; only the flattened resource set survives (acceptable per ADR-0006's lossless-resource — not lossless-history — promise, but a real reduction vs Option 1)

Secrets capture must be added as an explicit, easily-forgotten step; the flatten serializer touches branch-committed content by default, and gitignored secrets are exactly the files a naive `git`-based serializer skips — high risk of the F1 regression unless coded deliberately
Per-profile uncommitted working-tree changes (design §9:698-700) and gitignored secrets both need bespoke handling in the serializer, increasing the surface that must be correct
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P1 only if the explicit secrets-inclusion clause is added (otherwise it strains P1 by dropping user-authored secret values). Upholds P6/P8 (STATE, never-synced). Consistent with ADR-0010/0011/0015 (profiles->tags at the artifact). Mild tension with ADR-0006's minimal-machinery ethos (adds a writer-side serializer). Aligns path with ADR-0016 D6 / C1. Fixes the contradiction by editing line 706 rather than the artifact.

options.name: Option 3 — Hybrid: raw tar.gz incl .git as the canonical backup, PLUS a flatten step performed lazily by cco migrate writing a derived plain cache; relocate path to STATE
options.description: The first-run safety net produces the raw faithful archive (Option 1's tar incl .git + gitignored secrets) as the single source-of-truth backup at <state>/cco/backups/vault-<date>.tar.gz. The profiles->tags flatten is NOT done at save time and NOT baked into the archive; instead `cco migrate` performs it lazily/on-demand and may materialise a derived, branchless plain view under CACHE (<cache>/cco/ ... regenerable, ADR-0016 D7) purely as a working scratch for the retrieve. Thus the canonical artifact is history-preserving (line 706 true) while the reader still operates on a flat view (design §9:702-703 'no branch traversal' true for the reader's effective input). Path relocation identical to Options 1/2.
pros: Both seemingly-contradictory statements become simultaneously satisfiable on different artifacts (canonical archive preserves history; reader sees a flat view) — fully reconciles §9:702-703 and §9:706
Lossless like Option 1 (history + secrets), reader simplicity like Option 2
The derived flat view is correctly classified as CACHE (regenerable from the canonical archive), consistent with the bucket taxonomy
cons: Most moving parts of the three options: a raw archive PLUS a lazy flattener PLUS a CACHE materialisation — directly against ADR-0006's drive to minimise the legacy reader/migration surface for a 2-user base
Introduces a second derived artifact (CACHE plain view) whose lifecycle, idempotency, and cleanup must be specified and tested — disproportionate for a one-time migration

Over-engineering risk: the flat CACHE view is an internal implementation detail of the reader and arguably should not be a documented artifact at all (it can be an in-memory/tmp step, which collapses this into Option 1)
Two places now describe 'the backup', re-inviting the very ambiguity this finding is closing
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P1/P6/P8 like Option 1, and is taxonomy-clean (raw->STATE, derived flat view->CACHE per ADR-0016 D7). But it strains ADR-0006's minimal-machinery principle the most and adds a documented-artifact surface the design otherwise avoids. Path aligned with ADR-0016 D6 / C1. Sound but heavier than warranted.

options.recommended: Option 1 (RECOMMENDED) — Raw tar.gz of the whole legacy vault dir (incl .git); drop the flatten-at-save step; flatten happens at read time in cco migrate; relocate path to STATE
options.rationale: Code grounding is decisive: in the legacy vault (lib/cmd-vault.sh) profiles ARE git branches and secrets (secrets.env, *.env, .credentials.json, *.key, *.pem) are GITIGNORED — they exist only on disk, never in git history, and .cco/backups/ is itself gitignored inside the vault. This means the artifact-shape choice is not cosmetic: only a raw tar that includes .git AND the gitignored working-tree files captures BOTH the full profile history and the user's secret values. Option 1 therefore makes design §9 line 706 ('preserves full vault history') literally true instead of stray wording, and most directly satisfies ADR-0006's lossless-migration promise and the F1 secrets-capture concern (P1: secrets.env is user-authored config whose only copy must survive). It is also the SIMPLEST writer — a single `tar czf` — pushing the only branch-aware logic into cco migrate, the sole legacy reader ADR-0006 §39 already mandates; this honours ADR-0006's ethos of removing migration machinery rather than adding a branch-walking serializer (Option 2) or a second derived artifact (Option 3). The trade-off — the reader must extract from the archived .git, contradicting the current §9:702-703 'no branch traversal' wording — is acceptable and is a deliberate rewrite, not a regression. Both sub-decisions of this finding resolve together: pick Option 1's raw-tar artifact AND adopt the already-settled STATE relocation. The path question is NOT genuinely open — ADR-0016 D6, inventory C1, and design §2.2/§9 all place the backup at <state>/cco/backups/ (STATE: machine-local, never-synced, 0700, P6/P8), correctly avoiding the authored-config-only ~/.cco (P6; and the ADR-0008 whitelist .gitignore would never stage backups/ anyway, but co-locating a secrets-laden archive under the config bucket is exactly the cleanliness violation P6 forbids). The residual edits are: (a) leave design.md canonical with line 706 verbatim under Option 1; (b) rewrite design §9:694-697/702-703 + FR-M1:275-279 so the flatten/no-branch-traversal text describes the cco migrate READER, not the archive; (c) edit ADR-0006 §Decision-2 and requirements.md FR-M1 from ~/.cco/backups/ to <state>/cco/backups/vault-<date>.tar.gz citing ADR-0016 D6 / C1. This is a maintainer decision because it trades reader complexity vs writer complexity and fixes a lossless-vs-history promise; the recommendation favours faithful capture + minimal writer, consistent with every non-superseded decision.

options.title: Profile→tag seeding: one timing/owner for all resource kinds, with a defined pack/template path

options.name: A. Seed tags.yml once at first-run flatten for ALL kinds; per-project prompt becomes a one-time global choice recorded by a STATE marker
options.description: Make the first-run backup/flatten (FR-M1, design §9:694-697) the SINGLE seeding point for profile→tag origin across all three resource kinds (projects, packs, templates). The flatten already iterates every profile branch with all resources in hand, so it is the only place where the full profile→resource membership is simultaneously known. At first run, cco asks ONE global question — 'convert legacy profiles into tags, or start untagged?' — and records the answer in a STATE marker (alongside the existing 'backed-up' idempotency marker, design §9:680). If the user chooses convert: the flatten writes profile origins directly into <data>/cco/tags.yml under the typed keys packs:/projects:/templates: (ADR-0016 D5). Because templates are ALWAYS shared in the legacy vault (profile-resource-model-analysis §1.1) they carry NO profile origin and get no seeded tags; packs are seeded ONLY when they were profile-exclusive (sync.packs); projects (default-exclusive) carry their branch profile as a tag. The per-project `cco migrate <project>` prompt at design §9:641-643 / ADR-0010 §5 is REMOVED and replaced by reading the already-recorded STATE marker (no re-prompt). tags.yml is keyed by resource identity, not by project, so seeding it before any `cco migrate <project>` runs is sound: tags for a project name persist whether or not its repo has been migrated yet, and `cco list --tag` reads them lazily. Update design §9 (drop the per-project prompt, fold the choice into FR-M1) and ADR-0010 §5 (recast 'cco migrate prompts' → 'first-run global choice, seeded at flatten') to agree.
pros: Single source of truth for seeding timing: all profile knowledge exists exactly once (at flatten), so the data is captured where it is complete — directly satisfies P10/P11 method (classify/seed from the fully-known role, not lazily/partially).

Closes the pack/template gap structurally: they need no migrate command because tags.yml is identity-keyed DATA, not project-scoped — seeding it at flatten reaches all kinds with zero new entry points.
Eliminates the N-times prompt UX wart (user is asked once, globally, not per project).

tags.yml is per-user DATA synced cross-PC (ADR-0015): seeding it at first run means the organization is immediately present and travels via `cco config push/pull` regardless of which projects get migrated, and on which PC.
Idempotent and lossless: a STATE marker prevents re-seeding on a second first-run, mirroring the existing 'backed-up' marker (M8 ordering).
cons: Seeds tags for projects the user may never `cco migrate` (orphan tag entries for never-migrated projects). Mitigated: harmless — tags are advisory metadata; `cco list --tag` simply shows a tag with no resolved resource, and a future `cco config gc` could prune. Arguably correct: it preserves the user's full organization losslessly per AD2.
Requires the flatten step to WRITE into <data>/cco/tags.yml — coupling the migration/backup path to the DATA bucket (must run after J0 bootstraps DATA; ordering invariant to document, extends M8/M6).
Moves a decision point earlier (first run) — the user must answer convert-vs-untagged before seeing any project, with slightly less context than a per-project prompt would give.
options.principleAlignment: UPHOLDS P10/P11 (seed from complete, validated role at the one moment all profile membership is known — not lazily from partial state) · P2/P6 (tags.yml stays internal DATA, identity-keyed, never in config buckets) · ADR-0015 D3/D5 + ADR-0016 D5 (writes the typed packs:/projects:/templates: keys as specified) · AD2 (lossless profiles→tags, per-user, never team). Consistent with ADR-0006 breaking cutover (one-shot data seed, ADR-0010 §5 wording). STRAINS only ADR-0010 §5's literal 'cco migrate prompts per project' phrasing — which this option explicitly proposes to amend to a one-time global choice (the finding's recommended direction).

options.name: B. Keep flatten as the seed-into-backup step; add a one-time 'cco migrate --tags' (or auto on first migrate) that applies the WHOLE seed to tags.yml, independent of per-project migrate
options.description: Two-stage but non-contradictory: the flatten (FR-M1) records each resource's profile origin AS METADATA INSIDE THE BACKUP (e.g. a manifest/sidecar in the archive listing resource→origin-profile), but does NOT write tags.yml yet. A separate, project-independent seeding step applies the full mapping to <data>/cco/tags.yml for all kinds at once: triggered either by an explicit `cco migrate --tags` or implicitly on the FIRST `cco migrate <project>` invocation (guarded by a STATE marker so it runs once, covering packs/templates/projects together regardless of which project triggered it). The per-project prompt at design §9:641-643 is reframed: the FIRST migrate prompts the global convert-vs-untagged choice; subsequent migrates do not re-ask. Packs/templates get seeded by this same one-shot step (no per-pack command), reading their origin from the backup metadata. Update design §9 and ADR-0010 §5 to describe this 'flatten records origin in backup; first-migrate (or explicit --tags) applies it globally' two-stage model.
pros: Keeps tags.yml writes inside the migrate command family (closer to ADR-0010 §5's current 'cco migrate' framing — smaller wording delta to the ADR).
Separates 'capture origin' (flatten, mechanical) from 'commit to tags' (user-chosen, applied once) — clean responsibility split.
Still resolves the pack/template gap: the one-shot global apply covers all kinds from backup metadata, no per-pack/template command needed.
User answers convert-vs-untagged at first migrate, with marginally more context than at raw first-run.
cons: If the user `cco config push`-syncs ~/.cco to another PC but never runs `cco migrate` there, packs/templates seeded into tags.yml on PC-A will travel via DATA sync — but the trigger 'first migrate' is project-centric and conceptually odd for pack/template seeding (the seeding rides a project command it has nothing to do with).
Backup must carry an explicit origin sidecar (more structure in the archive) rather than translating to tags directly at flatten — slightly more migration machinery than Option A.

Two-stage flow is more moving parts than a single seed point; the 'first migrate triggers global tag seed' coupling is a subtle ordering dependency E must implement carefully (what if the user runs `cco tag add` before any migrate?).
Still leaves a window where tags.yml is empty (between first run and first migrate) even though the origin data already exists in the backup — partially re-introduces the lazy gap the finding flags.
options.principleAlignment: UPHOLDS P2/P6 (tags.yml internal DATA) · ADR-0015/0016 D5 (typed keys) · AD2 (lossless) · keeps closest to ADR-0010 §5's 'cco migrate' wording. STRAINS P10/P11 mildly: the seed is applied later than when the role is fully known (origin lives in the backup but isn't committed until a project command fires), re-introducing a lazy window. The project-command trigger for pack/template seeding is a slight role/owner mismatch (the seed of a non-project resource rides a project verb).

options.name: C. Per-resource-kind seeding owners: projects seeded by per-project cco migrate; packs/templates seeded by a dedicated one-shot global step; flatten only records origin
options.description: Embrace the natural ownership split rather than unifying. The flatten records each resource's profile origin in the backup. (1) PROJECTS: keep the per-project `cco migrate <project>` prompt EXACTLY as design §9:641-643 / ADR-0010 §5 state — each project's profile origin is seeded into tags.yml ONLY when that project is migrated (lazy, per-project, matching the lazy migration philosophy AD12/ADR-0006). (2) PACKS/TEMPLATES: since they have no per-resource migrate command and land directly in ~/.cco at first-run bootstrap, add an explicit one-time pack/template seed at the moment the global store is populated from the backup — i.e. when packs/templates are restored into ~/.cco/packs|templates/ during first-run/migration (the same step that physically places them). This makes the SEEDING OWNER == the RESTORE OWNER for each kind. Document explicitly in design §9 and ADR-0010 §5 that projects seed lazily-per-migrate, packs/templates seed at store-restore, and the flatten is only an origin-recorder.
pros: Matches the lazy, per-project migration philosophy (AD12/ADR-0006): a project's tags appear exactly when the project is materialized — no orphan tags for never-migrated projects (avoids Option A's main con).
Preserves ADR-0010 §5's per-project prompt verbatim for projects (zero ADR wording change for the project path).
Closes the pack/template gap by binding their seed to the step that already moves them into ~/.cco — the seed owner is well-defined and reachable.
Seeding owner == placement owner for each kind: conceptually clean per-resource responsibility.
cons: Two distinct convert-vs-untagged decisions (one global for packs/templates at restore, one per-project at migrate) — risk of inconsistent user answers (convert packs but skip a project's tag, or vice versa), producing a partially-tagged store that doesn't faithfully reflect the legacy profile organization.
Highest complexity: two seeding mechanisms, two prompt points — exactly the kind of split the finding flags as the root problem; only partially heals it.

Requires defining a 'restore packs/templates into ~/.cco from backup' step that the current design does NOT spell out (design only documents `cco migrate <project>` and direct authoring) — this is net-new migration surface, not just a wording fix.
Per-project prompt repeated N times remains a UX wart (the finding's secondary complaint is unaddressed for projects).
options.principleAlignment: UPHOLDS AD12/ADR-0006 (lazy per-project migration, most literally) · ADR-0010 §5 project prompt (unchanged) · P2/P6 + ADR-0015/0016 D5 (DATA placement, typed keys). STRAINS P10/P11 (seeding is split across two owners/timings → the very ambiguity the method warns against) and the spirit of a single coherent seed flow; risks an inconsistent tags.yml across kinds. Adds migration surface (pack/template restore step) beyond a documentation reconciliation.

options.recommended: A. Seed tags.yml once at first-run flatten for ALL kinds; per-project prompt becomes a one-time global choice recorded by a STATE marker
options.rationale: Option A is the only one that removes the contradiction at its root rather than papering over it. The flatten (FR-M1) is the single moment when ALL profile→resource membership is simultaneously known — across projects, packs, AND templates — because it iterates every profile branch (design §9:688-702). Seeding tags.yml there satisfies the P10/P11 method directly: classify/seed from the complete, validated role at the one point it is fully known, never lazily from partial state. tags.yml is identity-keyed DATA (ADR-0015 D5 / ADR-0016 D5 typed packs:/projects:/templates: keys), NOT project-scoped, so it has no natural home in the per-project `cco migrate` command — which is exactly why the pack/template origin is currently unreachable. Binding the seed to first-run flatten dissolves that gap with zero new per-resource commands: packs/templates need no migrate entry point because the DATA bucket is global, not per-unit. Legacy semantics make this clean: templates are always-shared (no profile origin → no seeded tags), packs are seeded only when profile-exclusive, projects carry their branch profile — so the seed is faithful and lossless (AD2). The per-project prompt becomes a one-time global choice guarded by a STATE marker (mirroring the existing 'backed-up' idempotency marker, M8), eliminating the N-times-prompt UX wart. The only real cost — orphan tag entries for never-migrated projects — is harmless (tags are advisory; a future gc can prune) and arguably correct, since it preserves the user's full organization losslessly. Options B and C keep the seed inside the migrate command family for a smaller ADR-wording delta, but both re-introduce a lazy window and (C especially) split the seeding owner across kinds — recreating the exact ambiguity the finding raises. The required edits for A are a clean reconciliation: amend design §9 (fold the convert-vs-untagged choice into FR-M1 flatten, drop the per-project prompt) and recast ADR-0010 §5 from 'cco migrate prompts per project' to 'first-run global choice, seeded at flatten' — matching the finding's proposedResolution.

options.title: Memory relocation on migrate re-run must be non-clobbering — the STATE memory copy is the only copy and a second migrate can overwrite live-accumulated auto-memory with frozen backup data

options.name: Copy-if-missing per-file with confirm-gated overwrite (mirror the .cco/ re-run guard, extended to STATE)
options.description: Specify in ADR-0009 §Decision-6 and design §9 step 2 that the memory relocation is non-clobbering at file granularity: cco migrate copies each backup memory file into <state>/cco/projects/<id>/memory/ ONLY if the destination file does not already exist (copy-if-missing). If the destination memory dir already exists and is non-empty, missing files are backfilled but existing live files are NEVER overwritten silently; an overwrite only happens behind an explicit per-run confirm (e.g. --force-memory or interactive y/N), exactly mirroring the existing '.cco/ re-runs never overwrite without confirm' guard at design:705. The 'idempotent' claim is qualified: the memory step is idempotent in the safe direction (re-run is a no-op once memory exists) rather than a blind re-copy. test_migrate.sh adds a re-run case: pre-seed live memory, re-run migrate, assert accumulated memory is preserved and backup files only fill genuine gaps.
pros: Directly closes the irrecoverable-data hole: the single machine-local copy (the only copy after ADR-0009 dropped cross-PC sync) can never be clobbered by a stale backup
Keeps the genuine value of re-runnable migrate — backfilling a project whose memory dir is empty/partial still works (true idempotency in the safe direction)
Consistent with the already-settled .cco/ guard at design:705 — one uniform 're-runs never overwrite without confirm' rule across both targets (config and STATE), not two divergent behaviors
Honors AD12 lossless migration: nothing the user accumulated is lost, and nothing from the backup is dropped either (gaps are backfilled)
Smallest spec change — refines existing wording rather than introducing new machinery
cons: File-granularity copy-if-missing has a subtle edge: a partially-populated memory dir (some files present, some absent) backfills the absent ones from a stale backup — usually correct, but a renamed/deleted live file could be 'resurrected' from backup. Acceptable for v1 (memory is append-mostly) but worth noting
Introduces a confirm prompt / --force path that the migrate flow must thread through (minor CLI surface)
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P8 (memory is STATE, the sole machine-local copy; protecting it is exactly what the deferred-sync regression demands), P6 (STATE stays hidden/internal, never re-derived from a config bucket), and AD12/ADR-0006 lossless migration. Strengthens ADR-0009 §Decision-6 (adds the missing non-clobbering qualifier) and aligns the memory step with the design:705 .cco/ guard. Consistent with ADR-0017 M6 per-root idempotency framing (idempotent = safe no-op on re-run, not blind overwrite).

options.name: One-shot guard via a per-project 'memory-migrated' marker in STATE meta (mirror the first-run 'backed-up' marker, M8)
options.description: Make the memory relocation a strictly one-time operation gated by a per-project marker persisted in STATE meta (e.g. memory_migrated=<id> alongside the existing 'backed-up' first-run marker that already guards re-archival, design:680). On the first cco migrate <project>, copy backup memory into STATE and set the marker; on any subsequent migrate the memory step is skipped entirely (logged as 'memory already migrated, skipping'). The .cco/ rebuild and index registration remain re-runnable as today; only the memory copy is one-shot. Spec it in ADR-0009 §Decision-6 + design §9 step 2; test_migrate.sh asserts second run does not touch memory and the marker is honored.
pros: Conceptually clean: a relocation is inherently a one-time event, and a marker makes that explicit — no per-file heuristics, no risk of resurrecting deleted files
Directly reuses an already-decided pattern (the M8 'backed-up' marker that guards first-run re-archival) — symmetric, low cognitive load
Zero risk of clobber: after the first migrate the backup never touches memory again, full stop
No new confirm/--force UX surface in the common path
cons: If the first migrate's memory copy was incomplete/failed (interrupted), the marker could block a legitimate re-attempt — needs the marker set only AFTER a verified copy (mirroring M8's 'remove vault only after verified backup' ordering)
Adds persistent STATE meta state and a skip-path that must be migrated/cleared correctly; slightly more machinery than copy-if-missing (mild tension with G4 minimalism)
A user who deliberately wiped memory and wants to re-pull from backup has no easy path (marker blocks it) — would need a documented escape hatch
Diverges from the design:705 .cco/ guard model (confirm-gated) toward a marker model — two different mechanisms for two targets unless .cco/ is also reworked
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P8 (one-shot protects the sole copy) and reuses the M8 marker pattern from the settled migration flow. Honors AD12 (lossless: backup applied exactly once). Mild strain on G4 (extra STATE meta + skip logic) and a coherence cost vs design:705 (introduces a second non-clobbering mechanism rather than unifying on the existing confirm-based one).

options.name: Skip-if-non-empty, no backfill (hard skip when STATE memory dir already exists with content)
options.description: Simplest rule: cco migrate copies backup memory into <state>/cco/projects/<id>/memory/ ONLY when that directory is missing or empty; if it exists and is non-empty, the entire memory step is skipped with a notice ('live memory present, not overwriting from backup'). No per-file backfill, no confirm, no marker. Stated in ADR-0009 §Decision-6 and design §9 step 2; test_migrate.sh asserts a non-empty memory dir is left untouched on re-run.
pros: Minimal spec and implementation — one existence/emptiness check, no new flags, markers, or prompts (strongest G4 alignment)
Guarantees the live copy is never clobbered (the core safety requirement)
Matches the literal proposedResolution ('skipped when ... already exists/non-empty, copy-if-missing only')
Easy to test deterministically
cons: Coarse: a partially-migrated dir (e.g. crash after a few files) is treated as 'done' and the remaining backup files are NOT backfilled — risks a silent partial loss, which is the more likely real failure than over-accumulation
Less aligned with AD12 'lossless' than copy-if-missing: backup content can be silently abandoned if any live content exists
Still diverges from the design:705 confirm-based .cco/ guard (hard skip vs confirm) — no user override to intentionally re-pull
Directory-level granularity is blunter than the file-level guard the .cco/ path implies
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P8 (protects the sole copy) and is the leanest re G4. Weakest on AD12 lossless (can abandon un-backfilled backup files on a partial prior run) and least coherent with the design:705 confirm model (offers no override). Acceptable but inferior to Option 1 on the lossless guarantee.

options.recommended: Copy-if-missing per-file with confirm-gated overwrite (mirror the .cco/ re-run guard, extended to STATE)
options.rationale: Option 1 is recommended because it closes the irrecoverable-data hole while staying maximally coherent with already-settled decisions. The root cause is that design:705's non-clobbering guarantee is scoped to the in-repo .cco/ only, leaving the STATE memory copy unguarded; Option 1 simply extends the SAME confirm-based guard to the memory target, so cco ends up with one uniform 're-runs never overwrite without confirm' rule across both migration outputs rather than two divergent mechanisms. It is the only option that fully honors AD12 lossless migration in BOTH directions: it never overwrites live machine-local memory (the single copy after ADR-0009 dropped cross-PC sync, per P8), AND it still backfills genuine gaps from the backup so nothing from the cutover is abandoned — whereas Option 3 (hard skip) can silently strand un-backfilled backup files after a partial prior run, the more likely real failure mode. It also correctly reframes the 'idempotent' claim: idempotent should mean a safe no-op on re-run, not a blind re-copy — matching the ADR-0017 M6 per-root idempotency framing. Option 2 (one-shot marker) is the strong runner-up and is cleaner conceptually, but it introduces a second non-clobbering mechanism (marker vs confirm) that diverges from the design:705 model and needs careful verified-before-marking ordering to avoid blocking a legitimate retry; it is a good fallback if the team prefers an explicit one-time-relocation semantic. The fix should be recorded in ADR-0009 §Decision-6 (add the non-clobbering qualifier to the memory copy) and design §9 step 2, and covered by a test_migrate.sh re-run case asserting accumulated memory is preserved while real gaps are backfilled.

options.title: Same project/pack name on multiple legacy profile branches must not silently collide into one <id> at migrate; flatten needs an explicit, lossless collision policy
options.recommended: Backup-side namespaced preservation + per-project migrate-time disambiguation (collision is detected at flatten, never silently merged)
options.rationale: This option is the only one that upholds the migration's foundational invariant — AD12 / ADR-0006: "migration is lossless" is the explicit justification for the breaking cutover with no dual-read. The lossiness is in the BACKUP (flatten) step, not the migrate-read step, so the fix must live where the data is first serialized (design §9:694), exactly as the existing note already locates the branch-awareness "entirely in the save/backup step". Detecting same-name-different-content across branches and writing namespaced backup entries (e.g. projects/<name>__<profile>/) guarantees no byte is lost at the moment the only source (the vault git branches) is collapsed — the backup is the single safety net (ADR-0006 §2, "verified before offer-to-remove"). Disambiguation is then deferred to the per-project, lazy, user-controlled `cco migrate <project>` step (ADR-0006 §3), which already prompts the user (profile→tag prompt, ADR-0010 §5) and already refuses to overwrite an existing .cco/ without confirm — so the chooser fits the established UX with no new interaction model. It reuses the deferred H7 "namespaced logical names" question (ADR-0016, owned by E) rather than inventing a parallel mechanism, and it keeps the profiles→tags model intact for the COMMON case (same-name-same-content = one resource carrying multiple origin tags) while handling the divergent edge case explicitly. With only ~2 known users (ADR-0006), the maintainer inspects the real data and the namespaced entries make any genuine collision visible and choosable rather than a silent merge. It is preferred over a hard refuse/rename (B) because B can strand a user mid-migration with no path forward for legitimately-divergent projects and contradicts ADR-0006's "lazy, granular, reversible" stance; and over content-hash auto-merge (C) because non-deterministic merging of .cco/ + memory/ is precisely the lossy behavior the finding flags and would reintroduce the merge-engine complexity the refactor is deleting.

options.name: Backup-side namespaced preservation + per-project migrate-time disambiguation (collision is detected at flatten, never silently merged)
options.description: Make the flatten (the save/backup step, design §9:694) collision-aware. While iterating profile branches it groups resources by logical name and compares content across branches: (1) if a name appears on multiple branches with IDENTICAL committed content -> collapse to ONE backup entry carrying ALL origin profiles as tag seeds (the documented common case, ADR-0010 §5 — no change to UX); (2) if a name appears with DIVERGENT content -> write namespaced backup entries `projects/<name>__<profile>/` (and likewise `packs/<name>__<profile>/`), preserving every branch's .cco/, memory/, source, and the active branch's uncommitted working tree as-is (design §9 already requires capturing the dirty tree). The active profile's variant is recorded as the 'default'. Then `cco migrate <project>` (lazy, per-project, ADR-0006 §3): when it finds namespaced variants for the requested name, it lists them and PROMPTS the user (CLI) which variant to materialize as the canonical <id>, optionally migrating the other(s) under a user-chosen new name (e.g. `cco migrate projectA --variant work` or `cco migrate projectA__work --as projectA-work`). memory/ follows its variant (ADR-0009 §6 copies that variant's backup memory into `<state>/cco/projects/<chosen-id>/memory/`); source and .cco/ likewise. Specify in design §9 + ADR-0010 §5 + extend ADR-0009 §6's per-project memory copy to honor the chosen variant. Add a `test_migrate.sh` case: project X exclusive on profileA and profileB with different project.yml + different memory -> backup has X__profileA and X__profileB, migrate prompts, both variants recoverable.
pros: Lossless at the exact point loss occurs — the flatten/backup, the single safety net (AD12/ADR-0006 §2). No byte is dropped when the vault branches are collapsed.
Common case (same-name-same-content) is unchanged: one resource, multiple origin tags — preserves the profiles->tags model (ADR-0010 §5) without forcing the user through a chooser they don't need.

Disambiguation happens in the lazy, per-project, user-controlled migrate step that ALREADY prompts (profile->tag, ADR-0010 §5) and ALREADY refuses to overwrite an existing .cco/ — fits the established UX, no new interaction model.

Reuses the deferred H7 'namespaced logical names' question (ADR-0016, owned by E) as the disambiguation key — consistent with how the design already parks namespacing for impl, no parallel mechanism invented.
No merge engine: variants are kept side-by-side, the user picks — the refactor's 'no custom diff/merge' simplification (design §11 'Net: a narrower surface') is preserved.
Tiny known base (ADR-0006): namespaced backup entries make any real collision visible and choosable; the maintainer can inspect actual data, exactly as the finding's downgrade rationale anticipates.
cons: Adds flatten-time content comparison (per-name across branches) and a migrate-time variant prompt — new code beyond the current single-pass serialize.

The `__<profile>` namespacing convention must be specified and tested, and must not collide with a legitimate user resource literally named `X__profileA` (needs a reserved separator / escaping rule, an E detail).
User must make a choice per divergent project at migrate (acceptable: rare edge case, and ADR-0006 already accepts per-project interaction).
options.principleAlignment: Upholds AD12 / ADR-0006 (lossless migration — the cutover's core justification) and ADR-0010 §5 (profile->tag, made genuinely lossless). Honors ADR-0006 §3 (lazy, per-project, user-controlled, non-overwriting). Consistent with ADR-0016 D5 (<id> = one logical name spanning buckets — namespacing yields a fresh distinct <id> per variant) and ADR-0016 H7 (namespaced logical names deferred to E — this is the consumer). Consistent with ADR-0009 §6 (per-project memory copy from backup — extended to follow the chosen variant). Respects P10/P11 (classify by role: same-name-different-content is genuinely two resources). Avoids the merge engine, aligning with the refactor's G4 net-reduction goal.

options.name: Refuse-and-require-rename: flatten hard-fails (or quarantines) on same-name-different-content; user renames on a profile branch before backup
options.description: The flatten DETECTS same-name-different-content across branches and, instead of namespacing, STOPS: it writes a clear report ('project X diverges across profiles profileA/profileB; rename one before migrating') and either (a) aborts the backup entirely until resolved, or (b) writes the conflicting variants into a `_conflicts/` quarantine area of the backup that `cco migrate` refuses to read until the user renames. Resolution path: the user uses the still-present legacy vault (the backup is taken BEFORE offer-to-remove, ADR-0006 §2) to `git checkout` the offending profile branch, rename `projects/X` -> `projects/X-work`, commit, then re-run first-run flatten (idempotent re-archive guarded by the 'backed-up' marker — would need a `--force-rebackup` escape). After rename there is exactly one <id> per name and migrate proceeds normally. Specify the detection + refusal in design §9 / ADR-0010 §5; add a `test_migrate.sh` case asserting the flatten refuses and names the conflict.
pros: Strictly safe by construction: never produces an ambiguous <id>; the new name-keyed namespace is guaranteed clean post-migration (ADR-0016 D5).
Minimal new model: no namespacing convention, no variant-chooser UX — just detect + report + block.
Forces the user to make the naming decision in the source-of-truth (the vault branch), so the resulting <id> is fully intentional.
Truthful: the user explicitly acknowledges the divergence rather than it being auto-handled.
cons: Friction at the worst moment: a hard block during first-run backup/migration can strand a user who just upgraded, contradicting ADR-0006's 'granular and reversible', low-ceremony stance.
Re-archive after rename fights the 'backed-up' idempotency marker (M8) — needs a `--force-rebackup` escape hatch, extra surface the cutover tried to minimize.
Pushes the user back into the legacy vault git mechanics (checkout a profile branch, rename, commit) — exactly the machinery the refactor is removing; awkward for a user who has mentally moved on.
Risk of an aborted backup leaving the user with no safety net if they remove the vault before resolving (must guarantee abort happens strictly before any offer-to-remove).
options.principleAlignment: Upholds AD12/ADR-0006 losslessness (nothing is dropped — it's blocked) and yields a clean ADR-0016 D5 namespace. But STRAINS ADR-0006's spirit of lazy, low-friction, per-project migration (a hard pre-migration block is the opposite of 'stays in Case A, opts in later'). Mildly strains the cutover's minimal-surface goal (G4) via the re-backup escape hatch. Neutral on P10/P11.

options.name: Content-keyed auto-resolution: identical content collapses (multi-tag), divergent content gets a deterministic auto-suffix, no user prompt
options.description: Fully automatic flatten with no migrate-time prompt. Group resources by name across branches; for identical committed content collapse to one <id> with all profiles as tag seeds (common case). For divergent content, deterministically derive distinct <id>s by appending the origin profile (`X` from the default/active branch keeps the bare name; others become `X-<profile>` chosen by a fixed branch-ordering rule). The mapping is printed in the migration report so the user can rename later via a normal `cco` rename. memory/, source, .cco/ each follow their derived <id>. No interactive disambiguation; `cco migrate <X>` and `cco migrate <X-profileB>` are then independent lazy migrations. Specify the deterministic suffix + ordering rule in design §9 / ADR-0010 §5; add a `test_migrate.sh` case.
pros: Zero added interaction — preserves the 'nothing migrated automatically, but the backup is complete' flow without a new prompt; fully scriptable.
Lossless: every variant survives under a distinct, deterministic <id> (AD12).
Deterministic and idempotent — re-running first-run produces the identical backup layout (M8-friendly).
Common case still collapses to multi-tag (ADR-0010 §5).
cons: The user does not choose which variant keeps the canonical bare name — the 'default/active branch wins' rule may pick the wrong one, leaving the user to rename afterward (papering over, not solving, the choice).

Silent auto-suffix can surprise: a project the user thinks of as 'X' may migrate as 'X-experimental' depending on branch ordering — a subtle correctness/UX trap the finding warns about ('non-deterministic' becomes 'deterministic but unexpected').
Still needs the namespacing/suffix convention + collision-with-real-name guard (same con as Option A) but without the human in the loop to catch a bad guess.
Weakest fit with ADR-0006's 'user controls A/B/C per project' — the naming decision is taken away from the user at the most consequential moment.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds AD12/ADR-0006 losslessness and ADR-0016 D5 (distinct <id> per variant) and reuses H7 namespacing. Idempotent (M8). But STRAINS ADR-0006 §3 / ADR-0010 §5's user-choice principle (migration is user-chosen — here the canonical-name choice is automated) and the P10 spirit (the user, not a branch-ordering heuristic, best knows which variant is the 'real' one). Note: the finding explicitly calls out non-deterministic overwrite as the defect; this fixes determinism but not the user-agency gap.

options.title: Reconcile `cco {pack,project} internalize` existing code vs ADR-0019 target semantics; correct the "net-new"/"Reuse" mislabels and resolve the verb collision under the no-project-publish model
options.recommended: Option A — Correct the verdicts (Refactor, not net-new/Reuse), retire project-install internalize, redefine `cco project internalize` as "vendor referenced packs as caches"
options.rationale: Option A is the only choice that makes ADR-0019 self-consistent AND consistent with its companion ADR-0018 D2 (projects are NOT published/installed). The finding is correct on every load-bearing point I verified: (1) cmd_project_internalize already exists (lib/cmd-project-update.sh:230) and serves the install model ADR-0018 D2 abolishes — so "net-new" (D4 line 118, Build-new table line 218) is factually wrong and a Refactor/retire verdict is mandatory; (2) cmd_pack_internalize (lib/cmd-pack.sh:750-861) does a knowledge.source COPY step that predates and contradicts the coordinate model — so "Reuse" (line 214) is wrong; the coordinate-cutting semantic D3/D4 describe is a Refactor; (3) export --bundle-packs does NOT yet exist in code (confirmed by grep), so the "vendor referenced packs" job is genuinely net-new and is the natural home for the project-level operation. Option A discharges the principle obligations directly: P15 (a shared copy is never its source — internalize is the deliberate cord-cut; the project-level operation produces caches that carry coordinates, never sources), P13 (the project-vs-pack referent asymmetry is real and must be made visible, not hidden behind one verb), and P10/P11 (classify by role, not by surface/name — the analysis must not assume an existing verb name maps to the target role). It also honors the update-system rule (a renamed/removed/redefined command is a breaking change → migration + changelog + behavior-change note for existing users). Options B and C are recorded because the maintainer may prefer to preserve the existing project-disconnect affordance under a different name (B) or to minimize churn by keeping project internalize out of v1 entirely (C); both are principle-consistent but leave more residual ambiguity than A. The choice that "internalize project = vendor referenced packs" vs "keep a distinct project-disconnect sense" is a genuine maintainer call (UX-affecting per P10b), which is why this is a decision finding, not a mechanical edit.

options.name: Option A — Correct the verdicts (Refactor, not net-new/Reuse), retire project-install internalize, redefine `cco project internalize` as "vendor referenced packs as caches"
options.description: Three coordinated edits to ADR-0019 (decision text only here; implementation deferred to E). (1) `cco project internalize`: change D4 line 118 from 'net-new' and the Build-new table line 218 from 'Build-new' to **Refactor + repurpose** — the verb exists but its current semantic (disconnect an installed project from a central-vault remote source, setting `.cco/source: local`, lib/cmd-project-update.sh:289-308) is **retired together with the project-install model** (ADR-0018 D2: projects are not published/installed, so there is no remote-source to disconnect). Redefine the verb's target meaning to the only coherent project-level operation under the coordinate model: **vendor/internalize the project's referenced packs into `<repo>/.cco/packs` as caches** (each cache carrying its coordinate per D3), i.e. the live-in-repo counterpart of `export --bundle-packs` (D6). A project carries no project-level coordinate to cut (only its referenced packs/repos/llms do), so the per-pack internalize-as-cache (D6) is the substance. (2) `cco pack internalize`: change line 214 from 'Reuse' to **Refactor** — keep the verb, but redefine it to the coordinate model: internalize = **delete the pack coordinate from the manifest** so the in-repo/`~/.cco` copy becomes the authored source (P15 'deliberate cord-cut'); **drop or re-specify the legacy `knowledge.source` copy step** (lib/cmd-pack.sh:788-832), which belongs to the pre-coordinate knowledge-source model. (3) Presentation: move `internalize` OUT of the 2×2 produce/consume matrix into a separate **Lifecycle** line, and disambiguate the referent per `<res>` (pack → cut a pack's own coordinate; project → vendor the referenced packs). Add an explicit **behavior-change note** for existing users (project internalize no longer means 'disconnect from publisher'; pack internalize no longer copies knowledge files) and flag the breaking change for E (migration + changelog per update-system.md).
pros: Makes ADR-0019 factually correct: the verb exists, so Refactor/repurpose is the truthful verdict (not net-new), preventing E from re-implementing tested behavior or being surprised by the collision
Resolves the cross-ADR contradiction: ADR-0018 D2 retires project install, so the existing project-disconnect semantic has no referent and must be retired alongside it — A states this explicitly
Honors P15: project-level internalize produces caches that carry coordinates (never sources), and pack-level internalize is the explicit, deliberate cord-cut — the two are clearly distinguished
Honors P13/P10: the project-vs-pack referent asymmetry is surfaced (different referents under one verb) rather than hidden; classification follows role, not the existing surface name
Ties project internalize to the already-net-new `export --bundle-packs` (D6), so both share one dependency-closure mechanism — no duplicate concept
Triggers the correct engineering hygiene: breaking-change migration + changelog + behavior-change note (update-system.md), protecting existing users of the abolished command
cons: Most edits to ADR-0019 (three verdict changes + matrix restructure) and a behavior-change note — more churn than a one-line fix
Repurposing an existing verb name (project internalize) risks user confusion during transition unless the changelog/migration is clear
Requires deciding now that project internalize == vendor-referenced-packs, foreclosing a distinct project-disconnect sense (addressed by Option B if the maintainer disagrees)
The pack-internalize redefinition must specify the fate of any pack that still uses the legacy `knowledge.source` field (migration concern handed to E)
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P15 (cache carries coordinate; internalize is deliberate cord-cut, never silent source promotion), P13 (asymmetry surfaced, not papered over), P10/P11 (role-based classification, not surface/name-based; behavior change flagged). Consistent with ADR-0018 D2 (no project publish/install → retire the install-model disconnect) and ADR-0019 D3/D4/D6 (internalize-as-cache, export --bundle-packs). Strains nothing settled; corrects ADR-0019's own internal inaccuracy. Respects update-system.md (breaking change → migration + changelog).

options.name: Option B — Keep a distinct, renamed project-source-disconnect verb; define `cco project internalize` purely as vendor-referenced-packs
options.description: Same corrections as Option A for the verdict labels (project internalize → Refactor/repurpose; pack internalize → Refactor; internalize moved to a Lifecycle line), but **preserve the existing project-disconnect affordance under a new, accurate name** instead of fully retiring it. Because ADR-0018 D2 abolishes the central-vault *install* model, the only legitimate residual is the **tar `import`-with-source** path (ADR-0018 D2: an imported project tar may carry a `source` and thus be `internalize`-able). So: rename the current `cmd_project_internalize` semantic to a verb scoped to that path (e.g. `cco project disconnect` or fold it into a generic `cco <res> internalize` that cuts an imported-tar `source`), and reserve `cco project internalize` exclusively for the new 'vendor referenced packs as caches' meaning. ADR-0019 D4/D6 + ADR-0018 D2 are cross-referenced so the import-source lifecycle and the pack-vendoring lifecycle are not conflated.
pros: Preserves a real (if narrow) need — an imported project tar that carries a `source` should be disconnect-able (ADR-0018 D2 explicitly says imported tars are internalize-able), so the affordance is not lost
Keeps `cco project internalize` semantically clean as exactly the pack-vendoring operation, with no overloaded referent
Still corrects all the mislabels (Refactor not net-new/Reuse) and surfaces the asymmetry
Lower regression risk than A for users who relied on the disconnect behavior (the behavior survives under a new name)
cons: Introduces a second verb name to define and document, increasing surface area for a narrow case (only imported-tar projects with a source)

Risk of over-engineering: the import-with-source disconnect case may be rare enough that a generic `internalize` on the imported resource (ADR-0018 D2 already says 'Either can be internalized') covers it without a new project-specific verb
Two internalize-adjacent verbs for projects could re-introduce the very 'cannot predict what it does' problem the finding raises, unless naming is crisp
Still a breaking rename for existing users (migration + changelog needed), same cost as A on that axis
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P15/P13/P10 like Option A. Additionally honors ADR-0018 D2's note that imported (tar-with-source) resources are internalize-able — so it does not silently drop a settled affordance. Slight tension with simplicity/YAGNI (a dedicated project-disconnect verb for a narrow case) but does not contradict any P1-P17 or settled ADR. Consistent with update-system.md (rename → migration).

options.name: Option C — Correct the labels and defer `cco project internalize` out of v1 (Build-new → Deferred), fix only pack internalize now
options.description: Minimal-scope correction. (1) Acknowledge in ADR-0019 that `cco project internalize` already exists with a now-obsolete semantic (ADR-0018 D2 retires the install model it serves) and **retire/remove** that obsolete behavior, but **defer the new 'vendor referenced packs' meaning to post-v1**, since `export --bundle-packs` (the tar form of the same self-containment, D6) already covers the snapshot use case and is the documented path. Change the Build-new table line 218 entry for `cco project internalize` from 'Build-new (→ E)' to **Removed/Deferred** (retire obsolete code in E; new meaning post-v1). (2) Still fix `cco pack internalize` now: change line 214 'Reuse' → 'Refactor' (cut-the-coordinate semantic; drop/redefine the knowledge.source copy), because pack internalize is load-bearing for the live D3/D4 model. (3) Move internalize to a Lifecycle line and note behavior changes. The 2×2 matrix stays pack/template-only for internalize in v1.
pros: Smallest v1 surface and least new code: only pack internalize is refactored; project internalize's obsolete behavior is removed, new behavior postponed

Avoids committing to the project-internalize == vendor-packs semantic before export --bundle-packs (its tar sibling) is even built — lets the simpler tar path prove the dependency-closure mechanism first
Still corrects both mislabels and removes the contradiction with ADR-0018 D2
Reduces user-facing churn to a single removal (with changelog) rather than a removal + repurpose
cons: Leaves a usability gap: a user who wants in-repo (non-tar) self-containment of referenced packs has only `export --bundle-packs` (a tar) in v1, not a live `<repo>/.cco/packs` cache — D6 internalize-as-cache is partly the same need and is the recommended last-layer fallback, so deferring the project verb may under-serve the access-asymmetry consumer
Risks re-opening the question post-v1 (re-discussion cost), which ADRs exist to prevent
If internalize-as-cache (D6) is wired at resolve/validate anyway, the project-level verb may effectively exist under another entry point, making the deferral cosmetic
Still a breaking removal for existing users (migration + changelog)
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P15 (no silent source promotion), P10 (corrects role mislabel), and ADR-0018 D2 (retire obsolete install-model code). Consistent with ADR-0019 D6 (export --bundle-packs as the snapshot path). Mild tension with ADR-0019 D6's internalize-as-cache intent (the live in-repo cache is the recommended access-asymmetry fallback; deferring the explicit project verb leaves that affordance thinner in v1). Does not contradict any settled decision; it scopes more conservatively.

options.title: Manifest removal: place the whole code refactor in implementation phase E, make structure-based discovery a same-phase prerequisite of the delete, and stop Phase-2 cco init from emitting a manifest

options.name: Option A — All manifest CODE in E (the dedicated impl phase), structure-discovery as in-phase prerequisite; reframe inventory:179 as DATA-cleanup-only; instruct new cco init to never emit a manifest
options.description: Resolve the contradiction by separating two distinct things the docs conflate: (1) the manifest CODE refactor (delete lib/manifest.sh + cco manifest + all manifest_init/manifest_refresh call-sites, build structure-based discovery + init-at-first-publish) and (2) the inert manifest.yml DATA file cleanup in existing installs. The code refactor is owned entirely by E (the implementation phase the roadmap/V-gate place AFTER the §9 0-3 phases) — exactly as ADR-0018 D3, design §6.2:511-513, §12:763-765, and the V-handoff already state. Add an explicit ordered sub-sequence WITHIN E: (E.1) build structure-based discovery (git ls-tree over packs/*/ and templates/*/ on a treeless/shallow clone, reusing lib/remote.sh sparse/shallow; keep cmd_pack_install's existing single-pack pack.yml-at-root branch as the fallback to generalize from) and init-at-first-publish/merge-on-existing → (E.2) rewrite the enumerated read/write call-sites to use it → (E.3) delete lib/manifest.sh + cco manifest LAST, once nothing references it. Enumerate the call-sites to convert: cmd-pack.sh:469-518 (install discovery + _manifest_get_names) and :1005-1006 (publish refresh), cmd-project-install.sh:105-222 (the die-if-no-manifest gate + _manifest_get_names templates list + trailing refresh), remote.sh:109 (_clone_for_publish empty-repo seed → replace with .gitkeep/first-resource commit), cmd-init.sh:138 and cmd-project-publish.sh:299-300. Reframe inventory:179 to say only the inert manifest.yml FILES in existing installs are cleaned during the Phase-3 breaking cutover (no standalone migrator, ADR-0012 Open:121) — the CODE that produced/consumed them is removed in E. For Phase 2: the new cco init (and J0 bootstrap) must NOT emit or refresh a personal-store manifest from day one (drop the cmd-init.sh:138 manifest_init call in the rewrite; ADR-0012 already classifies the personal-store manifest as consumer-less). Because the §9 0-3 phases run on feat/*→develop and only working versions reach main, and E lands after them, no in-between phase ever reads a manifest the new init never wrote.
pros: Single source of truth for placement: matches ADR-0018 D3, §6.2, §12, and the V-handoff verbatim (manifest deletion is an E item) — removes the contradiction by deciding inventory:179 was about DATA cleanup, not code placement
Makes the real dependency explicit and ordered: structure-based discovery is built BEFORE the delete (E.1→E.3), so 'delete lib/manifest.sh' can never silently break pack/project install discovery
Each §9 phase stays runnable + tests green (the §9 invariant): Phase 2's new init simply omits the consumer-less manifest, so no phase reads a file that was never written
Lowest churn: no brand-new code is written then ripped out; the manifest stops being emitted exactly where it stops being read
Builds on existing code: reuses lib/remote.sh sparse/shallow clone and the cmd_pack_install single-pack pack.yml fallback as the seed for structure discovery
cons: Requires recognizing E as a real (currently unnumbered) phase that the §9 list (Phases 0-3) does not enumerate — a doc fix to §9 to name/anchor E is needed
Phase 2 init and Phase-3 cutover both touch manifest concerns (init stops emitting; cutover deletes leftover files), so the reader must hold two phases in mind
Editing the existing cmd-init.sh during Phase 2 (to drop manifest_init) is a small change to soon-to-be-rewritten code
options.principleAlignment: Upholds ADR-0012 D4 (drop manifest_init; empty-repo init via .gitkeep/first commit) and Open:121 (inert-file cleanup rides Phase-3, no standalone migrator); ADR-0018 D3 (structure-based discovery + init-at-first-publish, owned by E) and its Reuse/Drop/Build-new table (reuse remote.sh, drop manifest.sh, build-new discovery in E). Upholds the §9 cutover invariant (each phase runnable + tests green) and AD12 breaking-cutover (no dual-read). Consistent with P6 (manifest was write-only internal, removable) and P13/P14 (sharing path is structure/coordinate-based, not catalogue-based). Strains nothing settled; only requires §9 to acknowledge E.

options.name: Option B — Pull the manifest-removal code into a new explicit Phase 4 (sharing-cutover) appended to §9, with structure-discovery as Step 1 of that phase
options.description: Same call-site rewrites, ordering, and Phase-2-init instruction as Option A, but instead of treating E as an out-of-band implementation phase, formally add a numbered 'Phase 4 — sharing-model cutover' to design §9 that contains: (4.1) structure-based discovery + init-at-first-publish/merge-on-existing; (4.2) rewrite all manifest read/write call-sites; (4.3) delete lib/manifest.sh + cco manifest; (4.4) 2x2 verb wiring + cco update --check + nomenclature migration (the rest of the ADR-0018/0019/0020 E-bucket). inventory:179 is then reconciled to point at Phase 4 for the code and Phase 3 for inert-file cleanup. This makes the manifest work first-class in the §9 phasing rather than living only in the 'E' shorthand used by §6.2/§12.
pros: Eliminates the §9-vs-E ambiguity at the source: the phasing list itself names the phase that removes the manifest, so an implementer reading §9 alone sees the prerequisite ordering
Co-locates ALL the S-cycle (ADR-0018/0019/0020) implementation in one phase with the manifest work, matching how V-handoff:52 and §12:763-765 already bundle them
Same correctness guarantees as A (discovery-before-delete; init stops emitting manifest in Phase 2)
cons: Re-numbers/extends the §9 phase model that ADRs 0006/0009 and the BL2 gate reference as Phases 0-3 — a larger doc edit touching cross-references and the GATE note
Risks implying the S-cycle work is gated behind the legacy-vault Phases 0-3 when much of it (pack coordinates, reachability) is independent — could distort sequencing
Duplicates the E bucket already enumerated in §12/ADR-0018 Open, creating two places that list the same work (E and Phase 4) that must be kept in sync
options.principleAlignment: Upholds the same ADR-0012/0018 decisions as Option A and the §9 runnable-per-phase invariant. Cleaner P10-style 'classify by role' framing for the phasing doc. Strains the established Phase 0-3 numbering referenced by ADR-0006/0009 and the BL2 GATE; risks conflating the independent S-cycle scope with the legacy-vault teardown sequence (a mild tension with how design §6.2 and the V-handoff scope E as post-review implementation).

options.name: Option C — Tombstone manifest_init/_refresh into no-ops in Phase 2, defer the lib/manifest.sh delete + discovery rewrite to E
options.description: Rather than rewriting discovery before deleting, neutralize the manifest in Phase 2 by stubbing manifest_init and manifest_refresh to no-ops (and making the install discovery treat a present-or-absent manifest leniently via the existing pack.yml/template-dir paths) so the new cco init writes nothing and the in-between phases tolerate a missing manifest. Keep lib/manifest.sh on disk as dead/stub code until E performs the full structure-based-discovery rewrite and the physical file deletion. inventory:179's 'Phase-3 cutover' then covers the inert data files, and the real delete + discovery build is still an E item.
pros: Minimal Phase-2 surface: a one-line stub instead of touching multiple call-sites; the new cco init trivially emits no manifest
Keeps the physical lib/manifest.sh deletion and the discovery build together in E (honors §6.2/§12 placement)
Avoids a window where install hard-dies on a missing manifest, by relaxing the discovery gate at stub time
cons: Leaves a misleading dead lib/manifest.sh + cco manifest command live across Phases 2-3, violating the spirit of P6 (hide/remove internal cruft) and AD12 (clean breaking cutover, no dual-read/transitional cruft)

The 'relax the discovery gate' stub IS most of the structure-based-discovery work done ad hoc in Phase 2 — it pulls E work earlier in a half-baked form, the opposite of the finding's 'prerequisite, properly ordered' fix
Two-step removal (stub now, delete later) is more total churn and more states to test than building discovery once and deleting once (Option A)
cmd_pack_install / cmd_project_install would silently 'work' against a structure they don't truly scan yet, risking subtle discovery gaps until E lands
options.principleAlignment: Partially upholds ADR-0012 (manifest stops being written) but violates AD12's no-transitional-cruft breaking-cutover intent and strains P6 (a dead internal command/lib lingers). Weakly serves the §9 runnable-per-phase invariant only by smuggling immature discovery logic into Phase 2, contradicting ADR-0018 D3's 'structure-based discovery is build-new in E'. Least aligned with the finding's own proposedResolution (make discovery an explicit, complete prerequisite of the delete).

options.recommended: Option A — All manifest CODE in E (the dedicated impl phase), structure-discovery as in-phase prerequisite; reframe inventory:179 as DATA-cleanup-only; instruct new cco init to never emit a manifest
options.rationale: Option A is the only option that resolves the contradiction without inventing new structure or leaving cruft. The apparent three-way conflict dissolves once you separate the two things the docs conflate: ADR-0012 Open:121 / inventory:179 'Phase-3 cutover' refers to cleaning up inert manifest.yml DATA FILES in existing installs (no standalone migrator), while §6.2:511-513, §12:763-765, ADR-0018 D3, and V-handoff:52 unanimously place the manifest CODE refactor in E. There is no real disagreement — only a doc that must state the data/code split explicitly. Within E, ordering structure-based discovery as E.1 (built before the delete at E.3) directly satisfies the finding's core point that discovery is a prerequisite, not a follow-up, and matches ADR-0018 D3's 'build-new in E' verdict while reusing lib/remote.sh and the existing single-pack pack.yml fallback. Instructing Phase-2's new cco init to drop the consumer-less manifest_init (cmd-init.sh:138) keeps every §9 phase runnable with tests green and produces zero rip-out churn, because the manifest stops being written exactly where it stops being read. Option B is a viable doc-structure variant but re-numbers the ADR-0006/0009/BL2-referenced Phase 0-3 model and risks implying the independent S-cycle work is gated behind the legacy-vault teardown. Option C contradicts AD12's clean-cutover intent and the finding's own resolution by smuggling half-built discovery into Phase 2 and leaving dead code live. The one genuine doc defect both A and B fix is that §9 never names E as a phase; Option A fixes it with the smallest, lowest-risk edit (anchor E after Phases 0-3 and reframe inventory:179), without disturbing settled phase numbering or ADR cross-references.

options.title: Phase ordering for pack-coordinate resolution/healing: cco resolve (Phase 1) depends on the 'E' pack schema and the Phase-3 ~/.cco store

options.name: A — Build Phase-1 resolve/start generic over referenced-resource coordinates; E adds only the pack backend; declare ordering in §9
options.description: Make the Phase-1 cco resolve / cco start resolution loop generic over the unified 'referenced-resource coordinate' abstraction (repos | llms | packs) from the outset, driven by a per-resource backend interface (resolve-mount, heal-coordinate). In Phase 1 only the repos backend (and the already-CACHE llms fetch where it exists) is wired; the loop, the heal-at-resolve hook (ADR-0019 D2 layer-b), and the passive-warn badge (layer-e) are written once, resource-type-agnostic. The 'E' work then ADDS the pack backend (D1 schema + name-only→coordinate migration, D5 three-layer lib/packs.sh resolution, sync-before-publish) by registering it against the existing loop — it does NOT re-touch the loop. §9 is amended: (1) Phase 1 text states resolve/start iterate referenced-resource coordinates generically and that the repos backend ships now while the pack backend lands in the sharing phase; (2) a new explicitly-numbered sharing phase (Phase 4) is added after Phase 3 carrying all 'E' pack/sharing work, with a one-line dependency note that pack mount-layer-1 (~/.cco/packs) requires the Phase-3 ~/.cco store surface and the validate/heal contract requires the Phase-1 generic loop.
pros: Directly upholds the unified, boundary-less reachability model — the loop is built once for all three resource types, so packs are not a bolt-on that fights P14
Eliminates the double-touch of the resolve/start loop the finding warns about: E adds a backend, never re-opens the resolution loop
Honors the already-settled §2.4 framing (packs join the SAME coordinate model as repos/llms; resolution via cco start/cco resolve two-axis) rather than contradicting it
Makes the cross-phase dependency explicit and acyclic (Phase 1 generic loop → Phase 3 ~/.cco store → Phase 4 pack backend), satisfying the finding's 'state the dependency and decide the order' ask
The generic interface is the natural code shape implied by P12/P14 (one coordinate model, only the resolution backend differs), so it reduces, not increases, total implementation surface
cons: Phase 1 must define the backend-dispatch seam up-front with only one or two backends to validate it — a small risk of over-abstracting before the pack backend exists to pressure-test the interface
Requires editing §9 to add a numbered Phase 4 and re-anchor several '→ E' references onto it (documentation churn, though purely structural)
If the pack backend later needs a resolution concept the generic loop did not anticipate (e.g. the cache last-layer + name-collision rule of D5/D6), the seam may need a minor extension in Phase 4
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P12 (one coordinate model, references resolved at the boundary, only the resolution backend differs), P14 (unified boundary-less reachability — heal/warn layers written once for all three resource types), P15/P16 (the pack backend slots its DRY/two-axis rules into the shared loop without special-casing). Consistent with ADR-0017 D2 (resolve is the single resolution verb), ADR-0019 D2/D5, and design §2.4 lines 288-300. Strains nothing; the only tension is the generic-seam-before-second-backend method risk (mitigated by P10's 'classify by role' — the role is identical across the three).

options.name: B — Explicitly scope Phase-1 resolve to repos+mounts only; defer ALL pack-coordinate behavior (incl. heal-at-resolve) to a new post-Phase-3 sharing phase, stated in §9
options.description: Keep Phase 1 deliberately narrow: cco resolve / cco start handle repos and extra-mounts only (the index local-path materialization that already exists). Add a one-line statement to §9 Phase 1 that 'resolve covers repos+mounts only; pack coordinate resolution, healing, and the three-layer mount order are owned by the sharing phase.' Add an explicitly-numbered sharing phase (Phase 4) strictly AFTER Phase 3, carrying ALL of 'E': the D1 pack-coordinate schema + name-only→coordinate migration, the D5 lib/packs.sh three-layer refactor, the D2 layered embed/heal/validate, sync-before-publish, the 2×2 wiring, cco update --check, cco config validate, and cco project internalize. A one-line dependency note records that the pack mount-layer-1 (~/.cco/packs) needs the Phase-3 ~/.cco store. Phase 4 owns the entire resolve/start pack integration in one place.
pros: Smallest, most honest change to the stated plan: it simply names what is already true (Phase 1 cannot implement pack healing because the schema does not exist yet) and orders the phases
Resolves the silent-gap risk: Phase 1 no longer carries an undocumented obligation it cannot meet, so reviewers/implementers are not misled
Keeps each phase's scope crisp and independently shippable (the §9 'each phase leaves cco runnable + tests green' contract), with packs as one cohesive later unit
No risk of over-abstracting a backend seam before the pack model is concrete — the pack resolution code is written once, fully informed by D1/D5/D6
cons: Accepts the double-touch the finding flags: Phase 4 re-opens the Phase-1 resolve/start loop to thread pack resolution + the heal/warn layers through it, rather than extending a pre-built seam
Risks Phase 1 hard-coding a repos/mounts-only resolution shape that later fights the unified P14 model when packs are retrofitted (the exact failure mode the finding names)

Sits in mild tension with §2.4's already-settled 'packs join the SAME coordinate model, resolution via cco resolve' — the implementation would treat packs as a separate later concern even though the design unifies them
Delays any pack reachability behavior until after the full cutover, so a shared project with a name-only pack reference stays silently broken through Phases 1-3 (acceptable pre-v1, but worth noting)
options.principleAlignment: Consistent with the breaking-cutover phasing (ADR-0006) and ADR-0019's own 'Hands off: E' framing. Honest about the dependency, satisfying the finding's ask. STRAINS P14/P12 at the implementation level: building resolve repos-only first risks a non-generic loop that contradicts the unified-reachability intent, and is in mild tension with design §2.4's unified-resolution statement. Does not violate any settled decision, but trades coherence-of-construction for scope-crispness.

options.name: C — Hybrid: pull a minimal pack-coordinate skeleton into Phase 2 alongside the migration, leaving the full reachability/lifecycle to a post-Phase-3 sharing phase
options.description: Split 'E' across two seams. Because Phase 2 already owns migration + first-run bootstrap (and the name-only→coordinate migration of pack references is itself a migration of project.yml/pack.yml), land the MINIMAL pack-coordinate pieces in Phase 2: the D1 schema (name + optional url/ref/resource), the name-only→coordinate migration, and read-side parsing so cco start/cco resolve can recognize a pack coordinate. The full layered reachability (D2 heal/validate/warn), the D5 three-layer mount refactor of lib/packs.sh, sync-before-publish, the 2×2 sharing wiring, and cco project internalize remain in an explicitly-numbered sharing phase (Phase 4) strictly after Phase 3. Phase 1 resolve is documented as repos+mounts only but with the schema-aware parsing arriving in Phase 2, so the heal hook in Phase 4 extends an already-parsing loop rather than introducing the schema cold. §9 records the schema-in-Phase-2 / store-in-Phase-3 / lifecycle-in-Phase-4 ordering with dependency notes.
pros: Co-locates the pack-reference schema migration with the other Phase-2 migrations, which is where a name-only→coordinate transform most naturally belongs (a project.yml/pack.yml schema change)
Gives Phase 4 a schema to build the heal/resolve layers against, reducing the cold-start of Option B's single big sharing phase
Still defers the genuinely sharing-coupled work (sync-before-publish, 2×2, validate, internalize) to after the cutover, keeping Phase 3's vault-removal isolated
Lower abstraction risk than Option A — no generic backend seam is committed to before the pack model is concrete
cons: Fragments 'E' across Phase 2 and Phase 4, splitting one cohesive design (ADR-0019) into two implementation seams — harder to reason about and review than either A or B keeping it whole
Phase 2 ships a pack schema with no consumer that uses its coordinate yet (parsing without resolution), risking dead/half-wired code until Phase 4 lands the resolution backend
Still re-touches the resolve/start loop in Phase 4 to add heal/resolution layers (the double-touch is reduced, not eliminated, as in Option A)
Introduces a three-way cross-phase dependency (schema P2 → store P3 → lifecycle P4) that is more intricate to keep acyclic and test-green at each boundary
options.principleAlignment: Consistent with ADR-0006 (phased breaking cutover) and ADR-0019 D1 (schema + migration). Partially honors P12/P14 by getting the unified schema in early, but FRAGMENTS the unified model across two phases, which strains the 'one mechanism' spirit of P14 and the analysis-coherence preference of P10 (a resource's full role decided/built in one place). Does not contradict settled decisions; weaker on coherence-of-construction than Option A and on scope-crispness than Option B.

options.recommended: A — Build Phase-1 resolve/start generic over referenced-resource coordinates; E adds only the pack backend; declare ordering in §9
options.rationale: Option A is the only one that resolves BOTH halves of the finding without trading one risk for another. It directly removes the double-touch failure mode (E adds a backend, never re-opens the resolution loop) AND the hard-coding-fights-P14 risk (the loop is generic from the start), while honoring the already-settled §2.4 statement that packs join the SAME coordinate model as repos/llms with resolution via cco resolve — a decision A builds toward rather than around. The generic seam is not speculative abstraction: P12 and P14 already declare that repos, llms, and packs are ONE category differing only in resolution backend, so a backend-dispatch loop is the design's own shape, not an invented one — which is exactly why the loop should be built once. Option B is the safe documentation-only fallback and is fully principle-consistent, but it knowingly accepts the double-touch and the repos-only-then-retrofit risk the finding explicitly warns against, so it under-delivers on the design's unified-reachability intent. Option C's schema-in-Phase-2 co-location is appealing but fragments one cohesive ADR-0019 mechanism across two phases and ships consumer-less schema, weakening coherence without buying enough over A. All three share the same required §9 edit — add an explicitly-numbered sharing phase strictly after Phase 3 with a one-line dependency note (pack mount-layer-1 ~/.cco/packs needs the Phase-3 ~/.cco store; the heal/validate contract needs the Phase-1 loop) — so the ordering fix is common ground; A is the recommendation because it also fixes the construction. Note: all options require maintainer sign-off because the choice changes the implementation contract of a Phase-1 command (UX/interface impact, P10 method-lesson b) and renumbers the phasing plan — this is a decision, not a mechanical edit.

options.title: Consolidated pack-publish-path spec: define the merge base, first-vs-subsequent split, and base storage now that base/ is STATE-only and three refactors converge on cmd_pack_publish

options.name: Per-pack STATE base keyed by identity + sync-class-correct 3-way publish spec (one consolidated §6.2 / E-handoff sequence)
options.description: Write ONE consolidated publish-path spec (in design.md §6.2, referencing an E-handoff) that sequences every converging refactor and pins the merge base explicitly. Sequence: (1) treeless/shallow discover the sharing-repo structure (ADR-0018 D3); (2) clone push-ready (reuse remote.sh _clone_for_publish, minus the manifest_init that ADR-0018 D3 deletes -> init-or-merge by structure); (3) sync-before-publish 3-way merge; (4) commit (init-on-empty or merge-on-existing); (5) push. Define the base precisely: introduce a pack-scoped base, stored in STATE keyed by pack identity (a new _cco_pack_base_dir under <STATE>/cco/update/packs/<name>/base/, mirroring _cco_project_base_dir), populated on cco pack install AND on cco pack publish (the published tree becomes the recorded base = last-published/last-installed commit).
