FIRST publish (no recorded source/base) = the init/add path: there is no theirs to merge, so write the pack into the (possibly empty) sharing-repo structure and push if fast-forward; if the remote already has a pack of that name but no local base, treat it as a divergence and fall through to the merge path (do not blind-overwrite). SUBSEQUENT publish = base=last-published commit's tree (from STATE), ours=local ~/.cco/packs/<name>, theirs=remote HEAD's packs/<name>/; run the existing _collect_file_changes / 3-way file merge (reused from update-discovery.sh / update-merge.sh, the same engine project-update uses), abort on conflict with a reminder to resolve (P7 non-FF -> abort+notify). State explicitly that base/ stays STATE never-sync (ADR-0013 D2): the base is a LOCAL merge ancestor per PC, NOT synced and NOT carried in the sharing repo, so cross-PC the user re-establishes it from install/publish. Correct ADR-0019 D4's 'fast-forward push that can silently clobber' wording to describe the ACTUAL clone-then-cp-R-overwrite behavior (the current code discards remote-only changes inside packs/<name>/, it is not a literal FF push). Note the cross-dependency on F4 (source relocation to DATA): the base lookup is keyed by the same pack identity the relocated source uses, so the two land together in E.
pros: Closes the actual data-loss the ADR exists to close: subsequent multi-maintainer publishes do a real 3-way merge against a defined ancestor instead of clobbering remote-only changes.
Pins all three undefined inputs (base/ours/theirs) for both the first-publish and subsequent-publish paths, removing the ambiguity E was told to 'just fix'.

Keeps base/ STATE-only and never-sync, honoring ADR-0013 D2 (base tied to local framework version) and P6/P8 (internal, never in a config/sharing bucket); the published sharing repo carries only pack content, not merge ancestors.

Reuses the existing _collect_file_changes / 3-way merge engine (ADR-0013 D7, ADR-0019 'Reuse' table) rather than inventing a second merge path; pack base mirrors the proven _cco_project_base_dir pattern.
One consolidated spec gives E a single owning sequence where ADR-0019 D4 + ADR-0018 D3 + ADR-0013 H6 converge, instead of three cross-linked-but-uncoordinated ADRs touching one function.
Correctly re-words the 'fast-forward clobber' description to match real code, removing a code-grounding inaccuracy a reviewer would re-flag.
cons: Net-new pack base machinery (_cco_pack_base_dir, save-base on install/publish) — packs have NO base today, so this is genuine build, not a relocation; H6 (passing STATE paths into merge fns) now extends to the pack path too.
First publish onto a pre-existing remote pack with no local base is a real edge that must be handled as divergence-not-overwrite, adding a branch to the sequence.

Cross-PC, the base is non-portable: a second PC publishing without having installed/published locally has no base and falls to the divergence path (acceptable but must be documented so it is not mistaken for a bug).
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P16 (sync-before-publish, source-of-truth-follows-publish, working-copy model), P7 (non-FF -> abort+notify; sync transports commits never fabricates), P6/P1 (base is internal, hidden, never in a config/sharing bucket), P8 (base in STATE /update never-sync). Honors ADR-0013 D2 (base sync-class=never, STATE) and D7 (reuse the 3-way engine), ADR-0019 D4/D5 (publish 3-way; mount-vs-update axes), ADR-0018 D3 (init-at-first-publish / merge-on-existing, structure-based, manifest_init removed). Resolves the ADR-0019 D4 vs ADR-0013 D2 tension (which base ancestor) by keying the STATE base to pack identity, parallel to projects. Mild strain on 'reuse-not-build' since pack base is net-new, but the engine itself is reused.

options.name: Git-native commit-level merge (no per-file base/); use the clone as the merge surface
options.description: Drop the file-level base/ 3-way model for the pack-publish path entirely and let git be the merge engine. Sequence: clone the sharing repo push-ready; on a pre-existing pack, rsync/overlay the local pack content into the clone's working tree, git add -A, commit; before pushing, git fetch + attempt git merge / git rebase onto remote HEAD; on a non-fast-forward that does not auto-merge, ABORT and tell the user to resolve in the IDE (P7). The merge base is then whatever git's three-way commit merge computes from the common ancestor in the clone's history (the sharing repo's own git history), NOT a cco-managed base/ snapshot. First publish (empty or absent pack) = plain add + push (FF). Subsequent publish = the clone already contains remote HEAD, so the commit-then-merge naturally diffs against the remote's last commit; cco never fabricates a base. Correct ADR-0019 D4 wording to say 'sync-before-publish via git fetch + merge, abort on non-FF' instead of 'reuse the project-update base/ merge'. This explicitly decouples pack publish from the project-update base/ mechanism (acknowledging base/ moved to STATE and is the wrong ancestor for a cross-PC/cross-version pack), and notes that ADR-0019's 'reuse the base/ merge' line is superseded for packs by the git-native path.
pros: No net-new per-pack base/ storage, no H6 extension to packs, no STATE keying — strictly less internal machinery to build and sync.

Sidesteps the ADR-0013 D2 problem cleanly: base/ being STATE-only and version-tied is simply not used; the merge ancestor is the sharing repo's own git history, which is the correct shared ancestor for a multi-maintainer remote.
Most faithful to P7/ADR-0008 (sync transports already-made commits; git is the engine) and to P17 (delegate to git) — the merge IS git's three-way, not a cco re-implementation.
Cross-PC works without local state: any PC that can clone gets the correct ancestor from the repo history.
Naturally non-FF-aborts: a co-maintainer's remote-only commit is in the clone, so the overwrite-and-commit produces a real divergence git refuses to FF, surfacing the conflict instead of clobbering.
cons: Contradicts ADR-0019 D4 / ADR-0018 'Refactor' table wording that explicitly says 'Reuse the cco project update 3-way base/ merge' — this option REWRITES that decision (must be recorded as a refinement/supersession, not a silent edit), so it is more than a spec consolidation.

The cp/overlay-then-commit still overwrites file content in the clone before the merge, so a true file-level interleave of two maintainers' edits to the SAME file degrades to a whole-file conflict (git can't 3-way a file cco just overwrote) — coarser than the project-update per-file merge.
Two different merge philosophies in the codebase (file-level base/ for projects, git-commit-level for packs) increases conceptual surface and divergent maintenance.
Conflict resolution happens in a throwaway clone dir, not in the user's ~/.cco working copy — the 'resolve in the IDE' UX (P7) is awkward unless the spec routes the conflict back to ~/.cco.
options.principleAlignment: Strongly upholds P7 and ADR-0008 (git transports commits; never fabricate), P17 (delegate to git), P16 (sync-before-publish, abort-not-clobber). Cleanly avoids the ADR-0013 D2 wrong-ancestor trap. BUT it contradicts the letter of ADR-0019 D4 / ADR-0018 D3-table ('reuse the base/ 3-way merge'), so it must be filed as a deliberate refinement of a settled decision (allowed by the principle 'a clash is a defect to fix in the decision OR the principle', but it is a decision change, raising it above a pure consolidation). Weaker on parity with the project-update path (two merge models).

options.name: Minimal defect-only fix: abort-on-divergence (non-FF guard), defer the full 3-way merge to a follow-up
options.description: Scope the E item down to the smallest change that stops the data loss, and explicitly defer the full 3-way merge. Sequence: clone push-ready; if the pack exists on remote, compute whether the local content is a descendant of (or identical to) the remote pack state using the recorded source commit (from .cco/source today, DATA after F4); if the publish would not be a clean fast-forward of the remote pack subtree (i.e. remote advanced since the local working copy's recorded commit), ABORT with a reminder: 'remote pack X has changed since you last installed/published; run cco pack update first, resolve, then re-publish' (P7 non-FF -> abort+notify). Only when the local copy is a strict descendant does publish proceed (cp + commit + push). First publish (no source) = init/add + push. This makes the merge base = the recorded last-installed/last-published commit used purely as a STALENESS GUARD (not a full merge ancestor), needs NO new base/ snapshot storage, and reuses .cco/source (-> DATA, F4). Update ADR-0019 D4 to state that v1 publish ABORTS on divergence (sync-before-publish = pull-or-abort), with the file-level 3-way auto-merge deferred to the same follow-up as pack update's merge (today pack update also just overwrites — _install_pack_from_dir force=true — so the merge engine is absent on BOTH pack paths). Correct the 'fast-forward clobber' wording to match the clone-then-overwrite reality.
pros: Smallest, lowest-risk change that actually closes the clobber: a co-maintainer's advanced remote causes an abort, never a silent overwrite — the data-loss the ADR exists to prevent is gone in v1.
No new base/ storage and no H6 extension to packs; reuses the already-recorded source commit as a staleness guard — minimal build surface for E.
Honest about the real code state: pack UPDATE also lacks a 3-way merge today (pure force-overwrite), so deferring auto-merge keeps publish and update symmetric and avoids half-building one side.
Cleanest fit to P7/ADR-0008 'pull non-FF -> abort + notify, resolve in IDE' — mirrors the personal-store push/pull semantics exactly.
Defers the genuinely hard decision (which base, file-level vs commit-level) to one follow-up that covers both pack publish AND pack update, instead of forcing it under V.
cons: Does NOT deliver the 3-way auto-merge ADR-0019 D4 promises — two maintainers editing different files of the same pack must serialize (one aborts, runs update, re-publishes), which is a UX regression vs the project-update experience.
Leaves a known gap that V/E must track (auto-merge deferred), so the 'merge engine convergence' the finding flags is postponed, not resolved.

Requires a reliable remote-descendant check from a recorded commit; the current publish path does not record/compare commits per-pack, so even the staleness guard is some build (though far less than full base machinery).
If F4 (source -> DATA) slips, the guard still reads <pack>/.cco/source, a config-bucket internal file ADR-0013 wants out — a temporary P6 wrinkle until F4 lands.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P7/ADR-0008 (abort on non-FF, transport commits, resolve in IDE) and P16 (sync-before-publish in its weakest faithful form: pull-or-abort, never clobber). Consistent with ADR-0013 D2 (no base/ misuse — none introduced). PARTIALLY satisfies ADR-0019 D4 (closes the clobber defect = the stated primary goal) while explicitly deferring the '3-way merge' clause as a recorded follow-up rather than contradicting it. Strain: the ADR's promise of a 3-way merge is downgraded to abort-and-retry for v1, which must be recorded as a scoped deferral (decision), not silently.

options.recommended: Per-pack STATE base keyed by identity + sync-class-correct 3-way publish spec (one consolidated §6.2 / E-handoff sequence)
options.rationale:
Option 1 is recommended because it is the only option that BOTH delivers what ADR-0019 D4 actually promises (a real 3-way merge that prevents multi-maintainer clobber) AND stays inside every settled decision without rewriting one. It resolves the finding's core ambiguity head-on: the merge base is a pack-scoped STATE snapshot keyed by pack identity (mirroring the proven _cco_project_base_dir pattern), populated on install and publish, with a defined first-vs-subsequent split. This is exactly the ADR-0013 D2 resolution the finding asks for -- base/ stays STATE never-sync (it is a LOCAL, version-tied merge ancestor, never carried in the sharing repo), so it answers 'which base/ ancestor does pack publish use now that base/ moved to STATE' with a concrete, principle-consistent mechanism. It reuses the existing _collect_file_changes/3-way engine (ADR-0019's own 'Reuse' verdict; ADR-0013 D7) rather than building a second merge model, and it folds the three converging refactors (0019 D4 merge + 0018 D3 init-or-merge/structure-discovery + 0013 H6 STATE-path threading) into ONE owning sequence for E -- precisely the 'single consolidated publish-path spec' the finding's proposedResolution calls for. It also forces the corrected 'clone-then-overwrite' wording and names the F4 cross-dependency.

Option 2 (git-native commit merge) is the most architecturally elegant and most faithful to P7/P17, and is the right answer if the maintainer wants to AVOID building per-pack base machinery -- but it CONTRADICTS the letter of ADR-0019 D4 ('reuse the project-update base/ merge'), so it is a decision change (a refinement/supersession), which is above the bar of a consolidation finding and should be a conscious maintainer choice; its coarser file-level conflict behavior is also a real regression vs the project-update experience.

Option 3 (abort-only defect fix) is the safest minimal v1 and is fully principle-consistent, and is the pragmatic fallback if E's budget is tight or if the auto-merge decision should be deferred to cover pack UPDATE too (which today also lacks any merge). But it knowingly under-delivers ADR-0019 D4's 3-way-merge promise and postpones the merge-engine convergence the finding flags, so it should be chosen only as a deliberate scope cut. Recommend Option 1 as the target spec, with Option 3 as the explicitly-labeled v1 fallback if the auto-merge proves too costly for the first E phase.

options.title: Index global-vs-namespaced logical names (H7) is a hidden decision wrongly deferred to E; atomicity/locking unspecified
options.recommended: Option A — Confirm the global-flat index for v1 now; reduce H7 to pure mechanism (atomic temp+rename, single-writer, no lock)
options.rationale: The whole design body already leans — in fact already commits — to the global-flat model. ADR-0016 D4 states the uniqueness invariant "one logical name → one absolute path per machine," explicitly justifies it with the shared-repo case ("a repo used by two projects = one paths: entry"), and design.md §3:323-324 already documents the cco init/join refusal UX that only makes sense under global uniqueness. Requirements AD3/AD5 are written against a single machine-local index keyed by logical name. The coordinate-registry namespacing question (the "global vs per-project" wording inherited from ADR-0014/0015) was already resolved global-free by ADR-0016 D2/D3 going per-unit with explicitly "no namespacing." So what remains genuinely open is (1) a formal confirmation that the v1 index is flat-global (with namespacing reserved post-v1 if ever needed) and (2) the write mechanism. Option A does exactly that and is the minimal-surprise path: it ratifies what the docs already assert, kills the self-contradiction (a schema/invariant question cannot be an "impl-time" detail per V-perspective #4), and grounds the mechanism in the codebase's existing convention — lib/local-paths.sh already writes every file via mktemp + atomic mv with no flock, so the index inherits temp-file+rename with a single-writer assumption and no lock for v1. This upholds AD5/G8, P6, and P10 without re-opening any settled decision (V's mandate forbids re-opening without principle-level cause; here there is none — the principles favor the global model). Options B and C are recorded for completeness but both introduce work or risk the design does not currently call for: B re-opens a settled invariant to add per-project namespacing (contradicts AD5 and the documented join UX, and resurrects a global-namespace concern ADR-0016 deliberately avoided), and C adds OS-level locking that the plain-git, single-user-per-machine model (P7/P8, sync transports commits, never concurrent daemons) does not justify for v1.

options.name: Option A — Confirm the global-flat index for v1 now; reduce H7 to pure mechanism (atomic temp+rename, single-writer, no lock)
options.description: Formally ratify the global-flat index already implied by ADR-0016 D4 and design.md §3: one machine-global `paths:` map (logical name -> abs path), the AD5 uniqueness invariant 'one logical name -> one absolute path per machine', the shared-repo-as-one-entry rule, and the `cco init`/`cco join` collision-refusal UX. Reserve per-project namespacing as an explicitly post-v1 possibility (documented as 'not v1; would require AD5 + join-semantics revision'). Then strip the contradictory 'global-vs-namespaced ... is impl-time (E)' clause from ADR-0016 D4 (lines 147-148) and the E open-item (lines 378-379), leaving E with ONLY the mechanism: atomic write = mktemp + rename (already the codebase convention in lib/local-paths.sh), 0700/0600 perms, single-writer assumption, and an explicit statement that NO file lock is needed for v1 (no concurrent cco daemons; `resolve --scan` and `start` are user-serial; last-writer-wins on the rare race is acceptable and self-healing via re-scan). V edits the docs to remove the contradiction; the maintainer's only decision is to confirm 'global for v1, namespacing reserved'.
pros: Resolves the literal contradiction the finding flags: a schema/invariant question stops being mislabeled an 'impl-time' detail (V perspective #4: surface hidden decisions, do not pass them to E).
Changes nothing substantive — it ratifies what ADR-0016 D4, design.md §3:323-324, and requirements AD3/AD5 already assert; no settled decision is re-opened (honors V's mandate).

Mechanism is already proven in-repo: lib/local-paths.sh writes every file via `mktemp ... .XXXXXX` + atomic `mv`, so the index inherits a known-good, bash-3.2-compatible pattern with zero new dependencies.

Keeps the index a flat single source of machine-specific truth, preserving truthful `git diff` (G8) and the simple `cco resolve`/`join`/compose-mount read path; shared-repo reuse (P5/P13) works by construction with one entry.
Unblocks Phase 0/1 cleanly: the index schema, AD5 invariant, and `join` refusal logic are fixed before any code reads them.
cons: If a future multi-tenant or per-project-name-reuse need emerges, a post-v1 migration to namespaced names would be required (mitigated: explicitly reserved, and the typed-key precedent in DATA tags.yml shows namespacing is addable later without data loss).

Last-writer-wins under a genuine concurrent write (two `cco` processes racing on the same machine) could drop one update; acceptable for v1 because writes are user-serial and `cco resolve --scan` is idempotent and rebuilds the index, but it is a documented, accepted limitation rather than a hard guarantee.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds AD5 (uniqueness invariant), AD3/G8 (machine-agnostic committed config; truthful diff), P6 (index is hidden internal STATE, never in a config bucket), P10 (classify by role: the index's role is single machine-local path materialization), P3/P8 (STATE is non-portable, never-synced; no concurrent sync daemon to lock against). Consistent with ADR-0016 D2/D3 which already resolved the coordinate-registry namespacing global-free ('no namespacing'). Strains nothing; it removes a self-contradiction inside ADR-0016 D4.

options.name: Option B — Adopt per-project namespaced logical names in the index for v1
options.description: Change the index schema so logical names are scoped per project (e.g. `projects.<id>.paths.<name> -> abs-path`, or a composite `<project>/<name>` key), allowing two projects to bind the same short name to different absolute paths. Revise AD5 from machine-global uniqueness to per-project uniqueness, and rewrite the `cco init`/`cco join` refusal logic to only refuse within a project scope. Shared-repo reuse across projects becomes an explicit cross-reference rather than a single shared entry.
pros: Lets two unrelated projects reuse a convenient short name (e.g. `api`) for different repos without collision.
Scopes the uniqueness invariant to the unit a user reasons about (a project), arguably more intuitive for users juggling many projects.
cons: Directly contradicts settled AD5 ('one logical name -> one absolute path per machine') and the design.md §3:323-324 join-refusal UX — this is re-opening a decided invariant, which V is explicitly forbidden to do without a principle-level reason (there is none here).

Breaks the shared-repo-as-one-entry win (ADR-0016 D4): a repo used by two projects would need duplicate or cross-referenced entries, reintroducing the duplication ADR-0002 rejected and complicating compose-mount generation.

Resurrects exactly the 'global namespace' problem ADR-0016 D2/D3 deliberately eliminated for coordinates by going per-unit; adds schema and resolution complexity for a need no requirement currently states.
It is a redesign, not a confirmation — larger blast radius across `cco resolve`, `join`, and compose mounting just before Phase 0.
options.principleAlignment: Strains AD5 (redefines the invariant), P5/P13 (shared-by-construction repo reuse becomes awkward), and ADR-0002/ADR-0016 D4 (reintroduces per-context duplication). Does not clearly advance any P1-P17 principle; the per-unit DRY logic of P12/ADR-0016 actually argues against re-introducing per-project name scoping at the index layer.

options.name: Option C — Global-flat index (as Option A) PLUS an advisory file lock around index writes
options.description: Adopt the same global-flat schema and AD5 confirmation as Option A, but additionally specify a concurrency mechanism beyond temp+rename: an advisory lock (e.g. `flock` on a lockfile, or a mkdir-based lock for portability) held for the read-modify-write of the index so that a concurrent `cco resolve --scan` and `cco start` cannot interleave and lose an update. Atomic rename remains; the lock adds serialization of the RMW window.
pros: Eliminates the last-writer-wins window Option A accepts, giving a hard guarantee against lost index updates under concurrent cco processes.
Makes the concurrency posture explicit and defensible if cco ever grows background/daemon writers.
cons: Adds a dependency/portability concern: `flock` is not present on macOS by default (the CLI targets bash 3.2 + standard Unix tools on macOS), forcing a mkdir-lock or noflock fallback — extra code and edge cases (stale lock cleanup) for a race that the single-user-serial usage model makes near-impossible in v1.

No current requirement or principle calls for concurrent index writers; P7/P8 establish that sync transports already-made commits with no per-command network daemon, so there is no background writer to contend with.
Over-engineers Phase 0/1 relative to the actual risk; the codebase has no existing locking convention to reuse (only temp+rename), so this introduces a new pattern.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds the same principles as Option A (AD5, AD3/G8, P6, P10) and additionally hardens consistency, but introduces friction with the 'standard Unix tools, bash 3.2, macOS-compatible, no extra deps' constraint and with P7/P8's no-daemon model. Defensible only if a concrete concurrent-writer scenario is identified — otherwise it adds cost without a matching requirement.

options.title: `cco new` (ad-hoc/temporary session) survival & resolution model under decentralized config

options.name: Option A — `cco new` survives as an explicit index-less ephemeral escape hatch (document the boundary; share only the compose/mount primitives)
options.description: Keep `cco new` as a top-level Run entry that takes literal `--repo <path>` mounts and bypasses the STATE index, `project.yml`, coordinates, and divergence/reminder logic entirely. It is the deliberate 'no config, no project, scratch session' path. Specifically: (1) Add a row to design.md §7 under 'Run': `cco new [--repo <path>]... [--name|--port|--teammate-mode]` — ad-hoc session, literal paths, no project.yml, no index read/write, no coordinates. (2) Note in §8 / J0 that J0 (four-root bootstrap) still runs first on `cco new` like any command (ADR-0017 D3), so DATA/STATE/CACHE/`~/.cco` get created — but `cco new` writes NOTHING into the index (its repos are not registered, by design). (3) Pin the shared-code contract for E: `cco new` and `cco start` must share the LOW-LEVEL compose/mount primitives being rewritten in Phase 0 — host-absolute mount sources (BL3), the host-side XDG resolver guard (H4), and `GLOBAL_DIR`→`~/.cco/global/.claude` — but `cco new` deliberately stops short of H1 resolution (no index lookup, no member resolution, no divergence notice). (4) Its ephemeral state (`claude-state`, `memory/`) is created in a throwaway `mktemp`/`$tmp_dir` (trap-cleaned) — confirmed STATE-class, never synced, consistent with ADR-0009; the design may optionally relocate the tmp root under STATE for cleanliness but this is not required. This formalizes the prior review M10 recommendation ('declare it stays an index-less ephemeral path and document it') that was logged but never carried into the corpus.
pros: Preserves a shipped 'Must' capability (spec FR-2.5/FR-6.2/US-2) and an existing UX with zero behavior change for the user — the scratch-session use case stays intact.

Closes the exact gap the finding names: E gets a written contract (share primitives, skip H1 resolution) instead of guessing, so it neither silently breaks `cco new` nor invents divergent ad-hoc behavior.
Smallest, lowest-risk change: documentation + a shared-primitive note, no new resolution code, no schema, no migration.

Conceptually clean: `cco new`'s literal `--repo` paths ARE the machine-specific truth for a one-shot session — there is no manifest to be machine-agnostic about, so the index (which exists to decouple committed names from machine paths) is simply not applicable. The index's reason-to-exist (truthful git diff of committed config, AD3/G8) does not arise for an unversioned ephemeral session.
Directly honors the already-recorded M10 disposition, restoring continuity of prior decisions (documentation-first).
cons: Two resolution behaviors coexist (`cco start` index/coordinate-resolved vs `cco new` literal) — a small conceptual surface the docs must make explicit to avoid user confusion about why `cco new` ignores configured names.
No auto-clone-from-url for `cco new` (paths must exist locally) — but that is the intended semantics of an ad-hoc session and matches today's `die` on a missing path.

Requires E to refactor `cmd-start.sh`/`cmd-new.sh` to share the rewritten compose primitives rather than duplicate them, or the BL3 absolute-mount and H4 resolver fixes must be applied in two places (a maintenance hazard if not factored).
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P1 (literal `--repo` is user-supplied transient input, not a managed config file); P2/P6 (ephemeral state stays STATE-class/hidden, nothing authored into a config bucket); ADR-0017 D3 (J0 still bootstraps the four roots on `cco new`); ADR-0009 (ephemeral memory/transcripts = machine-local STATE, never synced); AD6 (cwd/explicit-input as the start source — here the explicit `--repo` list). Does NOT engage P12/P14 coordinates (no manifest, by design). Honors design §9 Phase-0 BL3/H4 as the shared surface. Strengthens nothing it contradicts; resolves the open M10 review item.

options.name: Option B — Unify `cco new` onto the same H1 index/resolution path as `cco start` (synthesize an in-memory ephemeral project)
options.description: Make `cco new` construct an ephemeral, unnamed in-memory `project.yml`-equivalent from its `--repo` args, then route through the SAME resolver as `cco start`: register the repos in the STATE index (or a transient index overlay), apply the H1 ordered sequence (resolve source → resolve members → resolve/clone unresolved → compute notices → start). `--repo <path>` becomes a name+coordinate-less entry resolved exactly like a configured repo, including the unresolved→prompt/clone journey JF. Add a §7 row noting it shares the full `cco start` resolution stack.
pros: Single resolution code path — `cco new` and `cco start` cannot drift; H1 ordering, BL3 absolute mounts, and the resolver guard are exercised identically by both.
Could opportunistically gain clone-from-url / unresolved-prompt behavior for free if a `--repo <url>` form were ever added.
No 'two behaviors' conceptual surface to document.
cons: Over-engineers an intentionally throwaway path: `cco new` has literal absolute paths by definition, so index resolution (whose entire purpose is mapping machine-agnostic NAMES → machine paths) adds a layer with nothing to resolve.

Writing ephemeral repos into the persistent STATE index pollutes it with one-shot entries (and risks the AD5 uniqueness invariant firing if a scratch path collides with a real configured name) — or forces a transient-index mechanism that does not exist and would be net-new code.
Larger, riskier E change (new code, new tests) for a capability that works today, contradicting the breaking-cutover discipline of keeping each phase minimal and runnable.
Invites scope creep (coordinates, divergence notices, reminder aggregator) onto a path that has no project, no team, and no cross-PC story.
options.principleAlignment: Strains P10 (classify by role/problem — `cco new`'s role is index-less scratch, forcing it through the index is surface-driven, not role-driven); strains AD5 uniqueness (transient names in a persistent index); strains design §3 (the index exists for machine-agnostic committed config — `cco new` has none). Touches ADR-0017 D2 (`cco resolve`/H1) but applies it where it has no payload. Marginally upholds DRY of the resolution code, but at the cost of P1/P6 cleanliness (ephemeral input is not managed config) and the minimal-phase discipline (§9).

options.name: Option C — Drop `cco new`; fold scratch sessions into `cco start` with on-the-fly repos
options.description: Remove `cco new` entirely and add it to the §7 'Removed' list (alongside `cco vault *` and `cco project create`). Replace the scratch-session use case with an ephemeral form of `cco start` (e.g. `cco start --repo <path>...` with no project), or require users to `cco init` a throwaway project first. Update spec FR-2.5/FR-6.2/US-2 to reflect the removal/replacement.
pros: Single Run entry point — minimal command surface, one mental model for starting a session.
Eliminates the dual compose-generation code paths (`cmd-new.sh` vs `cmd-start.sh`) that E would otherwise both have to keep in sync.
Forces an explicit decision in the design rather than leaving `cco new` in limbo.
cons: Removes a 'Must' capability (spec FR-2.5/FR-6.2, US-2) without a like-for-like replacement unless `cco start --repo` is fully designed — i.e. it just relocates the unaddressed work into `cco start`.

Breaks an existing, documented, shipped UX (installation.md, cli.md §3.3) with no migration path for users who rely on `cco new` for one-off experiments — a gratuitous breaking change beyond the refactor's intended scope (AD12 cutover is about the vault, not about removing unrelated entry points).
Requiring `cco init` for a throwaway session contradicts the entire point of `cco new` (no project template needed, per US-2) and adds friction / leftover `.cco/` dirs in scratch repos.
The decentralized-config refactor has no mandate to delete `cco new`; doing so expands the blast radius and the spec-update/doc-sweep burden.
options.principleAlignment: Neutral-to-negative on the principles: nothing in P1–P17 requires removing `cco new`. AD12 (breaking cutover) is scoped to the vault/`project create`, not to ad-hoc sessions, so removal is out-of-charter. The §7 'Removed' list is deliberately narrow; widening it strains the design's stated supersession scope (requirements §9). Only marginal DRY benefit, outweighed by the loss of a Must requirement and an unforced breaking change.

options.recommended: Option A — `cco new` survives as an explicit index-less ephemeral escape hatch (document the boundary; share only the compose/mount primitives)
options.rationale: Option A is recommended because it resolves the finding with the least risk while staying fully consistent with settled decisions. `cco new` is a shipped 'Must' capability (spec FR-2.5/FR-6.2/US-2) and a documented UX (installation.md, cli.md §3.3); removing it (Option C) is an unforced breaking change outside the refactor's charter (AD12 targets the vault, not ad-hoc sessions), and routing it through the full index/coordinate stack (Option B) over-engineers an intentionally throwaway path — its literal `--repo` paths ARE the machine-specific truth, so the index (which exists to map machine-agnostic committed names to paths, design §3 / AD3 / G8) has nothing to resolve and would only risk AD5 collisions or require net-new transient-index code. Option A also restores continuity with the prior coherence review, which already logged M10 with exactly this disposition ('declare it stays an index-less ephemeral path and document it') but never propagated it into §7 — a documentation-first gap this closes. The load-bearing part of Option A for E is the explicit shared-code contract: `cco new` and `cco start` must share the Phase-0 compose primitives being rewritten (host-absolute mount sources BL3, host-side XDG resolver guard H4, `GLOBAL_DIR`→`~/.cco/global/.claude`) so E does not silently break `cco new` or fork the mount logic, while `cco new` deliberately stops short of H1 resolution. J0 (ADR-0017 D3) still runs first to bootstrap the four roots; ephemeral state remains STATE-class (ADR-0009). Concrete edits: add a 'Run' row in design.md §7 for `cco new [--repo <path>]...` (literal paths, no project.yml, no index, no coordinates), a §8/J0 note that J0 runs but the index is untouched, and a Phase-0 note pinning the shared-primitive vs skip-H1 boundary; optionally record an ADR-0021 capturing the M10 disposition so it is not re-litigated.

options.title: extra_mounts schema in §2.4 has no home for the machine-agnostic container target; M5 punted to E with no rule for compose generation or migration

options.name: Option A (RECOMMENDED) — Keep an explicit machine-agnostic `target:` in project.yml; index supplies only the host path
options.description: Define the new extra_mounts entry in §2.4 as: `- name:` (logical id, also the index key for the host path) + `target:` (container mount point, machine-agnostic config, OPTIONAL with default `/workspace/<name>`) + `readonly:` (default true). The host absolute path comes from the STATE index keyed by `name` (exactly like repos); the `target:` stays in the versioned manifest because a container path is identical on every machine — it is machine-agnostic config, not machine-local state. yaml.sh parser is rewritten to read `name`+`target`(+default)+`readonly`; the resolver/index keys on `name` (not on `target` as today). Migration: for each legacy `- source:/target:` entry, derive `name` (slugify the target's basename or prompt), write `name`+`target`+`readonly` into project.yml, and seed the index `name → expanded(source)`. §3 is updated to note the index supplies only the host path for an extra_mount `name`; the `target` lives in config. M5 is promoted from impl-detail to a stated schema decision before Phase 0.
pros: Loss-free: preserves the existing arbitrary-target capability (e.g. the template's own `target: /workspace/docs/api-specs` nested subpath), so no existing user config silently changes mount point on migration

Correct axis split: a container path is the same on every machine, so by AD3/G8 it is machine-agnostic config and belongs in project.yml; the host path (machine-specific) stays in the index — this exactly mirrors the §2.4 decomposition (name=config, coordinate=config, local-path=index/STATE)

Default `/workspace/<name>` keeps the common case as terse as the proposed name-only schema (omit target ⇒ /workspace/<name>), so §2.4's example `- name: shared-assets / readonly: true` still works verbatim
Re-keys index/dedup on `name` instead of `target`, removing the current local-paths.sh oddity of using the container target as the resolution key and aligning extra_mounts with the repo model
Migration is mechanical and writes one new field; the consolidated taxonomy table (ADR-0016) needs no change because target is config, host-path is STATE
cons: One extra optional field vs the leaner name-only sketch in §2.4 (but the field is genuinely needed to express non-default targets)
Requires choosing a `name` during migration for legacy entries that only had source/target (slugify-or-prompt rule must be specified)
Keeps a small amount of surface (target) that 90% of users will leave defaulted
options.principleAlignment: Upholds AD3/G8 (target is machine-agnostic ⇒ config, host path ⇒ index, truthful git diff preserved). Upholds P11/P12 decomposition (name=config handle, target=machine-agnostic config like a coordinate, host-path=machine-local materialization in STATE index, ADR-0016 D4). Upholds AD5 (index = name→absolute path, per-machine). Consistent with ADR-0016 D2 uniform manifest model. Does not strain any P1-P17.

options.name: Option B — Drop the target; extra_mounts deterministically mount at `/workspace/<name>`, mirroring repos
options.description: Adopt the §2.4 name-only schema literally: `- name:` + `readonly:`. The container target is no longer expressible — every extra_mount deterministically mounts at `/workspace/<name>`, exactly like repos. The host path comes from the index by `name`. yaml.sh emits `host:/workspace/<name>[:ro]`. Migration: derive `name` from the legacy `target` basename (or prompt), seed the index from `source`, and DISCARD the explicit target (with a warning when the old target was not `/workspace/<name>`). This collapses repos and extra_mounts into nearly the same code path (the only difference being read-only default and that extra_mounts are not full git repos).
pros: Simplest, leanest schema — matches the §2.4 example as written with zero extra fields
Maximal symmetry with repos: one mount-mapping rule `/workspace/<name>` for everything, simpler parser and allowlist (pathmap) logic
No machine-agnostic-vs-machine-local ambiguity to document — host path in index, mount point is a pure function of name
cons: LOSSY: breaks any existing config that mounts at a non-`/workspace/<name>` path, including nested subpaths like the shipped template example `target: /workspace/docs/api-specs` — migration would silently relocate the mount and could break CLAUDE.md references / hook paths that point at the old target
Removes a real capability (arbitrary reference-material placement) that project-setup.md explicitly documents as 'Mounted at an arbitrary path in the container'
Forces `name` to encode the desired path (e.g. name `docs-api-specs`), which is awkward and cannot reproduce a multi-segment subpath
A breaking behavioral change beyond the planned schema migration — needs louder migration warnings and possibly a user decision per entry anyway
options.principleAlignment: Upholds AD3/G8 and AD5 (no machine data in config). Aligns with AD6/repo determinism. STRAINS the documented extra_mounts contract (arbitrary target) and AD12's 'lossless migration' expectation, since non-default targets are dropped. Borderline with P10 (classify by role): extra_mounts' role explicitly includes arbitrary placement, which this erases.

options.name: Option C — Store the container target in the index alongside the host path (machine-local mount spec)
options.description: Keep §2.4 entries as `- name:` + `readonly:` (no target in config), but extend the STATE index so each extra_mount name maps to BOTH host path and container target: `paths: { shared-assets: { host: /Users/me/assets, target: /workspace/assets } }`. The resolver reads target from the index; compose mounts `host:target`. Migration writes the legacy target into the index, not into project.yml. project.yml stays name-only and fully machine-agnostic.
pros: project.yml stays maximally lean (name + readonly only), matching the §2.4 sketch exactly
Preserves arbitrary targets (no loss of capability) without adding a config field
Index already keys by name and already holds extra_mounts host paths — adding a sibling field is a localized change
cons: Misclassifies the target: a container path is machine-agnostic (same on every PC), so putting it in the per-machine index means each PC must independently re-specify the same value — a DRY violation and a re-fetch/divergence hazard the §2.4 model deliberately avoids for url coordinates

On a fresh machine `cco resolve` would prompt for (or default) the target per-PC, so the team-shared intent of the mount point is lost — a recipient cloning the repo has no idea where the maintainer intended the mount, defeating reproducibility
Complicates the index schema (mixed scalar paths for repos vs object {host,target} for mounts), increasing parser/atomicity surface (H7) for E
Contradicts the §2.4/P12 principle that machine-agnostic locators live in the manifest and only machine-specific materializations live in the index
options.principleAlignment: STRAINS AD3/G8 reasoning and P11/P12 (machine-agnostic data is being pushed into machine-local STATE, the inverse of the coordinate model). STRAINS DRY (P12) — same target re-entered per machine. Honors P6 (index is internal/hidden) but for the wrong datum. Weakest principle fit of the three.

options.recommended: Option A (RECOMMENDED) — Keep an explicit machine-agnostic `target:` in project.yml; index supplies only the host path
options.rationale: Option A is the only choice that is loss-free AND principle-correct. The decisive fact is the axis split mandated by AD3/G8 and formalized in P11/P12: a container mount point is identical on every machine (like a URL coordinate), so it is machine-agnostic CONFIG and belongs in the versioned project.yml; only the host absolute path is machine-specific and belongs in the STATE index. This exactly mirrors the §2.4 repo decomposition (name + machine-agnostic coordinate in the manifest, host path in the index). Option B is lossy — it discards arbitrary targets, breaking the documented extra_mounts contract and even the framework's own shipped template example (`target: /workspace/docs/api-specs`), which violates AD12's lossless-migration expectation. Option C puts the machine-agnostic target into the per-machine index, inverting the coordinate model, breaking DRY (each PC re-specifies the same value), and destroying the team-shared reproducibility of the mount point for a teammate who clones the repo. Option A keeps the common case as terse as the name-only sketch (omit `target` ⇒ default `/workspace/<name>`) while preserving the escape hatch, re-keys index/dedup on `name` (fixing the current local-paths.sh oddity of keying on target), and yields a mechanical migration. Concrete decision to record in §2.4 before Phase 0: extra_mounts entry = `name` (index key for host path) + OPTIONAL `target` (machine-agnostic, default `/workspace/<name>`) + `readonly` (default true); update §3 to state the index supplies only the host path for the mount name; specify migration source→index + name (slugify-or-prompt) with target preserved; feed name+target into the yaml.sh parser rewrite. Promote M5 from impl-detail to a stated schema decision.

options.title: cco config validate contract: invocation scope, per-failure exit codes, output format, machine-agnostic detection, hook installation, and non-TTY heal semantics

options.name: Option A — Pin the contract per the proposed resolution, presence-only by default with an opt-in --reachable probe, and a docs-only manual hook snippet (no installer command in v1)
options.description:
Specify in design.md §7 + ADR-0019 D9 the full I/O contract:

INVOCATION: `cco config validate [project] [--all] [--reachable]`. cwd-first discovery (mirrors §7 'Discovery is cwd-first': if cwd/ancestor has `.cco/project.yml`, validate that unit; else resolve `[project]` via the index; `--all` validates every indexed project). Validates the unit's `<repo>/.cco/project.yml` and any `~/.cco/packs/*/pack.yml` it references. No network by default.

PER-FAILURE EXIT CODES (composable / highest-severity-wins, like `grep`): 0 = clean; 1 = reachability/coordinate gap (a referenced id has no coordinate, or under `--reachable` an unreachable one); 2 = non-machine-agnostic content (an absolute/real path detected) or duplicate id within a section. When multiple classes fail, exit with the numerically-highest code and report all classes in the body. (Distinct codes let a CI/hook branch on failure type; the previous `cco sync --check` precedent is exit-code-only.)

OUTPUT: one line per failing id, `<section>.<id>: <reason>` (e.g. `repos.repo2: missing coordinate (no url)`, `llms.react: id duplicated`, `project.yml: absolute path '/Users/me/...' on line N (not machine-agnostic)`), then a one-line summary `validate: N error(s) across M id(s) [reachability=X agnostic=Y uniqueness=Z]`. Quiet on success unless `-v`. This is human-and-grep-friendly without being a full table.

MACHINE-AGNOSTIC DETECTION (mechanical): reuse the existing machine-agnostic notion already used by sync/diff (G8). Flag a value as a real/absolute path when it matches an anchored ERE for an OS-absolute path: `^(/|~|[A-Za-z]:\\)` after trimming quotes/whitespace, applied to scalar leaf values of `project.yml`/`pack.yml` only (never to `url:` http(s)/git scheme values, and never to the gitignored `secrets.env`). This is heuristic, documented as such, and intentionally conservative (false-negatives over false-positives) because P14 forbids hard-blocking — same philosophy as the secret content-scan in `lib/secrets.sh`.

REACHABLE = PRESENCE-ONLY BY DEFAULT: 'reachable coordinate' means the coordinate is PRESENT, well-formed, and unique — NO network fetch by default (offline/CI-safe, deterministic). `--reachable` opts into a network probe (`git ls-remote --exit-code <url>` / HTTP HEAD for llms) that adds exit-1 on an unresolvable upstream; without it, validate is pure local static analysis.

HOOK INSTALLATION: NO installer command in v1. ADR-0019 D9 layer d ('wired into the user's git hook') is satisfied by a documented manual snippet in the guides (`echo 'cco config validate || true' >> .git/hooks/pre-commit`, non-blocking by `|| true`). This matches the present codebase, where the secret scan is called inline by `cco vault save`/publish and there is no git-hook installer. (A `cco config protect`-style scaffold for the hook is deferred to a future, consistent with the optional-scaffold precedent of ADR-0020.)

HEAL/BACKFILL NON-TTY: heal-at-resolve (layer b) is TTY-interactive ONLY. In non-TTY/CI it is a no-op that emits a one-line notice (`note: <id> missing coordinate; run 'cco resolve' on a terminal to backfill`) and NEVER blocks — consistent with `_prompt_for_path` in lib/local-paths.sh:162 which already `return 2`s (skip) on `! -t 0`. `cco resolve` itself already cannot prompt non-TTY, so backfill simply does not occur silently; detection of the gap is left to `cco config validate` (which IS non-TTY-safe and exit-code-only).
pros: Directly implements the finding's proposedResolution, so E gets a contract test it can write line-for-line.
Presence-only default keeps validate deterministic, offline-safe, and fast — the right default for a CI/hook keystone (P14 layers c/d).
Per-class exit codes (0/1/2) give CI/hooks a branchable signal while remaining exit-code-only like the `cco sync --check` precedent (design §4.2).
Reuses the existing G8 machine-agnostic concept and the lib/secrets.sh heuristic-scan philosophy rather than inventing a new mechanism.
Docs-only hook + no-op non-TTY heal exactly mirror the already-shipped patterns (inline secret scan; _prompt_for_path return-2 on non-TTY), minimizing net-new surface for v1.
Never hard-blocks anywhere — fully honors P14's boundary-less, best-effort model and ADR-0008's reminder philosophy.
cons: Two exit codes (1 vs 2) is a small contract surface E must keep stable; collapsing-to-highest hides the lower-severity class in the exit code (mitigated by listing all classes in the body).

The absolute-path regex is a heuristic — a Windows UNC path or an env-var-templated path could slip through (acceptable: validate warns, never blocks; false-negatives are by-design safer than false-positives).
`--reachable` introduces a network code path that must be offline-tolerant (timeout → treated as 'unknown', not a hard fail) to avoid CI flakiness.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P14 (layered, never-hard-block; validate is layer c, hook layer d, heal layer b — all best-effort), ADR-0019 D2 (the layer table is the spec being completed), ADR-0016 D9 (exit-code-only, cf. sync --check, never blocks), ADR-0008 (non-blocking reminder philosophy), G8 / ADR-0002 (machine-agnostic = truthful git diff — reuses the existing notion), P1 (heal catches hand-edited IDE-editable config). Strains nothing; the presence-only default is consistent with ADR-0017 D1 (llms url mandatory, repo url optional → presence, not liveness, is the static-checkable invariant).

options.name: Option B — Single non-zero exit code, table output, presence + uniqueness + agnosticism only (no --reachable at all in v1), docs-only hook, non-TTY heal no-op
options.description:
Same invocation (`cco config validate [project] [--all]`, cwd-first), same machine-agnostic detection regex, same docs-only hook and same non-TTY heal-no-op as Option A, but two simplifications:

EXIT CODES: binary only — 0 = clean, 1 = any contract violation. Failure CLASS is conveyed in the OUTPUT, not the exit code. Rationale: the three predicates are all 'this unit is not share-ready'; a hook/CI almost always wants pass/fail, and a single non-zero matches the simplest reading of ADR-0016 D9 ('exit-code only') and the existing `cco sync --check` which is also binary.

OUTPUT: a compact per-id TABLE (`ID | SECTION | CHECK | RESULT`) plus a summary count, so a human running it interactively gets an at-a-glance share-readiness report; machines key off the exit code only.

NO NETWORK PROBE IN V1: 'reachable coordinate' is defined as presence + well-formedness + uniqueness, FULL STOP — the `--reachable` network mode is explicitly deferred to a future (listed in §12), keeping v1 purely static and removing any offline-flakiness risk entirely.
pros: Smallest, most stable contract: binary exit code is the least ambiguous thing for E to test and for users to wire into a hook.
Table output is the most legible for the interactive 'is my project share-ready?' use case the maintainer cares about.
Zero network surface in v1 → no timeout/flakiness design needed; fully deterministic and offline-safe by construction.
Closest literal reading of ADR-0016 D9 'exit-code only … cf. cco sync --check' (which is binary).
cons: Loses the finding's requested per-failure-class exit codes (the finding explicitly proposes 0/1/2) — a CI pipeline can't branch on reachability-gap vs non-agnostic without parsing stdout.
Defers --reachable entirely, so the 'presence vs network-fetch' fork the finding flags is resolved by omission, not by a designed opt-in — a future reintroduction risks a contract change.
Table output is harder to grep/diff in logs than one-line-per-id; CI consumers often prefer line-oriented output.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds the same set as A (P14, ADR-0019 D2, ADR-0016 D9, ADR-0008, G8) and is arguably the most literal fit to ADR-0016 D9's 'exit-code only, cf. sync --check' (binary). Mild tension with the finding's stated proposedResolution (which wants distinct codes) — not a principle conflict, a granularity choice. No strain on P15/P16/P17 (validate is read-only).

options.name: Option C — Option A's contract PLUS a first-class hook installer (`cco config protect --hook` / `cco config validate --install-hook`) that wires a non-blocking pre-commit hook
options.description:
Identical to Option A on invocation, exit codes (0/1/2), output, machine-agnostic detection, presence-only-default + `--reachable`, and non-TTY heal-no-op — but resolves the hook-installer fork in the OPPOSITE direction: ADR-0019 D9 layer d gets a REAL installer command.

Fold hook installation into the existing `cco config protect` scaffold (ADR-0020 — already the home for optional, opt-in, git-delegated setup assistance): `cco config protect --hook` writes a `.git/hooks/pre-commit` (or appends to an existing one via a guarded marker block) that calls `cco config validate || true` (non-blocking by construction). Idempotent (marker-guarded), opt-in, never installed automatically, and emits the same 'enforcement delegated to git' framing as the CODEOWNERS scaffold. Layer d is thus a real, supported, one-command path rather than a docs snippet; the manual snippet remains documented as the no-command alternative.
pros: Gives layer d (ADR-0019 D2 'opt-in pre-commit hook') a concrete, testable mechanism instead of leaving it to copy-paste — closes the exact 'no installer' gap the finding names.
Naturally co-located with `cco config protect` (ADR-0020), which already exists to scaffold opt-in, git-delegated setup assistance — no new command family, consistent mental model.
Idempotent marker-guarded install is friendlier than hand-editing `.git/hooks/pre-commit`, especially for non-expert users.
Keeps all of Option A's offline-safe, never-block, per-class-exit-code benefits.
cons: Adds net-new v1 implementation surface (hook file generation, idempotent marker handling, existing-hook merge, per-repo install) the codebase has NO precedent for — the current secret scan is inline, not a git-installed hook.

A cco-written git hook edges toward 'cco in the enforcement path', the very thing P17 warns against — must be carefully framed as opt-in assistance (mitigated by `|| true` non-blocking + opt-in), but it is more rope than needed for v1.
Risks scope-creep on the impl-readiness gate (V→E) when a documented snippet would satisfy P14 layer d for v1; the installer is a convenience, not a correctness requirement.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P14 (layer d made real; still never-block via `|| true`), ADR-0020 / P17 (installer rides the existing opt-in `cco config protect` scaffold; enforcement still delegated to git — the hook only runs cco's read-only check, host rules still gatekeep), ADR-0008 (non-blocking). STRAINS P17 most among the options (a cco-authored hook is closer to the push path than a docs snippet) — acceptable only because it is opt-in and non-blocking, but it is the reason this is not the recommendation for v1.

options.recommended: Option A — Pin the contract per the proposed resolution, presence-only by default with an opt-in --reachable probe, and a docs-only manual hook snippet (no installer command in v1)
options.rationale: Option A is recommended because it resolves every open fork the finding lists with the minimum net-new surface and maximum consistency with already-shipped patterns. It satisfies the keystone role of `cco config validate` in P14 (layers c/d) and ADR-0020 D4's footgun mitigation while never contradicting a settled decision: exit-code-only and never-block come straight from ADR-0016 D9 + ADR-0008; the machine-agnostic detection reuses the existing G8 notion and the lib/secrets.sh heuristic-scan philosophy rather than inventing a mechanism; the presence-only default (with an explicit `--reachable` opt-in) keeps the command deterministic and offline/CI-safe, which is exactly what a hook/CI keystone needs and is consistent with ADR-0017 D1 (presence, not liveness, is the static invariant). The non-TTY heal-no-op mirrors lib/local-paths.sh:162 (`_prompt_for_path` already returns skip on `! -t 0`) and `cco resolve`'s existing inability to prompt non-TTY, so it introduces zero new behavior — detection moves to the non-TTY-safe `validate`. The docs-only hook matches the current codebase, where the secret scan is called inline by cco's own commands and there is no git-hook installer; this avoids edging cco toward the enforcement path that P17 warns against.\n\nOption B is the strongest alternative and is preferable if the maintainer wants the absolute-minimum contract (binary exit, table output, no network ever) — but it deliberately drops the per-class exit codes the finding's proposedResolution asks for and resolves the presence-vs-network fork by omission, which risks a contract change when `--reachable` is later wanted. Option C is the right shape ONLY if layer d's hand-wiring is judged a real v1 footgun; it correctly homes the installer in the existing `cco config protect` (ADR-0020) but adds git-hook-generation surface the codebase has no precedent for and leans hardest on P17, so it is better deferred to a future. All three are read-only and never-block, so none strains P15/P16/P17 on substance; the choice is granularity and surface. Key files: design.md §2.4 (lines 268-300) and §7 (lines 517-533) are where the pinned contract lands; decisions/0019 §D2 (lines 65-85) holds the layer table to annotate; decisions/0016 §D9 (lines 268-275) holds the validity contract; lib/local-paths.sh:162 and lib/secrets.sh:1-40 are the grounding precedents (non-TTY guard; inline heuristic scan, no hook installer).

options.title: cco config protect contract: pin the scope decision (doc-only vs helper), fix the CODEOWNERS location no-op, and specify the host-detection + per-host emitter contract

options.name: Confirm doc-only v1 baseline; helper deferred with a fully-specified contract (RECOMMENDED)
options.description: Resolve the open decision by confirming the ADR-0020 recorded preference: v1 ships DOCUMENTATION ONLY (a governance guide covering the sharing-repo read/write split and the project-repo CODEOWNERS+rulesets setup, per host). The `cco config protect` helper is recorded as a near-term, opt-in addition — NOT scheduled in the v1 E phase. To make the deferral concrete and remove the ambiguity that caused this finding, the ADR/design are corrected so the helper, IF/WHEN built, has a pinned contract: (1) the scaffolded CODEOWNERS goes to a HOST-RECOGNIZED location — repo root `CODEOWNERS` (or `.github/CODEOWNERS` if a `.github/` dir exists; both are GitHub-honored) — NOT the literal `<repo>/.cco/CODEOWNERS`, with a rule line `/.cco/** @org/cco-maintainers`; (2) host is detected from the code repo's `origin` URL (reuse the existing `*github.com*`-style parsing in lib/remote.sh; fall back to a `--host` flag / prompt when origin is absent or unrecognized); (3) per-host instruction text is enumerated — GitHub: enable a branch Ruleset with fnmatch path `/.cco/**` + 'require review from Code Owners'; Gitea: protected-branch file-pattern glob on `.cco/**`; GitLab: push rules are filename-regex only, path-scoped block needs a pre-receive hook (advisory Code Owners noted as bypassable); generic git: server-side pre-receive hook. design.md §7 row is re-marked 'DOCS (v1) / helper deferred (post-v1)' and §9/§12 explicitly state the helper is NOT a v1 E item, so 'optional' cannot silently become 'unscheduled'.
pros: Directly honors the ADR-0020 recorded maintainer preference (documentation baseline + opt-in helper) — no new decision reversal required, lowest surprise
Removes the no-op risk entirely for v1: docs instruct the user to place CODEOWNERS where the host actually honors it, so nothing ineffective is built
Strongest P17 alignment: cco's v1 value-add is pure setup assistance via guidance; zero gatekeeping, zero parallel-enforcer surface area

Keeps E's scope minimal and unambiguous — E ships only the S8 token-leak checklist + the governance guide; the helper's build cost is not on the v1 critical path (YAGNI, mirrors ADR-0020 D2's v1 deferral of granular write)
Still pins the helper's contract (location, host-detection, per-host strings) so the deferred work is well-specified and a future session does not re-litigate it
cons: Users get manual setup only in v1 — slightly less convenience than a one-command scaffold (mitigated: CODEOWNERS+ruleset setup is a one-time, low-frequency action)
Requires editing the design.md §7 status cell and §9/§12 wording so the deferral is visible — a doc touch beyond a pure ADR confirmation
The 'recorded preference' becomes binding; if the maintainer actually wants the helper in v1, this option is the wrong call
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P17 (cco assists, never gatekeeps — guidance is the purest form of assistance) and ADR-0020 D1/D4 (delegate enforcement to git; value-add = setup assistance). Confirms the ADR-0020 Open recorded preference. Honors the documentation-first / ADR-as-continuity rule by closing the open question explicitly rather than leaving it dangling. Consistent with ADR-0020 D2's YAGNI stance (advanced/optional surfaces are documented, not built, for v1). Strengthens P14/G8 indirectly (guide still references `cco config validate` as the share-readiness surface). No strain against any settled decision.

options.name: Ship the helper in v1 with the corrected contract (host-recognized CODEOWNERS + host emitter)
options.description: Commit to building `cco config protect` as a v1 E deliverable, but with the literal-location defect fixed. The command: detects the host from the code repo's `origin` URL (reuse lib/remote.sh URL parsing; `--host` override / prompt on miss); writes CODEOWNERS to a host-recognized path (repo root, or `.github/CODEOWNERS` when `.github/` exists) containing `/.cco/** @org/cco-maintainers` (owner team prompted or defaulted to a placeholder with a TODO comment); then prints the exact per-host enforcement instructions enumerated in option 1 (GitHub Rulesets, Gitea protected-path patterns, GitLab pre-receive caveat, generic pre-receive). It explicitly NEVER writes to `<repo>/.cco/CODEOWNERS`. design.md §7 row stays 'NEW' (no longer 'optional/ambiguous'); §9 gains an explicit Phase-3/E line item for it; §11 adds tests (host-detection matrix from origin, location-correctness, idempotent re-run, instruction-string snapshots).
pros: Delivers the convenience the §7 surface already advertises ('NEW') — removes the gap between an advertised command and an unbuilt feature
Fixes the GitHub no-op decisively at the source: the scaffold lands where the host honors it, so the command is actually effective
Host detection is low-risk and already grounded in the codebase (origin-URL parsing exists in lib/remote.sh), so build cost is modest
A concrete, testable contract (location + per-host strings + detection) makes E's scope crisp instead of open-ended
cons: Contradicts the ADR-0020 recorded maintainer preference (doc-baseline first) — requires the maintainer to actively change that preference

Writing CODEOWNERS to the code repo ROOT/`.github/` (not under `.cco/`) means cco now touches code-repo files outside its `.cco/` surface — a broader footprint than ADR-0020's framing implied; needs an explicit nod that this is acceptable (it is still assist-only, the file is a plain artifact the host enforces)
Adds net-new code + tests to the v1 critical path for a low-frequency, one-time setup action (the exact YAGNI tension ADR-0020 D2 flagged)
Per-host enforcement quality still varies (GitLab partial); the helper can scaffold but cannot guarantee the host actually enforces — risk of users over-trusting a 'protect' command name
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P17 in spirit (still assist-only: it configures, the host enforces — D4) and fixes the ADR-0020 D4 location defect. BUT it overrides the ADR-0020 Open recorded preference (doc baseline first), so it is a deliberate decision change, not a confirmation — must be flagged as such. Slight tension with ADR-0020 D2's YAGNI/v1-deferral posture and with the project's '`<repo>/.cco/` is cco's surface' framing (P5/D3), since it writes outside `.cco/`. The command name 'protect' risks implying enforcement, lightly straining P17's 'never gatekeeps' messaging — mitigated by output copy ('configures, does not enforce').

options.name: Doc-only v1 with a thin 'instructions-only' protect command (no CODEOWNERS write)
options.description: Middle path: v1 ships documentation as the baseline AND a minimal `cco config protect` that EMITS the per-host instructions (detected from origin) and the recommended CODEOWNERS body text to STDOUT, but writes NOTHING to disk. This avoids the location problem entirely (no file is scaffolded, so it cannot be placed wrong), gives users a discoverable one-command path to the right guidance, and keeps the helper trivially within P17 (it only prints). The contract is: detect host from origin (lib/remote.sh; `--host` override), print the host-specific Ruleset/protected-path/pre-receive steps, and print a copy-paste CODEOWNERS block WITH an explicit note that it must be saved to repo root or `.github/` (NOT `.cco/`). design.md §7 row becomes 'NEW — prints instructions only (no scaffold)'; §9 adds it as a small, low-risk E line; §11 tests the host-detection + output strings.
pros: Sidesteps the no-op defect by construction — printing-only cannot put a CODEOWNERS where the host ignores it; the printed note tells the user the correct location
Keeps cco's footprint strictly inside its own surface (writes nothing to the code repo), the cleanest P17 / P5 posture of the build options
Cheaper and lower-risk than option 2 (no file-writing, no merge-on-existing-CODEOWNERS edge cases, no owner-team prompting logic)
Still improves discoverability over pure docs — a user finds the guidance via the CLI, not only the manual
cons: Diverges from ADR-0020 D4's literal wording ('scaffolds a CODEOWNERS') — the verb 'scaffold' becomes 'emit', so the ADR text must be amended to match
Less convenient than a real scaffold (option 2): the user must still create and place the file by hand from the printed block
Two surfaces to document (the guide AND the print command) for marginal added value over docs alone
Arguably redundant with the documentation baseline — a printed block is close to what the guide already contains, so the incremental benefit may not justify any v1 code at all (favoring option 1)
options.principleAlignment: Strongest literal P17 fit among the build options (prints only — categorically cannot gatekeep and cannot mis-place a file). Honors ADR-0020 D1/D4 intent (assist, delegate enforcement) but amends D4's 'scaffold' verb → 'emit', so it is a refinement of the ADR, not a contradiction. Stays inside P5/D3's '`.cco/` is cco's surface' framing (writes nothing to the code repo). Mild tension: it still adds a v1 surface that ADR-0020's recorded preference would defer, so it sits between option 1 (defer) and option 2 (full build).

options.recommended: Confirm doc-only v1 baseline; helper deferred with a fully-specified contract (RECOMMENDED)
options.rationale: Option 1 is recommended because it resolves the finding the way the settled decisions already lean, with the least disturbance and the strongest principle fit. (1) It confirms — rather than overrides — the ADR-0020 Open recorded maintainer preference (documentation baseline in v1; helper as an opt-in near-term addition), so it requires no decision reversal and is the lowest-surprise close. (2) It eliminates the GitHub no-op risk for v1 entirely: the guide instructs users to place CODEOWNERS at a host-recognized location (repo root or .github/), so nothing ineffective is ever built — directly answering the finding's core defect (the literal `<repo>/.cco/CODEOWNERS` is a no-op on GitHub per ADR-0020's own Facts table, lines 116-124). (3) It is the purest expression of P17 (cco assists, never gatekeeps): in v1 the assistance is guidance, with zero parallel-enforcer surface and zero footprint outside `.cco/`. (4) It keeps E's v1 scope minimal and unambiguous — E ships the S8 token-leak checklist plus the governance guide, and the helper is explicitly NOT scheduled — which closes the secondary defect the finding raises: an 'optional' §7 row silently becoming an 'unscheduled' phase item. Crucially, option 1 still PINS the deferred helper's full contract (host-recognized location with a `/.cco/** @team` rule, origin-URL host detection reusing lib/remote.sh, and the exact per-host instruction strings for GitHub/Gitea/GitLab/generic), so the deferral is well-specified and a future session will not re-litigate it. Options 2 and 3 are viable if the maintainer wants a v1 command: option 2 (full scaffold) delivers the most convenience but overrides the recorded preference, lands files outside `.cco/`, and adds low-frequency work to the v1 critical path (the YAGNI tension ADR-0020 D2 itself flagged); option 3 (print-only) is the cleanest build-option for P17 but amends D4's 'scaffold' verb and is largely redundant with the doc baseline. All three require the same two doc edits to remove the ambiguity: fix the CODEOWNERS location wording in ADR-0020 D4, and make the §7 status cell + §9/§12 phasing state the in/out decision explicitly. No option contradicts a settled, non-superseded decision; option 1 alone is a pure confirmation rather than a change.

options.title: F28 — Resolve `languages` config/internal classification vs the authored-only ~/.cco precondition

options.name: Option A — `languages` is the authored config preference (single source of truth); `language.md` is its GENERATED artifact → CACHE overlay, not committed
options.description: Decompose the resource per P10/P11 into (1) the user's language *choice* (the 3 values) and (2) the rendered rule file. The CHOICE is config: a small authored preference file `~/.cco/languages` (YAML/key-value), edited by the user in the IDE (or via a `cco config lang` setter — the CLI is merely the editor, the value is user knowledge, exactly the ADR-0014 D3 framing already accepted for coordinates). `language.md` becomes a GENERATED artifact governed by that choice: produced by substituting the template, materialized as a CACHE overlay into the global `.claude/` at init/start/sync (sibling to the F1 generated `.cco/managed/` and generated overlays already in CACHE per ADR-0016 D8/table). It is NOT committed in `~/.cco/global/.claude/rules/`. This makes `~/.cco` authored-only-true (only `languages` is committed; the rendered rule is regenerable), preserves DRY (one canonical preference, rendered on demand), and matches the existing `_regenerate_language_md` round-trip already in the code — just routed to CACHE instead of clobbering a committed file. The `_detect_languages_from_file` reverse-parse fallback is dropped (no committed artifact to reverse from; the authored `languages` file is now the only source). Removes `languages` from `.cco/meta` (already mandated by ADR-0013 D3/ADR-0016 D8 — meta is STATE).
pros: Honors P1 cleanly: the authored datum is the user's preference (a real config value, IDE-editable); the cco-written part (the rendered rule) is internal/regenerable, so nothing cco-written sits in the authored-only bucket
Keeps `~/.cco` authored-content-only — preserves the ADR-0007 clean-git-repo precondition and P6 truthful `git diff` exactly as D8 demands

DRY (P10/P11/ADR-0014 lens point 6 — separate canonical datum from its materialization, resolve-at-boundary): one preference, rendered on demand; eliminates the dual-storage (meta record + rendered file) smell
Directly reuses the existing `_regenerate_language_md` mechanism; the only change is its output target (CACHE overlay) and trigger points (init/start/sync) — minimal E cost

Consistent with the F1 / ADR-0016 D8 treatment of generated `.cco/managed/` and generated `.claude` overlays, which already go to CACHE — `language.md` joins the same regenerated-overlay class instead of being a special case

Resolves the ADR-0016 table mutator honestly: `languages` mutator = user (IDE) on the authored preference file; the rendered `language.md` is `cco` (overlay) — no longer a false 'user (IDE)' label on a do-not-edit record
cons: `language.md` stops being a hand-editable rule file: a user who wants finer-grained prose control over language rules loses direct edit (they edit the preference values, not the rendered Markdown) — a real UX narrowing vs today, where the deployed language.md is free prose

Requires wiring the overlay into the global-config materialization path (init + cco start + update --sync) so the rendered file is present where Claude Code loads rules; today it is simply committed in the global .claude tree
Slightly more machinery than just committing language.md (a generator + overlay placement) for what is a small file
If users have already hand-customized language.md beyond the 3 placeholders, the breaking cutover (ADR-0006) must preserve their intent — needs a migration note (parse-then-store, then regenerate)
options.principleAlignment: UPHOLDS P1 (authored preference = config; rendered artifact = internal), P6 + ADR-0016 D8 (~/.cco authored-only; regenerable artifacts → CACHE), P10/P11 + ADR-0014 D3 method (decompose datum from materialization; CLI-as-editor of a user-known value; resolve-at-boundary), DRY. Refines ADR-0013 D4 and ADR-0016 D8 (the 'config exception' is the *choice*, not the rendered file). No conflict with ADR-0007.

options.name: Option B — `languages` is hand-edited config; DROP the regenerator entirely and let `language.md` be authored prose
options.description: Take the finding's first horn literally: declare `language.md` the authored config (a normal rule file the user edits freely, as the deployed copy already is), and treat `languages` purely as an init-time scaffolding convenience. Concretely: `cco init` still prompts and substitutes placeholders into `language.md` ONCE (scaffold), but does NOT persist a `languages` record and does NOT regenerate on update. Remove `_read_languages`, `_regenerate_language_md`, the `languages:` block in `_generate_cco_meta`, and the `--sync` regeneration call (update.sh:121-123). `language.md` thereafter lives in `~/.cco/global/.claude/rules/` as ordinary authored config, synced like every other rule. This makes `~/.cco` authored-only because the only committed thing is the user-edited `language.md`; there is no cco-written `languages` datum at all.
pros: Maximally simple: deletes the dual-storage and the round-trip regenerator; `language.md` is just another rule file under the existing global-config sync — zero new buckets or overlays
Strictly authored-only: the committed file is genuinely user-edited prose; P6/D8 satisfied with no generator caveat
Preserves the user's freedom to write rich language rules (the current deployed language.md is full prose, not just 3 substituted tokens) — no UX narrowing

Aligns with the framework's 'all installed files are user-owned; framework never auto-modifies user files' rule (CLAUDE.md update-system section) — regeneration on --sync currently violates that spirit by clobbering a user-editable file
Smallest E surface and fewest moving parts (lean-direction, the same YAGNI logic ADR-0013 D6 used to remove pack-manifest)
cons: Loses the languages-as-structured-data capability: nothing else can read the 3 choices programmatically (e.g. a future config-editor agent, or surfacing 'your languages' in `cco` output) — they live only as prose inside Markdown

`cco update --sync` can no longer propagate template improvements to language.md wording (new sections, reworded guidance) keyed to the user's chosen languages — the user must re-merge by hand like any other opinionated rule (acceptable, but a capability regression vs today)

The init-time placeholder substitution remains a one-shot generator on an otherwise authored file — mild residual P1 fuzziness (cco writes it once, user owns it after), though no worse than any scaffolded template
If the maintainer values keeping language choice as machine-readable state (for tooling/telemetry/config-editor), this option forecloses it
options.principleAlignment: UPHOLDS P1 (authored prose = config), P6 + ADR-0016 D8 (~/.cco authored-only, no cco-written datum), and the CLAUDE.md user-owned-files invariant. STRAINS nothing structurally but DROPS the structured-preference capability ADR-0013 D4 implicitly assumed (regenerates language.md). Requires explicitly amending ADR-0013 D4 / ADR-0016 D8 to say `language.md` (not a `languages` field) is the config datum, and the `languages` row/field is removed.

options.name: Option C — `languages` is cco-written internal state → STATE (with the choice as input), and `language.md` is a generated overlay → CACHE
options.description: Take the finding's second horn: accept that, as coded, the stored `languages` record is cco-written and 'do-not-edit', therefore by P1 it is INTERNAL, not config. Reclassify it OUT of `~/.cco`: the persisted `languages` values live in STATE `/update` alongside the other meta-derived machinery (where ADR-0016 already puts `meta`), and `language.md` is a generated CACHE overlay (as in Option A). The user's *input* is captured only transiently at init (the prompt / `--lang`) and persisted as STATE; there is no config datum named `languages`. This keeps `~/.cco` authored-only by removing `languages` from the config bucket entirely, and treats the whole language pipeline (input → STATE record → rendered overlay) as cco-managed machinery, like the `template:` field or schema/policies.
pros: Most faithful to the literal code today: the stored record IS cco-written/'do-not-edit', so P1 says internal — this option just follows the edit-criterion mechanically
Removes the lone 'config exception' from ADR-0013 D4 / ADR-0016 D8 — `~/.cco` becomes uniformly authored-only with NO carve-out, simplifying the D8 precondition and the inventory note

Co-locates `languages` with `meta` (already STATE per ADR-0016 table) — keeps the meta-split coherent (everything machinery-ish in STATE, nothing straddling) and matches the existing storage site (inside .cco/meta) most closely, easing migration
language.md as CACHE overlay (shared with Option A) keeps the rendered artifact regenerable and out of the authored bucket
cons: Contradicts the *intent* behind ADR-0013 D4 / ADR-0016 D8, which deliberately called languages a genuine user PREFERENCE and the lone non-internal exception — this option overturns a settled (non-superseded) classification rather than aligning it; the maintainer chose 'config' precisely because language is a user preference, not machinery

STATE is `never`-synced (ADR-0013 D2 / ADR-0016 table): putting languages in STATE means the user's language choice does NOT travel across their PCs (Axis-1) — so on a second machine they would re-pick or fall back to English. That is a real loss of the multi-PC config experience the user reasonably expects for a preference

Treating a user preference as never-synced internal state is the weaker reading of P1: the value is genuinely user knowledge (per the ADR-0014 D3 framing) and SHOULD be portable — only the storage format is cco-written, which Option A handles better by separating choice (config) from record
Re-opens a settled decision; risks the very re-litigation the ADR process exists to prevent (memory-policy / documentation-first)
options.principleAlignment: UPHOLDS the literal P1 edit-criterion on the *current storage form* and P6/D8 (nothing cco-written in ~/.cco). But STRAINS P3/P4 (a portable user preference forced into never-sync STATE loses Axis-1) and CONTRADICTS the intent of ADR-0013 D4 / ADR-0016 D8 (languages = genuine user preference / config). Would require explicitly SUPERSEDING the D4 'config exception' verdict, not merely aligning it.
