options.recommended: Option A — `languages` is the authored config preference (single source of truth); `language.md` is its GENERATED artifact → CACHE overlay, not committed
options.rationale: Option A is the only one that resolves the contradiction by decomposition (P10/P11 + the ADR-0014 D3 'CLI is merely the editor; the value is user knowledge' framing) rather than by overturning a settled decision. The finding's dichotomy ('either languages is hand-edited or it is cco-written') is a false binary: the resource is TWO data with different natures — (1) the user's language CHOICE (a genuine, portable, IDE-editable preference = config) and (2) the rendered `language.md` (a cco-regenerated artifact = internal/CACHE). The code already round-trips this via `_regenerate_language_md`; Option A keeps that mechanism but routes the rendered output to a CACHE overlay (the same class as the F1 generated `.cco/managed/` and generated `.claude` overlays already in ADR-0016 D8/table), so the authored config bucket holds only the small authored `languages` preference. This (a) keeps `~/.cco` authored-content-only — the exact D8/ADR-0007 precondition — with no false 'user (IDE)' label on a do-not-edit record; (b) preserves Axis-1 portability of a real user preference (unlike Option C, which strands it in never-sync STATE); (c) upholds DRY and resolve-at-boundary (one canonical preference, rendered on demand); and (d) costs E only a retarget of an existing generator plus its trigger wiring, with a small migration to parse-then-store any pre-cutover hand edits. It REFINES ADR-0013 D4 / ADR-0016 D8 (the 'config exception' is the choice, not the rendered file) without superseding them. Option B is the lean fallback if the maintainer prefers free-prose language rules and is willing to drop structured-preference + template-propagation (it deletes the regenerator and makes language.md a plain authored rule) — recommend it only if those capabilities are judged unwanted. Option C is not recommended: it follows the literal current storage form but contradicts the deliberate D4 'languages = user preference' intent and breaks multi-PC sync of that preference, re-opening a settled classification. Maintainer decision needed on one point B vs A cannot decide alone: does cco need the language choice as machine-readable, multi-PC-portable data (→ A) or is free-prose, hand-merged language.md acceptable (→ B)? Files: ADR-0013 §D4 (decisions/0013-internal-metadata-split.md:106-111), ADR-0016 §D8 + authoritative table (decisions/0016-consolidated-resource-taxonomy.md:259, 288), guiding-principles.md P1/P6/P10/P11, design.md:203; code: lib/cmd-init.sh:37-78, lib/update-meta.sh:45-95, lib/update.sh:101-124, lib/update-merge.sh:259-286, defaults/global/.claude/rules/language.md.

options.title: Three pack resolution locations + url-presence discriminator: polymorphic in-repo pack location and local-wins shadowing of a stale authored pack
options.recommended: Option A — Document the invariant + worked-example resolution table; escalate ONLY the genuinely-ambiguous collision to ERROR

options.name: Option A — Document the invariant + worked-example resolution table; escalate ONLY the genuinely-ambiguous collision to ERROR
options.description: Keep the three-layer model and the coordinate-presence discriminator exactly as ADR-0019 D3/D5 decide them (no model change), but close the readiness gap on paper so E codes one resolver, not ad-hoc branches. Concretely: (1) State an explicit INVARIANT in ADR-0019/design §2.4: '<repo>/.cco/packs/X is a CACHE iff its referencing project.yml `packs:` entry carries a coordinate (url); it is an authored-in-repo SOURCE iff that entry has no url. The discriminator is the coordinate in the manifest entry, not a flag inside the pack files.' This is a precision fix: it relocates the discriminator from 'an in-file flag' (the finding's framing) to the manifest entry that already exists, so the location is read-disambiguated by data the resolver already holds, removing the SRP concern's sharpest edge. (2) Add a single worked-example TABLE enumerating all relevant 3-layer combinations (entry-has-url? × ~/.cco/packs/X present? × <repo>/.cco/packs/X present? × url-fetch reachable?) with the resolved winner and the validate verdict for each row — the canonical spec E implements as one resolver. (3) Refine the validate verdict: today D5 says validate 'flags' a name collision and mount picks ~/.cco. Keep WARN as the default (honors P14/P17 warn-not-block), but escalate to ERROR for exactly ONE row: a NO-coordinate authored-in-repo pack <repo>/.cco/packs/X that is ALSO present as ~/.cco/packs/X — because here mount-precedence silently runs a DIFFERENT pack than the one the project authored and committed, with no upstream relationship to reconcile (it is two unrelated sources colliding on a name, not a cache-vs-source). This is a genuine silent-wrong-build, distinct from the deliberate cache-degrades-gracefully case, so a hard validate error (exit non-zero) is principled, not a P14 violation.
pros: Zero model change: upholds every settled decision (D1/D3/D4/D5/D6, P12/P14/P15/P16). It only adds documentation precision + one narrow validate verdict, which V is explicitly chartered to do (impl-readiness on paper).

Removes the 'in-file flag' framing: the discriminator is the manifest `packs:` entry the resolver already reads, so there is no second read-time polymorphism source — the worked table makes this unambiguous for E.
The single resolution table is exactly proposedResolution (b): E codes ONE deterministic resolver from the table instead of ad-hoc branches — directly addresses the impl-readiness risk.

The escalation is surgical: ERROR fires only on the no-coordinate-source vs unrelated-global-pack collision (a true two-sources-one-name footgun), so the deliberate cache-last-layer graceful degradation (P14/P15/P16) keeps WARN.

validate is already a non-hook, exit-code-only share-readiness check (D2 layer c); making one row exit non-zero is within its existing contract, not a new gatekeeping path (so P17 is respected — validate is advisory tooling, not the push path).
cons: Introduces a second severity tier inside validate (warn vs error) — slightly more contract surface; must be specified crisply so E does not over-apply ERROR and accidentally hard-block the legitimate cache case (would strain P14).

Does not reduce the raw count of resolution locations (still three) — it documents and bounds the complexity rather than eliminating it; reviewers who see 'three layers' as inherent risk are only partially mollified.

Requires the maintainer to accept that 'silent wrong build on a same-name collision of two unrelated sources' is materially worse than 'graceful cache fallback', i.e. that the two collision shapes deserve different severities.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P12 (coordinate is config in the manifest — and clarifies it is THE discriminator), P14 (reachability layered, never hard-block — preserved for the cache case; the one ERROR is a name-collision-of-sources integrity error, not a reachability block), P15 (coordinate-presence = cache vs source discriminator restated verbatim), P16 (mount local-first vs update-from-remote axes unchanged), P17 (validate stays advisory tooling, not enforcement in the git push path — escalation is exit-code only). Strengthens G8/ADR-0016 D9 validity contract by typing the collision. No settled decision contradicted.

options.name: Option B — Documentation only (invariant statement + worked table), keep collision as WARN for all rows
options.description: The minimal, most conservative reading of proposedResolution (a)+(b) WITHOUT the ERROR escalation. State the cache-iff-coordinate invariant, add the all-combinations worked-example resolution table with resolved winner per row, and explicitly document the name-collision row's behavior (mount picks ~/.cco/packs/X local working copy; validate emits a WARN). No change to validate severity — every gap and collision remains a non-blocking warning, fully consistent with the existing D2/D5 text and the deliberate P14/P17 warn-not-block stance the finding itself calls out as 'accepted, hence medium'. The silent-shadow risk is mitigated purely by making the WARN louder/clearer (e.g. validate prints which pack wins and which is shadowed, and start/list badge it per D2 layer e), not by blocking.
pros: Lowest risk and most faithful to the letter of every settled decision — changes literally nothing in the decided behavior, only documents it and hands E one resolver table.
Strongest possible P14/P17 alignment: never blocks, treats the project author as responsible for resolving the warning in their IDE (the same posture as repo-url and unresolved-start).
Cheapest to implement and to validate; no new severity-tier contract; no risk of E over-applying a hard error.
Still fully delivers proposedResolution (a) invariant + (b) worked table — the two impl-readiness deliverables the finding asks for.
cons: Leaves the actual footgun the finding flags (a stale authored-in-repo pack silently shadowed by a same-named global pack, building the WRONG pack) as merely a warning a user can blow past — the finding's whole point is that this specific case is silent-wrong-build, not just a warning surface.
A warning in validate only helps users who RUN validate (it is opt-in / not hook-dependent by default); the silent shadow can still ship if no one runs it — the risk is documented, not contained.
Treats two materially different collision shapes (cache-of-upstream vs two-unrelated-sources-one-name) identically, missing the chance to type the genuinely dangerous one.
options.principleAlignment: Maximally upholds P14 (never hard-block) and P17 (validate purely advisory). Upholds P12/P15/P16 unchanged (documentation only). The cost is on the integrity side of the G8/ADR-0016 D9 validity contract: it does not strengthen the contract for the silent-wrong-build case, accepting the existing medium-severity footgun as a deliberate trade-off.

options.name: Option C — Move the discriminator out of the manifest into an explicit in-pack marker (pack.yml `origin:` / lock field) + document table
options.description: Address the 'polymorphic location disambiguated at read time' concern at its root by making each pack copy SELF-DESCRIBING about its role, instead of inferring source-vs-cache from the consuming project.yml entry. Concretely: a cache materialized at <repo>/.cco/packs/X carries an explicit marker (e.g. the pack's own `pack.yml` records `origin: <url>@<ref>` / a `.cco-cache` sentinel written at internalize-as-cache time), while an authored-in-repo pack has no such marker. Resolution and validate then read the marker on the FILESYSTEM copy rather than re-deriving role from the manifest coordinate. Also includes the Option-A worked-example table. This makes the location monomorphic-by-inspection (the copy declares its own role) and lets validate detect a mismatch (e.g. a copy marked cache whose manifest entry lost its url, or vice-versa).
pros: Directly attacks the SRP/polymorphism concern: the on-disk copy declares its own lifecycle role, so a reader does not infer it from a different file (the manifest).
Enables a stronger validate: it can cross-check the in-pack marker against the manifest coordinate and flag drift (cache-marker-but-no-url, or url-but-no-cache-marker) — richer integrity detection.

Aligns with existing provenance machinery: cco already records `source` provenance (DATA, ADR-0013/0015) and `.cco/source` for installs; an in-pack cache marker is the same shape, so it is reuse-friendly.
cons: Risks CONTRADICTING settled decisions. P15/ADR-0019 D3 fix the discriminator as 'the presence of a coordinate' (in the manifest) — introducing a second, on-disk discriminator creates exactly the dual-source-of-truth that ADR-0016 D3 / P12 worked to avoid (consistency by tooling, not by storage). Two discriminators can disagree, re-creating an internal-metadata leak the design already closed.

Authored-in-repo packs are CONFIG (P1, IDE-editable, versioned like <repo>/.cco/claude/) — writing a cco-managed marker file into a committed authored pack pollutes the truthful git diff (G8) and blurs the authored/internal boundary (P6) the whole refactor enforces.

Net-new schema + write path + migration with broader blast radius than the finding (medium) warrants; arguably re-opens a settled decision without a principle-level reason, which V is chartered NOT to do.
An internalized cache lives at <repo>/.cco/packs and would be committed; a marker file there is internal data committed into a config bucket — precisely the C4 leak ADR-0013 evicted.
options.principleAlignment: STRAINS P12 and ADR-0016 D3 (re-introduces a storage-based discriminator alongside the manifest coordinate → dual source of truth), STRAINS P6/G8 (writes cco-managed marker into an authored, committed config bucket), and partially RE-OPENS P15/ADR-0019 D3's settled 'coordinate-presence is THE discriminator'. Upholds P14/P16 (no behavioral change to mount/block). Recommended against on coherence grounds; listed to document why the seemingly-cleaner 'make the copy self-describing' route conflicts with the decided no-internal-data-in-config-buckets and consistency-by-tooling principles.

options.rationale: Option A is recommended because it is the only option that fully delivers both halves of the proposedResolution (invariant + worked table) AND contains the actual footgun, while staying strictly within settled decisions and V's read-only-on-paper charter. The finding's sharpest claim — 'one in-file flag with opposite lifecycle meaning' — is partly a documentation artifact: ADR-0019 D3 actually locates the discriminator in the project.yml `packs:` entry (coordinate present = cache, absent = source), which the resolver already reads. Option A makes that explicit, so E codes one deterministic resolver from a single combinations table rather than ad-hoc branches, which is exactly the impl-readiness deliverable. The one substantive move — escalating validate to ERROR for the narrow case of a no-coordinate authored-in-repo pack colliding by name with an unrelated ~/.cco/packs/X global pack — is principled, not a P14/P17 violation: that case is two unrelated sources colliding on a name (a genuine silent-wrong-build with nothing upstream to reconcile), categorically different from the cache-degrades-gracefully case that P14/P15/P16 deliberately keep as a non-blocking fallback. validate is already an exit-code-only advisory check (D2 layer c), so making one row exit non-zero stays advisory tooling, never the git push path (P17 intact). Option B is the safe fallback if the maintainer wants zero behavioral change and treats the silent-shadow as an accepted medium-severity trade-off — it still ships the invariant + table, just leaves the dangerous collision as a louder warning. Option C is documented but recommended against: making the on-disk copy self-describing re-introduces a storage-based discriminator alongside the manifest coordinate (contradicting P12 / ADR-0016 D3 'consistency by tooling not storage') and writes cco-managed markers into authored, committed config buckets (straining P6/G8 and re-creating the C4 internal-data-in-config leak ADR-0013 closed) — i.e. it re-opens a settled decision without a principle-level reason, which V must not do.

options.title: Disposition of the old maintainer-design subtrees (vault/, sharing/, resource-lifecycle/) at cutover: supersede-banner-as-history vs archive vs delete

options.name: Add an inventory 'maintainer-design history' subsection + in-place supersede banners at Phase-3 cutover (preserve as history)
options.description: Add a new item to resource-coherence-inventory.md 'Open items / sweep' section that explicitly scopes the ~15 sibling docs under docs/maintainer/configuration/{vault,sharing,resource-lifecycle}/ and assigns a disposition: at Phase-3 cutover, prepend a blockquote supersede banner to each file pointing to docs/maintainer/configuration/decentralized-config/ as the successor, then KEEP the files as design history (do not delete). Reuse the EXISTING repo convention verbatim — the same '> **STATUS: SUPERSEDED**' blockquote header already used in vault/design.md (§3), profile-isolation-design.md, and local-path-resolution-design.md. CRITICAL nuance the banner wording must capture: resource-lifecycle/ is only PARTIALLY superseded — its .cco/ directory-structure layout is replaced, but its file-policy (tracked/untracked/generated) and changelog dual-tracker concepts are partly carried forward and still referenced by the live .claude/rules/update-system.md, so its banner is 'PARTIALLY SUPERSEDED' (like vault/design.md), naming which sections survive. ALSO add a companion action to update configuration/README.md (the Document Map + Concept Ownership tables) so its 'canonical source' pointers for Vault profiles, Publish/install flow, and .cco/ layout redirect to decentralized-config/ — otherwise the index itself silently contradicts the new design. The banner edits ride the same Phase-3 documentation sweep as the rest of section C; the README/inventory edit can land Phase 0 (it is true the moment the new tree is source-of-truth). Maintainer confirms retire-vs-archive at the sweep.
pros: Matches the org documentation rule literally: superseded design docs are marked superseded and linked to the successor, not left contradicting (documentation.md ADR section).
Reuses an EXISTING in-repo convention (the PARTIALLY SUPERSEDED blockquote in vault/design.md) — zero new mechanism, instantly recognizable to future agents, consistent with documentation-first.

Preserves the reasoning trail: ADRs 0001-0020 frequently cite these docs (requirements.md, ADR-0001, design.md §456 all link back) — keeping them with banners keeps those back-references valid instead of producing dangling links.

Closes the doc-coherence gap the finding raises: a future agent re-discovering 'how does vault switch work' hits the banner first and is routed to the settled answer, preventing re-litigation (the whole point of the guiding-principles + ADR corpus).
Catches the second-order leak (configuration/README.md still names these as canonical) that the finding's own proposedResolution misses.
Phasing is honest: banners ride Phase 3 (when the model is actually removed), exactly like the rest of inventory section C — no half-migrated state.
Lowest-effort durable fix; ~15 one-line header inserts + one index edit, no file moves, no link rewrites across the repo.
cons: ~15 files keep living in the active configuration/ tree; a careless reader could still skim past a banner (mitigated: banner is the first line, blockquote-styled, matching existing precedent).
Two edits to confirm (banner wording per-file + README redirect) rather than one blanket action — slightly more sweep bookkeeping.

Requires per-file nuance for resource-lifecycle/ (PARTIALLY vs fully superseded) — a blanket 'whole subtree superseded' header would be WRONG there and would itself contradict the surviving update-system concepts.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds the org documentation.md ADR rule (mark superseded + link successor) and documentation-first (build on prior docs, note discrepancies, don't silently ignore). Consistent with P10 (classify by role not surface — the resource-lifecycle nuance respects that some concepts survive). Consistent with all settled decisions: ADR-0001 (vault retired), ADR-0018 (config-repo nomenclature retired), ADR-0012 (manifest removed), design.md §9 Phase-3 breaking cutover. No principle strained.

options.name: Move the subtrees into a docs/maintainer/configuration/_archive/ (or .../history/) directory at cutover, with a stub redirect
options.description: Instead of in-place banners, physically relocate docs/maintainer/configuration/{vault,sharing,resource-lifecycle}/ into an explicitly-named archive folder (e.g. configuration/_archive/<subtree>/) at Phase-3 cutover. Add the inventory item to record the move; update configuration/README.md to list an 'Archived (superseded by decentralized-config/)' section; leave a short stub or rely on the folder name to signal 'historical'. resource-lifecycle/ would need splitting (archive the .cco/-layout design, keep the still-live file-policy/changelog concepts) — or be archived whole with the surviving concepts re-homed into decentralized-config/ first.
pros: Strongest visual signal that these are not current: the directory name itself screams 'archive', so no reader mistakes them for live design.
Keeps the active configuration/ tree clean — only current designs (decentralized-config/, update-system/ live parts, scope-hierarchy/, etc.) remain at the top level.
Still preserves history (P-friendly): nothing is deleted, the reasoning trail survives.
Aligns with documentation.md 'periodically evaluate if docs need reorganization for better discovery'.
cons: Breaks every existing back-reference: requirements.md, ADR-0001, design.md §456, and the inventory all link to ../vault/... and ../sharing/... — a move turns those into dangling links unless ALL are rewritten (large, error-prone edit across the settled ADR corpus, which we are told not to contradict/destabilize).

resource-lifecycle/ cannot be cleanly archived: its file-policy + changelog concepts are still LIVE and referenced by .claude/rules/update-system.md and the update-system/ design — archiving the folder either orphans live concepts or forces a pre-move content migration (extra scope).
Heavier than the doc-coherence gap warrants: the finding is severity=medium and explicitly 'lower user-facing risk' — a repo-wide path refactor is disproportionate.
No existing precedent in this repo for an _archive/ tree — introduces a new convention where a perfectly good one (the supersede banner) already exists, against documentation-first/consistency.
options.principleAlignment: Honors the spirit of documentation.md (mark + relocate superseded material) but STRAINS it by breaking the 'link to the successor' chain in reverse (predecessor links from ADRs become dangling). Tension with P10/documentation-first because resource-lifecycle's live concepts resist a clean blanket move. Does not contradict ADR-0001/0018/0012, but the move-cost works against the 'build on prior work, don't destabilize settled decisions' posture.

options.name: Delete the subtrees at cutover; capture only a one-paragraph 'historical model' note in the decentralized-config tree
options.description: At Phase-3 breaking cutover, delete docs/maintainer/configuration/{vault,sharing,resource-lifecycle}/ outright (matching the code teardown in design.md §9 which deletes lib/cmd-vault.sh, manifest.sh, etc.). Replace the entire body of design history with a single short 'superseded model' note inside decentralized-config/ (or in the README) summarizing what existed and why it was replaced, plus the relevant ADR links. The .cco/-layout, file-policy, and changelog concepts from resource-lifecycle that survive would first be migrated into the live decentralized-config/update-system docs.
pros: Maximally clean: the contradiction cannot exist if the contradicting docs are gone — zero risk of a future agent reading stale design as current.
Symmetric with the code teardown (design.md §9 deletes the implementation files), so docs and code disappear together — conceptually tidy.
Smallest possible active doc surface going forward.
cons: Directly violates the finding's own guidance and the org documentation rule: superseded ADRs/designs should be MARKED and LINKED, not deleted — deletion destroys the reasoning trail that the ADR corpus exists to preserve ('ADRs are essential for agent continuity — they preserve the reasoning behind decisions').

Destroys back-reference targets: ADR-0001, requirements.md, design.md §456 cite these docs as the basis/predecessor of current decisions — deletion makes those citations unverifiable, weakening the settled-decision chain.

A one-paragraph summary cannot carry the depth (profile git-mechanics, FI-7 merge scenarios, file-classification matrix) that future re-discovery may need — irreversible information loss for medium-severity, low-user-risk gain.

Asymmetric with code: code teardown deletes IMPLEMENTATION (regenerable, version-controlled in git history); design docs are the RATIONALE, which the org rule deliberately keeps. Conflating the two is the bias the documentation rule guards against.
Requires the riskiest pre-work (migrating still-live resource-lifecycle concepts before deletion) with the least margin for error.
options.principleAlignment: CONTRADICTS the org documentation.md ADR rule ('mark as superseded, link to the new ADR' — not delete) and undermines documentation-first (prior decisions' rationale must remain consultable). Although it aligns superficially with design.md §9's deletion of code, it misapplies that to RATIONALE docs. Does not violate P1-P17 directly (these are about resource classification, not doc retention), but it is the option most at odds with the settled documentation conventions the sweep is meant to honor.

options.recommended: Add an inventory 'maintainer-design history' subsection + in-place supersede banners at Phase-3 cutover (preserve as history)
options.rationale: Recommend Option 1. It is the only option that literally satisfies the org documentation rule the finding invokes — mark superseded, link to the successor, keep as history — while reusing an EXISTING in-repo convention (the '> **STATUS: (PARTIALLY) SUPERSEDED**' blockquote already on vault/design.md, profile-isolation-design.md, local-path-resolution-design.md), so it introduces zero new mechanism and is instantly legible to future agents. It preserves the reasoning trail that ADRs 0001-0020 actively cite (requirements.md, ADR-0001, design.md §456 all link back into these subtrees), avoiding the dangling-reference damage that Options 2 and 3 cause to the settled ADR corpus. It is also the only option that catches a second-order leak the finding's own proposedResolution misses: configuration/README.md's Document Map and Concept Ownership tables still name these subtrees as the CANONICAL source for 'Vault profiles and sync', 'Publish/install flow', and '.cco/ layout' — that index must be redirected to decentralized-config/ or it silently contradicts the new design exactly as the body docs do. Two refinements over the raw proposedResolution: (1) the banner for resource-lifecycle/ must be 'PARTIALLY SUPERSEDED' (its file-policy and changelog-dual-tracker concepts are still live and referenced by .claude/rules/update-system.md), not a blanket whole-subtree supersede, which would itself be wrong; (2) the banner edits ride the Phase-3 documentation sweep (alongside inventory section C), while the inventory/README item itself can be recorded immediately since it is a checklist decision, not a code change. Maintainer confirms retire-vs-archive at the sweep, as the finding requests. Severity is medium and user-facing risk is low, so the heavier path refactor (Option 2) and the irreversible deletion (Option 3) are disproportionate and, in Option 3's case, at odds with the documentation rule itself.

options.title: §6.2 mislabels removed project publish/install files as merely "revised" and the §7 Sharing-row "transform" tag hides 6 net-new + 2 removed verbs; templates' 2×2 scope (ADR-0018 D2) conflicts with scaffold-only (ADR-0019 D7)
options.recommended: Option A — Verdict-faithful rewording: split §6.2/§7 by Reuse/Build-new/Drop and constrain templates to export/import (reconcile D7)

options.name: Option A — Verdict-faithful rewording: split §6.2/§7 by Reuse/Build-new/Drop and constrain templates to export/import (reconcile D7)
options.description:
Make design.md §6.2 and §7 mirror the per-element verdicts the ADRs already carry, and resolve the template scope tension by narrowing templates to export/import only.

(1) §6.2:511-513 — rewrite the implementation note as three verdict groups instead of a flat 'revised': REMOVED = cmd-project-publish.sh + cmd-project-install.sh (the project publish/install verbs are deleted, ADR-0018 D2; the files are removed or — if any non-publish logic is salvageable — reduced to feed cco project export/import); REFACTORED/FIXED = cmd-pack.sh (sync-before-publish defect, ADR-0019 D4), cmd-remote.sh / remote.sh (sharing-repo endpoints, structure-based discovery); DROPPED = lib/manifest.sh + manifest call-sites (ADR-0012/0018 D3). Also retire the bin/cco dispatch arms project publish/install (bin/cco:172,170) and the stale cco share/cco manifest arms (bin/cco:224-225).

(2) §7 — split the single Sharing 'transform' row into three by verdict, matching the ADR 'Reuse/Drop/Build-new' tables: Refactor existing verbs (pack publish/install, project internalize, pack internalize); Build-new (pack import; project export/import; project internalize wiring; plus cco update --check, sync-before-publish, structure-based discovery, 2×2 wiring, pack-coordinate schema+migration); Removed (project publish/install — breaking, no alias, joining the existing 'Removed (breaking, no alias)' note at §7:543).

(3) Templates — reconcile ADR-0018 D2 ('<res> ∈ {pack, template, project}' full matrix) with ADR-0019 D7 (templates scaffold-only, no live reference). Adopt D7 as authoritative: templates get export/import only (snapshot is coherent with scaffold-time consumption — a tar of a template is a frozen scaffold), and explicitly NOT publish/install (which imply a live-updatable source a scaffold-only resource cannot have). Add a one-line note in §6.2 and a footnote/parenthetical on the ADR-0018 D2 matrix clarifying that the publish↔install column is packs-only and templates ride the export↔import column. This also means template publish/install are NOT advertised in the 2×2 dispatch, removing the half-built risk.

(4) §11 Test Plan — add a sharing-surface phase (or extend the existing rows): assert dispatch presence for the kept/new verbs (pack import, project export/import, template export/import) AND absence for removed/never-built verbs (project publish/install rejected with a clear error; template publish/install rejected); import/export round-trip for pack/project/template; pack publish sync-before-publish 3-way merge (no fast-forward clobber); cco update --check; structure-based discovery; manifest call-site removal.
pros: Highest fidelity to already-settled decisions: §6.2/§7 become a faithful projection of the ADR Reuse/Drop/Build-new tables (ADR-0018 §'Reuse/Drop/Build-new', ADR-0019 same), eliminating the scope/estimation drift the finding flags.

Removes the concrete implementer hazards named in 'why': an implementer can no longer read 'revised' and preserve the project-publish path, nor ship 'import advertised but undispatched' — the matrix is split into built vs removed vs never-built.

Resolves the ADR-0018 D2 vs ADR-0019 D7 contradiction decisively and in the direction the deeper principle points (a scaffold-only resource has no live source, so publish/install are meaningless for it; export/import snapshots are coherent).

Keeps the documents purely descriptive of decided verdicts — no new design choice is invented, so it does not require re-opening any ADR's core decision, only a clarifying note on the D2 matrix's template column.

Test additions close the 'half-built' gap by asserting both presence (new verbs dispatched) and absence (removed verbs rejected), which is the cheapest guard against the regression the finding warns about.
cons: Touches three places (§6.2, §7, §11) plus an ADR-0018 D2 clarifying note — more edit surface than a one-line fix (acceptable: the finding itself asks for all three; this is a docs edit, deferred to E for the code).

Treating ADR-0018 D2 as needing a clarifying footnote means acknowledging the original matrix overstated template scope; a reader of D2 in isolation still sees '<res> ∈ {pack, template, project}' unless they read the footnote.

Requires a judgement call (export/import-only for templates) that, while strongly implied by D7, was not previously stated as such — best confirmed by the maintainer rather than asserted unilaterally.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P13 (sharing path follows the resource's role: project=unit-of-work rides the code remote → no publish/install; pack=reusable library → full 2×2; template=scaffold-only → snapshot-only) and P15/P16 (a scaffold-only template has no live upstream source, so publish/install — which presuppose a working-copy↔remote source-of-truth — correctly do not apply). Honors ADR-0018 D2 (projects get export/import only; publish/install are packs/templates) while correcting its overstated template scope via ADR-0019 D7. Honors ADR-0012/0018 D3 (manifest dropped). No principle is strained; the only tension (D2 'template' in the publish/install column vs D7 scaffold-only) is resolved in favor of the later, more specific ADR-0019 D7, consistent with the documentation rule that a more specific later decision refines an earlier general one.

options.name: Option B — Minimal correction: fix only the 'revised' label and the 'transform' tag; defer the template-scope reconciliation
options.description:
Address strictly the two mislabels the finding's title names, and leave the ADR-0018 D2 / ADR-0019 D7 template question to E as a flagged note.

(1) §6.2:511-513 — change 'is revised accordingly' to enumerate verdicts: cmd-project-publish.sh + cmd-project-install.sh are REMOVED (project publish/install deleted, ADR-0018 D2); cmd-pack.sh / cmd-remote.sh / remote.sh are revised; lib/manifest.sh dropped.

(2) §7 — relabel the Sharing row from a blanket 'transform' to 'transform + NEW + removed' and add a parenthetical listing the net-new verbs (pack import; project export/import) and the removals (project publish/install), without fully restructuring the row.

(3) Templates — do NOT decide the scope; instead add an explicit 'OPEN (→ E / maintainer)' note next to the §7 Sharing row and in §6.2 recording the ADR-0018 D2 vs ADR-0019 D7 tension, so it is visible but not prematurely resolved.

(4) §11 — add a single line noting import/export test cases are owed (without enumerating the full matrix).
pros: Smallest possible change; directly fixes the two labels the finding's title calls out and nothing more.
Avoids asserting a template-scope decision that arguably belongs to the maintainer, keeping the doc honest about an unresolved tension.
Low risk of introducing a new inconsistency because it touches the fewest lines.
cons: Leaves the ADR-0018 D2 vs ADR-0019 D7 contradiction live — exactly the 'sitting oddly' the finding flags — so an implementer in E still faces 'do templates get publish/install or not?' with no answer.

A flagged OPEN note in a design doc that is heading into an impl-readiness gate (V) re-introduces an ambiguity the review exists to eliminate; the finding's severity (medium, kind=decision) implies it should be resolved, not re-deferred.
Partial §7 relabel (parenthetical rather than a verdict split) still under-specifies the build scope; the 'half-built matrix' risk (import advertised, undispatched) is only weakly mitigated.
Thin §11 update leaves the regression guard (assert removed verbs are rejected) unspecified.
options.principleAlignment: Partially upholds P13 (it correctly records that projects do not publish/install) but does NOT resolve the template/role question P13 would settle (scaffold-only ≠ library, so publish/install should not apply). Consistent with ADR-0018 D2's projects-no-publish but leaves the D2-vs-D7 strain unaddressed, which conflicts with the documentation-first principle that contradictions between decisions must be reconciled, not silently carried. Neutral on P15/P16. Weaker alignment than A precisely because it stops at the surface labels and defers the substantive coherence fix.

options.name: Option C — Reconcile by giving templates the full 2×2 (publish/install + export/import) and revising ADR-0019 D7
options.description:
Resolve the tension in the opposite direction from A: keep ADR-0018 D2's literal '<res> ∈ {pack, template, project}' full matrix for templates, and amend ADR-0019 D7 so 'scaffold-only at consumption' coexists with a publish/install distribution channel for templates.

(1) §6.2/§7 — same removed/revised relabeling for project files as A, but the Sharing row's net-new set GROWS to include template publish AND install AND export AND import (4 net-new template verbs), plus pack import and project export/import — matching the finding's '6 net-new' count exactly.

(2) ADR-0019 D7 — add a clarifying decision: a template IS published/installed as a distributable artifact (its bytes live in a sharing repo and a working copy lands in ~/.cco/templates), but it remains scaffold-only at PROJECT-consumption time (no coordinate in project.yml/pack.yml, no template-update propagation into a scaffolded project). The publish/install live-source applies to the template library entry in ~/.cco/templates (like a pack working copy), not to scaffolded outputs.

(3) §11 — add the full matrix of import/export/publish/install test cases for pack, template, and project (where applicable).
pros: Maximizes surface symmetry: templates and packs become fully uniform in the 2×2 (publish/install + export/import), which is the simplest mental model for the user-facing matrix and matches ADR-0018 D2's literal text.
Matches the finding's stated arithmetic ('6 net-new verbs … template publish/install/export/import') without having to argue the count down.
Gives templates a live-updatable distribution channel, so a maintainer publishing an improved template can offer cco template update to library consumers.
cons: Strains P13/P15/P16: a publish/install live-source model presupposes a working-copy↔remote source-of-truth and update propagation — exactly what ADR-0019 D7 says a template does NOT have. Bolting publish/install onto a scaffold-only resource re-creates the 'self-contained copy as source' confusion P15 warns against.

Requires AMENDING an already-Accepted ADR (0019 D7) toward more surface, which the finding itself notes 'sits oddly' — i.e., it adds scope rather than removing a contradiction; this is the heavier, more speculative change.

ADR-0018 D1/D5 already routes opinionated template distribution through cco update --sync from the official sharing repo (F-opin), so a separate template publish/install channel is largely redundant with an existing decided mechanism — risks two overlapping update paths for templates.

Larger net-new build surface (4 template verbs) increases the very over-estimation/half-built risk the finding wants to contain, and contradicts ADR-0018's 'Build-new' list which does NOT enumerate template publish/install.
options.principleAlignment: Strains P13 (forces library-style sharing onto a scaffold-only role, blurring the role-based path the principle protects), P15 (publish/install imply a propagating live source; a scaffolded template has no such relationship — risks treating a vendored scaffold as a source), and P16 (working-copy↔remote source-of-truth does not cleanly map to scaffold-only consumption). It does honor ADR-0018 D2's literal '<res> includes template' text, but at the cost of reopening ADR-0019 D7 and overlapping with ADR-0018 D5/F-opin's already-decided opinionated-template update channel. Lower coherence with the settled principle set than A.

options.rationale: Option A is recommended because it makes the design docs a faithful projection of decisions the ADRs ALREADY carry, without inventing or reopening any core decision. The 'Reuse/Drop/Build-new' tables in ADR-0018 and ADR-0019 are explicit that project publish/install are dropped (cmd-project-publish.sh / cmd-project-install.sh implement removed verbs, ADR-0018 D2), that pack publish is a refactor/defect-fix, that lib/manifest.sh is dropped, and that pack import + project export/import + cco update --check are build-new — so §6.2's flat 'revised' and §7's blanket 'transform' simply under-report verdicts that exist one layer down. Splitting them removes the two concrete hazards in the finding's 'why' (an implementer preserving the project-publish path; a matrix shipping 'import advertised but undispatched'). On the genuine contradiction (ADR-0018 D2 lists templates in the full 2×2 vs ADR-0019 D7 making templates scaffold-only with no live reference), A resolves it in the direction the principles point: a scaffold-only resource has no live upstream, so publish/install — which presuppose a working-copy↔remote source-of-truth (P15/P16) — do not apply, while export/import snapshots remain coherent (a tar is a frozen scaffold). This is consistent with P13 (sharing path follows role: unit-of-work → code remote, library → full 2×2, scaffold-only → snapshot) and treats the later, more specific ADR-0019 D7 as refining the earlier general matrix, per the documentation-first reconciliation rule. Option C resolves the same tension by adding scope (full 2×2 for templates) — it matches D2's literal text and the finding's '6 net-new' count, but it strains P13/P15/P16, requires amending an Accepted ADR toward more surface, and overlaps with the already-decided F-opin/cco update --sync template channel (ADR-0018 D5). Option B is the minimal label fix but re-defers the substantive coherence question into the very impl-readiness gate (V) that exists to eliminate it, which is inappropriate for a medium-severity decision finding. Note: all options are READ-ONLY proposals — no files were edited; the §6.2/§7/§11 rewordings and the ADR-0018 D2 clarifying note are described for maintainer approval, and the code-side removals (bin/cco dispatch arms, file deletion) remain owned by E.

options.title: Carried items H6/M3/H7 are inconsistently phased across §9 phases, the "E" workstream, and "absorbed" claims

options.name: Option A (RECOMMENDED) — Distinguish the three facets explicitly, then anchor each impl item to a single phase; let V ratify
options.description: Treat the apparent 'triple placement' of H6 as three orthogonal facets that the docs conflate, and fix the conflation rather than just deleting duplicates. (1) Classification/decision facet: 'absorbed by M/R3' means the (bucket, sync-class) verdict for base/meta is SETTLED (ADR-0013 D5 / ADR-0016 D6: base/+meta -> STATE /update, never-sync). Keep this as a decision statement only. (2) Implementation-work facet: 'H6 -> E' means the merge-engine path-remap CODE belongs to the E follow-up workstream. (3) Cutover facet: §9 Phase 3 + §11 Phase 3 is WHEN in the cutover sequence the relocation physically lands and is tested. The fix is a one-line cross-walk stating that the 'E' workstream items are scheduled INTO specific §9 phases, not a separate timeline: H6 -> Phase 3 (co-located with the base/meta->STATE relocation it implements, where the legacy committed .cco/ is removed); M3 -> Phase 3 (cmd-remote.sh is rewritten there when the de-tokenized registry->DATA + token->STATE split happens, and this is declared a PREREQUISITE for the S/sharing S8 no-token-leak invariant); H7 -> Phase 0/1 (index atomicity+locking where the index is first built, with cross-project namespacing as a Phase-1 decision). Each surviving mention is annotated 'decision=ADR-00xx; impl=E workstream; lands in Phase N' so the three facets never read as three competing homes. Because the V impl-readiness review (§5 explicitly re-validates §9 phasing and every '-> E' item) is the NEXT gate, this option records the proposed phase map as the input V ratifies rather than hard-committing it before the cross-scope pass.
pros: Resolves the implementer-facing ambiguity (do H6 once, in Phase 3) without contradicting any settled ADR — the decision verdicts (ADR-0013/0015/0016) stay untouched; only the phase/workstream cross-walk is added.
Makes the M3 -> S8 dependency explicit (M3 in Phase 3 precedes the sharing phase that carries the ADR-0020 S8 no-token-leak invariant), closing the 'undeclared prerequisite' risk the finding raises.
Anchors H7 to Phase 0/1 where the index is actually built, so concurrency/namespacing is decided when the schema is created, not retro-fitted.
Honors V's mandate: §5 of the V-handoff already owns '§9 phasing re-validation' and §4 owns triaging '-> E' items as mechanism-vs-decision, so this feeds V instead of pre-empting it.
Preserves the useful 'E' grouping (impl-detail follow-ups best resolved against real code) while removing the false impression that E is a timeline parallel to Phase 0-3.
cons: Touches three living docs (design.md §9/§11, analysis-roadmap §M/§E, inventory) to add the cross-walk — more edit surface than a pure dedup.
Defers the final phase numbers to V ratification, so it is a 'proposed map' until V signs off (acceptable since V is the immediate next step and explicitly owns phasing).
Requires care that the §11 test-plan rows match the chosen phase (H6 test already in Phase 3; M3/H7 tests would need rows added).
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P10/P11 (classify by role: separates the classification verdict from the impl-scheduling, which are different questions). Honors AD12 (each phase leaves cco runnable+green) by co-locating H6 with the relocation it depends on. Respects the settled ADR-0013 D5 / ADR-0016 D6 (base/meta->STATE) and ADR-0015 (registry->DATA, token->STATE) without reopening them. M3-before-sharing directly serves ADR-0020 S8 + P6/P17 (de-tokenized registry is team-share-safe). Defers to the V gate per analysis-roadmap §V and V-handoff §4/§5.

options.name: Option B — Minimal dedup: single phase for H6, place M3 and H7, no facet model
options.description: Apply the finding's proposedResolution literally and minimally. Pick Phase 3 as H6's single home; in analysis-roadmap and inventory, replace 'H6 -> E' and the parenthetical 'H5/H6/M3 absorbed by M/R3' with a pointer to design.md §9 Phase 3 as the authoritative location for H6. Add M3 to Phase 3 (co-located with the cmd-remote.sh rewrite + registry->DATA / token->STATE split) and explicitly note it gates the sharing-phase S8 invariant. Add H7 to Phase 1 (index built) or label it a hardening follow-up. Do not introduce the decision/impl/cutover facet distinction — just make Phase 3 the one canonical mention and strip the others to cross-references.
pros: Smallest, most mechanical change — directly matches the finding's proposedResolution and is fast to apply.
Eliminates the literal triple-mention an implementer trips over (a single canonical Phase-3 home for H6).
Still surfaces the M3->S8 prerequisite and gives H7 a home (Phase 1 or hardening).
cons: Loses information: 'absorbed by M/R3' is a TRUE and useful statement (the classification verdict IS settled); flattening it to 'see Phase 3' discards the decision-vs-impl distinction and could read as if H6 were still an open design question.

Risks a new inconsistency with the 'E' workstream framing in ADR-0016 (D6/Reuse-Drop table) which routes H6/M3/H7 -> E; if E still lists them but §9 now 'owns' them, the two taxonomies still disagree unless E is also reconciled.
Does not resolve the deeper structural mismatch (E is a workstream, Phase 0-3 is a timeline) — the same ambiguity can resurface for other E items (M1/M2/M4/M5/H8).
options.principleAlignment: Satisfies the immediate intent of the finding and keeps ADR-0013/0015/0016/0020 verdicts intact. Weakly strains P10/P11 by collapsing the classification facet into the scheduling facet (the 'role' of the statement is lost). Neutral on AD12. Does not leverage the V gate as cleanly as A (it pre-commits phase numbers V is meant to ratify).

options.name: Option C — Defer entirely to V: tag the three placements as a known phasing inconsistency, no edits now
options.description: Make no content change to phase assignments. Instead, record F35 as a flagged input for the V impl-readiness review, whose §5 ('Phasing (§9) re-validation — the Phase 0-3 plan predates S; produce an updated phase map') and §4 ('for each -> E open item, mechanism vs hidden decision') already own exactly this question. Add F35 to the V-handoff scope list (alongside H7/M5 already named at line 48) so the cross-scope agents produce ONE authoritative phase map for ALL carried items (H2/H5/H6/H7/M1-M5/M3/H8) in a single pass, rather than fixing H6/M3/H7 in isolation now and risking divergence from the rest.
pros: Avoids a piecemeal fix that V would immediately revisit — V is the very next step and is chartered to rebuild the post-S phase map holistically.
Prevents introducing a new phase commitment that the broader S-era re-phasing (manifest deletion, sync-before-publish, pack coordinates) might contradict.
Lowest risk of creating fresh inconsistencies across the ~20-ADR body before the dedicated coherence gate runs.
cons: Leaves the documented triple-placement live until V completes — an implementer reading today still gets three answers for H6 and none for M3/H7.
M3's status as an S8 prerequisite stays implicit until V, a real correctness gap if any pre-V scaffolding touches cmd-remote.sh.
Under-uses cheap, safe, already-settled-direction edits (the H6 verdict and M3 split ARE decided; only the cross-walk is missing) — defers work that does not need a decision.
Risks F35 being treated as 'noted' and never actually closed if V's phase-map output is not fed back into design.md §9.
options.principleAlignment: Maximally respects the V gate (analysis-roadmap §V, V-handoff §4/§5) and the 'do not re-open settled decisions without a principle-level reason' constraint. Neutral on P10/P11. Mild tension with the documentation-first / coherence intent (a known inconsistency is left in the living docs deliberately) and with the AD12 expectation that each phase is implementable from the docs as written.

options.recommended: Option A (RECOMMENDED) — Distinguish the three facets explicitly, then anchor each impl item to a single phase; let V ratify
options.rationale: The finding's premise is slightly stronger than reality: H6's three mentions are not three competing homes but three FACETS the docs fail to distinguish — the classification verdict ('absorbed by M/R3', settled by ADR-0013 D5 / ADR-0016 D6), the implementation-work routing ('-> E'), and the cutover slot (§9/§11 Phase 3). Option A fixes the actual defect — the missing cross-walk between the 'E' workstream and the Phase 0-3 timeline — without contradicting any settled decision: it keeps every ADR verdict intact and only states that E items are SCHEDULED INTO phases (H6/M3 -> Phase 3, co-located with the relocations they implement; H7 -> Phase 0/1 where the index is built). This honors AD12 (H6 lands exactly where base/meta leave the committed .cco/, so the phase stays runnable), makes the M3 -> S8 (ADR-0020) prerequisite explicit per P6/P17, and respects P10/P11 by keeping decision-vs-scheduling as separate questions. Crucially, the V impl-readiness review is the immediate next gate and its §5 explicitly owns '§9 phasing re-validation' and §4 owns mechanism-vs-decision triage of '-> E' items — so A frames the phase map as V's ratification input rather than a unilateral pre-commit. Option B is the acceptable fallback if minimal edits are preferred, but it flattens the useful 'absorbed' verdict and leaves the E-vs-Phase taxonomy mismatch unresolved. Option C is safest-by-deferral but leaves a live documented inconsistency and an implicit S8 prerequisite until V, under-using edits whose direction is already settled. A best balances closing the implementer ambiguity now with deferring the final numbers to the gate chartered to set them.

options.title: Where the project.yml coordinate fields (repos/llms url/ref/variant) land in the phasing, reconciling §2.4 "coordinates from the start" with Phase-0 "logical names only, new layout only" and the inventory→S / §12→E drift

options.name: A — Phase 0 owns the full coordinate-bearing project.yml/pack.yml schema (repos/llms url+ref+variant); only the pack coordinate is added in the sharing phase
options.description: Make Phase 0 (machine-agnostic layout) the canonical home for the entire repos[]/llms[] coordinate schema that §2.4 already documents: each repos[] entry carries optional url + optional ref, each llms[] entry carries mandatory url + optional variant, embedded per-unit (the package.json / ADR-0016 D2 model). Phase 0's 'new layout only, no dual-read' clause is amended to read 'new layout INCLUDING the embedded repos/llms coordinate fields' so the very first committed project.yml is already the final schema. The migration writer (cco migrate / cco init / cco join, Phase 2) populates those fields at write time: llms url comes from the legacy central llms source registry, repo url is derived from git remote get-url origin (ADR-0017 D1), ref defaults to the remote default branch. The ONLY coordinate work left to the sharing phase (E, ex-S) is the pack-entry coordinate (packs[].url/ref) and the resolve-at-publish/structure-discovery wiring — which is genuinely sharing-specific (a pack is a referenced library, P13). Update inventory:188 from 'llms:/repos: schema/migration → S' to 'repos/llms coordinate schema → Phase 0 (machine-agnostic layout); pack coordinate + resolve-at-publish → E'. Update §12/§6.2 'schema + migration → E' to scope the E-owned 'schema' to the PACK coordinate + Domain-B realignment, not the repos/llms coordinate.
pros: Eliminates the AD12 contradiction at its root: Phase 0 ships the final project.yml schema, so no later phase schema-migrates a Phase-0-written file — exactly the 'new layout only, no dual-read' guarantee.
Matches §2.4's already-written intent ('the manifest carries coordinates from the start', 'package.json model', 'closing the repo auto-resolve gap that ADR-0014 left open') without weakening it.

repos/llms coordinates are needed even WITHOUT team-sharing (Axis-1 auto-resolve, cco resolve clone-from-url, llms re-fetch) — by the P11(a) scope test they belong to the local+Axis-1 config/layout track (Phase 0/1), NOT the sharing track (S/E). This corrects the inventory's mis-routing.

The migration writer already needs origin-derivation logic (ADR-0017 D1 reuses _sanitize_project_paths) and the legacy llms source registry is available at migrate time — so populating coordinates in Phase 2 is cheap and lossless.

Cleanly separates the two genuinely different schema concerns: repos/llms coordinate (local-relevant, Phase 0) vs pack coordinate + resolve-at-publish (sharing-relevant, E) — each lands where its role lives (P10/P13).
Test Plan §11 Phase 0 ('machine-agnostic layout + index tests, new layout only') naturally absorbs coordinate-field round-trip tests; no new phase needed.
cons: Phase 0's scope grows: it must now generate AND populate coordinate fields (origin derivation, llms-source read at migrate), increasing Phase-0/Phase-2 coupling slightly.

Requires touching three documents to reconcile (§2.4 wording is fine; Phase-0 bullet, inventory:188, §12/§6.2 must be re-scoped) — a documentation edit fan-out, though it removes the contradiction permanently.

Pack coordinate is split off to E, so the 'uniform schema' (ADR-0016 D2: project.yml and pack.yml share one format) is delivered in two phases — must be stated explicitly so the schema is understood as additive-by-section, not schema-breaking (the packs[] section is additive, AD/update-system 'new optional section').
options.principleAlignment: Upholds AD12/ADR-0006 (no dual-read — the decisive constraint), §2.4 + ADR-0016 D2 (embedded coordinate is core schema), ADR-0017 D1 (origin derivation, optional repo url, mandatory llms url), P11(a) (scope test routes local-relevant coordinates off the sharing track), P12/P10/P13 (coordinate role: repos/llms are local+Axis-1 referenced resources; only the pack coordinate is sharing-bound). Strains nothing settled; it CORRECTS the mis-routing in inventory:188 (which the finding flags as the drift).

options.name: B — Phase 0 ships logical-names-only project.yml; coordinate fields are an explicit additive step (Phase 1, alongside cco resolve)
options.description: Keep Phase 0 strictly to the machine-agnostic layout + index + path helpers it already lists (logical names, no coordinates), and make the embedded repos/llms coordinate fields an EXPLICITLY-SCHEDULED additive step in Phase 1 — the phase that already introduces cco resolve (incl. clone-from-remote), which is the first consumer of the url coordinate. Frame the coordinate fields as an ADDITIVE optional section (per the update-system rule: new optional config field → code handles missing fields with defaults), so a Phase-0 project.yml without coordinates is forward-compatible and Phase 1 adds them without a schema-breaking migration — i.e. it is NOT a dual-read, it is an additive extension that the same parser reads. Pack coordinate + resolve-at-publish remain in E. Reconcile inventory:188 and §12/§6.2 to point repos/llms coordinate → Phase 1, pack coordinate → E.
pros: Keeps Phase 0 minimal and focused (pure layout/index/path-helper boundary), matching its current charter and the 'each phase leaves cco runnable + tests green' rule.
Co-locates the coordinate fields with their first runtime consumer (cco resolve clone-from-url lands in Phase 1, design.md:626-632) — schema and the code that reads it ship together.

Avoids AD12 violation by framing coordinates as an ADDITIVE section (optional fields, code-level defaults) rather than a schema migration — additive ≠ dual-read, so no deprecation window is introduced.
Resolves the doc drift by giving the schema a definite phase home (Phase 1) distinct from the pack/sharing work (E).
cons: Tension with §2.4's explicit framing that the manifest carries coordinates 'from the start' and is 'the per-unit source of truth' — a Phase-0 project.yml without coordinates is momentarily NOT the full source of truth, which reads as a soft contradiction with §2.4 unless §2.4 is also softened.

The migration writer (cco migrate, Phase 2) runs AFTER Phase 1 in the phase order but writes project.yml — so coordinates must be populated by the Phase-2 writer regardless; splitting 'schema defined in Phase 1' from 'schema populated in Phase 2' is slightly awkward and risks a Phase-1 project.yml that legitimately lacks coordinates until Phase 2 backfills, blurring the 'additive vs migration' line.

Relies on 'additive section' framing holding for a field §2.4 calls core; if a reviewer reads the coordinate as load-bearing (not optional) the additive defense weakens and it looks like the very schema-migration AD12 forbids.
Two-phase delivery of the 'uniform schema' (repos/llms in Phase 1, packs in E) — same uniform-schema-split caveat as Option A but with the repos/llms half later.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds AD12/ADR-0006 ONLY via the additive-section framing (no dual-read because the parser is forward-compatible). Aligns with P11(a) (coordinates are local+Axis-1, not sharing → off the S/E track) and pairs schema with its consumer (cco resolve). Strains §2.4 + ADR-0016 D2's 'coordinate is core / from the start' wording (needs softening to 'additive'), and creates a Phase-1-defines / Phase-2-populates seam that is more fragile than Option A's single Phase-0 home.

options.name: C — Keep repos/llms coordinate schema in the (now-resolved) sharing track and explicitly defer it to E, with Phase 0 writing a coordinate-ready-but-empty project.yml
options.description: Honour the inventory:188 'schema/migration → S' routing as-written (now subsumed by E per §12/§6.2 F3-resolved): Phase 0 writes a project.yml whose schema is COORDINATE-READY (the parser tolerates url/ref/variant keys) but Phase 0 itself populates only logical names; all actual coordinate population, the llms-source→manifest migration, repo origin-derivation, AND the pack coordinate land together in E (the sharing/Domain-B realignment phase). This keeps a single phase owning all coordinate work (repos, llms, packs) under one roof. Reconcile by deleting the §2.4 implication that Phase 0's project.yml is fully coordinate-bearing and stating §2.4 describes the END-STATE schema delivered in E.
pros: One phase (E) owns ALL coordinate work — repos, llms, AND packs — under the uniform-schema banner (ADR-0016 D2), avoiding the two-phase schema split of Options A/B.
Literally honours the documents that say 'schema + migration → E' (§12:764, §6.2) and the inventory's '→ S' (S now folded into E), so the least re-routing of the routing statements themselves.
Phase 0 stays purely structural (layout/index), the cleanest possible Phase-0 boundary.
cons: Directly contradicts §2.4 and ADR-0016 D2 / ADR-0017 D1, which make the repos/llms coordinate CORE machine-agnostic config 'carried from the start' and decided PRE-S — deferring it to E demotes a settled pre-S decision into the sharing track it explicitly does NOT belong to (P11(a): repos/llms coordinates are needed without team-sharing). This is the mis-routing the finding identifies, not a fix for it.

Breaks AD12 in practice: a freshly-migrated machine on Phases 0-2 (pre-E) would run with logical-names-only project.yml and NO auto-resolve url — cco resolve (Phase 1) and llms re-fetch would have no coordinate to use, so Phases 1-3 would ship a degraded cco, and E would have to schema-migrate every project.yml written in Phases 0-2 (the exact dual-read/migration AD12 forbids).

Couples local-only functionality (Axis-1 auto-resolve, llms content fetch) to the sharing phase, violating the P11(a) scope separation and the P13 role distinction (referenced-resource coordinate ≠ sharing mechanism).
Requires WEAKENING §2.4/ADR-0016 D2 (settled, non-superseded) — contradicting the 'never contradict settled decisions' constraint.
options.principleAlignment: STRAINS the most: contradicts §2.4 + ADR-0016 D2 + ADR-0017 D1 (coordinate is core, pre-S, carried-from-start) and violates AD12/ADR-0006 (forces a later schema-migration of Phase-0/2-written project.yml). Mis-applies P11(a)/P13 by binding local+Axis-1 coordinates to the sharing track. Listed only to document the literal 'follow the →E/→S routing' reading and show why it is the wrong reconciliation.

options.recommended: A — Phase 0 owns the full coordinate-bearing project.yml/pack.yml schema (repos/llms url+ref+variant); only the pack coordinate is added in the sharing phase
options.rationale: The decisive constraint is AD12/ADR-0006 (breaking cutover, no dual-read, each phase runnable): Phase 0 writes the canonical committed project.yml, and §2.4 + ADR-0016 D2 + ADR-0017 D1 (all settled, pre-S, non-superseded) make the embedded repos/llms coordinate a CORE part of that schema. If Phase 0 omits it, a later phase must schema-migrate every Phase-0-written project.yml — the precise violation AD12 forbids. So the schema must land in Phase 0. The drift in inventory:188 ('→ S') and the §12/§6.2 '→ E' attribution are reconcilable once you apply the P11(a) scope test: repos/llms coordinates are needed WITHOUT team-sharing (Axis-1 auto-resolve, cco resolve clone-from-url in Phase 1, llms re-fetch), so they belong to the local+layout track (Phase 0/1), NOT the sharing track; only the PACK coordinate + resolve-at-publish + structure-discovery are genuinely sharing-bound and correctly stay in E. Option A therefore both removes the contradiction and corrects the mis-routing, while Option B introduces a fragile Phase-1-defines/Phase-2-populates seam and softens §2.4, and Option C actively contradicts §2.4/ADR-0016 D2/AD12. Recommended reconciliation edits (for E/doc-sweep, not done here): amend the Phase-0 bullet (design.md:607-609) to 'new layout INCLUDING embedded repos/llms coordinate fields'; re-scope inventory:188 to 'repos/llms coordinate → Phase 0; pack coordinate + resolve-at-publish → E'; re-scope §12:764 / §6.2 'schema + migration → E' to mean the PACK coordinate + Domain-B realignment only; note the packs[] section as an additive Phase-E section of the same uniform schema (ADR-0016 D2) so 'uniform' is understood as additive-by-section, not delivered-in-one-phase. This is a maintainer decision because it re-routes work across phases and lightly re-words a settled section (§2.4) — it should be confirmed before the E implementation wires it.

options.title: Pack name-only→coordinate migration: define an idempotent "embed-at-migrate" backfill (best-effort from DATA source, never fabricate) + add pack/template migration scope dirs

options.name: Option A (RECOMMENDED) — Conservative embed-at-migrate: bare list → structured map, backfill url ONLY from local DATA source, leave absent otherwise (defer to heal layer)
options.description: Add the missing migrations/pack/ and migrations/template/ scope dirs (currently only global/ and project/ exist) so CLAUDE.md/update-system.md's declared scopes are real. Author a single project-scope migration (next id = migrations/project/013_...) that converts each bare `packs: - name` list item into the structured map form `{name: <name>}`. For each entry, the migrator reads the INSTALLED pack's machine-local provenance at `~/.cco/packs/<name>/.cco/source` (yaml `source:`/`ref:`/`path:` keys): (1) if `source` is a real url (not `local`/empty) → backfill `url` (+ `ref` from source `ref`, `resource` from source `path` when present); (2) if `source` is `local` → leave url absent (intentional authored-in-repo source, P15); (3) if the pack is NOT installed on this machine (no `~/.cco/packs/<name>` dir) → leave url absent and rely on the layered heal-at-resolve / warn-at-validate model (P14 layers b/c/e) to backfill later when the pack is in hand. The migration NEVER fabricates a url it cannot read from a recorded source. It is idempotent: a `packs:` entry already in map form (has `name:`) is skipped. The discriminator is preserved exactly: url-presence = cache of upstream; url-absence = authored-here. Document in the ADR-0019 §Open→E hand-off and the new sharing/Phase-3 migration note that absence-after-migrate is the SAFE default that the heal/validate layers complete — migration is one (best-effort) layer among five, not the sole gate.
pros: Exactly matches the finding's proposedResolution and is uniform with embed-at-add (layer a of the P14 table) — same derivation source (.cco/source) the CLI already uses for `cco pack update`.
Never severs DRY/update (P15): a url is written only when a real upstream source is recorded; the authored-in-repo case correctly stays bare.
Never fabricates a wrong coordinate (the precise failure the finding warns about): unknown source → absent, never guessed.

Graceful under the migration's structural reality — migrations run per-project but the source provenance lives in machine-local DATA (~/.cco/packs); when DATA is absent the migrator degrades to absence and the heal layer (P14 b/c/e) finishes the job when the pack is resolved. No hard dependency on DATA being present.
Idempotent and minimal: pure schema-shape transform (list→map) for the common case; backfill is opportunistic. Reuses existing yml_get + .cco/source machinery (Reuse verdict in ADR-0019).
Closes the concrete gaps the finding lists: missing scope dirs created; bare list items become coordinate-ready; derivation rule specified.
cons: On a machine where the pack is not installed, the migration leaves url absent even for packs that DO have an upstream — the coordinate is only completed later at resolve/validate. (Mitigated: this is exactly the P14 layered design; absence is surfaced by validate, never silently shared.)

Per-project migration cannot see cross-project consistency; two projects referencing the same upstream pack may get backfilled on different machines/times. (Acceptable per P12: cross-unit consistency is by tooling — `cco config coords` — not storage.)

Requires the migrator to YAML-rewrite a user-owned file (project.yml) that may have comments/formatting; needs careful awk (the existing _project_yml_add_pack awk handles the list shape and is a reuse base, but list→map needs new logic) — implementation care, not a design risk.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P14 (migration = one best-effort layer; absence completed by heal/validate, never hard-block), P15 (url written only from a recorded upstream; authored-in-repo stays bare = source), P12 (coordinate is config embedded per-unit; DRY scoped-to-unit; cross-unit consistency by tooling not storage), P1/P6 (project.yml stays user-authored config; no internal data injected). Consistent with ADR-0019 D1/D2/D3, ADR-0017 D1 (warn-not-enforce, derivation), ADR-0016 D2 (uniform schema). Honors CLAUDE.md/update-system.md migration idempotency + sequential-id rules.

options.name: Option B — Pure schema-shape migration only (list→map, NO backfill); coordinate acquisition entirely delegated to the runtime heal/validate layers
options.description: Same as A for the missing scope dirs and the idempotent list→map transform, but the migration does ZERO coordinate backfill: it never reads ~/.cco/packs/<name>/.cco/source. Every migrated `packs:` entry becomes `{name: <name>}` with url absent. All coordinate population happens at runtime via embed-at-add (for new adds), heal-at-resolve (`cco resolve` backfills from the in-hand pack's source/origin), and is surfaced by `cco config validate`. The migration's sole job is to make project.yml schema-forward-compatible so the heal layers have a structured slot to write into.
pros: Maximally safe and trivially idempotent — the migration touches only structure, never derives a value, so it can never write a wrong url under any machine state.
Cleanest separation of concerns: migration = schema shape; heal/validate = coordinate semantics. Single derivation code path (heal-at-resolve) instead of two (migrate + heal).
No dependency on DATA presence at migration time — works identically on any machine.
Smallest, lowest-risk migration script.
cons: Loses the easy win: a pack with a known, locally-recorded upstream source could have been backfilled immediately but instead shows as unresolved until the user runs `cco resolve`/`validate` — extra friction and a window where shared projects look broken.

Diverges from embed-at-add: add embeds a coordinate, but migrate would not, so a freshly-added pack and a migrated-legacy pack differ for no semantic reason. The finding explicitly asks for uniformity with embed-at-add ('embed-at-migrate' layer).
Relies on the user actually running a heal-triggering command before sharing; until then validate-only awareness — weaker continuity than A.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P14 (heal/validate carry the load), P15 (never fabricates a source), P1/P6. Slightly STRAINS the finding's intent and ADR-0019 D2 layer-(a) symmetry (embed-at-add ↔ embed-at-migrate) by omitting the migrate-time embed that the layered model would naturally include. Consistent with ADR-0017 warn-not-enforce and ADR-0016 D2 schema.

options.name: Option C — Aggressive embed-at-migrate with cross-project source index + heal-at-migrate prompt for ambiguous/missing cases
options.description: Builds on A but maximizes backfill: in addition to reading each pack's ~/.cco/packs/<name>/.cco/source, the migration first builds an in-memory index of ALL locally installed packs and their sources, so a pack referenced by a project but whose name resolves to an installed upstream is backfilled even if provenance lookup needs name-matching. For entries with NO recoverable source, the migration emits an interactive (or --yes-skippable) heal prompt mirroring the unresolved-start prompt (ADR-0017 D2): (i) specify a sharing-repo url, (ii) leave bare as authored-in-repo, (iii) defer. The migration thus tries to fully resolve coordinates at migration time rather than deferring to a later `cco resolve`.
pros: Highest immediate completeness — most projects come out of migration fully coordinate-populated, minimizing the post-migration unresolved window.
Single user touchpoint: the user resolves ambiguity once, during the migration they already invoked, rather than later at resolve/validate.
cons: Migrations must be non-interactive and idempotent by the framework contract (CLAUDE.md / update-system.md: `migrate()` receives a target dir, runs unattended under `cco update`, must return 0 and be re-runnable). An interactive heal prompt inside a migration violates this contract and breaks `--all`/CI runs.

Cross-project source indexing inside a per-project migration overreaches the migration's scope (it gets one target_dir) and risks name-collision mis-binding — fabricating a url for a same-named-but-different pack, the exact P15-severing error the finding warns against.
Duplicates the heal-at-resolve logic the layered model already owns (ADR-0019 D2 layer b), creating two code paths for the same decision — higher maintenance and divergence risk.
Heavier, riskier migration for marginal benefit over A (whose heal layer already completes the unresolved tail).
options.principleAlignment: Intends to serve continuity but STRAINS P14 (collapses the layered, runtime heal into the migration — turning a best-effort layer into a quasi-gate with prompts) and risks VIOLATING P15 (name-matched cross-project backfill can fabricate a wrong upstream). CONFLICTS with the framework's idempotent/non-interactive migration contract (CLAUDE.md, update-system.md). Partially consistent with ADR-0017 D2 prompt UX but in the wrong execution context (resolve, not migrate).

options.recommended: Option A (RECOMMENDED) — Conservative embed-at-migrate: bare list → structured map, backfill url ONLY from local DATA source, leave absent otherwise (defer to heal layer)
options.rationale: Option A is the precise resolution the finding proposes and it is fully consistent with the settled decisions. It reads the coordinate ONLY from the machine-readable provenance the code already maintains (~/.cco/packs/<name>/.cco/source — `source`/`ref`/`path`, with `local` meaning authored), so it never fabricates a coordinate and never reclassifies an authored-in-repo pack as a cache — the exact DRY/update-propagation severing (P15) the finding flags. It honors the url-presence discriminator of ADR-0019 D3 exactly (real source → url; `local`/absent/uninstalled → bare). It is uniform with embed-at-add (the P14 layer-a symmetry the finding asks for as 'embed-at-migrate') while correctly treating migration as just one best-effort layer: when DATA is absent the migrator degrades to bare and the runtime heal-at-resolve / warn-at-validate layers (P14 b/c/e) complete the coordinate — so it imposes NO hard dependency on DATA being present and never hard-blocks. It satisfies the framework's idempotent, non-interactive migration contract (unlike C), and it delivers the immediate-backfill win that B forgoes. The two concrete on-disk gaps the finding lists are closed: create migrations/pack/ and migrations/template/ scope dirs (matching the scopes already declared in CLAUDE.md and .claude/rules/update-system.md), and ship the list→map project migration with the specified derivation rule, documented in the ADR-0019 §Open→E hand-off and the Phase-3 sharing/migration note. Severity is correctly medium: the discriminator semantics are already defined (ADR-0019 D3) and the obvious, code-grounded rule resolves it — this is a Phase-3 (E) implementation item, not a design contradiction.

options.title: cco resolve --scan index-rebuild semantics: merge vs clobber and scan-time name-conflict policy

options.name: Merge-upsert + AD5-refusal on conflict (scoped, non-destructive)
options.description: Specify in design.md §3 that `cco resolve --scan <dir>` is an UPSERT into the existing index, never a wholesale replace. Concretely: (1) it walks `<dir>` for `.cco/project.yml` and, for each discovered logical name, inserts or updates only that name->path entry plus the project's `repos[]` membership; (2) it NEVER deletes index entries whose path lies outside `<dir>` (out-of-scan-dir mappings) nor entries created by `cco path set` (machine-local overrides are preserved as siblings, mirroring §3:323-324 'cco path set retained as a sibling'); (3) on a scan-time conflict — a discovered name already bound to a DIFFERENT absolute path — it applies the existing AD5 invariant (§3:323-324): warn + keep the existing binding by default, and (interactive) offer to update it; never silently overwrite. Bootstrap (empty index, fresh machine) is the trivial degenerate case of upsert: every discovered entry is a pure insert, so a fresh clone is correctly populated with no special-casing. This is exactly the finding's proposedResolution, made precise about WHICH entries are protected (out-of-dir + path-set) and WHICH conflict default applies (keep-existing + warn/prompt).
pros: Eliminates the silent-data-loss risk that motivated the finding: cco path set overrides and out-of-scan-dir mappings survive a re-scan on an existing machine.
Carries over the 'absorbed' cco index refresh merge behavior the finding flags as dropped, so the consolidation onto cco resolve --scan (ADR-0017 D2) loses no semantics.
Reuses the already-settled AD5 uniqueness/refusal invariant verbatim instead of inventing a new conflict rule — minimal new surface for E, one consistent rule across init/join/scan.

Bootstrap (fresh machine) and re-scan (existing machine) are the SAME code path, just with an empty vs non-empty starting index — simplest possible implementation, matches §3:334-337 'fresh clone not stranded'.
Keeps the index the single machine-local source of truth (P2 STATE / P6) without ever letting a scan mutate it destructively.
cons: A stale entry whose .cco/project.yml was deleted or moved out of <dir> is NOT pruned by --scan (it persists until cco path set/manual edit) — re-scan can accumulate orphan name->path mappings.

Interactive conflict prompting adds a branch to the resolver (warn/keep vs update), and non-interactive/CI invocation must pick a default (keep-existing) that a user expecting 'rebuild = adopt newest' might find surprising.
The word 'rebuild' in ADR-0017 D2 / §3 stays slightly misleading (it is really 'reconcile/upsert'); the prose must be reworded to avoid implying a clobber.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P6 (index is internal STATE, mutated only via cco, never destructively losing machine-local state), P2 (index = machine-local non-portable STATE; merge keeps it truthful), P12 (index is the local-path materialization of the repo coordinate — preserving per-PC overrides is exactly the 'machine-specific, local-only' contract). Directly applies the settled AD5 invariant (design.md §3:323-324) and ADR-0002 (machine-agnostic config + index). Consistent with ADR-0017 D2 (resolve consolidation) and ADR-0003 sync-as-copy spirit (no opaque reconciliation; the only 'reconciliation' is an explicit, surfaced conflict prompt). Strains nothing; mild tension only with the literal word 'rebuild'.

options.name: Merge-upsert + explicit opt-in prune flag (--prune / --replace)
options.description: Same non-destructive upsert + AD5-refusal default as Option A, but additionally define an explicit, opt-in destructive mode for the genuine 'rebuild from scratch' use case. Default `cco resolve --scan <dir>` = merge-upsert (Option A semantics). An explicit `cco resolve --scan <dir> --prune` (or `--replace`) additionally removes index entries whose path is UNDER <dir> but no longer has a discoverable .cco/project.yml (handles moved/deleted projects within the scanned root). The destructive scope is still bounded to <dir>: entries outside <dir> and cco path set overrides are never touched even with --prune; only stale in-<dir> mappings are pruned. AD5 conflict policy (warn + keep/prompt) applies in both modes.
