pros: Gives users a clean way to garbage-collect stale in-<dir> entries (the orphan-accumulation con of Option A) without ever exposing a footgun by default.

Default behavior is identical to Option A — same safety guarantees, same silent-loss elimination; the destructive path is conscious and surfaced (consistent with ADR-0017 D2's 'conscious user choice' philosophy for the unresolved-start prompt).
Bounded prune (only paths under <dir>) means even the destructive mode cannot wipe out-of-scan-dir or path-set entries — the data-loss class in the finding stays closed in every mode.
cons: Adds a new flag to the CLI surface, which ADR-0017 D2 was explicitly CONSOLIDATING — risks re-introducing the 'extra verb/flag to learn' the ADR rejected (it dropped index refresh --scan precisely to shrink surface).
v1 has no demonstrated need for prune (the finding only asks for safe merge); a flag with no concrete v1 use case is speculative scope, against the design's documentation-first/minimal-surface bias.
Two modes mean two test matrices and a subtle distinction (prune is in-dir-only) that users may misread as 'prune = wipe everything'.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds the same P6/P2/P12/AD5 alignment as Option A in its default mode. The opt-in --prune mirrors ADR-0017 D2's 'conscious, surfaced choice' design for unresolved-start. Strains the ADR-0017 D2 CLI-consolidation goal (adds surface the ADR was reducing) and the design's minimal-v1-scope principle (P9-adjacent: ship the smallest correct thing; defer extras to futures). Best treated as a future enabler, not v1.

options.name: Per-name idempotent re-scan with sticky path-set protection (no whole-index concept)
options.description: Reframe --scan so the 'index' is never treated as a single rebuildable blob at all: define the operation purely as 'for each .cco/project.yml found under <dir>, idempotently ensure its name->path and repos[] are present and current'. There is explicitly NO notion of 'the rest of the index' being in or out of scope — entries simply are what they are, and --scan only ever touches the names it physically discovers. cco path set entries carry a sticky 'manual' marker (machine-local STATE flag) so the resolver knows never to auto-update them on scan; a scan that rediscovers a manual-marked name leaves it untouched and warns. This makes merge-vs-clobber a non-question by construction (there is no clobber operation in the model).
pros: Strongest possible guarantee against the finding's data-loss class: 'clobber' is not expressible in the model, so no future flag/regression can reintroduce it.
The 'manual' sticky marker gives a principled, durable distinction between scan-derived and user-set entries — cleaner than relying on path-locality (Option A/B) to infer intent.
Fully idempotent and order-independent; trivially correct on both fresh and existing machines.
cons: Introduces a NEW per-entry STATE field (the 'manual'/'scanned' provenance marker) into the index schema — more impl + a migration concern for the index format, beyond what the finding asks for.
Storing provenance/intent inside the index edges toward the kind of internal-metadata complexity ADR-0013 worked to keep minimal and keyed-by-identity; risks scope creep into the index.
Still leaves stale entries un-pruned (no GC story), and the conflict UX (warn-and-skip on manual-marked) is a third behavior to specify vs Option A's single AD5 rule.
More divergence from the AD5 'refuse a name already bound to a different path' wording, which is phrased around init/join, not around a provenance marker.
options.principleAlignment: Strongly upholds P6 (internal STATE never destructively mutated) and P2. But it adds index provenance metadata, creating mild tension with ADR-0013's discipline (internal-metadata kept minimal, centralized, keyed-by-identity) and with ADR-0017 D2's intent to REUSE the existing resolver rather than grow the index schema. Consistent with P12 but heavier than the coordinate model requires. AD5 is honored in spirit but re-expressed, slightly diverging from the settled wording.

options.recommended: Merge-upsert + AD5-refusal on conflict (scoped, non-destructive)
options.rationale: Option A is the recommended resolution. It is exactly the finding's proposedResolution, sharpened to name precisely which entries are protected (out-of-<dir> mappings + cco path set overrides, kept as siblings per design.md §3:323-324) and which conflict default applies (AD5 refusal: warn + keep-existing, optionally prompt to update). It closes the medium-severity silent-data-loss gap with the smallest possible change: it REUSES the already-settled AD5 uniqueness invariant (§3:323-324) as the scan-time conflict policy instead of inventing a new rule, and makes bootstrap (empty index) the trivial degenerate case of the same upsert path — so fresh-machine and existing-machine flows share one code path. This respects ADR-0017 D2's CLI-consolidation goal (no new flags/verbs), upholds P6/P2/P12 (the index is internal STATE that is the machine-local materialization of the repo coordinate and must never be destructively rebuilt), and contradicts no settled decision. The only follow-up E should note is rewording 'rebuild' to 'reconcile/upsert' in ADR-0017 D2 and §3 to avoid implying a clobber. Option B (opt-in --prune) is a sound FUTURE enabler for stale-entry GC but adds CLI surface the ADR was deliberately shrinking and has no v1 need — defer it, not adopt it now. Option C (provenance markers) over-engineers the index schema against ADR-0013's minimalism and ADR-0017's reuse intent. Note: there is currently NO index/scan-rebuild code in lib/ — the index and cco resolve --scan are new design — so E builds this from the decision directly; there is no legacy merge behavior to reconcile against, only the prose claim that index refresh's merge semantics carry over, which Option A makes explicit. This is a maintainer decision because the conflict-default UX (keep-existing vs adopt-newest, prompt vs warn-only in non-interactive/CI contexts) affects how the toolkit is used and is not derivable from code alone (P10 method-lesson b).

options.title: sync-meta last-synced fingerprint: pin the minimum write/clear/divergence contract (format stays deferred)

options.name: Option A (RECOMMENDED) — Pin the minimal per-repo semantic contract; leave only hash algorithm + rollback richness as impl detail
options.description: Specify in design.md §4.6 + FR-Y-S6 the load-bearing contract, NOT the byte format. (1) Fingerprint domain = a hash over the EXACT synced set already defined in §4.1 (project.yml + claude/** + secrets.env.example; never secrets.env, repo-root .claude/, system dirs) — anchoring the fingerprint to the same set sync copies guarantees one definition feeds both write and compare. (2) WRITE points: cco writes/updates a repo's fingerprint (a) after that repo SUCCESSFULLY RECEIVES a sync (target side), and (b) for the SOURCE repo at the same sync (its current synced-set hash) — so immediately after `cco sync` every touched repo's fingerprint == its on-disk synced-set hash. (3) DIVERGENCE / 'edited-locally-since-last-sync' is a per-repo, lazily-evaluated read-time predicate: `current synced-set hash(repo) != stored fingerprint(repo)` ⇒ this repo was hand-edited since cco last touched it; equal (or no fingerprint at all) ⇒ pristine/received. (4) CLEAR/RECOMPUTE is implicit and lazy: there is no eager clear; staleness is detected at read time by recomputing the hash and comparing. A repo with NO stored fingerprint (code-only Case A, fresh machine, never-synced) is treated as 'unknown/pristine', never as divergent. (5) Keep the inter-repo 'do A and B differ' verdict as the existing §4.3 truthful content diff (source↔target) — the fingerprint is NOT redefined to compute that; it strictly answers the per-repo 'changed since cco touched it' question that §4.3's diff cannot (a diff can't tell which side the dev edited). Document that the Case-B-vs-C branch in `cco sync`/`cco join` and the §4.4 notice consume BOTH signals: §4.3 content-diff = 'are they different now', fingerprint = 'who moved'. Truly deferred: the hash algorithm/encoding, file byte layout, and rollback-snapshot richness (ADR-0003 Open).
pros: Removes exactly the ambiguity the finding names: two engineers now compute the same divergence verdict because the hash domain (§4.1 set), the write points (post-receive + source-side), and the compare predicate (lazy hash != stored) are all fixed.

Lazy read-time recompute needs no event hooks, no eager clear-on-edit watcher — consistent with P7 (sync transports, no per-command machinery) and the existing §4.6 'lightweight bookkeeping, not a sync-base' framing; nothing new to maintain.

Anchoring the fingerprint to the §4.1 synced set keeps the metadata truthful even when unrelated files (secrets.env, repo .claude/, generated overlays) change — those are correctly invisible to divergence, matching what sync actually copies.

Keeps the fingerprint's job (dev-edit vs received-sync) cleanly distinct from the §4.3 content diff (A-vs-B), so the H1 reminder aggregator and the §4.4 notice get one stable, well-typed answer from each source instead of overloading one mechanism.

Strictly additive to settled decisions: ADR-0003 #6, FR-Y-S6, §4.6 all already say this metadata exists and what it drives — Option A only writes down the previously-implicit write/clear/compare rule. No ADR is reopened; format stays deferred exactly as ADR-0003 Open / FR-Y-S6 promised.

'No fingerprint = pristine, never divergent' makes fresh-machine and code-only (Case A) members safe by construction, honoring H1 (notices computed only after resolution, against possibly-empty state).
cons: Recompute-at-read-time hashes the synced set on each divergence check; for large claude/** trees this is a few file reads per repo per `cco start`/`cco sync` (negligible at cco's scale, but it is real work vs a cached value).
Specifying the contract in both design.md §4.6 and requirements.md FR-Y-S6 means two doc touch-points must stay in sync (mitigated: FR-Y-S6 can reference §4.6 as the normative source).
Still leaves the hash algorithm unspecified — intentional, but a pedantic reader may want it named; the contract is algorithm-agnostic precisely so any stable content hash works.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P7 (no fabricated state, no per-command network/event machinery — purely local lazy bookkeeping), P6 (sync-meta stays internal STATE, never committed, never team), P10/P11 (classify by role: the datum's role = 'who moved since cco touched this repo', a per-machine `never`-sync STATE datum, exactly as P11(b) classifies it). Consistent with ADR-0003 #6 (sync-state tracking in scope; format deferred), ADR-0007/0016 (sync-meta in STATE, sync-class `never`), ADR-0008 (reminder aggregator consumes divergence as one facet), H1 (notices after resolution). Does NOT strain any principle; it makes the FR-Y-S6/§4.6 promise precise without changing classification or format.

options.name: Option B — Derive divergence purely from the §4.3 content diff; drop the per-repo fingerprint as a divergence input
options.description: Take the position that the fingerprint is redundant for the divergence VERDICT: §4.3 already computes a truthful content diff between any source and target. Define 'divergent project' = any two member repos' synced sets differ by that diff; the Case-B-vs-C branch and the §4.4 notice key off the pairwise diff alone. Retain sync-meta ONLY as a sync-set MEMBERSHIP record (which repos carry a copy vs code-only) plus the optional rollback snapshot — and explicitly DROP the 'edited-locally vs received-a-sync' distinction as a v1 input to selection, treating it as future (it would only matter for an auto-'who is newer' heuristic, which ADR-0003 deliberately rejected).
pros: Simplest possible model: one source of truth for divergence (the content diff), zero hashing, zero write/clear lifecycle to specify — eliminates the ambiguity by deleting the ambiguous mechanism.
Aligns with ADR-0003's spirit of rejecting timestamp/'newest-wins' heuristics: if cco never auto-picks a source, 'who edited last' arguably never needs to be known.
No fresh-machine edge cases for the fingerprint (none exists); membership record is trivial and already needed.
cons: Directly contradicts settled wording: ADR-0003 #6, FR-Y-S6, and §4.6 ALL explicitly require distinguishing a repo 'edited locally since the last sync' from one that 'merely received a sync'. Dropping it is reopening a settled decision — outside V's mandate (V must not reopen settled decisions without a principle-level reason).

Loses real UX signal: the §4.4 notice and reminder aggregator can no longer say 'repo X has unsynced local edits' — only 'repos differ', which is exactly the weaker message the design chose to improve on.

The diff alone cannot tell the user WHICH repo moved, so the `--from`/source-pick prompt loses the hint that makes Case C actionable; the maintainer's design intent (knowing dev-local edits) is discarded.
options.principleAlignment: Strains the documentation-first rule and V's charter by contradicting ADR-0003 #6 / FR-Y-S6 / §4.6 (a settled, non-superseded decision) without a principle-level justification. It does not violate P-level principles per se, but it removes a deliberately-scoped capability rather than disambiguating it — the finding asked to DEFINE the semantic, not delete it.

options.name: Option C — Pin the contract AND elevate it to an ADR-0003 amendment (or a small new ADR) for cross-session durability
options.description: Same normative content as Option A (synced-set hash domain, write-on-receive + source-side, lazy read-time compare predicate, no-fingerprint=pristine, fingerprint distinct from §4.3 diff), but additionally record it as an explicit amendment to ADR-0003 (extend decision #6) and/or a short dedicated ADR, then propagate to design.md §4.6 and FR-Y-S6. The difference from A is governance weight: the divergence semantic becomes a first-class recorded decision rather than living only in the design/requirements prose.
pros: Maximal durability: the load-bearing semantic gets an ADR home, so a future session can't re-ambiguate it; matches the project's ADR practice (decisions recorded for agent continuity).
Makes explicit that this refines — not contradicts — ADR-0003 #6 (its 'format deferred' Open clause), with a clean superseded/refined link.
Same technical guarantees as Option A for implementation determinism.
cons: Heavier process for what is arguably a clarification of an existing decision, not a new one — ADR-0003 #6 + FR-Y-S6 already SAY the metadata exists and what it drives; an ADR may be ceremony where a design.md/requirements edit suffices.
Adds another ADR (0021) to an already large chain (0001–0020) that V is trying to consolidate, not grow; risks the very ADR-sprawl V flagged.
V is a read-only gate; spinning a new ADR is an E-phase / decision-phase action, so this defers resolution rather than closing it inline as a doc edit.
options.principleAlignment: Same principle alignment as Option A (P7/P6/P10/P11, ADR-0003/0007/0016/0008, H1). Additionally honors the documentation/ADR convention (record significant decisions, refine-don't-silently-change). Mild tension with V's 'consolidate, don't grow the ADR set' intent and with the proportionality of effort (a clarification vs a new decision).

options.recommended: Option A (RECOMMENDED) — Pin the minimal per-repo semantic contract; leave only hash algorithm + rollback richness as impl detail
options.rationale: The finding correctly separates the deferred-and-fine part (byte format, hash algorithm, rollback richness — ADR-0003 Open / FR-Y-S6) from the load-bearing-and-under-defined part (the write/clear/compare semantic that drives Case-B-vs-C selection and the §4.4 notice). Option A closes exactly the gap the finding names — when the fingerprint is written (post-receive + source-side at sync), what 'divergent' means (lazy current-synced-set hash != stored fingerprint, over the §4.1 set), and the no-fingerprint=pristine rule — so two implementations converge on the same divergence verdict, while keeping format genuinely deferred. It is strictly additive: ADR-0003 #6, FR-Y-S6, and design §4.6 already state the metadata exists and what it drives, so writing the implicit rule down reopens nothing. It also preserves the clean division of labor between the §4.3 content diff (are A and B different now) and the fingerprint (which repo moved since cco touched it) — overloading one for the other is what produced the ambiguity. It needs no new event hooks or eager clear (lazy read-time recompute), staying within P7's no-machinery posture and §4.6's 'lightweight bookkeeping, not a sync-base' framing. Option B is rejected because it contradicts the settled requirement (ADR-0003 #6 / FR-Y-S6 / §4.6) without a principle-level reason — outside V's charter. Option C is the same content as A but adds ADR ceremony for what is a clarification of an existing decision; it can be a fallback if the maintainer wants the semantic recorded at ADR weight, but a design.md §4.6 + FR-Y-S6 edit is the proportionate close. Maintainer decision needed only to confirm the write-points (post-receive + source-side) and the no-fingerprint=pristine rule, since these touch how `cco sync`/`cco join`/`cco start` behave for the user (P10 method-lesson (b): UX-affecting choices need maintainer confirmation).

options.title: cco update --check: specify three-bucket spanning contract, output format, ref-vs-HEAD advancement rule, and graceful degradation on a freshly-synced second PC

options.name: Option A — DATA-source-driven, install-presence-gated check with a 3-state output contract (RECOMMENDED)
options.description:
Make the DATA `source` files the canonical iteration set, but gate each row on local install presence (STATE/CACHE), so the check reports one of three states per resource and degrades gracefully on a freshly-synced PC.

Concretely specify in §7 / ADR-0018 D5:

(1) ITERATION SET = DATA. `cco update --check` enumerates `<data>/cco/{projects,packs,templates}/<id>/source` (the synced, `required` provenance — ADR-0016 D5). This is the only bucket guaranteed to exist on a second PC after a private-multi-PC sync, so it is the correct driver. DATA `source` carries only upstream coordinates (`url`, `ref`, optional `resource`) — no local baseline.

(2) PER-RESOURCE STATE. For each `source` entry, the local baseline = the *installed commit*. Today `_check_remote_update` reads `commit` from the `source` file itself; under the split the recorded installed commit must live where the install lives. Resolve this by storing the installed commit in STATE `/update` meta (keyed by identity, `never`-sync) alongside `base/` — the install's own machine-local record — NOT in DATA (so it never travels and never lies on a second PC). The check then classifies:
  - NOT INSTALLED HERE — DATA `source` exists but STATE has no installed-commit / `base/` for that id (the freshly-synced-PC case). Report `not installed on this machine` (advisory, exit 0). This is the natural degradation the finding calls for.
  - INSTALLED, comparable — STATE installed commit present → run the existing `_check_remote_update` logic against the advertised ref (see advancement rule below), using CACHE `remote_cache` (HEAD+ts) when fresh, else a lightweight `git ls-remote` refresh.
  - INSTALLED, indeterminate — installed but no recorded commit / unreachable remote → report `unknown` / `unreachable` (the states `_check_remote_update` already emits).

(3) ADVANCEMENT RULE (ref-vs-HEAD). Compute the *available* commit by resolving the SAME ref the `source` pins, via `git ls-remote <url> <ref>` (the existing call): pinned ref (tag/branch/commit in `source.ref`) → resolve that ref's current remote commit; UNPINNED (`ref` empty) → resolve `HEAD`. `advanced` ≡ available-commit != installed-commit. A pinned immutable tag (v1.0) resolves to a fixed commit → never reports advancement even if the default branch moves (correct). A pinned *branch* that moves DOES report advancement (the user pinned a moving target — correct). This makes 'advanced' well-defined for the contract test.

(4) OUTPUT CONTRACT. One line per resource, stable machine-greppable shape:
  `<kind> <name>: <state>` where state is one of `update available (<short-installed> → <short-available>)`, `up to date`, `not installed here`, `unknown`, `unreachable (offline)`.
  Followed by a summary line: `N updatable, M up-to-date, K not installed here, …`. Exit 0 always (read-only discovery, never a gate — ADR-0008 reminder philosophy). `--check` performs NO merge and writes nothing except refreshing CACHE `remote_cache` (regenerable).

(5) FLAG INTERACTION (reuse cmd-update.sh conventions verbatim). `--offline` → skip all `ls-remote`; classify solely from CACHE `remote_cache` if fresh, else emit `unknown (offline, no cache)` (mirrors existing `offline_mode`). `--no-cache` → force a fresh `ls-remote` ignoring `remote_cache` (existing `cache_mode=force`). `--offline --no-cache` together → `--offline` wins (can't fetch and skip simultaneously; no network ⇒ no fresh check), documented explicitly.
pros: Directly resolves the finding: DATA is the only co-location-independent driver, so the check works on a freshly-synced second PC instead of erroring or silently reporting 'no updates'.
'Not installed here' is the natural, correct degradation — exactly what the finding's proposedResolution asks for — and is computed from a real signal (STATE install presence), not a guess.

Reuses the existing `_check_remote_update` state machine (`update_available|up_to_date|unreachable|unknown`), `remote_cache`, `ls-remote`, and the `--offline`/`--no-cache` plumbing already in cmd-update.sh — minimal net-new build, honoring ADR-0018 D5 'reuse R3 machinery' (now made true by gating, not by assuming co-location).
Ref-vs-HEAD rule is unambiguous and testable: immutable tag pins never false-positive; moving branches correctly do; a contract test can pin both cases.
Stable per-resource line + summary gives E a contract test target and keeps it greppable/scriptable; exit-0-always keeps it advisory (never a gate).

Keeps the installed-commit baseline in STATE (machine-local, never-sync) — it is version/install-tied, so it must not travel; this also keeps DATA `source` a pure upstream coordinate (no machine leak), exactly as ADR-0016 D5 specifies.
cons: Requires pinning down WHERE the installed commit is recorded post-split (STATE `/update` meta keyed by identity). Today `_check_remote_update` reads `commit` from the `source` file; this must move to STATE so it stays machine-local — a small but real refactor for E (analogous to the H6 base/ relocation).

On a freshly-synced PC the check is honestly limited: it can say 'not installed here' and (with network) 'an update exists upstream' but cannot diff against a non-existent local baseline — the user must install before --check is fully meaningful. This is correct but may surprise users expecting a full diff.

Three-bucket reads (DATA iterate + STATE presence + CACHE freshness) make the data-flow more explicit than the old single-file read — slightly more code paths to test (mitigated by the explicit state table).
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P2/P6 (DATA=synced provenance, STATE=never-sync install baseline, CACHE=regenerable remote_cache — each datum read from its correct bucket; nothing internal leaks into config). Upholds P3/P4 (respects the orthogonal destination×sync split that the finding says the bucket split 'breaks' — the gate is precisely what makes the split work). Upholds P8 (installed-commit stays in STATE `/update`, never swept into a future state-sync). Upholds ADR-0008 reminder philosophy (read-only, exit 0, never blocks). Realizes ADR-0018 D5 / design §6.2:506 / §7:531 by making 'reuse R3 machinery' actually hold across the bucket boundary. Consistent with ADR-0013 D3 / ADR-0016 D5 (`source` carries only upstream coords; baseline is local). Honors ADR-0019 D5 mount-vs-update separation (the check is purely the update/source-of-truth axis; it never touches what is mounted). Strains nothing settled; it *operationalizes* ADR-0016's split rather than contradicting it.

options.name: Option B — Live-clone-on-demand check (ignore CACHE baseline; compare DATA source ↔ current installed working copy)
options.description: Drop reliance on the recorded installed-commit and `remote_cache` entirely for `--check`. For each DATA `source`, locate the live installed working copy (`~/.cco/packs/<name>` for packs; the project's resolved repos via the STATE index for projects; CACHE `llms/` content for llms) and compute the installed commit live (`git -C <wc> rev-parse <ref>`), then `git ls-remote` the upstream and diff. On a freshly-synced PC with no working copy, emit 'not installed here'. CACHE `remote_cache` is updated as a side effect but is not the source of the baseline.
pros: Eliminates the 'where is the installed commit recorded' question — it is always derived live from the working copy, so no STATE meta field needs to move and there is no risk of a stale recorded commit.
Always-accurate: no cache-staleness window; the baseline is whatever is actually on disk right now.
Still degrades correctly on a freshly-synced PC (no working copy ⇒ 'not installed here'), satisfying the finding.
Ref-vs-HEAD rule (Option A item 3) applies identically and remains testable.
cons: Defeats the explicit purpose of `remote_cache` (CACHE), which ADR-0016 D7 / R3 created specifically to 'avoid network on update checks' — a live `ls-remote` per resource on every `--check` is exactly what the cache exists to prevent; this strains the settled CACHE design.

For projects, deriving 'the installed commit' is ambiguous: a project references multiple repos/packs/llms — there is no single working copy to rev-parse; the model has to fan out per referenced resource, complicating the 'one line per resource' contract the finding wants.
More network/IO per invocation; slower and offline-fragile unless `--offline` falls back to... nothing (no cached baseline to read), making `--offline` far less useful than in Option A.
Larger behavioral departure from the existing `_check_remote_update` (which is cache-first) — more to rewrite and re-test, contradicting ADR-0018 D5's 'read-only front-end over existing machinery'.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P6 (reads each datum from its bucket) and the graceful-degradation goal, and keeps the ref-vs-HEAD correctness. But it strains the CACHE `remote_cache` rationale (ADR-0016 D7 / ADR-0013 D3 — cache exists to avoid network) and weakens `--offline` (P-consistent flag behavior). It also fits the project↔referenced-resource fan-out less cleanly than Option A. Does not contradict any ADR outright, but it under-uses settled machinery the ADRs deliberately built.

options.name: Option C — Scope --check to the unambiguous case only (packs/templates), defer projects; minimal contract
options.description: Acknowledge that 'a project advanced' is ill-defined (a project has no single upstream — it is a bundle of referenced coordinates) and restrict v1 `cco update --check` to packs and templates, which DO have a single sharing-repo `source`. Specify the same 3-state output (update available / up to date / not installed here), the same ref-vs-HEAD rule, and the same `--offline`/`--no-cache` semantics as Option A, but explicitly mark projects as out-of-scope for `--check` (they ride the code-repo remote and are updated by plain `git pull`, not `cco update`). Reserve a future `--check` extension for project-referenced-resource reachability (which P14/ADR-0019 already covers via `validate`).
pros: Removes the genuine ambiguity the finding implies for projects: a project's `<repo>/.cco` is team-shared by construction and updated by `git pull` on the code repo (P5/P13) — it has no sharing-repo `source` to 'advance' against, so 'project update available' is conceptually murky.
Smallest, cleanest contract: packs/templates have exactly one upstream `source`, so the per-resource line and ref-vs-HEAD rule are unambiguous and trivially testable.
Aligns with ADR-0018 D2 (projects are NOT published/installed) — only packs/templates have the publish/install live-source lifecycle that `--check` is meaningful for.
Still fully resolves the second-PC degradation for the in-scope resources via the same DATA-driven + install-presence gate as Option A.
cons: Narrows the feature: ADR-0018 D5 explicitly says `--check` lists 'installed resources (packs/templates/PROJECTS)' — dropping projects is a (small) reduction of the stated scope and needs a maintainer's blessing to align the ADR text.

A project CAN carry a `source` today (imported from a tar, ADR-0018 D2 — `cco project import` records a source if the tar carries one), so there is a legitimate, if narrow, 'project has an updatable upstream' case that this option would exclude or punt.
Leaves the project-update-discovery question half-answered (defers to `validate`/`git pull` story), which may resurface.
options.principleAlignment: Strongly aligned with P5/P13 and ADR-0018 D2 (projects ride the code remote, no publish/install boundary — so they are not the natural subject of an install-update check). Upholds P2/P6/P8 identically to Option A for the in-scope resources. The tension is with ADR-0018 D5's literal wording (which includes projects) and the imported-project-with-source edge (ADR-0018 D2) — so it requires a deliberate ADR-text refinement rather than a pure spec-fill. Otherwise contradicts nothing settled.

options.recommended: Option A — DATA-source-driven, install-presence-gated check with a 3-state output contract (RECOMMENDED)
options.rationale: Option A is the minimal, principle-clean way to make ADR-0018 D5's 'reuse R3 machinery' actually true after the ADR-0016 bucket split. The finding's root cause is that the old machinery assumed co-location of source/remote_cache/base in one `<repo>/.cco`; the split scatters them across DATA (synced), CACHE (never-sync), and STATE (never-sync). Option A fixes this by driving iteration from the ONE bucket guaranteed present on a freshly-synced PC (DATA `source`) and gating each row on local install presence (STATE) — which is exactly the 'not installed on this machine' degradation the finding's proposedResolution requests, but derived from a real signal rather than assumed. It reuses the existing `_check_remote_update` state machine, `remote_cache`, `ls-remote`, and the `--offline`/`--no-cache` flags already in cmd-update.sh, so the net-new work is small and the conventions match `cco update`. It gives E three things it currently lacks: an unambiguous iteration source, a well-defined `advanced` rule (resolve the pinned ref's remote commit; an immutable tag never advances, a moving branch does), and a stable per-resource-line + summary output contract that is exit-0-always (advisory, never a gate — ADR-0008). The one real decision E must carry is relocating the recorded installed-commit baseline into STATE `/update` (keyed by identity) so it stays machine-local and DATA `source` remains a pure upstream coordinate — consistent with ADR-0016 D5 and the H6 base/ relocation already accepted. Option B abandons the CACHE `remote_cache` that R3/ADR-0016 D7 deliberately built (and fits the project fan-out poorly); Option C is a sound narrowing but contradicts ADR-0018 D5's literal 'projects' scope and the imported-project-with-source case, so it should only be chosen if the maintainer also wants to refine the ADR text. Option A keeps full scope, upholds P2/P3/P6/P8 and ADR-0008/0013/0016/0019, and contradicts nothing settled.

options.title: Solo-adopter Case C "reserved hooks": clarify that the regenerable STATE index makes them additive (no v1 schema change needed)

options.name: Option A — Reframe the hooks as inherently-additive (remove the schema-reservation framing); document precedence as post-v1; no v1 index field
options.description: Adopt the finding's core insight as the canonical resolution. Edit ADR-0018 D6 / §Open and design.md §12 so they no longer say v1 'reserves the enabling hooks' as if a schema slot must be carved out. Instead state explicitly: (1) the index is machine-local, non-portable, scan-rebuildable STATE (design.md §178; ADR-0016 D4), so any future per-project field such as config_path is an ADDITIVE, non-breaking schema change that can be introduced when Case C is analyzed — nothing needs reserving because the index is regenerable and never synced/committed; (2) cco start discovery precedence (<repo>/.cco -> ~/.cco/projects/<name>) is explicitly POST-V1 — v1 has a single discovery path (the existing source-precedence is --from > entry > prompt over project MEMBERS, design.md §4.4, which is a different axis and stays unchanged); (3) drop config_path from any implied v1 read path. Net effect: one-paragraph clarification in two places, the 'reserved hook' phrasing is retired in favor of 'additive-by-construction'.
pros: Directly resolves the cited ambiguity: states unambiguously that config_path is NOT in v1 and that precedence is post-v1.
Matches the authoritative facts already in the docs: index is 'scan-rebuildable STATE' (design.md §178) and 'never committed, never synced' (§3 / ADR-0016 D4) — no contradiction introduced.
Zero v1 code/schema surface: nothing built, read, or carried for a deferred feature (honors YAGNI, consistent with the manifest-removal posture of ADR-0012/0018 D3 — no speculative scaffolding).
Keeps the v1 index schema as the single authoritative shape (version/paths/projects), so §3, ADR-0016 D4, and ADR-0018 stay in sync.
Preserves the deferral decision (ADR-0018 D6: C is post-v1) while removing the false implication that deferral required carving a slot now.
cons: Loses the explicit 'we already thought about config_path' breadcrumb in the v1 schema; the future Case-C analyst must re-derive the field name (mitigated: D6 still NAMES config_path as the intended mechanism, just labels it additive-not-reserved).
Relies on the reader trusting that 'additive to regenerable STATE' is truly non-breaking — needs the one-line justification spelled out so it is not re-litigated.
Touches two documents (ADR-0018 + design.md §12), a small propagation cost.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P10 (classify by ROLE: the index's role is disposable machine-local STATE, so additivity follows from role, not from a reserved slot) and P11(b) (sync class 'never' for STATE — a regenerable, never-synced store tolerates additive fields without migration). Consistent with ADR-0016 D4 (one authoritative index shape) and ADR-0018 D6 (C stays post-v1). Honors the ADR-0012/0018 anti-speculative-scaffolding stance (no manifest/index slot built ahead of need). Does not strain any P1-P17.

options.name: Option B — Add an OPTIONAL config_path per-project field to the v1 index schema now (read-but-unused), document precedence as a v1 no-op code path
options.description: Take the finding's first sub-option literally. Add config_path as an optional field under projects.<id> in the authoritative v1 index schema (§3 and ADR-0016 D4), defaulting (when absent) to <repo>/.cco. v1 code reads the field but, because no writer ever sets it, the value is always the default — the cco start precedence (<repo>/.cco -> ~/.cco/projects/<name>) is wired as a no-op branch that is dormant until Case C ships a writer. This makes the 'reserve' literal: the slot and the precedence path both exist in v1, inert.
pros: Makes 'reserved hook' literally true and unambiguous: the field and the precedence branch exist in v1.
If Case C is approved soon, less follow-on work — the read path and schema slot are already present.
Eliminates any future migration anxiety about the index shape (though that anxiety is already unfounded for regenerable STATE).
cons: Adds a v1 field and a dead code branch for a POST-V1, not-yet-analyzed feature — speculative scaffolding the project has repeatedly rejected (ADR-0012 manifest removal; ADR-0018 D3 'no projects/ dir reserved'; the 'reconsider only if a real need' posture).
The benefit it buys (non-breaking later addition) is ALREADY GUARANTEED by the index being regenerable STATE — so the field pays cost for value that exists for free (the finding's own core point).

A read-but-unused field invites confusion: a reader of the schema sees config_path with no writer, no behavior, no docs of when it activates — exactly the kind of internal-shape ambiguity P6/G8 want to avoid.
Risks pre-committing the future Case-C analysis to a specific mechanism (a per-project config_path string) before that analysis runs, weakening the 'dedicated analysis' deferral in ADR-0018 D6.
Touches more surface (schema + a live-but-dormant code path) than the ambiguity warrants — the finding itself was downgraded to nit-level impact.
options.principleAlignment: Strains P10/P11 lightly: it places a field by anticipated future surface rather than current role/need, the opposite of 'classify by role & problem, not by surface'. Tension with the ADR-0012/0018 anti-speculative-scaffolding stance (reserving a slot the manifest-removal philosophy explicitly avoids). Neutral on P3/P8 (still STATE, sync class 'never'). Does not violate any hard principle, but adds cost the regenerable-STATE nature (P11(b)) makes unnecessary.

options.name: Option C — Minimal one-line clarifying footnote only (keep 'reserved' wording, annotate it as a no-op note)
options.description: Smallest possible edit: leave ADR-0018 D6 / §Open and design.md §12 essentially as-is, but append a single clarifying clause in each: 'reserve here means no v1 schema field and no v1 code path; because the index is regenerable, never-synced STATE, the config_path field and cco start precedence will be an additive post-v1 change.' Do not restructure the surrounding sentences; do not retire the 'reserved hook' phrasing.
pros: Lowest-effort resolution; matches the finding's nit-level severity assessment.
Answers the two concrete questions (config_path not in v1; precedence post-v1) with one clause each.
Leaves the historical ADR text maximally intact (minimal churn to an Accepted ADR).
cons: Keeps the misleading 'reserved hooks' framing in the lead sentence and only corrects it in a trailing clause — a reader skimming D6 still absorbs the wrong mental model first.
Half-measure: the finding explicitly recommends REMOVING the 'reserved hook' framing because it is neutralized; this option preserves the very phrase the finding flags as misleading.
Two near-identical footnotes risk drifting if one doc is later edited and the other is not (the consolidation P10/P11 aim to avoid).
options.principleAlignment: Weakly consistent with P10/P11 (acknowledges STATE-regenerability) but does so as an afterthought rather than reframing the classification, so the role-first framing of P10 is only partially honored. No hard-principle conflict; strictly a documentation-clarity compromise.

options.recommended: Option A — Reframe the hooks as inherently-additive (remove the schema-reservation framing); document precedence as post-v1; no v1 index field
options.rationale: Option A is the only choice that fully aligns the documents with facts already asserted elsewhere in the design: the index is explicitly 'machine-local, non-portable, scan-rebuildable STATE' (design.md §178) and 'never committed, never synced' (§3 / ADR-0016 D4). That regenerable, sync-class-'never' nature (P11(b)) makes ANY future per-project field additive by construction — so there is nothing to reserve, and the 'reserved hook' framing is, as the finding notes, neutralized. Reframing it (rather than carving a literal slot) keeps the v1 index schema authoritative and singular (ADR-0016 D4), avoids the speculative scaffolding the project has consistently rejected (ADR-0012 manifest removal; ADR-0018 D3 deliberately reserves NO projects/ directory), and preserves the post-v1 deferral of Case C (ADR-0018 D6) without pre-committing its mechanism. Option B pays real cost (a dead schema field + dormant code branch) for value the regenerable-STATE nature already provides for free, and it pre-commits the future dedicated analysis to a specific design — both contrary to P10's classify-by-role discipline. Option C answers the two literal questions but leaves the misleading lead phrasing intact, which is exactly what the finding asks to remove. Concretely, Option A means: (1) edit ADR-0018 D6/§Open and design.md §12 to state config_path is NOT a v1 field and the <repo>/.cco -> ~/.cco/projects/<name> precedence is POST-V1; (2) add one sentence noting both are non-breaking additive changes because the index is regenerable, never-synced STATE; (3) retire 'reserved hooks' in favor of 'additive-by-construction', while still naming config_path as the intended future mechanism so the breadcrumb survives. This is a maintainer DECISION (whether to retire the framing vs. carve a literal slot), not a mechanical edit — the recommendation is to decide for Option A and apply it during E/doc-sweep, not now.

options.title: Profile→tag migration for projects: reframe as an accepted behavioral change (not "lossless") and specify how `cco list --tag` renders a tagged-but-unresolved project

options.name: Option A — Reframe as accepted regression (ADR-0009 precedent) + tags render unresolved projects with an explicit marker
options.description: Two coupled moves, no new mechanism. (1) FRAMING: amend ADR-0010 §4/§Consequences/§9 and design.md §3/§9 to state explicitly that profile→tag seeding for PROJECTS records ORGANIZATIONAL METADATA ONLY — it does NOT reproduce the legacy profile's workspace-selection/visibility behavior ("switch profile → these projects appear/are on-disk"). That selection capability is INTENTIONALLY DROPPED in v1, recorded as an accepted change exactly like ADR-0009's cross-PC memory regression — not 'lossless'. State that a seeded tag may reference a project NAME that is not yet in the STATE index (lazy per-project migration, ADR-0006), and that the canonical 'does this project exist/run' authority is the STATE index, never tags.yml. (2) RENDER SEMANTICS (the gap E must implement): `cco list --tag <t>` enumerates names from tags.yml, joins against the STATE index, and SHOWS every match — resolved projects with their repos/status, and tagged-but-unresolved names with an explicit '(unresolved — run cco migrate/cco resolve)' marker and no path/status. tags.yml stays the source of truth for the tag set; the index stays the source of truth for resolvability. The two are joined at read time, never merged in storage.
pros: Directly matches the maintainer's proposedResolution and the explicit ADR-0009 framing precedent the finding cites — consistency across the ADR set.
Showing unresolved names is genuinely useful: it doubles as a migration TODO surface ('these tagged projects still need cco migrate'), turning the gap into a feature.

Zero new storage or coupling: the index↔tags.yml split (ADR-0010 §4, ADR-0016) is preserved; the join is a pure read-time operation, honoring P6 (tags internal, index STATE) and P4 (destination ≠ behavior).
Truthful to the lazy-migration model (ADR-0006): a tag legitimately existing before its project is resolved is now a documented, handled state rather than an undefined one.
Closes the 'undefined render' gap that E would otherwise have to guess at — gives implementation a concrete contract.
cons: Requires editing three ADR sections + two design sections (framing) and a one-line spec for `cco list --tag` — more doc churn than a pure label change.

Does NOT restore the lost selection/visibility behavior; users who relied on profiles for workspace selection still have no equivalent (this is accepted, but must be communicated in migration output, not just buried in an ADR).
The marker introduces a minor rendering branch in the new `cco list` code (ADR-0011 build-new surface) — small but real.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P4 (a resource's destination/storage is orthogonal to behavior — tags.yml stores metadata, the index governs resolvability; never conflate). Upholds P6 (tags stay internal/DATA, index stays STATE; the join doesn't leak tags into a config bucket or fabricate index entries). Consistent with ADR-0006 (lazy per-project migration legitimately produces tagged-but-unresolved names). Mirrors ADR-0009 precedent (name a dropped capability an 'accepted regression', not 'lossless'). Upholds ADR-0010 §4 (index = paths/repos only, no tags) and ADR-0015/0016 (tags.yml in DATA, keyed by name). Strains nothing; it makes an already-implied state explicit.

options.name: Option B — Reframe identically, but `cco list --tag` HIDES unresolved names (index-gated tag filter)
options.description: Same framing correction as Option A (organizational-metadata-only, accepted change not 'lossless', index is the resolvability authority). RENDER SEMANTICS differ: `cco list --tag <t>` shows ONLY projects that are BOTH tagged AND present in the STATE index; a tagged-but-unresolved name is silently omitted (tag survives in tags.yml as personal metadata but contributes no list output until the project is migrated/resolved). Optionally, a separate diagnostic — e.g. `cco migrate` output or `cco list --tag <t> --all` — can surface the omitted names so the information is recoverable on demand. The migration's profile→tag seeding still runs; the user just doesn't see seeded tags in `cco list` until each project is actually brought into the index.
pros: Cleanest list output: `cco list` only ever shows runnable/known projects, never dangling names — closest in spirit to the old profile UX where switching a profile only ever surfaced present resources.
Avoids a potentially confusing 'unresolved' row for users who never migrate certain projects (e.g. abandoned ones whose origin profile was seeded as a tag).
Simplest render path (a single inner-join filter) — minimal new code in the build-new `cco list` surface.
Still consistent with the framing correction: 'lossless' is replaced with 'accepted change'; tags are explicitly metadata-only.
cons: Hiding makes the migration gap INVISIBLE precisely where the finding warns it bites: a user who seeded tags from profiles runs `cco list --tag oldprofile`, sees fewer (or zero) projects than the old profile showed, and gets no hint that the missing ones just need `cco migrate` — re-creating the 'masking' problem in the UX even after fixing the docs.

Information asymmetry: tags.yml (DATA, synced cross-PC) can hold tags for projects not yet resolved on THIS machine; silently dropping them means `cco list --tag` output varies per machine with no explanation.
Needs an extra `--all`/diagnostic affordance to be recoverable, partially re-adding the surface Option A gets in one render.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P4 and P6 identically to Option A (storage split intact, no leakage). Consistent with ADR-0006/0010/0015/0016. However it strains the spirit of P10/P14's 'never silently mask, surface gaps' bias (ADR-0019's layered 'warn-never-hide' reachability philosophy and the finding's own 'masking' critique) — hiding unresolved names is the storage-clean but UX-opaque choice, the same trap ADR-0009 was careful to avoid by NAMING the regression rather than letting it pass silently.

options.name: Option C — Framing-only correction; defer all `cco list --tag` render semantics to E as an explicit open item
options.description: Minimal documentation move. Amend ADR-0010 (§4, §Consequences, §5/§9 migration note) and design.md §3/§9 to: (1) replace 'lossless' with 'accepted change' and the ADR-0009-style wording; (2) state that profile→tag seeding for projects is organizational metadata only, NOT selection/visibility, and that no decentralized equivalent of profile-as-workspace-selection exists in v1; (3) note that seeded tags may reference not-yet-resolved names. Then DEFER the concrete `cco list --tag` render behavior (show-with-marker vs hide) to implementation phase E, recording it as a flagged Open item / ADR follow-up rather than deciding it now. The migration text must still warn the user at runtime that selection behavior is gone.
pros: Smallest change that removes the false 'lossless' claim — fixes the documentation-integrity problem the finding centers on.
Keeps the decision surface narrow for a medium-severity item; doesn't pre-commit a UX detail that might be better judged with code in hand during E.
No risk of specifying a render behavior that E later finds awkward against the actual `cco list` rewrite.
cons: Leaves the exact gap the finding says E must handle ('how cco list --tag renders a tagged-but-unresolved project') still unspecified — only relabels it as a known-open question rather than answering it.
Defers a decision the maintainer is being asked to make NOW; risks the same ambiguity resurfacing at impl-readiness with no settled answer.
Weakest of the three on actionability: an implementer reaching this code in E still has to choose show-vs-hide without guidance, reintroducing the variance the V review is meant to eliminate.
options.principleAlignment: Consistent with all settled decisions (no contradiction). Honors the documentation-first / ADR-as-continuity discipline (don't leave a false claim in an accepted ADR). But it under-delivers on P10's mandate that an analysis 'flag/resolve' conflicts (it flags but defers resolution) and on the impl-readiness goal of removing ambiguity before coding. Neutral on P4/P6 since it makes no storage or render commitment.

options.recommended: Option A — Reframe as accepted regression (ADR-0009 precedent) + tags render unresolved projects with an explicit marker
options.rationale: Option A is the only option that fully discharges the finding: it (1) corrects the misleading 'lossless' framing using the exact precedent the finding cites (ADR-0009's named 'accepted regression' for the dropped cross-PC memory sync), (2) explicitly separates the two things legacy profiles did — organizational grouping (preserved as tags) vs workspace selection/visibility (intentionally dropped, no v1 equivalent) — and (3) gives E a concrete, testable contract for the previously-undefined behavior: `cco list --tag` joins tags.yml against the STATE index at read time and SURFACES tagged-but-unresolved names with an '(unresolved)' marker. This honors the orthogonality the whole refactor rests on (P4: tags.yml stores metadata, the STATE index governs resolvability — ADR-0010 §4 / ADR-0016) and P6 (no leakage, no fabricated index entries, pure read-time join), while turning the lazy-migration artifact (ADR-0006) from an undefined state into a useful migration-TODO surface. Option B fixes the docs identically but its hide-on-unresolved render re-creates the very 'masking' the finding objects to — a user seeing fewer projects under a seeded tag with no hint to run `cco migrate` — which runs against the 'name the regression, never silently mask' bias that ADR-0009 and ADR-0019's warn-never-hide reachability model both embody. Option C fixes only the wording and defers the render decision, under-delivering on the impl-readiness mandate to remove ambiguity before coding (P10). Note this is a maintainer decision: whether to SHOW (A) or HIDE (B) the unresolved names, and the precise marker text, are UX choices that per P10/method-lesson-(b) need maintainer confirmation — but the framing correction (drop 'lossless') should be adopted regardless of which render choice wins.

options.title: First-run backup idempotency marker has no defined home in the STATE layout — define a global STATE migration-state file and decouple backup-guard from vault-removal
options.recommended: Option A — Dedicated global STATE file `<state>/cco/migration-state`, with the backup archive in `backups/` as the authoritative idempotency signal
options.rationale: Option A is the smallest, most principle-coherent fix. It places the marker exactly where the STATE layout already keeps global (non-per-project) flat files — `index`, `last_seen`/`last_read`, `sync-meta`, `remotes-token` — so it introduces NO new structural concept and needs no §2.2/D6 grab-bag invention (the §2.2 STATE layout literally lists global root-level markers already; adding one more is consistent, not novel). Critically, A treats the marker as a *fast-path hint* but makes the **`backups/` archive the authoritative idempotency signal**, and it **decouples** the backup guard (run iff legacy-vault-present AND no verified backup) from the *separate, independently-guarded* vault-removal step. This directly closes both failure modes the finding raises: (a) a vanished marker after a STATE wipe/relocation no longer causes a destructive re-archive, because the guard also consults the verified archive co-located in the same STATE tree (lost together, found together — they share fate by construction, unlike a marker in a different volume); (b) a stale/mistakenly-present marker cannot make a genuine first run skip a real backup, because the predicate is grounded in observable artifacts (vault presence + archive integrity), not a bare boolean. This is the most faithful reading of the M8 ordering invariant ("vault removed only after a verified backup"): A makes "verified backup" a checkable on-disk fact rather than an unverifiable claim. It upholds P1/P6 (internal, CLI-managed, hidden, never-team, never-sync — same class as `backups/`), P4/P11 (sync-class `never`, STATE), and ADR-0016 D6 without re-opening any settled decision. Option B (global `meta` dir mirroring per-project meta) is a reasonable alternative if E expects more global update/migration state later, but it adds a directory where a single file suffices today (YAGNI; mild P-altitude strain). Option C (marker-free, derive purely from vault+archive state) is the most robust in theory and is partially folded INTO A as the authoritative layer, but on its own it loses the cheap fast-path and the human-readable record of when/what was archived, and it makes "user declined to remove the vault" indistinguishable from "never backed up" — so A's hybrid (cheap marker + authoritative archive) is preferred.

options.name: Option A — Dedicated global STATE file `<state>/cco/migration-state`, with the backup archive in `backups/` as the authoritative idempotency signal
options.description: Add ONE global root-level file to the §2.2 / ADR-0016 D6 STATE layout: `<state>/cco/migration-state` (sibling of `index`, `last_seen`, `last_read`, `sync-meta`, `remotes-token`, `backups/`). It records the migration's first-run outcome (e.g. `backed_up: <archive-name> <date> <sha/size>`, `vault_removed: yes|no|declined`). Specify the guard as a two-part predicate grounded in on-disk facts: (1) BACKUP runs iff `legacy-vault present AND no verified backup exists` — where 'verified backup exists' is true when `migration-state` records a backup AND/OR a matching integrity-verified archive is present in `<state>/cco/backups/`; on success, write/update `migration-state` and write the archive. (2) VAULT REMOVAL is an independent, separately-guarded step that runs ONLY after a verified backup, and is idempotent on its own (legacy vault already gone ⇒ no-op). The `backups/` archive is the authoritative signal; `migration-state` is a fast-path hint and human-readable record. Because the marker and the archive live in the SAME STATE tree, a CCO_STATE_HOME relocation/wipe loses both together — so the guard never sees 'marker gone but archive still claims done', and a fresh STATE simply re-detects the (now-absent) vault and correctly does nothing destructive. Update the §2.2 STATE layout text and ADR-0016 D6 to list `migration-state`. Add a Phase-2 test for the second-first-run no-re-archive path (and a STATE-relocated-with-vault-already-gone path).
pros: Minimal, surgical change: adds exactly one global flat file where the STATE layout ALREADY keeps global markers (index, last_seen/last_read, sync-meta, remotes-token) — no new structural concept, no grab-bag dir.

Closes both failure modes: vanished marker can't trigger destructive re-archive (archive co-located, shares fate; guard also consults backups/), and stale marker can't skip a real backup (predicate grounded in vault-presence + archive integrity).

Faithfully implements M8: 'vault removed only after a verified backup' becomes a checkable on-disk fact, not an unverifiable claim. Backup and vault-removal are explicitly decoupled and independently idempotent.
Keeps a cheap fast-path (skip re-archive without re-hashing the whole archive) AND a human-readable audit record (what/when archived, whether vault removed/declined).
Gives E a concrete, named location — eliminates the 'E will invent an ad-hoc location' risk the finding calls out.
cons: Two sources of truth (marker file + archive) need a defined precedence rule (archive authoritative); slightly more spec text than a pure marker.
Adds a (tiny) new file to the layout that must be documented in §2.2, D6, and the inventory — small propagation cost.
If a future need for more global migration/update state arrives, a single file may grow into a grab-bag (mitigated: split per P11 when it does).
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P1 (CLI-managed, never hand-edited → internal), P6 (hidden, never in a config bucket, STATE), P4/P11 (classified: STATE, sync-class `never`, same class as backups/), P8 (lives under no /session boundary issue — it is top-level STATE `never`, never swept by future state-sync). Consistent with ADR-0006 (breaking cutover, lazy backed-up migration) and ADR-0016 D6 (extends the STATE global-marker set rather than contradicting it). No conflict with ADR-0007 XDG mapping (it is a STATE-tier file, honoring CCO_STATE_HOME). Strains nothing; the only nuance is the dual-signal precedence which the option resolves explicitly.

options.name: Option B — Global per-machine `<state>/cco/meta` directory/file, mirroring the per-project `projects/<id>/meta`
options.description: Introduce a global meta home at `<state>/cco/meta` (the missing global counterpart to `projects/<id>/meta` that the finding notes is absent), holding machine-wide cco state including the migration `backed_up` outcome and, optionally, future global update/migration markers. Fold the existing global root markers (`last_seen`/`last_read`) into it OR leave them as-is and add `meta` alongside. The backup guard predicate and decoupled vault-removal step are specified identically to Option A (backup iff vault-present AND not-yet-backed-up; archive in backups/ as the integrity anchor; vault removal independent and only post-verification). Document the new global `meta` in §2.2 and ADR-0016 D6.
pros: Provides the missing symmetry the finding points at: a global meta to match the per-project meta, a natural home for any machine-wide cco state (not just migration).
Future-proofs other global markers (migration history, one-time prompts, schema_version of the global layout) under one roof, reducing future ad-hoc files.
Same idempotency robustness as A when paired with the archive-as-authoritative rule and decoupled removal.
cons: Heavier than the problem requires today: introduces a directory/aggregate where a single file suffices (YAGNI). Risks recreating the `.cco/meta` grab-bag that P11 explicitly split apart.

If it absorbs `last_seen`/`last_read`, that is a layout churn touching the update engine for no functional gain right now; if it sits alongside them, it competes with existing global markers (two places for 'global STATE markers').
More propagation surface (design §2.2, D6, inventory, possibly update-engine code paths) for a medium-severity guard fix.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P1/P6 (internal, hidden, STATE) and P4/P11 (STATE, `never`-sync) like A. BUT mildly strains P11's 'co-locate by answer profile, split grab-bags' lesson — a global `meta` aggregate invites heterogeneous data unless disciplined. Consistent with ADR-0006 and ADR-0007; extends ADR-0016 D6. Slight altitude/scope creep vs the medium-severity finding.

options.name: Option C — Marker-free guard: derive idempotency purely from legacy-vault presence + verified archive in `backups/`
options.description: Add NO new marker file. Define the guard entirely from observable artifacts: BACKUP runs iff `legacy vault is present AND no integrity-verified archive for it exists in <state>/cco/backups/`. After a successful, integrity-verified backup, VAULT REMOVAL is offered/performed as an independent step. A second first-run finds either (i) the vault already removed ⇒ nothing to do, or (ii) the vault still present (user declined removal) ⇒ a matching verified archive already in backups/ ⇒ skip re-archive. No boolean state to vanish or go stale. Specify the archive naming/lookup so the guard can deterministically find 'the archive for THIS vault' (e.g. content/path fingerprint), and add tests for both the removed and declined-removal second-run paths.
pros: Eliminates the entire class of marker bugs the finding raises — no marker means nothing to lose, relocate, or falsify; the guard is grounded only in artifacts that share STATE fate.
Smallest possible layout change: zero new files; just a precise guard spec and an archive-lookup rule.

Naturally robust to CCO_STATE_HOME relocation: a fresh STATE re-derives state from the (possibly still-present) vault and the (now-absent) archive, and simply re-backs-up safely if and only if needed — never destructively, since removal is post-verification.
cons: Loses the cheap fast-path and the human-readable record: each first-run must scan/verify `backups/` to decide, and there's no recorded 'when/what/declined' audit trail.

Must solve 'which archive belongs to THIS vault' deterministically (fingerprint/naming), or risk false 'already backed up' when multiple/old archives exist — pushing complexity into archive identity instead of a marker.
Cannot distinguish 'user declined to remove the vault' from 'vault legitimately re-appeared / different vault' without extra heuristics; the marker in A/B captures intent that pure artifacts cannot.
options.principleAlignment: Strongly upholds P6 (no extra internal file at all) and P1 (purely cco-derived). Consistent with ADR-0006 (backed-up lazy migration) and M8 (removal only after verified backup — here verification IS the guard). No conflict with ADR-0016 D6 (no layout change). Trade-off is operational, not principled: it is the most principle-pure but loses recordkeeping the maintainer may value; Option A folds C's authoritative-artifact insight in while keeping the fast-path/record.

options.title: No rollback/abort handling if `cco migrate` fails mid-write (partial .cco/ + index entry); the re-run guard then blocks recovery
options.recommended: Atomic staged migrate: build under temp, scan, then atomic move + index register; cleanup partials on failure
options.rationale: Option A is the only one that fully closes the finding while honoring every settled decision. The migration flow is already declared "lazy, per-project, idempotent, backed-up" (design.md §9:672) and the design already relies on the same staging+marker idiom elsewhere (the "backed-up" marker in STATE meta guards first-run idempotency, :679-680; J0 is "per-root idempotent", M6). Making the write atomic (build in a temp dir under STATE/CACHE, run secret-scan + gitignore-heal on the staging copy, then a single near-atomic rename into `<repo>/.cco/`, then index-register last) is the natural extension of M8 (which already protects the read precondition) to the write/register steps — it turns the existing aspirational "idempotent" claim into a true contract. Crucially it preserves the re-run guard (P6 / FR) untouched: because a half-finished `.cco/` never lands in the repo, a re-run always sees either a complete migrated `.cco/` (correctly protected) or none (cleanly re-creatable). It also respects P15/P16/AD11 (writes only into the config bucket and STATE/CACHE staging, no tool code, no source-of-truth confusion) and keeps the index authoritative-but-rebuildable (§3: `cco resolve --scan`), so index registration last is safe and self-healing. Option B is a strictly weaker fallback (good as a complement, insufficient alone: it relaxes the guard rather than preventing the partial, and a "complete-looking" partial still strands the user). Option C reuses the existing backup/marker idempotency idiom but adds re-run friction and does not by itself give a clean repo state mid-failure. Recommend A, optionally folding in B's partial-detection as the re-run safety net, and adding the §11 interrupted-migrate test the finding calls for.

options.name: Atomic staged migrate: build under temp, scan, then atomic move + index register; cleanup partials on failure
options.description: Specify cco migrate as a transactional write. (1) Materialize the migrated config into a temp staging dir under an internal bucket (STATE/CACHE, e.g. <cache>/cco/migrate-staging/<project>/), NOT in the repo. (2) Run secret-scan + gitignore-heal on the staging copy. (3) Only on full success, move/rename the staging tree into <repo>/.cco/ in a single near-atomic operation (same-filesystem rename where possible; otherwise copy-then-verify-then-swap). (4) Register the index entry LAST (the index is STATE, scan-rebuildable per §3, so it is the cheapest step to redo/heal). (5) A trap/cleanup removes any staging dir AND any partially-moved <repo>/.cco/ on any failure or interrupt, so a re-run starts clean. The re-run guard ('never overwrite an existing .cco/ without confirm') stays exactly as written because the repo only ever sees a complete .cco/ or none. Document the transaction in §9 and add a test_migrate.sh case for an interrupted migrate (kill mid-write -> assert no .cco/, no index entry, clean re-run succeeds).
pros: Fully closes the finding: no half-migrated install can persist; the re-run guard is never the thing blocking recovery because there is nothing partial to block.
Makes the already-declared 'idempotent' property (§9:672) actually true rather than aspirational; M8 currently protects only the read, this protects the write+register symmetrically.
Re-run guard is preserved unchanged (no relaxation of the P6 protection of an existing committed/complete .cco/).

Reuses an idiom the design already uses (STATE-meta 'backed-up' marker idempotency :679-680; J0 per-root idempotent M6) and the index's scan-rebuildability (§3) — registering the index last is safe and self-healing via `cco resolve --scan`.
Staging lives in internal buckets (STATE/CACHE), keeping <repo>/.cco truthful for `git diff` (G8/P6) until the move completes — no transient noise in the repo working tree.
Order-of-operations (secret-scan before the tree is visible in the repo) tightens the secret-scan guarantee: a partial leak can never be left exposed in the repo.
cons: Cross-filesystem move is not truly atomic (e.g. repo on a different mount than CACHE); needs a copy-then-verify-then-swap fallback and a careful trap, adding implementation nuance.

Interrupt handling (SIGKILL) cannot run a trap, so a partially-moved .cco/ must still be detectable+cleanable on the NEXT run — pushes toward also adopting Option B's partial-detection as a safety net.
Slightly more code than the current single-pass write; one more test scenario (interrupted migrate) to maintain.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P6/G8 (internal staging keeps the config bucket truthful; index stays internal & rebuildable), AD11/P9 (no tool code in buckets; staging in STATE/CACHE), P15/P16 (no source-of-truth confusion — the repo .cco becomes authoritative only once whole). Extends M8 (read precondition) consistently to write+register. Honors the migration-flow 'idempotent' contract (§9:672) and the index-as-STATE/scan-rebuildable design (§3, ADR-0016 D4). Does not contradict the re-run guard FR; reinforces it.

options.name: Detect-and-recreate: treat an incomplete .cco/ as re-creatable rather than protected (relax the re-run guard for partials)
options.description: Keep the current single-pass write but redefine the re-run guard so it protects only a COMPLETE .cco/. Define a completeness predicate (e.g. presence of a well-formed project.yml plus any required-on-migrate markers) and a 'migrate-in-progress'/'migrate-complete' sentinel in STATE meta. On re-run, if .cco/ exists but fails the completeness check (or the in-progress sentinel is set without a matching complete marker), migrate treats it as a partial and re-creates it without the confirm prompt; if .cco/ is complete, the existing confirm prompt stands. Document the predicate and the sentinel lifecycle in §9; test the interrupted-migrate recovery path.
pros: Directly removes the specific failure the finding flags: the guard no longer refuses to fix a partial write — recovery needs no manual deletion.
Smaller change to the write path than Option A (no staging/move plumbing); mostly a guard predicate + a STATE-meta sentinel, reusing the existing meta-marker idempotency idiom (:679-680).
Composes well as a safety net for Option A's non-trappable SIGKILL case.
Keeps the index-register-last ordering compatible (a present .cco/ with no index entry is one signal of incompleteness).
cons: Weaker than A: it tolerates the partial landing in the repo, then heals on the NEXT run — between failure and re-run the install is genuinely half-migrated (the exact state the finding wants to avoid).

A partial that happens to LOOK complete (e.g. project.yml written but a later step like memory relocation or index register failed) can pass the predicate and silently strand the user — the completeness predicate is fragile.
A failed secret-scan mid-write may leave secret-bearing content visible in the repo working tree until the next run cleans it (weaker secret-leak posture than A).
Subtly changes the meaning of the re-run guard (now 'protect complete only'), which must be re-documented carefully so it is not read as a general weakening of P6 protection.
options.principleAlignment: Compatible with P6 (still protects a complete .cco/; only narrows 'existing' to 'complete') and reuses the STATE-meta marker idiom (idempotency, §9:679-680). Strains the §9 'idempotent, backed-up' intent less directly than A because it heals rather than prevents. No contradiction with ADR-0016 (index in STATE) or the migrate FRs, but the secret-scan-after-write exposure mildly strains the secret-scan guarantee (G8 truthfulness during the window).

options.name: Re-migratable from backup: backup is authoritative source, migrate is fully idempotent re-run from it (no in-repo staging)
options.description: Lean on the fact that the verified legacy-vault backup (M8) is preserved and authoritative. Specify migrate so that re-running it for a project is always safe: on any failure the user simply re-runs `cco migrate <project>`, which (a) verifies the backup (M8, unchanged), (b) on detecting an existing .cco/ from a prior incomplete attempt, offers to RE-DERIVE it from the backup (confirm prompt explicitly framed as 'overwrite incomplete migration from backup' rather than a generic block), and (c) re-registers the index. The backup-as-source plus a per-project 'migrated' STATE-meta marker (mirroring the first-run 'backed-up' marker) make the operation declaratively re-runnable. No temp-dir/atomic-move machinery; correctness comes from the backup being immutable and re-readable.
pros: Conceptually simplest: exploits an invariant the design already guarantees — the backup is verified and never removed until success (M8), so the source of truth for re-derivation always exists.

Reuses the existing per-marker idempotency pattern (the first-run 'backed-up' STATE-meta marker, :679-680) for a symmetric per-project 'migrated' marker — consistent with the design's established idioms.
No cross-filesystem atomic-move concerns; minimal new plumbing.
Keeps the migrate read precondition (M8) front and center as the safety anchor.
cons: Does not prevent the partial .cco/ from existing in the repo between failure and re-run — same window weakness as B, just resolved by re-derivation instead of cleanup.

Still routes recovery through a confirm prompt on an existing .cco/ (reframed but not removed), so it does not eliminate the re-run-guard friction the finding calls out as cleanly as A — the user must understand they are overwriting.

Relies on the backup remaining present; if Phase 3 (legacy-vault removal) has already run for that vault, re-derivation from backup is impossible and the user is stranded — the backup is in STATE and machine-local, not guaranteed long-lived.
If a partial write left secret-bearing content in the repo, it persists until the user chooses to re-migrate — same secret-exposure window as B.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds M8 (backup verified, authoritative) and the §9 'idempotent, backed-up' framing most literally. Consistent with ADR-0016 (index in STATE, rebuildable) and the STATE-meta marker idiom. Strains nothing in P1-P17 directly, but the dependency on the backup's continued presence sits in tension with the Phase-3 'remove the legacy vault entirely' breaking step (§9:644) — re-derivability is only guaranteed pre-Phase-3 / while the backup archive survives.

options.title: coords-lookup: persistence-vs-on-demand left undecided, forking the CACHE layout and invalidation logic
options.recommended: Option A — On-demand-only for v1 (pure scan function; drop the coords-lookup CACHE file from D7)
options.rationale: F45 is a low-blast-radius, advisory/regenerable concern. Option A is recommended because it (1) keeps the v1 spec minimal and removes a whole class of correctness risk — invalidation/staleness handling has no consumer that justifies it; (2) is the cheapest correct thing two engineers can build identically (a deterministic scan function), eliminating the very ambiguity F45 flags; (3) directly serves the principles: P6/P7 mark coords-lookup as internal, never-synced, derived-not-authoritative — and a never-persisted scan is the strongest possible guarantee that it can never drift or be mistaken for a source of truth (closing any residual C3/C4-style leak risk); P10/P11/D3 explicitly say consistency is enforced by tooling not by storage, and the on-demand scan is the purest expression of that — it introduces no storage at all. The only consumer, the advisory cco config coords --diff/--sync (whose mechanism ADR-0017 hands to S, and cco config validate which is exit-code-only), runs interactively on small manifest sets where a fresh scan cost is negligible, so persistence buys nothing user-visible. The phrasing in D3 ("may also be computed on demand without persisting") and the §Open deferral already lean this way; Option A simply makes it the decided v1 baseline and records persistence as a future optimization with a defined trigger, so a later cache can be added without re-litigating. ADR-0016 is Accepted and its D3/D7/§Open wording is internally non-contradictory only if one of these is the v1 truth — picking A resolves the fork while staying fully consistent with every settled decision and changing nothing about the authoritative table (the coords-lookup row in D7/design.md §CACHE is the only edit, and it is a deletion of an internal-only line).

options.name: Option A — On-demand-only for v1 (pure scan function; drop the coords-lookup CACHE file from D7)
options.description: Decide that coords-lookup is computed on demand by scanning the known unit manifests (project.yml/pack.yml entries reachable via the STATE index projects: membership and ~/.cco/packs) every time cco config coords --diff/--sync runs, with no persisted artifact. Remove the coords-lookup line from ADR-0016 D7 and design.md §CACHE (lines ~169), and from the authoritative table's CACHE row. Record persisted caching as a future optimization in §Open/§Futures, to be added only if a real performance need appears, at which point it would gain a defined regeneration trigger (scan on miss + invalidate on any cco repo/llms add and on manifest write). v1 has zero invalidation logic and zero staleness window.
pros: Eliminates the F45 ambiguity at its root: there is exactly one thing to build (a deterministic scan), so two engineers cannot diverge.
No invalidation/regeneration triggers, no staleness window, no risk of a stale cache producing a wrong --diff verdict — the scan is always fresh.
Strongest fit for 'derived, advisory, not authoritative, never synced' (D3): a never-persisted value cannot drift, cannot be hand-edited, and cannot be mistaken for a source of truth.
Smallest v1 surface; matches the design philosophy that consistency is enforced by tooling, not storage (D3, P10/P11).
Cost is borne only on an interactive, infrequent advisory command over small manifest sets — negligible in practice.
Reversible: persistence can be layered in later behind the same scan function (scan-on-miss), with no schema or migration impact since the file was never part of any synced/committed surface.
cons: If the number of units grows very large, every --diff/--sync re-scans (re-reads + re-parses N manifests) — a repeated cost the cache would amortize.
Requires a minor edit to two already-Accepted documents (remove the CACHE entry), so the 'real CACHE file' wording in D7/design must be corrected.
Defers, rather than fully specifies, the eventual cache design — though it pins the v1 behavior unambiguously.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P6 (internal, hidden — and now nothing to hide because nothing persists), P7 (no sync channel — a never-written file trivially honors 'derived, not a sync channel'), P10/P11 + ADR-0016 D3 (coherence by tooling not storage; no global source of truth re-introduced). Consistent with ADR-0017 (the consuming CLI mechanism is owned by S; this decides only the backing store). Strains nothing; only refines ADR-0016 D7 by removing the optional CACHE entry and resolves the §Open deferral.

options.name: Option B — Persisted CACHE file with a defined regeneration trigger (scan-on-miss + invalidate on coordinate mutation)
options.description: Keep coords-lookup as the real CACHE file D7/design.md already list, and pin its lifecycle: it is (re)built by a full manifest scan when absent or when a recorded fingerprint (set of known manifest paths + their mtimes/hashes, taken from the STATE index) no longer matches; it is invalidated/refreshed on every cco repo/llms add, on cco resolve --scan (which already rebuilds the index), and lazily on read if the fingerprint is stale. It remains internal/CACHE/never-sync/advisory/non-authoritative (P6/P7/D3). cco clean may delete it freely (regenerable). The persisted lookup powers auto-fill and --diff without a per-invocation full scan.
pros: Amortizes scan cost across repeated --diff/--sync/auto-fill calls — better at scale.
Keeps D7 and design.md §CACHE as-is (no doc deletion), so the 'CACHE layout entry exists' question is answered 'yes' with the least churn to the layout tables.
Auto-fill on cco repo/llms add gets a ready index without scanning every time.
Still fully internal/regenerable: cco clean can wipe it; loss is harmless.
cons: Reintroduces exactly the complexity F45 warns about: a staleness model, a fingerprint, and invalidation hooks on every manifest edit and on hand-edits the CLI does not mediate (IDE edits to project.yml bypass cco repo/llms add, so the cache can silently go stale unless the read-path always re-fingerprints).
A stale cache can produce a wrong advisory verdict (--diff misses a divergence or reports a phantom one) — worse than slightly slower-but-always-correct, for an advisory tool.
