More code to build and test for a feature whose only consumers are infrequent interactive commands where scan cost is already negligible — optimizing a non-hot path.
The fingerprint must track the index's projects: membership and ~/.cco/packs set; any gap there becomes a correctness hole.
options.principleAlignment: Consistent with P6 (hidden/internal), P7 (never-sync; it is a derived read-cache, not a sync channel) and ADR-0016 D3's letter (it stays CACHE, advisory, non-authoritative). The risk is to the spirit of D3/P10-P11 ('coherence by tooling, not storage'): a persisted derived store, if it goes stale, behaves momentarily like a (wrong) source of truth. Acceptable because it is regenerable and advisory, but it carries correctness obligations the recommended option avoids. Honors ADR-0017's CLI-owned-by-S boundary.

options.name: Option C — On-demand by default, persistence as an explicit opt-in cache flag (best-effort, no auto-invalidation)
options.description: Adopt Option A's on-demand scan as the canonical, always-correct path, but allow an opt-in persisted snapshot: cco config coords --refresh writes coords-lookup as a one-shot materialization (a frozen scan result) that subsequent commands may consult only when explicitly asked (e.g. a --cached flag) — never consulted implicitly. There is no automatic invalidation: the snapshot is understood to be a manual, possibly-stale convenience, and any implicit consumer (auto-fill, default --diff) always uses the fresh scan. D7/design.md keep the coords-lookup line but annotate it 'opt-in snapshot, manual refresh, never implicitly read'.
pros: Default behavior is always correct (fresh scan), so the advisory tooling never reports a stale verdict.
Gives a power-user / large-repo escape hatch to materialize a snapshot without imposing invalidation machinery.
Keeps a CACHE entry in D7 (minimal doc churn) while making its semantics unambiguous (manual, opt-in, never the implicit source).
No automatic invalidation triggers to build — the staleness is the user's explicit responsibility, mirroring the never-blocking / explicit-action philosophy (ADR-0008).
cons: Adds CLI surface (--refresh / --cached) and two code paths (scan + read-snapshot) for a non-hot, advisory command — more to build and document than Option A for marginal benefit.
A 'manually-refreshable, possibly-stale' snapshot is a subtle UX contract; risks confusing users about whether --diff is authoritative.
The persisted snapshot, even if opt-in, sits in the same conceptual space F45 flags (a derived store with no automatic freshness guarantee) — pushes complexity onto the user instead of removing it.
Two engineers could still diverge on the snapshot's exact semantics (what --cached reads, when it warns about age), so it only partially closes the F45 ambiguity.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P6/P7 (internal, hidden, never-sync, regenerable) and D3 (advisory, non-authoritative, coherence-by-tooling). Aligns with ADR-0008's 'explicit, never silently blocking' ethos (the snapshot is opt-in and user-driven). Mild strain on simplicity/P10-P11 minimalism: it introduces optional storage where Option A shows none is needed, expanding the CLI surface S/E must own (ADR-0017) without a demonstrated need.

options.title: Disambiguate the cco config grab-bag: split by bucket (personal ~/.cco vs project <repo>/.cco) and enumerate the full subcommand set in design.md §7

options.name: Option A — Bucket-scoped split: cco config = ~/.cco ONLY; move project-scoped verbs to cco project (validate/coords) and keep cco config protect as the documented exception
options.description: Re-scope cco config to mean exactly ONE bucket — the personal/global ~/.cco store — keeping only the ADR-0008-named verbs: cco config save / push / pull. Relocate the project <repo>/.cco verbs to the existing cco project namespace: cco config coords --diff/--sync -> cco project coords --diff/--sync, and cco config validate -> cco project validate. cco config protect (ADR-0020 D4) is the ONE deliberate exception that stays under config: it is named verbatim in ADR-0020/P17, and its job (scaffold a CODEOWNERS that governs who may edit .cco/ config) reads naturally as 'protect the config' — so keep it but annotate it explicitly as project-bucket-scoped (cwd repo), with a one-line note in §7 that this is the single config verb that touches <repo>/.cco rather than ~/.cco. Then enumerate the WHOLE set in one §7 table block: each row annotated with (a) the exact subcommand, (b) the bucket it touches (~/.cco vs cwd <repo>/.cco), (c) cwd-vs-global scoping. Net rule the user learns: 'cco config almost always = my personal store (~/.cco); cco project = the project I am standing in (<repo>/.cco); the lone protect verb is documented as the exception because it is intrinsically a config-governance scaffold.'
pros: Restores a single, learnable noun->bucket mapping for the dominant case: cco config = ~/.cco (the verb tells you the bucket).

coords and validate are inherently project-scoped (they read/operate on <repo>/.cco/project.yml of the cwd unit), so cco project <verb> matches their real cwd-relative scope and the existing cco project / cco resolve cwd-first convention (design §7, ADR-0017 D2).
Reuses an already-existing namespace (cco project ...) — no new top-level verb invented; consistent with cco project export/import already defined for projects in §7 / ADR-0018.
Keeps the ADR-0008-blessed names (cco config save/push/pull) byte-for-byte — no rename of a settled, maintainer-confirmed verb.

Eliminates the dual-bucket overload the finding flags: a user can tell from the noun which bucket they touch (config=personal, project=project), so E gets unambiguous cwd-vs-global scoping per subcommand.
Keeps protect where ADR-0020 named it (cco config protect) — no contradiction with a settled ADR — while making its single-exception status explicit rather than accidental.
cons: cco config protect remains a documented exception (one config verb that touches <repo>/.cco), so the noun->bucket rule is 'almost always' rather than 'always' — a small residual irregularity (mitigated by the explicit annotation).

Moves coords/validate out of the namespace they are cited under in ADR-0016 D3/D9 and ADR-0019 D2 (which all say 'cco config coords' / 'cco config validate'); requires a propagation pass + ADR clarification note (the ADRs named them illustratively, mechanism explicitly deferred to S/E, so this is a refinement, not a reversal).
validate today doubles as a git-hook target (exit-code only, like cco sync --check); renaming to cco project validate means the documented pre-commit/CI snippet must be updated in guides.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P1/P2 (clear config-bucket boundaries: ~/.cco vs <repo>/.cco), P10/P11 (classify by role+scope, not by surface — coords/validate's role is project-scoped), P14 (validate stays the boundary-less reachability check, just renamed). Consistent with ADR-0017 D2 cwd-first resolution convention and ADR-0018 cco project export/import. Keeps ADR-0008 names intact. Lightly refines (does NOT contradict) ADR-0016 D3/D9 and ADR-0019 D2 illustrative naming and keeps ADR-0020 D4 'cco config protect' verbatim while clarifying its single-exception scope.

options.name: Option B — Keep cco config as the umbrella, but formally enumerate every subcommand in §7 with an explicit per-row bucket+scope annotation (no relocation)
options.description: Do NOT move any verb. Accept cco config as a deliberate umbrella for 'config-management operations' spanning both buckets, but cure the finding's real defect — that §7 never enumerates the set and the bucket is implicit — by replacing the two vague rows ('cco config …' + 'cco config protect') with a single dedicated §7 sub-table that lists ALL subcommands explicitly: cco config save / push / pull (bucket: ~/.cco, scope: global), cco config coords --diff/--sync (bucket: <repo>/.cco of cwd project, scope: cwd/cross-unit), cco config validate (bucket: <repo>/.cco of cwd project, scope: cwd), cco config protect (bucket: <repo>/.cco of cwd repo, scope: cwd). Each row carries an explicit 'Bucket' and 'Scope' column so neither E nor the user has to infer it. Add a short legend: 'config = configuration-management verbs; the Bucket column tells you what each touches.'
pros: Zero churn to settled ADR naming — every verb stays exactly as ADR-0008/0016/0019/0020 wrote it; pure documentation completion.
Directly fixes the concrete blocker the finding raises for E (no authoritative enumerated list, implicit per-subcommand scope) by making bucket + cwd/global scope explicit per row.
Lowest-risk, smallest-diff resolution; no guide snippets or hook targets change.
Frames cco config as 'the configuration toolbox' — a coherent single mental anchor ('all config admin lives under one verb') that some users prefer over two namespaces.
cons: Does not resolve the underlying conceptual overload the finding names: cco config still simultaneously means 'manage ~/.cco' AND 'operate on <repo>/.cco' — the noun still does not tell you the bucket; you must read the table.
save (personal) sitting beside validate/protect (project) under one verb keeps the latent footgun the finding describes (a user reasoning 'config = my personal store' is wrong for half the verbs).
Annotation cures discoverability but not the two-buckets-under-one-verb design smell — a future reviewer may re-raise it.
Cross-unit coords --diff/--sync operating on project files from a verb that elsewhere means 'my personal store' remains semantically muddy.
options.principleAlignment: Neutral-to-mildly-straining on P1/P2 (the bucket boundary is documented but not encoded in the verb). Fully consistent with ADR-0008/0016/0019/0020 (no rename). Satisfies P14 (validate unchanged). Honors the documentation-first / authoritative-enumeration spirit and the 'classify explicitly' thrust of P10/P11 at the documentation level, but does not push the role-based separation into the command surface as P10/P11 would prefer.

options.name: Option C — Three-way split by concern: cco config = ~/.cco; cco project = project lifecycle/coords/validate; a new cco protect (or fold protect under cco project) for governance scaffolding
options.description: Most aggressive separation. cco config save/push/pull = ~/.cco only (as Option A). cco project validate and cco project coords --diff/--sync = project <repo>/.cco share-readiness/consistency (as Option A). AND additionally remove protect from the config namespace entirely: either promote it to a top-level cco protect (cwd repo) or fold it under cco project protect, so that NOTHING that touches <repo>/.cco lives under cco config — making the noun->bucket rule absolute ('config = ~/.cco, full stop'). §7 enumerates all three namespaces with bucket annotations.
pros: Cleanest possible invariant: cco config means ~/.cco and ONLY ~/.cco — no exception, no asterisk; the verb fully determines the bucket.

Each concern gets a home matching its role: personal-store versioning (config), project share-readiness/consistency (project), governance scaffolding (protect/project) — maximal P10/P11 role-alignment.
Removes every residual footgun the finding flags.
cons: Directly renames cco config protect, which is written verbatim and deliberately under config in ADR-0020 D4 and P17 ('cco's value-add is setup assistance ... cco config protect scaffolds CODEOWNERS') — this is the closest thing to contradicting a settled, maintainer-recorded ADR decision, so it needs explicit maintainer re-confirmation, not a silent refactor.

A new top-level cco protect adds a top-level verb for a single optional, deferred (→ E, possibly post-v1) command — poor verb-budget economics; YAGNI-adjacent (ADR-0020 itself flags protect as optional and possibly documentation-baseline only).
Largest propagation surface (three ADRs + design §6.2/§7 + guides) for a marginal gain over Option A.
If protect folds under cco project, it sits beside validate/coords which read project state — reasonable — but still costs the ADR-0020 rename and re-confirmation.
options.principleAlignment: Maximally upholds P1/P2 and P10/P11 (verb fully encodes bucket+role). BUT strains the 'never contradict a settled, non-superseded decision' constraint by renaming ADR-0020 D4 / P17's explicitly-named cco config protect — only acceptable with maintainer approval (P10 method-lesson (b): maintainer confirmation required for choices affecting how the toolkit is used). YAGNI tension with ADR-0020's own 'optional/deferred' framing of protect.

options.recommended: Option A — Bucket-scoped split: cco config = ~/.cco ONLY; move project-scoped verbs to cco project (validate/coords) and keep cco config protect as the documented exception
options.rationale: Option A resolves the finding's core defect — a verb that silently spans two buckets — by encoding the bucket in the noun for the dominant case (cco config = personal ~/.cco; cco project = the cwd project's <repo>/.cco), which is exactly what E needs for unambiguous per-subcommand cwd-vs-global scoping. It does this while honoring the settled-decision constraint: the ADR-0008 names (save/push/pull) are kept byte-for-byte, and cco config protect stays exactly where ADR-0020 D4/P17 named it — the single, explicitly-annotated exception, justified because protect is intrinsically a config-governance scaffold ('protect the config'), not a personal-store op. coords and validate are genuinely cwd-project-scoped (they read <repo>/.cco/project.yml of the unit you are standing in), so cco project <verb> matches both their real role (P10/P11: classify by role, not surface) and the existing cwd-first cco project / cco resolve convention (ADR-0017 D2, ADR-0018 cco project export/import) — no new namespace is invented. The ADRs that wrote 'cco config coords/validate' deferred the actual mechanism/naming to S/E, so relocating them is a refinement of illustrative naming, not a reversal of a decision. Option B is the safe fallback if the maintainer wants zero ADR churn (it still fixes the missing-enumeration blocker), but it leaves the two-buckets-under-one-verb smell intact. Option C is rejected for v1 because it renames the deliberately-named, optional cco config protect, costing an ADR-0020 contradiction and a top-level verb for a deferred optional command — disproportionate to its marginal gain over A, and requiring explicit maintainer re-confirmation. All three options must also do the shared, non-negotiable doc fix the finding demands: enumerate the full subcommand set in ONE §7 block with explicit per-row bucket + cwd/global scope annotations. Per the workflow rules this is a maintainer decision: recommend A, but the choice between A (clean split, one annotated exception) and B (umbrella + full annotation) is the maintainer's call, and any move of protect (Option C) requires explicit maintainer approval.

options.title: Reconcile template sharing verbs: ADR-0018 D2 (templates on the full 2×2) vs ADR-0019 D7 (templates scaffold-only, no live reference), and make §7 + cmd-template.sh consistent with the chosen reading

options.name: Option A (RECOMMENDED) — Keep templates on the full 2×2; add the missing §7 Templates row + template verbs; document that D7 governs the SCAFFOLDED OUTPUT, not the template's distribution channel
options.description: Affirm ADR-0018 D2/D3 unchanged: templates are a reusable-library unit that lives in ~/.cco/templates and is distributed via the sharing repo with the full 2x2 (publish|install + export|import). Resolve the apparent D2-vs-D7 contradiction by stating explicitly that the two ADRs address DIFFERENT lifecycle stages: D2/D3 = the template artifact's DISTRIBUTION (how a template reaches another machine/user, exactly like a pack); D7 = the SCAFFOLDED OUTPUT's lifecycle (the project/pack created via `cco <res> create --template` carries NO coordinate back to the template and never auto-updates). The 'no live reference' D7 denies is the scaffold->template link in project.yml/pack.yml, not the template's own publish/install channel. Concretely: (1) add an explicit §7 row 'Sharing (templates): cco template publish|install (sharing repo) + export|import (tar)' marked Build-new; (2) keep the §7.530 combined 'packs/templates' Sharing row but make the template surface visible (list the four verbs); (3) add a one-line scope note to design.md §6.2 and to ADR-0019 D7 (or a cross-ref in ADR-0018 D2) clarifying the distribution-vs-scaffold-output distinction so the two ADRs are non-contradictory; (4) E wires cco template publish/install/export/import into cmd-template.sh, reusing the pack sharing path (cmd-pack.sh publish/install + remote.sh + sync-before-publish), since templates and packs share the identical sharing-repo layout (D3) and provenance/update machinery (D5). Templates deliberately do NOT get a referenced-resource coordinate in any consuming manifest (P14 stays packs/repos/llms only), preserving D7 verbatim.
pros: Preserves the symmetric 2x2 (P13/ADR-0018 D2) and the packs/ + templates/ sharing-repo layout (ADR-0018 D3) without re-litigating a settled decision — the safest path against 'never contradict settled decisions'.

Resolves the contradiction by DISAMBIGUATION rather than by overturning either ADR: D2 (distribution) and D7 (scaffold-output lifecycle) are shown to be orthogonal, so neither needs to be weakened or reversed.

Maximal code reuse for E: template sharing rides the exact same sharing-repo machinery as packs (structure-based discovery already enumerates templates/*/ per D3; remote.sh, sync-before-publish, source/remote_cache already exist), so the net-new surface is verb wiring, not new transport.

Closes the 2x2 completeness gap the finding raises: §7 gains an explicit, visible template verb surface that E can read directly; cmd-template.sh gets a clear build target (publish/install/export/import).

Keeps the consumer mental model crisp: templates ARE shareable libraries you can publish/install like packs; what differs from packs is only that the scaffolded artifact keeps no coordinate (an intentional, documented property, not a missing feature).
cons: Largest net-new build of the three options: four new template verbs to wire (though heavily reusing pack code).

Requires a small clarifying edit to ADR-0019 D7 (or a cross-ref in ADR-0018 D2) — touching an accepted ADR (allowed as a clarification, not a reversal, but must be done carefully and flagged as 'refines, does not supersede').
Templates having publish/install but no live coordinate is a subtlety that must be documented well or it re-reads as the same tension later (mitigated by the explicit scope note).
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P13 (sharing path follows role; template = reusable library, same path as packs) and P14 (referenced-resource model stays packs/repos/llms only — templates explicitly excluded, exactly per D7). Honors ADR-0018 D2/D3 verbatim and ADR-0019 D7 verbatim by separating distribution from scaffold-output lifecycle. Consistent with ADR-0012 (structure-based discovery already covers templates/). Reuses ADR-0013 D7 / ADR-0018 D5 provenance+merge machinery (DRY). No strain on P15/P16 (a published template is a working-copy with a source like a pack; scaffolded output is a one-shot copy with no upstream, which is precisely what D7 asserts).

options.name: Option B — Templates use export/import ONLY; publish/install is packs-only; correct ADR-0018 D2/D3 and §6.2 accordingly
options.description: Take ADR-0019 D7 ('scaffold-only, no live reference') as the dominant intent and conclude that a 'live source -> updatable via cco update' channel makes no sense for templates (D7 explicitly says a scaffolded project does NOT receive template updates, and even framework template files update via the opinionated channel, not a 'template update'). Therefore restrict templates to the SNAPSHOT half of the 2x2 only: cco template export|import (tar). Remove templates from the publish/install row. Edits: (1) ADR-0018 D2 'publish<->install: packs/templates only' -> 'packs only'; D2 'export<->import: packs/templates and projects' stays; (2) ADR-0018 D3 sharing-repo layout drops templates/ (packs/ only) OR keeps templates/ but documents it as reachable by install only if a future need arises — since with no publish there is nothing to discover; (3) design.md §6.2 'Packs/templates use the full 2x2' -> 'Packs use the full 2x2; templates use export/import (snapshot) only'; (4) §7 Sharing row -> 'packs: publish|install + export|import; templates: export|import; projects: export|import'; (5) E wires cco template export|import only.
pros: Internally the most consistent with D7's literal claim that templates have no live/updatable relationship — a published+installed template that can never be updated is arguably a publish/install with no payoff.
Smaller build surface (two verbs, both snapshot) and no need to invent a template-update story.
Eliminates the awkwardness of 'publish/install but no coordinate' that Option A must document.
cons: DIRECTLY CONTRADICTS settled ADR-0018 D2 ('packs/templates only' for publish/install) and D3 (sharing-repo layout is packs/ + templates/) — this is a reversal of two accepted decisions, which the task forbids unless the maintainer deliberately supersedes them.

Breaks the symmetric 2x2 (P13) for templates, reintroducing exactly the kind of surface asymmetry ADR-0018 worked to settle — and a NEW asymmetry (template vs pack) that has weaker role justification than the project-vs-pack one.

Loses a real use case: a maintainer wants to publish a curated project/pack TEMPLATE to a sharing repo so a team can `cco template install` it and then scaffold from it. Export/import-only forces ad-hoc tar passing for templates while packs get the repo channel.
ADR-0018 D3's structure-based discovery already enumerates templates/*/; dropping templates/ from the layout wastes that and contradicts the merge-on-existing publish design.
options.principleAlignment: Strains P13 (templates and packs are both reusable libraries with no intrinsic code remote — same role -> same sharing path; demoting templates to snapshot-only splits the role class without role-based justification). DIRECTLY conflicts with ADR-0018 D2/D3 as written (a supersession, not a clarification). Honors ADR-0019 D7's literal wording most tightly. Neutral on P14/P15/P16. Because it overturns accepted, non-superseded decisions, it should only be chosen if the maintainer explicitly re-opens ADR-0018 D2/D3.

options.name: Option C — Templates get publish/install/export/import but as PURE SNAPSHOTS (no `cco update`/source coordinate), formalizing 'distributable but never live-updatable'
options.description: Middle path: keep all four verbs for templates (satisfying ADR-0018 D2/D3's surface and the §7 packs/templates row) BUT explicitly classify cco template publish/install as a snapshot-style transfer that records NO `source` and is NOT a `cco update` participant — i.e. install copies the template into ~/.cco/templates as an authored-here artifact (no coordinate, P15 absence-of-coordinate = source). This reconciles D7 by making the 'no live reference' apply uniformly: not just to the scaffolded output, but to the installed template itself, which is never tracked for updates. Edits: (1) add §7 template Sharing row with all four verbs, annotated 'snapshot lifecycle (no cco update participation)'; (2) refine ADR-0018 D2 note that 'publish<->install: live source updatable' applies to PACKS; templates publish/install share the transport but are snapshot-lifecycle (cross-ref D7); (3) E wires the four verbs but skips source/remote_cache recording for templates and excludes them from `cco update --check`.
pros: Keeps the full 2x2 surface and packs/+templates/ layout (ADR-0018 D2/D3) so no layout/discovery rework — structure-based discovery still enumerates templates/.
Honors D7 strongly and uniformly: the installed template, like the scaffolded output, carries no live/update relationship — one consistent 'templates are never live' rule.
Gives the maintainer the publish-a-template-to-a-team use case (install via sharing repo) that Option B loses, without inventing a template-update story.
Aligns with P15 (absence of coordinate = authored-here source): an installed template legitimately has no upstream coordinate, so it is its own source — consistent.
cons: Partially strains ADR-0018 D2's exact wording: D2 lumps 'publish<->install: packs/templates only. Live source -> updatable via cco update.' Making templates publish/install but NON-updatable carves an exception inside D2 (a refinement/clarification, milder than Option B's reversal but still an edit to an accepted ADR).

Introduces a third lifecycle flavor (live-updatable packs vs snapshot-via-tar vs snapshot-via-repo for templates) — more nuance for the user to hold than Option A's clean 'templates ship like packs, scaffold output is one-shot'.

`cco template publish` that pushes a snapshot to a sharing repo but records no source is close in spirit to export-to-a-repo — a reviewer may ask why it isn't just export/import (Option B's argument resurfaces).
More E special-casing (exclude templates from update --check / source tracking) than Option A, which simply reuses the pack path wholesale.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P13 (templates keep the reusable-library sharing path) and P14 (no manifest coordinate for templates). Most explicitly aligned with P15 (installed template = authored-here, no coordinate = source). Refines (does not reverse) ADR-0018 D2 by scoping its 'live/updatable' clause to packs; honors ADR-0019 D7 uniformly. Slightly strains the 'reuse the pack machinery wholesale' efficiency that Option A gets, since template publish/install must opt out of source/remote_cache (ADR-0013 D7 / ADR-0018 D5).

options.recommended: Option A (RECOMMENDED) — Keep templates on the full 2×2; add the missing §7 Templates row + template verbs; document that D7 governs the SCAFFOLDED OUTPUT, not the template's distribution channel
options.rationale: The finding reads ADR-0018 D2 and ADR-0019 D7 as contradictory, but they address different lifecycle stages and are reconcilable without overturning either: D2/D3 govern how the template ARTIFACT is distributed (a reusable library, identical sharing path to packs, P13); D7 governs the SCAFFOLDED OUTPUT (the created project/pack carries no coordinate back to the template and never auto-updates). The 'no live reference' D7 denies is the scaffold->template link in a consuming manifest — not the template's own publish/install channel. Option A is therefore the only option that respects BOTH accepted ADRs verbatim: it disambiguates rather than supersedes. It also closes the actual gap the finding cares about — the invisible template surface in §7 and cmd-template.sh — by adding an explicit Templates Sharing row and tasking E to wire the four verbs, which it can do almost entirely by reusing the pack sharing path (ADR-0018 D3 already enumerates templates/ in structure-based discovery; remote.sh, sync-before-publish, and the source/remote_cache merge machinery already exist). Options B and C both require editing accepted ADRs: B is a hard REVERSAL of D2/D3 (forbidden unless the maintainer explicitly re-opens them) and reintroduces a role-unjustified pack/template asymmetry that strains P13; C is a milder refinement but carves an exception inside D2's 'live-updatable' clause and adds a third lifecycle flavor plus E special-casing for little gain over A. The only ADR touch Option A needs is a one-line clarifying cross-reference (distribution vs scaffold-output), explicitly framed as 'refines, does not supersede' — the lightest possible change to make the design self-consistent. This is a maintainer decision because the publish-a-template-to-a-team use case and the distribution-vs-scaffold-output framing are UX/interface choices not derivable from code (P10 method-lesson b: maintainer confirmation required on interface/sharing-strategy choices).

options.title: cco config coords --sync/--sanitize need a pinned conflict-resolution rule (no auto-elected winner) to preserve P12 "consistency by tooling, no implicit authority"

options.name: Explicit source unit, never auto-elect (--from <unit> or interactive pick), mirroring cco sync --from
options.description: Pin the semantics of cco config coords --sync/--sanitize: when units disagree on the same id, the user MUST name the authoritative coordinate, never the tool. Two equivalent ways to supply it: (1) --from <unit> — read the coordinate for <id> from <unit>'s manifest and write it into every other unit that references <id>; (2) interactive pick — coords --sync (no --from) lists the distinct coordinate values per id with the units holding each, and the user selects which value to propagate. coords --diff stays strictly read-only/advisory (lists divergent units, exit-code only, no writes). --sanitize is the same bulk-edit verb scoped to normalization (e.g. canonicalizing url form) and likewise refuses to pick a winner — it normalizes only deterministic, value-preserving differences and defers genuine value disagreements to the same --from/pick path. Document explicitly: --diff = read-only reconnaissance; --sync/--sanitize = user-directed bulk edits, NOT a reconciliation algorithm. This is the proposedResolution, made symmetric with the already-decided cco sync --from / cco start --from source-selection model (ADR-0017 D2, design §4.2). Owner: this pins the contract in ADR-0016 D3 / the S-handoff S4; mechanism stays with S/E.
pros: Directly upholds P12 and ADR-0016 D3's stated invariant: 'consistency by tooling, not storage, no global source of truth' — an explicit user-named source establishes no PERSISTENT authority (it is a one-shot edit), so no de-facto winner emerges.

Reuses an already-settled, maintainer-confirmed pattern (cco sync --from, cco start --from, ADR-0017 D2): same --from semantics across sync/start/coords → minimal new surface, consistent mental model, no new vocabulary to learn.

Keeps --diff cleanly read-only/advisory (matches ADR-0017 D1's 'integrity check warns, never enforces' philosophy and ADR-0008's reminder-not-gate stance) while making the write verbs honest about being user-directed bulk edits.
Prevents E from silently inventing a winner-selection heuristic (alphabetical / first-scanned / newest-mtime) — exactly the 'silent de-facto authority' the finding warns about.
Symmetry: the user already learns 'positional = target, --from = source, default source = cwd' for sync; coords inherits it for free, including the cwd-default source when invoked from a unit dir.
cons: Requires the user to make a choice every time units genuinely disagree (no one-keystroke 'just make them all consistent') — slightly more friction than an auto-pick would give, but this friction is the point (it surfaces the divergence as a conscious decision).

--from <unit> for coords needs a sensible default-source rule (cwd unit, mirroring sync) plus a per-id resolution when the same invocation spans ids that disagree differently — a small spec detail to pin for S/E.

Interactive pick is harder to use non-interactively (CI); needs a documented non-interactive form (--from <unit> is that form), so the contract must state --from is the scriptable path and bare --sync is interactive-only.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P12 (consistency by tooling not storage; no global source of truth; no implicit authority) and ADR-0016 D3 (the exact invariant the finding cites). Consistent with ADR-0017 D2 (--from source-selection precedent, sync/start symmetry) and ADR-0017 D1 + ADR-0008 (advisory/warn-not-enforce, reminder-not-gate → keeps --diff read-only). Respects P1 (the value is user knowledge; the CLI is merely the editor). Strains nothing; it fills a deferred impl-detail without contradicting any settled decision.

options.name: --from <unit> mandatory for any write; drop the bare interactive-pick form
options.description: Stricter variant of the recommended option: coords --sync and coords --sanitize REQUIRE an explicit --from <unit> (or operate on the cwd unit as the implicit source, exactly like cco sync's default). There is no interactive 'list values and pick' mode for writes at all — to propagate a coordinate you always name the source manifest. coords --diff remains the read-only advisory listing. This makes the write verbs purely scriptable and removes any interactive winner-selection UI; if a user wants to compare before choosing, they run --diff first, then --sync --from <chosen-unit>.
pros: Maximally faithful to the cco sync --from model: source is ALWAYS an explicit unit (or cwd), identical to design §4.2 — zero divergence from the settled sync contract.
Fully scriptable / CI-friendly by construction (no interactive-only path); --diff (read) + --sync --from (write) compose cleanly into a hook or pipeline.
Smallest possible new semantics surface — one rule ('source is a named unit, like sync'), nothing else to specify; lowest risk of E inventing anything.
Strongest guarantee against implicit authority: there is no code path that could ever auto-elect, because the only input is a user-named source.
cons: Less discoverable for an interactive user who doesn't yet know which unit holds the 'right' coordinate: they must run --diff, read it, then re-invoke with --from — a two-step dance the recommended option collapses into one interactive prompt.

Slightly more rigid than the maintainer's own pattern elsewhere: ADR-0017 D2 pairs --from with an interactive fallback (resolve-now prompt), so dropping the interactive pick is marginally less consistent with cco resolve/start UX than the recommended option.
Pushes all 'which value is right?' cognition onto --diff's text output rather than a guided selector — fine for power users, rougher for casual ones.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P12 and ADR-0016 D3 as strongly as the recommended option (no auto-elect; user names the source). Maximally consistent with ADR-0017 D2 / design §4.2 (--from = source). Slightly less aligned with ADR-0017 D2's interactive-fallback ergonomics (resolve prompt) than the recommended option, which is why it is the runner-up rather than the pick. Respects P1, ADR-0008 (advisory --diff).

options.name: Keep --sync/--sanitize semantics deferred to S/E; pin only a guard-rail constraint now (no persisted authority, must be user-directed)
options.description: Do not fully specify the command UX in the ADRs now. Instead record a single binding CONSTRAINT in ADR-0016 D3 / the S-handoff: 'cco config coords --sync/--sanitize MUST be user-directed (explicit source or interactive selection) and MUST NOT auto-elect a winner or persist any unit as authoritative; --diff MUST stay read-only.' Leave the concrete --from-vs-interactive choice to S (which owns the coordinate CLI mechanism per ADR-0016 §Open and S-handoff S4). This treats F48 as the nit it is classified as: pin the invariant that protects P12, defer the surface.
pros: Matches the finding's own severity (nit, decision deferred to S/E) and the ADRs' explicit 'mechanism → S/E' boundary — does not pull mechanism decisions forward into a taxonomy ADR.
Still fully closes the actual risk the finding names ('E will invent a winner → silent de-facto authority') by making 'no auto-elect, no persisted authority' a hard constraint S/E must honor.
Minimal documentation churn now; lets S design the coords CLI holistically alongside coords-lookup, cco repo/llms add, and cco config validate (all S4) rather than pinning one verb's UX in isolation.
Preserves design optionality: if S finds a better UX than --from (e.g. a guided 3-way picker reusing update's merge UI), it is not boxed out by a prematurely frozen surface.
cons: Leaves the user-facing UX unspecified, so two reviewers/implementers could still build subtly different surfaces — the constraint bounds the SEMANTICS but not the ergonomics, so the symmetry-with-sync win of the recommended option is only a recommendation, not a decision.

A bare constraint is easy to under-implement: 'user-directed' without naming --from risks an interactive-only tool that's hard to script in CI (the validate-hook use case), unless S remembers to add a scriptable form.
Defers a question that is cheap to settle now given the clear cco sync --from precedent — arguably over-cautious for a gap whose answer the codebase already models.
Slightly weaker continuity for future sessions: 'pinned to --from like sync' is a more self-explanatory ADR note than 'must be user-directed', which still invites re-discussion of the exact form.
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P12 / ADR-0016 D3 (the no-implicit-authority invariant becomes binding). Consistent with ADR-0016 §Open and S-handoff S4 (mechanism owned by S/E) and ADR-0011 method-lesson (fix nature/constraint now, hand mechanism to the owning analysis). Neutral on ADR-0017 D2 symmetry (neither adopts nor precludes the --from surface). Respects ADR-0008 (advisory --diff). Does not strain any settled decision; it simply pins less than the recommended option.

options.recommended: Explicit source unit, never auto-elect (--from <unit> or interactive pick), mirroring cco sync --from
options.rationale: The recommended option pins exactly the invariant P12/ADR-0016 D3 already assert ('consistency by tooling, not storage; no global source of truth; no implicit authority') by routing every write through a user-named source — a one-shot bulk edit, never a persisted winner — so no de-facto authority can emerge. Crucially it reuses an ALREADY-SETTLED, maintainer-confirmed pattern: cco sync --from and cco start --from (ADR-0017 D2, design §4.2) establish 'positional = target, --from = source, default = cwd, never auto-elect'. Inheriting that for coords --sync/--sanitize costs no new vocabulary and gives sync/start/coords one consistent mental model, while keeping --diff strictly read-only/advisory (aligned with ADR-0017 D1's warn-not-enforce and ADR-0008's reminder-not-gate). It directly forecloses the finding's risk — E inventing an alphabetical/first-scanned/newest-mtime winner. Option B (mandatory --from, no interactive pick) is the close runner-up: equally safe and maximally scriptable, but it drops the interactive fallback that ADR-0017 D2 pairs with --from elsewhere, making it marginally less consistent with cco resolve/start UX, so it is listed second. Option C (defer, pin only a guard-rail constraint) honors the nit severity and the 'mechanism → S/E' boundary and is a legitimate fallback if the maintainer prefers not to freeze the surface in a taxonomy ADR, but given the clean cco sync --from precedent the answer is cheap to settle now, and a concrete '--from like sync' note gives future sessions less to re-discuss than a bare 'must be user-directed' constraint. None of the three contradicts any settled, non-superseded decision; all three keep --diff read-only and forbid auto-election. Relevant files: /workspace/claude-orchestrator/docs/maintainer/configuration/decentralized-config/decisions/0016-consolidated-resource-taxonomy.md (D3 lines 116-126, §Open line 374), /workspace/claude-orchestrator/docs/maintainer/configuration/decentralized-config/decisions/0017-coordinate-cli-lifecycle-refinements.md (D2 lines 63-82, D1 lines 50-61), /workspace/claude-orchestrator/docs/maintainer/configuration/decentralized-config/design.md (§2.2 lines 261-271, §4.2 lines 359-365), /workspace/claude-orchestrator/docs/maintainer/configuration/decentralized-config/guiding-principles.md (P12 lines 193-225), and the S-handoff S4 at /workspace/claude-orchestrator/docs/maintainer/configuration/decentralized-config/S-handoff-sharing-unification.md line 56.

options.title: CACHE llms name-collision: re-fetch on url-mismatch vs key by url-hash vs warn-and-reuse

options.name: Option A (RECOMMENDED) — Keep name-keyed CACHE + re-fetch-on-url-mismatch (last-writer-per-machine, with notice)
options.description: Keep the existing name-keyed CACHE layout `<cache>/cco/llms/<name>/` and its `.cco/source` (which already records `resolved_url`). On every llms resolve for a consuming unit, compare the unit's requested coordinate (the manifest `url`+`variant`, resolved to `resolved_url`) against the cached `resolved_url`. If they differ → re-fetch from the requesting unit's url and overwrite the name-keyed entry (last writer wins per machine), emitting a single-line notice (e.g. 'llms <name>: coordinate changed (<cached-host> → <requested-host>), re-fetched'). If they match → reuse the cached content with no network. The cache stays a per-machine, regenerable, deduped-by-name materialization of whatever coordinate the currently-resolving unit declares. Cross-unit divergence (two units, same name, different url) is surfaced — not silently resolved — by the already-specified `cco config coords --diff` (ADR-0016 D3) and the opt-in `cco config validate` hook (D9); the cache mechanism itself simply never serves a stale/foreign url's content to a unit that asked for a different one.
pros: Guarantees correctness: a unit never silently runs with another unit's docs in context — the cited correctness-fork risk ('wrong docs in context') is closed by construction.

Zero schema/layout change: CACHE stays name-keyed exactly as design.md §2.2 (line 166) and ADR-0016 D2/D7 already specify ('name-keyed, deduped per-machine'); `resolved_url` is the disambiguator the ADR already says records for this purpose (D2 note, line 113-114). No migration, minimal new code.

Preserves per-machine dedup: the common case (all units agree on the coordinate, the package.json norm) needs exactly one download and one cache entry — the 'expensive datum stays globally deduplicated per machine' goal of D2 is upheld.
Cheap and self-healing: content is regenerable (CACHE, `never`-synced), so overwrite has low blast radius; a wrong overwrite is corrected on the next resolve of the other unit.

Divergence is not hidden — it is routed to the purpose-built tooling (`coords --diff`, `config validate`) rather than to the cache, keeping 'coherence by tooling, not by storage' (D3) intact: the cache is not asked to encode policy about which url is 'right'.
cons: Cache thrash if two co-mounted units genuinely pin the same name to different urls: each resolve of the 'other' unit re-fetches and overwrites — repeated network cost and churn until the user reconciles the divergence (mitigated by the notice + coords --diff nudging them to fix it; truly-intended divergence is the rarer case the model calls 'a user-level naming choice').

Within a single `cco start` that mounts two units referencing the same name with different urls, only the last-resolved content is on disk — the two cannot coexist (acceptable: a single workspace with one logical name resolving to two different doc bodies is itself a config smell that validate flags).
Requires resolve to read+compare `resolved_url` before serving cache (a small added step in the start/resolve path).
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P6 (cache stays internal/hidden, never config), P12 / ADR-0014 D2 (content→CACHE re-fetchable; coordinate is the config source of truth; cache is a materialization of the resolving unit's coordinate), ADR-0016 D2 note (uses the recorded `resolved_url` as the disambiguator exactly as written; honors 'name-keyed, deduped per-machine'), ADR-0016 D3 (divergence detection stays in tooling, not storage — no global source of truth introduced), P15/ADR-0019 (the local copy is governed by the live coordinate, never becomes its own source — so it must track the requesting unit's url). Strains nothing; it is the literal realization of the ADR's deferred 'cache-state records resolved_url to disambiguate' note. Aligns with ADR-0008 reminder/notice philosophy (notify, never block).

options.name: Option B — Key the CACHE directory by name + short-hash(resolved_url)
options.description: Change the CACHE layout to `<cache>/cco/llms/<name>-<short-hash(resolved_url)>/` (or `<name>/<short-hash>/`) so a same-name-different-url collision becomes two physically distinct entries, both retained. Resolve computes the hash of the requesting unit's resolved coordinate and reads/writes the matching sub-entry; a true coordinate match always hits the same entry (still deduped per distinct url). `coords-lookup` and `coords --diff` still surface divergence at the config layer. No overwrite, no thrash; both doc bodies coexist on disk.
pros: No re-fetch thrash and no last-writer ambiguity: each distinct url keeps its own materialization, so two co-mounted units that genuinely diverge each get their correct content within the same workspace.
Still correct: a unit only ever reads the entry hashed from its own coordinate — it can never see a foreign url's content.
Deduplication is preserved at the granularity of (name, url) rather than name alone — arguably the more precise dedup key.
Robust to concurrent resolves of divergent units (no clobbering).
options.principleAlignment: Upholds P6 (still internal/hidden CACHE) and P12/ADR-0014 (content materialized from coordinate). It does NOT contradict any settled decision, but it strains the letter of ADR-0016 D2/D7 and design.md §2.2 line 166, which explicitly specify the CACHE path as name-keyed (`llms/<name>/`, 'name-keyed, deduped per-machine') and call hashing unnecessary because content is re-fetchable; choosing B reverses that stated stance, so it should be recorded as a deliberate refinement of D7's layout rather than a neutral pick. Neutral-to-positive on D3 (divergence still tooling-detected).
cons: Contradicts the explicit name-keyed layout in ADR-0016 D7 / design.md §2.2 (line 166: `llms/<name>/`) — adopting it means amending a settled byte-level layout, not just resolving the open note.

Adds a hashing/path-mangling mechanism the ADR deliberately avoided ('low-stakes since content is re-fetchable' was the reason name-keying was deemed fine); more code in the cache-path resolver, and `cco llms list`/`show`/`update`/`clean` and the `_llms_*` helpers must all become hash-aware.
Accumulates orphaned entries when a coordinate changes (old hash dir lingers until `cco clean`), partially negating the dedup benefit and needing GC.

Encodes coordinate policy into storage (which url 'is' which) — mild tension with D3's 'coherence by tooling not storage', since the cache now structurally distinguishes urls instead of leaving that to the config layer.

options.name: Option C — Warn-and-reuse (serve cached content on url-mismatch, emit a warning, do not re-fetch)
options.description: Keep name-keyed CACHE. On resolve, if the requesting unit's coordinate differs from the cached `resolved_url`, do NOT re-fetch: serve the existing cached content but emit a warning ('llms <name>: this unit pins <requested-url> but cache holds content for <cached-url>; run `cco config coords --diff` / `cco llms update <name>` to reconcile'). The user explicitly re-fetches via `cco llms update`.
pros: Cheapest at resolve time — never re-downloads automatically; fastest `cco start`.
Stable cache (no thrash, no clobbering between divergent units).
Fully delegates the divergence decision to the user, consistent with the 'never auto-fabricate' ethos of sync (ADR-0008).
options.principleAlignment: Partially aligns with ADR-0008 'notify, don't act' philosophy and D3 (tooling reconciles). But it directly strains the finding's core correctness concern and arguably P12/ADR-0014's model that the cached copy is a materialization of the resolving unit's coordinate: under C the cache can serve content that does NOT correspond to the requesting unit's url, i.e. the local copy stops faithfully representing that unit's coordinate (mild tension with P15: the copy is governed by the live source, but here it's governed by a different unit's source). Upholds P6.
cons: Re-introduces the exact correctness fork the finding warns about: a project can silently run with another project's llms content in context (a warning is easy to miss in a busy start log), which is the 'wrong docs' failure mode.
Warning-only is weak protection for a context-correctness issue; the wrong docs are already in the model's context by the time the user reads the warning.
Behavior depends on cache-population order (whichever unit resolved first 'owns' the content), making it non-deterministic and confusing.
Pushes a manual reconciliation step onto the user for what is a regenerable, cheap-to-fix asset — more friction than Option A for no durability benefit.

options.recommended: Option A (RECOMMENDED) — Keep name-keyed CACHE + re-fetch-on-url-mismatch (last-writer-per-machine, with notice)
options.rationale: Option A is the minimal, principle-faithful realization of what ADR-0016 D2 already deferred: it uses the `resolved_url` that the cache-state was explicitly told to record 'to disambiguate' (D2 note, lines 113-114), keeps the name-keyed layout that D7/design.md §2.2 already mandate, and adds no schema or migration. It closes the actual correctness fork the finding raises — a unit can never silently run with a foreign url's content, because a mismatch forces a re-fetch — while keeping per-machine dedup for the overwhelmingly common case where all units agree on the coordinate (the package.json norm the model assumes). It honors P12/ADR-0014 (the cached copy is a regenerable materialization of the *resolving* unit's coordinate), P15 (the local copy tracks the live source, never becomes its own), and D3 (cross-unit divergence is surfaced by `cco config coords --diff` and `cco config validate`, not encoded into storage — no global source of truth is created), and matches the ADR-0008 notify-don't-block ethos via the one-line notice. Option B (url-hash keying) is technically clean and avoids thrash, but it overturns the explicitly settled name-keyed layout and re-adds the hashing machinery the ADR deliberately rejected as unnecessary for regenerable content — it should only be chosen if real-world co-mounting of genuinely-divergent same-name units proves common (a YAGNI bet today), and would require amending D7 rather than just resolving the note. Option C is rejected because warn-and-reuse leaves the wrong-docs-in-context failure live behind an easy-to-miss warning, contradicting the finding's correctness intent. Net: pick A, record it as the resolution of ADR-0016's deferred-to-E note, and keep B as a documented fallback if divergent co-mounts become a measured problem.

uncoveredScope: EXISTING TEST SUITE TEARDOWN (highest gap): tests/ has 35 test files auto-discovered+run by bin/test:147, all built on the shared tests/helpers.sh harness which generates the OLD project.yml schema (helpers.sh:409-411 'repos:\n  - path: ... name:') and old user-config/ model. §11 only names test_vault.sh (shrink), test_vault_profiles.sh (remove), test_local_paths.sh (rewrite) — it is SILENT on test_manifest.sh (manifest removed), test_pack_publish.sh/test_pack_install.sh/test_publish_install_sync.sh (publish/install rewritten), test_project_install.sh/_enhanced.sh/test_project_publish.sh (project install/publish removed), test_remote.sh (remotes store split DATA/STATE), test_tutorial.sh (tutorial rewrite), test_docker_security.sh/test_invariants.sh/test_managed_scope.sh/test_yaml_parser.sh/test_packs.sh (old schema + user-config). The §9 'each phase leaves cco runnable + tests green' invariant is unachievable without a phased existing-test teardown/migration plan that NO doc provides. Neither the phasing lens (scoped to §9 NEW-test placement), doc-coherence (scoped to docs/, excluded tests/), nor code-grounding (explicitly deferred 'test files' adequacy to lens #2/#11', which neither owned) covered this.

CONTAINER-SIDE / ENTRYPOINT CONTRACT: config/entrypoint.sh consumes hard-coded container paths (/workspace/.managed, /workspace/setup.sh, /workspace/mcp-packages.txt, /home/claude/.claude/mcp-global.json, claude-state at /home/claude/.claude/projects/-workspace, memory child-mount). The inventory B.1 (lines 95-102) asserts entrypoint is 'model-agnostic, no change' — TRUE for the container side, but this is load-bearing on the compose HOST-source re-point: cmd-start.sh:457 claude-state→STATE, :459 memory→STATE, :495 managed→CACHE, global secrets/MCP→~/.cco. §9 BL3 says only 'convert relative ./ to host-absolute' and does NOT enumerate the per-mount BUCKET destinations (claude-state/memory→STATE, managed→CACHE, project.yml/.claude→<repo>/.cco). The entrypoint↔compose mount contract was examined by no lens (consumer-ux looked at command surface; code-grounding deep-audited cmd-start mount generation for BL3 relative-paths but not the entrypoint consumption side).

GLOBAL SECRETS / OAUTH ENV-INJECTION PATH: lib/secrets.sh load_global_secrets() reads $GLOBAL_DIR/secrets.env; called by cmd-start and cmd-new.sh:161 to inject -e env flags into the container. The design relocates global secrets to ~/.cco/secrets.env but §9 never wires the start-time env-injection re-point (GLOBAL_DIR→~/.cco). Intersects F18 (cco new) but distinct: the secrets/auth env-injection plumbing for BOTH start and new is unscheduled. No lens covered the secrets env-injection path.

LIVE AUTHORITATIVE MAINTAINER DOCS (architecture/spec.md + architecture.md): repo CLAUDE.md 'Key Files' cites docs/maintainer/architecture/spec.md (requirements) + architecture.md (ADRs) as primary. spec.md FR-2.1/FR-2.3/NFR-3.3/line 186 hardcode the centralized projects/ directory model + cco project create at the FR/requirements level (structural, not 'mechanical path swap'). The inventory C4 lists spec.md only at lines 50,91,186 as 'Light — mechanical command substitution', understating that the centralized projects/ model is abolished. These LIVE docs are distinct from the retired vault/ subtree (F30). Doc-coherence lens confirmed user-facing docs/ coverage but treated spec.md as C4-light; design-adr-req-sync focused on the decentralized-config tree's requirements.md, not the repo's architecture/spec.md.

ROADMAP + INVENTORY-C5 INTERNAL STALENESS: roadmap.md body (line 77, Vault Simplification section) still says ADRs '0001–0017' though its own header (line 4) and S-cycle reached 0001–0020; inventory C5 (line 149) instructs 'bump ADR range 0001–0006 → 0001–0010' — itself stale (predates M+S, should be 0001–0020). Doc-coherence excluded the decentralized-config tree + roadmap C5; design-adr-req-sync did not audit roadmap.md body.

NOT RE-EXAMINED (accepted, deferred-by-design, or sibling-owned and adequately found): proxy/ Go code (model-agnostic, confirmed); cmd-vault.sh internals beyond migration-reader (138KB legacy, to be deleted); the 58 deduped findings themselves are well-sourced and I did not re-litigate them; T/post-v1 state-sync; the byte-format of sync-meta fingerprint (F39 covers the semantic).

additionalFindings.title: Existing test suite (35 files + shared helpers.sh harness on the old schema) has no teardown/migration plan — the §9 'tests green per phase' invariant is unachievable as specified
additionalFindings.location: design.md §11:722-733 + §9:605 ('each phase leaves cco runnable + tests green'); tests/helpers.sh:402-411 (generates old 'repos:\n  - path:' schema + user-config model); bin/test:147 (auto-runs ALL tests/test_*.sh); tests/{test_manifest,test_pack_publish,test_pack_install,test_publish_install_sync,test_project_install,test_project_install_enhanced,test_project_publish,test_remote,test_tutorial,test_docker_security,test_invariants,test_managed_scope,test_yaml_parser,test_packs}.sh
additionalFindings.issue: bin/test auto-discovers and runs all 35 tests/test_*.sh; the shared helpers.sh emits the OLD project.yml schema (repos: - path:/name:, the exact shape F5 says the new parser breaks) and old user-config/ paths, so EVERY test depends on the removed model through the harness. §11's table names only test_vault.sh (shrink), test_vault_profiles.sh (remove), test_local_paths.sh (rewrite) for the WHOLE cutover. It is silent on the dozen-plus tests that exercise REMOVED surfaces: test_manifest.sh (manifest deleted, ADR-0012), test_pack_publish/test_pack_install/test_publish_install_sync (publish/install rewritten with sync-before-publish + structure discovery), test_project_install(_enhanced)/test_project_publish (project install/publish removed, ADR-0018 D2), test_remote (remotes store split DATA/STATE, M3), test_tutorial (tutorial rewrite, F21), and test_docker_security/test_invariants/test_managed_scope/test_yaml_parser/test_packs which hard-code the old schema via helpers.sh. There is no phased plan to rewrite helpers.sh's project.yml generator, delete the removed-surface tests, or keep the suite green through each cutover phase.

additionalFindings.title: §9 BL3 'absolute mounts' under-specifies the per-mount BUCKET destinations, and the compose↔entrypoint container-path contract is not stated as an invariant
additionalFindings.location: design.md §9 Phase 0 BL3:615-619; lib/cmd-start.sh:441-543 (claude-state:457, memory:459, mcp-global:465, managed:495, policy.json:542); config/entrypoint.sh:93-95,139,189,197 (/workspace/.managed, /workspace/setup.sh, /home/claude/.claude/projects/-workspace consumers); resource-coherence-inventory.md:95-102 (B.1 'entrypoint: no change')
additionalFindings.issue: BL3 says only 'convert every framework compose mount source from relative ./… to host-absolute and pick the compose base dir (STATE)'. But the cutover ALSO re-homes each mount to a DIFFERENT bucket: cmd-start.sh:457 claude-state (./.cco/claude-state) → STATE, :459 memory (./memory) → STATE, :495 managed (./.cco/managed) → CACHE, :465 global mcp + global secrets → ~/.cco, :454-455 .claude/project.yml → <repo>/.cco. The inventory B.1 correctly says config/entrypoint.sh itself needs no change because it reads CONTAINER paths (/workspace/.managed, /home/claude/.claude/projects/-workspace) — but that correctness is LOAD-BEARING on the compose generator re-pointing every HOST source to the right bucket AND preserving the exact container-side targets the entrypoint hard-codes (e.g. the memory child-mount nested under claude-state, the /workspace/.managed dir the MCP-merge + browser/policy logic scans). §9 states neither the per-mount bucket map nor the compose↔entrypoint target-path invariant, so an implementer changing the bucket layout could break the entrypoint's /workspace/.managed or memory child-mount contract silently. (F55 touches the relative-path mechanics as a nit but does not surface the bucket-destination enumeration or the entrypoint dependency.)

additionalFindings.title: Global secrets.env and OAuth env-injection path (used by both cco start and cco new) is not re-pointed to ~/.cco anywhere in §9
additionalFindings.location: lib/secrets.sh:109-112 (load_global_secrets reads $GLOBAL_DIR/secrets.env); lib/cmd-new.sh:160-161; lib/cmd-start.sh (load_global_secrets + get_oauth_token env injection); design.md §2.3:201 (~/.cco/secrets.env) + §9 (no mention)
additionalFindings.issue: load_global_secrets() injects -e flags into the container from $GLOBAL_DIR/secrets.env at session start (both cmd-start and cmd-new.sh:161). The design relocates global secrets to ~/.cco/secrets.env (§2.3:201) but §9 never schedules re-pointing the env-injection plumbing (GLOBAL_DIR→~/.cco) for either entry point. This is distinct from the COMMIT/allowlist handling of ~/.cco/secrets.env (covered) — it is the RUNTIME env-injection into the container. Unaddressed, the secret loading either reads the wrong (removed) GLOBAL_DIR path or is silently skipped, so global secrets never reach the session. Intersects F18 (cco new survival) but applies to cco start regardless of cco new's fate.

additionalFindings.title: docs/maintainer/architecture/spec.md and architecture.md (the repo's CLAUDE.md-cited authoritative maintainer docs) assert the centralized projects/ model at the FR/requirements level and are misclassified C4-light
additionalFindings.location: resource-coherence-inventory.md:120 (architecture.md = C2 heavy) and :139 (spec.md = C4 light, only lines 50,91,186); docs/maintainer/architecture/spec.md:48 (FR-2.1 'Projects are defined as directories under projects/'), :141 (NFR-3.3), :186 (user story 'cco project create'); CLAUDE.md 'Key Files' cites both as primary maintainer docs
additionalFindings.issue: spec.md FR-2.1/FR-2.3/NFR-3.3/line-186 define the entire centralized projects/-directory + cco project create requirements model that the decentralized cutover abolishes (projects are now in-repo <repo>/.cco, entry = init/join/migrate). The inventory C4 treats spec.md as 'Light — mechanical command substitution' citing only project-create lines, understating that the FRs assert a structural model (parallel to the F33 concepts.md/knowledge-packs.md misclassification). The repo CLAUDE.md 'Key Files' section points implementers to spec.md (requirements) and architecture.md (ADRs) FIRST; following spec.md's FRs, an implementer reads a requirements doc describing the removed centralized model. These are LIVE authoritative maintainer docs, distinct from the retired vault/ subtree (F30).

additionalFindings.title: roadmap.md body and inventory C5 carry stale ADR ranges (0001–0017 / instruction '→0010') after the S cycle reached 0001–0020
additionalFindings.issue: roadmap.md line 4 header correctly says ADRs 0005–0020, but the Vault Simplification section body (line 77) still says decisions are 'recorded in ADRs 0001–0017' — predates S (0018-0020). The inventory C5 reconcile instruction (line 149) says 'bump ADR range 0001–0006 → 0001–0010', itself stale (predates M+S; should target 0001–0020). Both are internal-doc coherence lags the doc-coherence lens missed (it excluded the decentralized-config tree + did not audit roadmap C5) and design-adr-req-sync missed (did not audit roadmap.md body). Recoverable, reader-confusion only.

critic.confidence: High. All five additional findings are source-verified against the actual files: tests/helpers.sh:402-411 generates the old schema, bin/test:147 auto-runs all 35 tests, §11 names only 3 of them; config/entrypoint.sh hard-codes container paths that the compose HOST-source bucket-split must preserve; lib/secrets.sh load_global_secrets reads GLOBAL_DIR; spec.md FR-2.1/186 assert the centralized model; roadmap.md:77 + inventory:149 carry stale ADR ranges. The dedup set (58 findings) is already very comprehensive and high-quality — it captured the §9/§11 NEW-test gap (F2/F7), the requirements.md drift (F3), the parser rewrites (F5), the entrypoint-adjacent BL3 (F55 nit), and cco new (F18). My additions are the cross-cutting concerns that fell strictly BETWEEN those lenses: (1) the EXISTING test SUITE + shared harness teardown (the single most material new gap — a hard blocker on the 'tests green per phase' invariant that no lens owned), (2) the compose↔entrypoint container-path contract + per-mount bucket map (BL3 is under-specified beyond relative-paths), (3) the runtime global-secrets env-injection re-point, (4) the LIVE maintainer spec.md/architecture.md FR-level model (vs the retired vault/ subtree), and (5) minor internal-doc ADR-range staleness. I rate the test-suite finding HIGH (it directly defeats a stated §9 invariant and was genuinely uncovered); the rest medium/nit. I could not exhaustively line-audit cmd-vault.sh (138KB) or run any code (read-only gate), but the structural conclusions hold from the harness, dispatcher, and design-doc evidence.

coverage.coverageNotes: EXAMINED: all 20 ADRs (0001-0020) in full; guiding-principles.md (P1-P17); design.md (§1-§13) in full; requirements.md (AD1-AD12, FR-*, §8 decisions) in full; resource-coherence-inventory.md header + legend; analysis-roadmap.md statuses. Traced every named refinement chain — tags 0010→0011→0015 (nature flip + DATA placement), internal/DATA 0013→0015→0016, coordinate 0014→0016→0017→0019 (central-registry→per-unit reversal), manifest 0012→0018, perms 0008→0020 — confirming each later ADR's reversal/refinement and whether it is reflected in the earlier ADR and in the living docs. Spot-verified two code-grounded claims: (a) the `cco pack publish` fast-forward clobber defect ADR-0019 D4 cites — CONFIRMED at lib/cmd-pack.sh:958 (rm -rf + replace, no 3-way merge); it is a known defect already owned by E, so NOT reported as a coherence defect. (b) ADR-0008 allowlist line does NOT contain `!tags.yml` (correctly dropped). Confirmed the project's convention is to NOT edit superseded text inside earlier Accepted ADRs (cf. requirements.md 'Decision history, do not edit'), so stale-clause findings are scoped to: living docs that lag (requirements.md), and earlier-ADR clauses lacking a forward annotation that later ADRs DID supply elsewhere. NOT COVERED by this lens (owned by sibling lenses): design↔ADR↔requirements completeness/gap sweep beyond the coherence contradictions found here; §9 phasing feasibility; ambiguity/impl-readiness of individual command specs; migration/cutover safety mechanics; deep code-grounding/feasibility of every ADR claim (only the two load-bearing ones above were verified); the resource-coherence-inventory's per-row accuracy. I did NOT find any case of two non-superseded ADRs DIRECTLY contradicting each other on a live decision, nor a decision that clashes with P1-P17 (all checked decisions are consistent with or derived from the principles — e.g. ADR-0016 D2 is derived from P5, ADR-0020 from P7/P17). The defects found are all propagation-lag (stale references in requirements.md and un-annotated clauses in early ADRs) plus one genuinely under-specified shared artifact (the ~/.cco allowlist, finding 5, which needs a maintainer decision because the three specs diverge and one would break committed skeletons).
